title: CV upload shows a 'parsing…' success toast even when the parse was never queued (Inngest dispatch failed)
description: When inngest.send('cv/uploaded') throws, uploadCVAction catches it, flips the candidate_cvs row to parsing_status='failed', but STILL returns { ok: true, candidateCvId }. The client (cv-upload.tsx) then unconditionally fires toast.success('CV uploaded — parsing…'). The row is already in the failed state, so the panel will render the amber 'CV parsing failed' alert at the same time the toast claims parsing is underway. The success message is factually wrong for the dispatch-failure path.
userImpact: Recruiter uploads a CV, sees a green 'CV uploaded — parsing…' toast, then watches the panel immediately show 'CV parsing failed. Try again.' Contradictory signals during a demo read as a flaky product. The user may also wait for parsing that never started.
suggestedFix: Return a discriminator from uploadCVAction indicating the dispatch outcome (e.g. { ok: true, queued: false, candidateCvId }) and have cv-upload.tsx show a neutral/warning toast like 'CV saved, but couldn’t start parsing — use Try again.' when queued is false, instead of the unconditional success-parsing toast.
verifierReasoning: The claim is accurate end-to-end. In src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/actions.ts, uploadCVAction's inngest.send catch block (lines 219-237) captures the error to Sentry and calls updateCandidateCVParse with status:'failed' and parseError:'Could not queue CV for parsing. Try again.', then falls through with no early return to revalidatePath(`/candidates/${candidateId}`) (line 239) and return { ok: true, candidateCvId } (line 240). updateCandidateCVParse (src/lib/db/candidate-cvs.ts:150-180) genuinely persists parsing_status='failed' to the row. The client (cv-upload.tsx:34-40) only branches on result.ok: it shows toast.error when !ok, otherwise unconditionally fires toast.success('CV uploaded — parsing…'). Because the action returns ok:true on the dispatch-failure path, the green "parsing…" toast fires. Meanwhile CvReviewPanel (cv-review-panel.tsx:264-265) routes parsing_status==='failed' to FailedState, which renders the amber "CV parsing failed." alert with a "Try again" button. revalidatePath being called before the return means the re-rendered panel reflects the failed row, so the contradictory signals coexist exactly as described. Notably, the sibling retryParseAction handles the identical dispatch-failure case correctly — its catch returns { ok:false, error:'Couldn't queue the CV for parsing…' } (line 317) — which confirms uploadCVAction's ok:true return is an inconsistency, not an intentional design, and the suggested fix (return a queued discriminator and a neutral/warning toast) is the right shape. I downgraded severity from medium to low: the contradiction is real and the success copy is factually wrong, but it only surfaces when inngest.send throws (an Inngest/network outage), which is rare in normal operation. No data loss occurs (the CV is in Storage, the row is recorded, and the Retry button does appear), so the user-facing impact is misleading feedback on a degraded path rather than a broken core flow.

title: Add float has no duplicate guard — repeat submits create identical float rows
description: addFloatAction inserts an application with application_type='float', job_id=NULL. Per migration 20260520010419 the unique(candidate_id, job_id, application_type) constraint treats NULL job_id as distinct, so it does NOT block duplicate floats — that is intentional for floating one candidate to many clients. But there is no per-submission idempotency: FloatForm's button is only disabled={isPending} (covers a single in-flight request), and the action has no guard against the user pressing 'Add float' twice in succession with the same empty/identical note. Each press creates another float row with no distinguishing job, producing visually identical entries on the floats list.
userImpact: A recruiter who clicks 'Add float' twice (e.g. unsure the first click registered) ends up with two identical 'No note' float rows for the same candidate, which then both need to be manually deleted. Clutters the floats list and the global /floats view.
suggestedFix: Either (a) before inserting, check for an existing float for the same candidate created within a short window / with no note and short-circuit, or (b) accept that multiple floats are valid but make the row visibly distinct (timestamp is already shown) and rely on the disabled-while-pending button. If duplicates are genuinely desired, this can be downgraded to a non-issue — but the lack of any guard plus identical rendering is a UX trap.
verifierReasoning:
The factual mechanics of the claim all check out against the code:

1. addFloatAction (src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/floats/actions.ts:52-67) inserts application_type='float' with job_id=null and no dedup pre-check.
2. Migration 20260520010419_phase3_applications_nullable_job_id.sql confirms the unique(candidate_id, job_id, application_type) constraint treats NULL job_id as distinct, and this is EXPLICITLY intentional (constraint rationale lines 19-26, plus smoke test #3 at lines 66-70: "two floats for the same candidate — succeeds (NULL job_id distinct)").
3. FloatForm (float-form.tsx:62) disables the submit button only with disabled={isPending}.
4. There is genuinely no per-submission idempotency guard server-side.

So the claim is technically accurate. However, the user-facing impact is much smaller than 'medium':

- useTransition sets isPending synchronously, so the disabled button blocks any second click WITHIN the in-flight request. A true duplicate requires the user to wait for completion then click again.
- On completion the action fires a success toast ("Float added.", float-form.tsx:36) AND calls revalidatePath('/candidates/${id}/floats') (actions.ts:100), which re-renders the exact list the form sits on. So before a second click is even possible, the recruiter already sees the first float appear with an "Added Xs ago" timestamp. The 'unsure the first click registered' premise is largely mitigated.
- A duplicate float renders as a distinct timestamped row (page.tsx:120-121) and is trivially deletable via FloatRowActions. It is not a data-integrity error — the schema deliberately allows N floats per candidate.
- This matches the established codebase pattern: log-activity-form, the shortlist convert action, and other candidate add-actions also lack content-hash idempotency guards, because repeated identical entries are semantically valid in those domains too.

For a live client demo, a recruiter producing two deletable timestamped rows by deliberate double-clicking is not a demo-breaking defect. It is at most a low-severity UX polish item (could add an optimistic disable-after-success or a short-window dedup), which the claim itself flags as downgradeable. It is real but over-graded.

title: Racing CV uploads surface a generic 'Couldn’t record this CV' instead of the documented re-derive behaviour
description: version is computed via nextCVVersion BEFORE the upload, and the inline comment claims the (candidate_id, version) unique constraint 'surfaces racing uploads as a 409 we can re-derive from.' In practice the code does NOT re-derive: when two uploads race, the second createCandidateCV insert hits the unique violation, returns { ok:false, code:'internal' }, the orphaned Storage object is removed, and the user gets the generic 'Couldn’t record this CV. Please try again.' There is no retry/version bump — behaviour and comment diverge.
userImpact: If a recruiter (or two teammates on the same candidate) upload CVs almost simultaneously, one upload fails with a vague error and they must manually retry, despite the code comment implying automatic recovery. Low likelihood in a 2–3 person agency but possible.
suggestedFix: Either implement the promised re-derive (on a 23505 from createCandidateCV, recompute nextCVVersion and retry the insert once), or correct the comment and return a clearer message such as 'Another CV was uploaded at the same time — please try again.'
verifierReasoning: Verified every technical assertion against the code. (1) The unique (candidate_id, version) constraint exists at supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:257, so two racing uploads to the same candidate that compute the same version will produce a Postgres 23505 unique violation on the second createCandidateCV insert. (2) createCandidateCV (src/lib/db/candidate-cvs.ts:83-113) does NOT inspect the error code, does NOT recompute the version, and does NOT retry — it captures to Sentry and returns the generic { ok:false, code:'internal' } for ALL errors. (3) uploadCVAction (src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/actions.ts:176-180) reacts to the failure by removing the orphaned Storage object and returning the generic 'Couldn’t record this CV. Please try again.' message — there is no retry/version-bump 're-derive' logic anywhere. (4) The inline comment at lines 148-149 ('the (candidate_id, version) unique constraint surfaces racing uploads as a 409 we can re-derive from') overclaims: the phrase 'we can re-derive from' implies automatic recovery that does not exist. So comment and behaviour genuinely diverge, exactly as the reviewer states. Severity remains low: the race window is the few ms between nextCVVersion's SELECT and createCandidateCV's INSERT for two simultaneous uploads on the SAME candidate; a single user cannot trigger it through the normal per-upload form; the failure is non-destructive (no data loss, no orphaned bytes since Storage cleanup runs) and self-corrects on a manual retry. The only user-facing harm is one vague-error retry in an already-rare two-recruiters-same-candidate-same-instant scenario in a 2-3 person agency. The suggested fix (either implement the one-shot retry-on-23505 re-derive, or correct the comment and use a clearer message) is reasonable, though arguably the cheapest correct fix is just to align the comment with the message — the bug is primarily a documentation/UX-copy discrepancy, not a correctness defect.

title: Raw Supabase/Postgres errors logged to Sentry can leak candidate PII (email / LinkedIn URL) — beforeSend scrub does not touch exception messages
description: The Sentry beforeSend scrub only redacts keys inside event.extra and event.contexts (and deletes request.cookies / user.email). It NEVER scrubs event.exception.values[].value, which is where a thrown error's message lands. Throughout the candidate domain, raw PostgREST/Postgres error objects are passed straight to Sentry.captureException(error, ...): createCandidate (candidates.ts:385), updateCandidate (candidates.ts:464), markCandidateFieldsFromCV (candidate-cvs.ts:417), getCandidateByEmailForOrg (candidates.ts:424), findCandidateByEmail (candidates.ts:699), and the LinkedIn insert/update path (candidates-linkedin.ts:184, 237). On a constraint violation Postgres returns a DETAIL like `Key (organization_id, source_detail)=(<uuid>, https://www.linkedin.com/in/jane-doe) already exists.` (the candidates_linkedin_source_detail_uniq_idx unique index fires a 23505 on concurrent LinkedIn capture — see migration 20260520065652). That detail string is part of the error message/details and is shipped to Sentry unredacted because beforeSend never walks event.exception. A LinkedIn profile URL identifies a named individual; on other tables a check/unique violation can echo email or name. This violates the CLAUDE.md hard rule 'Never log CV text, candidate names, or any PII to Sentry.'
userImpact: Candidate-identifying data (LinkedIn profile URLs, and on some constraint violations emails/names) can land in the agency's Sentry project. For a GDPR-bound UK recruitment agency this is a compliance breach (PII processed/stored by a sub-processor without basis), and it undermines the 'audit-ready, PII-safe' selling point during the buyer demo.
evidence:
PII_KEYS scrub only applied to extra/contexts:
  beforeSend(event) {
    if (event.request?.cookies) delete event.request.cookies
    if (event.user?.email) delete event.user.email
    if (event.extra) event.extra = scrub(event.extra)...
    if (event.contexts) event.contexts = scrub(event.contexts)...
    return event
  }
No handling of event.exception.values[].value. Meanwhile candidates-linkedin.ts:237 does `Sentry.captureException(error ?? new Error('linkedin-upsert: empty insert'), ...)` passing the raw 23505 whose DETAIL contains the LinkedIn URL.
suggestedFix: Extend beforeSend to also scrub exception values: walk event.exception?.values and run each .value through a redactor that masks email-like, URL-like, and known-PII substrings (or at minimum strip Postgres `Key (...)=(...)` DETAIL fragments). Better still, stop passing raw Supabase error objects to Sentry from the candidate db helpers — wrap them as `new Error(error.code ?? 'db_error')` with PII-free tags, mirroring the pattern already used in parse-cv.ts (which deliberately wraps name+status only).
verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL, with one mechanism correction. (1) sentry.server.config.ts:27-33 beforeSend only handles request.cookies, user.email, scrub(event.extra), scrub(event.contexts) — it never walks event.exception.values[].value. Confirmed. (2) Raw Supabase error objects ARE passed to Sentry.captureException at every cited line (candidates.ts:385/424/464/699, candidate-cvs.ts:417, candidates-linkedin.ts:184/237) plus ~20 others. (3) The unique index that raises 23505 exists: migration 20260520065652 creates candidates_linkedin_source_detail_uniq_idx on (organization_id, source_detail) where source='linkedin', and source_detail = the verbatim LinkedIn URL (candidates-linkedin.ts:224). 

CORRECTION to the exact leak path: every cited helper uses the non-throwing { data, error } destructuring, so `error` is the PLAIN OBJECT { message, details, hint, code } that PostgrestBuilder produces via JSON.parse(body) (PostgrestBuilder.ts:513), NOT a PostgrestError instance. In Sentry core getException() (eventbuilder.js), a plain object hits the isPlainObject branch: the ENTIRE object — including details — is serialized into event.extra.__serialized__, and the exception value is set to error.message. For a 23505, Postgres puts the constraint name in MESSAGE and the PII string `Key (organization_id, source_detail)=(<uuid>, https://www.linkedin.com/in/jane-doe) already exists.` in DETAIL, which PostgREST maps to `details`. So the LinkedIn URL lands in event.extra.__serialized__.details. beforeSend DOES run scrub(event.extra), but scrub only redacts keys in PII_KEYS=['email','phone','cv_text','extracted_data','candidate_email','full_name']; `details` is not in that list, so the PII survives unredacted. The claim's title says the leak is in the exception value; the real location is event.extra.__serialized__.details — but the conclusion (candidate PII reaches Sentry, beforeSend fails to strip it) is correct.

Decisive corroboration: parse-cv.ts:103-105 comment explicitly states 'never pass the original error object — wrap the name + status so any prompt fragments in error.message can't bypass the beforeSend PII scrub,' and uses new Error(`${error.name}: ${status}`). The author knew this vector existed and applied the safe wrap in the Inngest path but NOT in the candidate DB helpers — confirming the inconsistency the claim describes.

title: extract-text NonRetriableError includes truncated CV/library error text that bypasses the Sentry PII scrub
description: In the extract-text step, on a corrupt PDF/DOCX the code throws `new NonRetriableError(\`extract-text failed: ${name}: ${message}\`)` where message is `err.message.slice(0, 500)` from unpdf/mammoth. The surrounding outer catch (parse-cv.ts:343-366) and onFailure handler deliberately wrap only error.name + status for Sentry — BUT this NonRetriableError's message becomes the error that Inngest surfaces and that the outer catch re-throws; while the outer Sentry capture uses err.name only, the NonRetriableError message itself (which can embed fragments of the extracted document) is recorded in Inngest's own run history / dashboard as the function failure reason. The comment asserts 'Library parsing errors don't contain candidate PII — they're library internals', but mammoth/unpdf errors can and do include snippets of malformed document content (e.g. a stray token from the file). This is a softer leak than finding #1 but still puts document-derived text into a third-party dashboard.
userImpact: On a malformed CV, fragments of the document body may be stored in the Inngest run log, outside the app's PII controls. Low likelihood of sensitive content per event, but it is document-derived text the project rules say must not leave the controlled path.
evidence:
const message = err instanceof Error ? err.message.slice(0, 500) : ''
throw new NonRetriableError(
  `extract-text failed: ${name}: ${message}`,
)
suggestedFix: Throw the NonRetriableError with name only (`extract-text failed: ${name}`) and, if message detail is needed for debugging, log it to Sentry as a PII-free tag set rather than embedding the raw library message in the error surfaced to Inngest's dashboard.
verifierReasoning: Verified the claim against the actual code. In src/lib/inngest/functions/parse-cv.ts:204-207 the extract-text step does exactly what the claim states: on a corrupt PDF/DOCX it builds `message = err.message.slice(0, 500)` from the underlying library and throws `new NonRetriableError(\`extract-text failed: ${name}: ${message}\`)`. The inline comment (lines 201-202) asserting "Library parsing errors don't contain candidate PII — they're library internals" is demonstrably false: unpdf (1.6.2) wraps pdf.js, whose readable error strings interpolate raw document-derived content into messages, e.g. `Invalid number: ${String.fromCharCode(e)} (charCode ${e})`, `Illegal character: ${n}`, `Bad filter name "${i}"`, `Invalid dictionary name "${e}"`, `getHexString - ignoring invalid character: ${e}`. These embed bytes/tokens lifted straight from the malformed file. The two-sink distinction the claim draws is correct and material: (1) The Sentry path is safe — the outer catch (lines 353-361) wraps only `name: status` in a fresh Error, and sentry.server.config.ts beforeSend only redacts object KEYS (email/phone/cv_text/etc.) in extra/contexts, never message strings — so the wrapped error carries no library text. (2) But the outer catch then re-throws the original NonRetriableError verbatim (line 366 `throw err`), and the code's own comment at line 347 states "Inngest re-throws NonRetriableError to surface in its dashboard." There is no Inngest middleware in src/lib/inngest/client.ts to scrub that output, so the full `extract-text failed: <name>: <truncated library message>` string lands in Inngest's third-party run history — outside the app's PII controls. So document-derived text genuinely can leave the controlled path on a corrupt-file event. Severity adjusted down from medium to low: the leaked fragments are short, low-entropy tokens (a stray charcode, an operator name, a malformed dictionary key) rather than coherent CV PII like names/emails/salaries; it only fires on genuinely corrupt files (scanned-image PDFs are caught earlier by the MIN_EXTRACTED_CHARS guard at line 211, not via this exception path); and it has zero impact on a live client demo. The fix is trivial and matches the project's own R4 pattern already used everywhere else in this file: throw `extract-text failed: ${name}` only, and if message detail is wanted, log it as PII-free tags to Sentry rather than embedding the raw library message in the error surfaced to Inngest.

title: search_candidates RPC concatenates the raw query into ILIKE patterns — '%' / '_' act as wildcards (over-match within the org)
description: search_candidates builds its filter as `c.full_name ilike '%' || p_query || '%'` (and the same for email/company/role/location). The query text is parameterised so this is NOT SQL injection, but `%` and `_` inside p_query are interpreted as LIKE wildcards. A recruiter typing a name containing an underscore (e.g. an email-like search) or pasting a `%` gets surprising matches, and a bare `%` matches every candidate in the org. The RPC is security invoker so RLS still scopes results to the caller's org — there is no cross-tenant leak — but the matching is incorrect/misleading for any query containing LIKE metacharacters.
userImpact: Search returns confusing results when the query contains '%' or '_' (e.g. searching a username with an underscore silently broadens the match). Not a security issue, but a correctness/UX wart on the headline 'find a candidate in seconds' flow being demoed.
evidence:
where
  c.full_name ilike '%' || p_query || '%'
  or coalesce(c.current_role_title, '') ilike '%' || p_query || '%'
  ... (p_query interpolated directly into the LIKE pattern; word_similarity branches are fine)
suggestedFix: Escape LIKE metacharacters in p_query before interpolation (replace('\\','\\\\'), replace('%','\\%'), replace('_','\\_') and add `escape '\\'` to the ILIKE), or rely on the word_similarity branches for partial matching and drop the raw-ILIKE branch.
verifierReasoning: Verified at code level. supabase/migrations/20260603120000_search_candidates_partial_match.sql lines 90-94 concatenate p_query directly into ILIKE patterns: `c.full_name ilike '%' || p_query || '%'` (same for current_role_title, current_company, location, lower(email)). This migration is the active definition (latest CREATE OR REPLACE; timestamp 20260603120000 supersedes 20260517215939). The input is never escaped anywhere in the chain: search-input.tsx -> URL `q` -> candidates/page.tsx -> listCandidates (default mode = trigram) -> search_candidates RPC with `p_query: q.trim()`. The jobs/[id]/actions.ts and shortlist callers likewise pass only-trimmed/length-validated `q`. PostgreSQL ILIKE treats `%` and `_` in the right-hand pattern as wildcards, so a query of `%` becomes `ilike '%%%'` (matches every row in the caller's org) and an `_` matches any single char — exactly as claimed. The word_similarity branches are unaffected (claim correctly excludes them). It is NOT a security issue: the function is `security invoker` and RLS on candidates scopes to the caller's org (no cross-tenant leak). Strong corroboration that the team treats this as a real bug class: findCandidateByEmail (src/lib/db/candidates.ts:672-705) carries an explicit 'Phase 2 review M1 fix' comment stating '.ilike interprets `_` and `%` in the pattern as wildcards... use .eq instead' — they fixed the identical issue in the email-lookup path but the search_candidates RPC still interpolates raw text. Severity stays low: it only triggers when a recruiter actually types `%`/`_`, and the result ordering is `word_similarity desc`, so over-matched noise ranks below genuinely relevant rows — a UX/correctness polish item, not a demo-breaker or security flaw. Suggested fix (escape `\`,`%`,`_` and add `escape '\'`, or rely on the word_similarity branch) is correct.

title: markCandidateFieldsFromCV / bumpCandidateEmbedding update candidates by id only — latent cross-tenant write under service-role if candidate_id is ever unverified
description: markCandidateFieldsFromCV reads and updates candidates with `.eq('id', args.candidateId)` and NO organization_id predicate. It is invoked from the Inngest parse-cv function via createServiceClient() (RLS bypass). Today it is safe because parse-cv.ts:152-160 binds candidate_id into the storage_path tenant check before any write, so a forged event is rejected. But the helper itself carries no tenant guard, unlike the LinkedIn upsert (candidates-linkedin.ts:134) and embed-candidate-from-linkedin (which assert org equality before writing). The same applies to bumpCandidateEmbedding (candidates.ts:551) which writes by candidate_id alone. If a future caller invokes these with a candidate_id that isn't tenant-verified, it becomes a silent cross-tenant write. This is a defence-in-depth gap, not an active bug.
userImpact: No current user-visible impact (the one service-role caller verifies tenancy upstream). Risk is future regression: a new caller reusing this helper under service-role without the upstream storage_path check would write across tenants undetected.
evidence:
const { error: updateError } = await supabase
  .from('candidates')
  .update(updatePayload)
  .eq('id', args.candidateId)   // no .eq('organization_id', ...) and runs under service-role from parse-cv
suggestedFix: Thread an organizationId through markCandidateFieldsFromCV / bumpCandidateEmbedding and add `.eq('organization_id', organizationId)` to the WHERE clause when called from a service-role context (the org is already in scope inside parse-cv as organization_id). This mirrors the explicit-org-in-WHERE pattern the LinkedIn embed function already documents as the rule for service-role writes.
verifierReasoning: Every claimed code fact verified exactly. markCandidateFieldsFromCV (src/lib/db/candidate-cvs.ts:324 read, :414 write) and bumpCandidateEmbedding (src/lib/db/candidates.ts:554 write) both target candidates with .eq('id', candidateId) and NO organization_id predicate. Both are invoked under createServiceClient() (RLS-bypassing) inside src/lib/inngest/functions/parse-cv.ts (lines 249, 320). The only tenant protection there is the upstream storage_path boundary check at parse-cv.ts:152-160 (binds org_id AND candidate_id into the storage path; forged event throws NonRetriableError before any write) — exactly as the claim states. The contrast cases are also real and confirmed: upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn (candidates-linkedin.ts:134) explicitly asserts existing.organization_id !== organizationId and fails closed, with a comment naming the service-role-future-caller risk; embedCandidateFromLinkedIn (embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts:95-99) asserts result.data.organization_id !== organization_id before calling bumpCandidateEmbedding, with a documented rule (lines 27-31) that every service-role write must enforce org equality and that this helper relies on the caller's check because it only takes candidateId. So the asymmetry the reviewer cites genuinely exists. Crucially, the claim self-identifies as a defence-in-depth gap, NOT an active bug, and that framing is correct: I traced all four callers and every one is currently tenant-safe — acceptCVFieldsAction uses the authenticated RLS-scoped client (actions.ts:347 createClient), parse-cv guards via storage_path, embed-candidate-from-linkedin guards via org-equality, and embed-batch (embed-batch.ts:153) writes back only the exact ids it just selected (optionally org-scoped at :114). No path produces a cross-tenant write today. The issue is a legitimate latent-regression risk: a future service-role caller reusing these helpers without an upstream tenant check would write across tenants silently. The suggested fix (thread organizationId into the WHERE clause) matches the project's own documented service-role-write rule. Real, accurately scoped, low severity, zero impact on a live demo.

title: CV upload accepts the client-reported MIME type only — no magic-byte / extension validation before Storage write
description: uploadCVAction gates uploads with `ACCEPTED_CV_MIME.has(file.type)` where file.type is the browser-supplied MIME string on the multipart File. A crafted request can set Content-Type to application/pdf for arbitrary bytes; the file is then written to the private cvs bucket and a cv/uploaded event dispatched. The downstream parser (extractTextFromBuffer) will fail on non-PDF bytes and mark the row failed, so the blast radius is limited — but an authenticated tenant can store arbitrary <=10MiB blobs under their own org prefix. The storage bucket has allowed_mime_types set (migration 20260517204501) which provides a second gate at the Storage layer, partially mitigating this. Within-tenant only (storagePath is org/candidate-prefixed and RLS-enforced), so not a cross-tenant issue.
userImpact: Minimal for the demo: a determined authenticated user could stash non-CV bytes in their own CV bucket; parsing fails gracefully. Worth hardening before opening apply-form/public ingestion more widely.
suggestedFix: Sniff the leading bytes (PDF '%PDF-', DOCX zip 'PK\x03\x04') and/or validate the filename extension against file.type before the Storage upload. The bucket-level allowed_mime_types already helps; add the server-side byte check for defence in depth.
verifierReasoning: Verified against source. In src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/actions.ts line 125, uploadCVAction gates the upload solely with ACCEPTED_CV_MIME.has(file.type), where file.type is the browser/client-supplied MIME on the multipart File part. The only other pre-upload checks are file.size === 0 (line 120) and file.size > MAX_CV_BYTES (line 131). slugifyFilename (lines 98-109) normalizes the filename but performs no content/extension validation. No magic-byte sniffing occurs anywhere before the Storage write on line 161-163, which passes contentType: file.type. So a crafted multipart request declaring Content-Type: application/pdf for arbitrary bytes passes the gate, and the blob is written to the private cvs bucket under {org}/{candidate}/... and a cv/uploaded event is dispatched. The downstream parser src/lib/ai/cv-extract.ts (extractTextFromBuffer) routes by the DB-stored mime_type (not byte content) and feeds the bytes to unpdf/mammoth, which throw on non-PDF/DOCX content, so the row is marked failed — confirming the limited blast radius. The path prefix and RLS policies in migration 20260517204501 confirm it is within-tenant only. One refinement to the claim's mitigation argument: the bucket's allowed_mime_types (migration 20260517204501, line 14) is enforced against the upload request's content-type, and line 163 passes contentType: file.type — the same attacker-controlled value — so the bucket-level gate is defeated by the same single spoofed value rather than acting as an independent control. This makes the 'second gate' weaker than the claim states, but does NOT raise severity: the impact ceiling is unchanged (an authenticated user stashes a <=10 MiB blob in their own org's private bucket; parsing fails gracefully; no cross-tenant exposure, no code execution, no public serving). Low / defence-in-depth hardening item, correctly self-graded, and the noted concern about public apply-form ingestion is legitimate.

title: Floats and shortlist rows render inside the candidate "Applications" panel as fake jobs with Move/Reject actions
description: listApplicationsForCandidate selects all applications for the candidate with NO application_type filter (only .eq('candidate_id', ...)). Floats (application_type='float', job_id IS NULL) and shortlist rows (application_type='shortlist') are stored in the same `applications` table, so they are returned alongside real standard applications. The CandidateApplications client component then renders every returned row in the "Applications" section. A float row has job_id=null, so it renders as a plain 'Untitled job' with a stage badge of 'Applied' (floats default to stage='applied'), and the row's actions dropdown exposes 'Move to stage' (offer/placed/etc.), 'Remove from job', and 'Reject…' — none of which make sense for a speculative submission that has its own dedicated /floats UI. Shortlist rows show their real job title but as stage 'Applied', mixed in with genuine pipeline applications. The dedicated job-side helper listApplicationsForJob DOES filter `.eq('application_type', 'standard')` (line 131) precisely to keep floats/shortlists out of the pipeline; the candidate-side helper omits that guard.
userImpact: On the candidate detail page (the screen the recruiter will demo), every float they recorded appears as a phantom 'Untitled job' application card with interview/offer/placed move actions and a Reject button. Shortlist entries appear as 'Applied' stage jobs. The recruiter sees confusing duplicate/ghost entries and can trigger a 'Move to → Placed' (opening the placement-capture modal) or 'Reject' on a float that has no real pipeline meaning — undermining trust in the data in front of the client.
evidence:
const { data, error } = await supabase
    .from('applications')
    .select(APP_WITH_CANDIDATE_AND_JOB_SELECT)
    .eq('candidate_id', candidateId)
    .order('stage_changed_at', { ascending: false })   // no .eq('application_type','standard') — floats + shortlists leak in
suggestedFix: Add `.eq('application_type', 'standard')` to listApplicationsForCandidate (mirroring listApplicationsForJob line 131) so the candidate detail Applications panel shows only real pipeline applications. Floats already have their own /candidates/[id]/floats tab and shortlists have /jobs/[id]/shortlist, so excluding them here loses no information.
verifierReasoning: Every link in the claim's causal chain is verified in the actual code. (1) src/lib/db/applications.ts:90-112 — listApplicationsForCandidate selects from `applications` filtered ONLY by .eq('candidate_id', candidateId) + .order('stage_changed_at'); no application_type filter (matches the cited evidence verbatim). (2) The sibling helpers listApplicationsForJob (line 131) and listAllApplicationsByStage (line 225) DO carry .eq('application_type','standard'), with comments calling it "the invariant — shortlists and floats live in their own tabs and MUST NOT appear" — proving the candidate-side omission is the anomaly. (3) Floats ARE stored in `applications`: addFloatAction (floats/actions.ts) inserts application_type='float', job_id=null, stage defaulting to 'applied' (schema line 305: `stage ... not null default 'applied'`); the applications_job_id_required_unless_float CHECK lets float rows have null job_id. (4) Shortlists ARE stored in `applications`: addToShortlistAction inserts application_type='shortlist' with a real job_id and no explicit stage (defaults to 'applied'). (5) page.tsx:153-199 passes the unfiltered result straight into CandidateApplications, which maps over EVERY row and renders a job link or 'Untitled job' span (lines 169/173), an "Applied" stage badge, and a dropdown exposing "Move to stage" (all PIPELINE_STAGES, including placed → opens PlacementModal), "Remove from job", and "Reject…" (lines 211-236). Actions are gated only by isTerminal (rejected/withdrawn), so float/shortlist rows at stage 'applied' have fully active, functional actions — performMove calls the move_application RPC and performRemove calls deleteApplication, both keyed by application id regardless of type. So the impact is not merely cosmetic: a recruiter can trigger a real placement-capture or rejection on a speculative float that has no pipeline meaning. No test, RLS policy, or downstream guard prevents these rows from reaching the panel. High severity confirmed: phantom/ghost cards plus dangerous mutating actions on the candidate detail screen used in client demos.

title: CV upload toasts 'parsing…' but the review panel does not appear until a manual refresh (RSC not revalidated client-side)
description: On a successful upload, uploadCVAction calls revalidatePath(`/candidates/${candidateId}`) server-side and returns { ok: true }. The CvUpload client handler shows a success toast and resets the file input, but it never calls router.refresh(). revalidatePath marks the server cache stale but does not re-render the currently-mounted client tree until a navigation/refresh occurs. The CvReviewPanel (with its PendingState spinner + auto-poll) is rendered by the server page based on listCandidateCVs; since the page isn't re-fetched on the client, the newly-uploaded CV row — and therefore the parsing spinner — does not appear after upload until the user manually refreshes or navigates. Contrast with LogActivityForm, CandidateApplications, FloatRowActions, FloatNoteEditor which all explicitly call router.refresh() after their mutations for exactly this reason.
userImpact: Recruiter selects a CV, clicks Upload, sees 'CV uploaded — parsing…' toast and the file picker clears — but no parsing spinner or CV review panel appears. It looks like nothing happened / the upload was lost. They only discover the parsed CV after a page refresh, which they won't think to do mid-demo.
suggestedFix: After toast.success in handleSubmit, call router.refresh() (add `const router = useRouter()`), matching the pattern used by LogActivityForm/CandidateApplications. The server revalidation + client refresh together surface the new CV row and its parsing spinner immediately.
verifierReasoning:
I attempted to refute the claim but the code confirms it end-to-end.

1. src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/cv-upload.tsx: handleSubmit (lines 26-42) awaits uploadCVAction, shows toast.success('CV uploaded — parsing…'), calls reset(), and never calls router.refresh(). Confirmed the file imports only useRef/useState/useTransition — there is no useRouter import and no router.refresh() anywhere in the component.

2. actions.ts:239 — uploadCVAction calls revalidatePath(`/candidates/${candidateId}`) server-side then returns { ok: true, candidateCvId }. revalidatePath marks the server cache stale; when a server action is invoked imperatively via `await action()` from inside a startTransition (not bound as a form `action`), Next does not automatically re-render the mounted client tree. A navigation or router.refresh() is required to re-fetch the RSC payload.

3. page.tsx:146-148 + 395-400 — CvReviewPanel is rendered ONLY when `latestCv` (cvRows[0]) is non-null, and cvRows comes from listCandidateCVs run during the server render. On a fresh candidate the first upload means latestCv was null at render time, so neither CvReviewPanel nor its PendingState spinner is even mounted. The newly-inserted candidate_cvs row will not appear until the RSC tree is re-fetched.

4. cv-review-panel.tsx PendingState (lines 96-137) self-polls via router.refresh() every 3s — but only AFTER it is mounted, which requires latestCv to exist, which requires the page to have already re-fetched. It cannot bootstrap itself into existence, so it does nothing to surface the very first upload.

5. The four cited comparison components all DO call router.refresh() after their mutations: log-activity-form.tsx:63, candidate-applications.tsx:129 and :274, floats/float-note-editor.tsx:45, floats/float-row-actions.tsx:40. CvUpload is the lone outlier.

User impact is exactly as described and matches this app's primary candidate-creation flow (LinkedIn Save-to-PDF import, per project memory): recruiter uploads a CV, sees the toast and the cleared file picker, but no parsing spinner / review panel appears until a manual refresh. The data is safely written (Storage + candidate_cvs row + Inngest dispatch all succeed), so this is a pure client-revalidation feedback gap, not data loss — hence medium, not high. The one-line fix (add useRouter + router.refresh() after toast.success) is correct and consistent with the surrounding components.

title: 'Accept all' CV fields succeeds but the candidate detail fields don't update until manual refresh
description: CompleteState.onAcceptAll calls acceptCVFieldsAction which writes the merged fields and revalidatePath(`/candidates/${cv.candidate_id}`) server-side, then closes the review sheet (setOpen(false)) and toasts how many fields were filled. Like the CV-upload case, it never calls router.refresh(), so the currently-mounted detail page (Contact/Employment/Skills/Sectors FieldGroups, headline/about/experience sections) does not re-render with the newly-populated values. The toast claims 'Filled N fields from the CV' but the on-screen fields still read '—'.
userImpact: Recruiter clicks 'Accept all', gets a toast saying e.g. 'Filled 7 fields from the CV', the sheet closes — but the candidate's Employment/Skills/Location panels on the page still show '—'. It looks like the accept silently did nothing, contradicting the success toast, until they refresh.
suggestedFix: Add `const router = useRouter()` to CompleteState and call router.refresh() after setOpen(false) on the success path, so the freshly-filled candidate fields render immediately.
verifierReasoning:
Verified true by reading the code. In src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/cv-review-panel.tsx, CompleteState.onAcceptAll (lines 190-207) calls acceptCVFieldsAction, then on success calls toast.success(...) and setOpen(false) and stops — it never calls router.refresh(). CompleteState does not even instantiate useRouter (only PendingState does, line 97). The server action acceptCVFieldsAction (actions.ts:408) DOES call revalidatePath(`/candidates/${cv.candidate_id}`), but revalidatePath only marks the route cache stale server-side; it does not push a new RSC payload into an already-mounted client tree. The detail page (page.tsx) is a pure async Server Component that renders candidate fields via FieldRow `value || '—'` with no client subscription, so its rendered values only change on a fresh RSC render triggered by a client navigation/refresh.

Decisively, the codebase's OWN established convention confirms revalidatePath alone is insufficient: three sibling handlers in the same directory call router.refresh() immediately after server actions that already call revalidatePath, with explicit comments — log-activity-form.tsx:61-63 ('refresh re-runs the parent RSC so the timeline re-renders'), candidate-applications.tsx:127-129 ('The action revalidates /candidates/[id]; refresh so this RSC sibling re-renders'), plus float-row-actions.tsx:40 and float-note-editor.tsx:45. The Accept-all path is the outlier that omits the refresh, so after a successful accept the Contact/Employment/Skills/Sectors FieldGroups keep showing '—' while the toast claims 'Filled N fields' — exactly as described. Unlike the cv-upload case (which has a PendingState polling loop as a partial fallback once the panel switches to pending), Accept-all has no polling fallback at all: nothing refreshes until the user manually reloads.

Severity is genuinely medium: no data loss (the write succeeds and revalidatePath means any later navigation shows correct values) and no crash, but on a live demo it directly contradicts the explicit success toast on the product's flagship AI-extraction feature, making it look like the accept silently did nothing. The suggested fix (add useRouter to CompleteState, call router.refresh() after setOpen(false) on the success path) is correct and matches the codebase convention exactly.

title: Candidate detail / edit / floats pages have no loading.tsx — blank delay on navigation
description: Only the list route (/candidates) has a loading.tsx skeleton. The detail page does four sequential server fetches (getCandidate which also writes an audit row, listCandidateCVs, listApplicationsForCandidate, listCandidateActivities) before first paint, and the edit + floats routes also fetch synchronously, but none of /candidates/[id], /candidates/[id]/edit, or /candidates/[id]/floats provides a loading.tsx. During the navigation the user sees the previous page frozen (App Router holds the old UI until the RSC resolves) with no skeleton or spinner.
userImpact: Clicking a candidate row, Edit, or Floats produces a perceptible 'nothing happening' pause (compounded by the audit-log write inside getCandidate) before the new page appears, on a network the demo client may be on. The list route feels responsive (it has a skeleton); the detail/edit/floats routes feel sluggish by comparison.
evidence: Route inventory: /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/candidates/loading.tsx exists; no loading.tsx under [id], [id]/edit, or [id]/floats. page.tsx awaits getCandidate -> listCandidateCVs -> listApplicationsForCandidate -> listCandidateActivities sequentially.
suggestedFix: Add a minimal loading.tsx under src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/ (and optionally edit/ and floats/) rendering a header + body skeleton, so navigation shows immediate feedback.
verifierReasoning: Verified every factual element against the code. A find across the entire src/app tree returns exactly one loading.tsx — /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/candidates/loading.tsx (the list route). There is no loading.tsx under [id], [id]/edit, or [id]/floats, and no parent (app)/loading.tsx fallback. The detail page (src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/page.tsx) performs four sequential, non-parallelised awaits before its single JSX return: getCandidate (line 132), listCandidateCVs (146), listApplicationsForCandidate (153), listCandidateActivities (158). None are wrapped in <Suspense> with a fallback. getCandidate (src/lib/db/candidates.ts:274-312) does write an audit row via an awaited record_audit RPC (line 293) inside the same call before returning, so the first fetch carries an extra round-trip exactly as claimed. edit/page.tsx:22 awaits getCandidate, and floats/page.tsx awaits getCandidate (35), listFloatsForCandidate (46), and an inline activities query (54) — all synchronous, all with no loading boundary. With no loading.tsx/Suspense on these segments, App Router holds the previous route's UI until the RSC resolves, so the user gets no skeleton/spinner during navigation — matching the described behaviour. The list route does have a skeleton, so the comparison-of-responsiveness framing also holds. This is a real missing-feedback UX issue, not a correctness/data bug: the pages render correctly once resolved, there is no error path or tenancy risk, and the delay is a few short tenant-scoped queries plus one audit write. Honestly graded as low severity — perceptible-but-minor polish gap, worst case noticeable on a slow demo network.

title: Candidate email stored verbatim on create/edit but all dedup lookups compare lowercased — creates duplicate candidates and breaks Outlook/apply-form matching
description: createCandidate (and updateCandidate) store the recruiter-typed email verbatim — the Zod schema only `.trim()`s, never `.toLowerCase()`, and candidates.email is plain `text` with no lowercasing trigger (verified in 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:203). Meanwhile every dedup/association path compares against a lowercased value with `.eq`: getCandidateByEmailForOrg (candidates.ts:414), findCandidateByEmail (candidates.ts:689), and getCandidateByEmailLowercase (candidates-linkedin.ts:66). So a candidate added through the New Candidate form as `John.Doe@Acme.com` is invisible to those lookups. When the same person self-applies (apply form lowercases to `john.doe@acme.com`), the dedup misses and a SECOND candidate row is created. Outlook email-sync association (`findCandidateByEmail`) and LinkedIn email-fallback dedup also silently miss the existing record.
userImpact: The recruiter sees the same person appear twice in their candidate list after an apply-form submission or LinkedIn capture, and emails synced from Outlook fail to attach to the manually-added candidate. Classic 'why are there two of this guy?' moment a client will spot in a demo.
evidence: createCandidate payload: `email: input.email || null` (no lowercasing). Dedup side: `const normalisedEmail = args.email.toLowerCase().trim()` then `.eq('email', normalisedEmail)` (getCandidateByEmailForOrg). Schema: `email: z.string().trim().max(255).optional().refine(...)` — no .toLowerCase(). Column: `email text` (no trigger).
suggestedFix: Lowercase email at the create/edit write boundary so it matches every read path: in createCandidate and updateCandidate do `email: input.email ? input.email.toLowerCase().trim() : null` (LinkedIn upsert already does this at candidates-linkedin.ts:216). Best to centralise in a small helper so all four write/read sites agree.
verifierReasoning:
Claim verified end-to-end in the actual code.

WRITE BOUNDARY (stores verbatim, mixed-case): createCandidate stores `email: input.email || null` (src/lib/db/candidates.ts:360) with no lowercasing. Its Zod schema only `.trim()`s — src/app/(app)/candidates/new/schema.ts:39-47 has `z.string().trim().max(255).optional().refine(...)` and NO `.toLowerCase()`. The action passes `parsed.data.email ?? null` straight through (new/actions.ts:31). The edit path is identical: editCandidateSchema reuses createCandidateSchema (only omitting consent fields, edit/schema.ts:7-10) and updateCandidateAction passes `parsed.data.patch.email || null` (edit/actions.ts:39) into updateCandidate, which writes the patch unmodified (candidates.ts:455-458).

READ/DEDUP PATHS (all lowercase then case-sensitive `.eq`): getCandidateByEmailForOrg normalises `args.email.toLowerCase().trim()` then `.eq('email', normalisedEmail)` (candidates.ts:414,419) — apply-form dedup; findCandidateByEmail does `email.toLowerCase().trim()` then `.eq('email', normalised)` (candidates.ts:689,695) — Outlook-sync association; getCandidateByEmailLowercase does the same (candidates-linkedin.ts:66,71) — LinkedIn email-fallback dedup.

SCHEMA: candidates.email is plain `text` with no constraint/trigger (20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:203). The only candidates triggers are set_org, set_updated_at, and invalidate_embedding (which does not touch email). There is NO email-normalisation trigger and NO lower()=email check on candidates. By contrast org_invitations HAS `check (lower(email) = email)` (20260524000100:75) and the search index/RPC use lower(email) — proving the team knows the normalisation pattern but never applied it at the candidate write boundary. The raw-email index candidates_email_idx is on (organization_id, email), and Postgres `=` on text is case-sensitive, so a recruiter-typed `John.Doe@Acme.com` will not match a lowercased `.eq` lookup.

DIVERGENCE CONFIRMED: apply-form schema lowercases (apply/[orgSlug]/schema.ts:49 `.toLowerCase()`) and LinkedIn upsert lowercases (candidates-linkedin.ts:216 `profile.email.toLowerCase().trim()`), so those paths store lowercase. The recruiter create/edit forms store verbatim. Result: a manually-added mixed-case candidate is invisible to apply-form dedup (→ duplicate row created), to Outlook-sync association (→ emails fail to attach), and to LinkedIn email-fallback dedup. Every fact, line number, and the suggested fix in the claim check out.

Severity stays high: the trigger (recruiter typing a capitalised email) is natural, the failure is silent, and the symptom (two of the same candidate, missing email attachment) is exactly the kind of thing a client spots in a demo. The only reason it isn't critical is that it requires both a mixed-case manual entry AND a later same-person apply/LinkedIn/Outlook event to surface, and many emails are already lowercase.

title: Edit-candidate server validation errors are keyed under 'patch' and never reach the form fields (silent no-op on a failed save)
description: updateCandidateAction validates against `z.object({ id, patch: editCandidateSchema })`, then returns `parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors`. Because the candidate fields are nested under `patch`, flatten() keys the errors as `{ patch: [...] }`, NOT `{ full_name: [...], email: [...] }` (verified by running the exact schema: flatten() yields `{"id":[...],"patch":["Name is required.","Enter a valid email."]}`). The edit form then does `form.setError('patch', ...)` — but there is no field named `patch`, so the message is dropped and no FormMessage renders. The create-form action (new/actions.ts) parses the schema directly so its keys are correct; only the edit path is wrong because it wraps the schema.
userImpact: If the server rejects an edit (the client resolver normally catches first, but a stale/edge submission can still reach the server), the user clicks Save, the spinner clears, and absolutely nothing visible happens — no inline error, no toast. They cannot tell why the save didn't go through.
evidence: `fieldErrors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors as Record<...>` over `updateCandidateActionSchema = z.object({ id, patch: editCandidateSchema })`; form: `for (const [field, messages] of Object.entries(result.fieldErrors)) { form.setError(field as keyof EditCandidateInput, ...) }`.
suggestedFix: Return the nested fieldErrors: validate id and patch separately, or do `parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors` only on a direct `editCandidateSchema.safeParse(rawPatch)` and validate id with a separate guard. Then keys are real field names and form.setError targets the right inputs.
verifierReasoning: The claim is verified, including empirically. In src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/edit/actions.ts:14-17 the action wraps the field schema: updateCandidateActionSchema = z.object({ id, patch: editCandidateSchema }), then at line 32 returns parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors. I ran the exact installed schema (zod 4.4.3, via a script run from the project root): for a bad patch (empty full_name + invalid email) the nested schema yields flatten().fieldErrors = {"patch":["Name is required.","Enter a valid email."]}, whereas a direct editCandidateSchema.safeParse yields the correct {"full_name":["Name is required."],"email":["Enter a valid email."]}. So the field-level messages collapse under the key "patch". The edit form (candidate-edit-form.tsx:57-63) iterates result.fieldErrors and calls form.setError('patch', ...). No form field named 'patch' is registered (fields are full_name, email, phone, etc.), so the setError is a no-op and no FormMessage renders. The create action (new/actions.ts:20) parses the schema directly, so its keys ARE correct — confirming the claim that only the edit path is broken because it wraps the schema. The id-only error path also surfaces as 'id' (also a non-field) but id comes from the URL and is always a valid uuid in practice. Reachability: the client form uses zodResolver(editCandidateSchema) (line 49), so invalid input is normally blocked client-side; the server fieldErrors branch is the belt-and-braces path for a submission that bypasses the client validator. When that path IS hit, the outcome is a fully silent no-op: spinner clears, no inline error, no toast. The DB-failure path returns {formError} which DOES toast correctly (lines 48-49 / form lines 65-68), so genuine save failures are not silent — only schema-rejections-on-the-server are. The fix the reviewer suggests (parse editCandidateSchema directly and validate id separately) is correct and trivial.

title: CV-extracted years_experience can overflow numeric(4,1) and fail the merge UPDATE (silent in Inngest, error toast on Accept all)
description: markCandidateFieldsFromCV writes parsed.years_experience_total straight into candidates.years_experience, which is `numeric(4, 1)` (max 999.9, verified schema line 217). The CV-parse tool defines years_experience_total as an unconstrained `number` (claude.ts:177) and salaries as `integer` (no upper bound). A CV the model misreads (e.g. months/hours as years, or a salary in a foreign denomination) can yield a value beyond the column's precision; the UPDATE then fails with a Postgres numeric overflow. In the Inngest parse path this is swallowed (parse is still marked complete, embed proceeds) so the candidate page silently shows no merged fields; on the manual 'Accept all' button it surfaces as a generic 'Couldn't merge CV fields' error toast.
userImpact: Occasionally a CV parses 'successfully' but none of its fields land on the candidate, or 'Accept all' fails with an opaque error, with no indication that one out-of-range number was the culprit.
evidence: Column: `years_experience numeric(4, 1)`. Patch built with `patch[col] = parsedValue` for years_experience_total via SCALAR_FIELD_MAP `['years_experience_total', 'years_experience']`; no clamp/validation. Tool schema: `years_experience_total: { type: 'number' }`.
suggestedFix: Clamp/validate before writing: cap years to e.g. 0–80 and salaries to a sane integer range in markCandidateFieldsFromCV (or in the parse step), dropping out-of-range values rather than letting the whole patch UPDATE fail.
verifierReasoning:
Every load-bearing fact in the claim checks out against the actual code.

1. Schema: supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:217 defines `years_experience numeric(4, 1)` — 3 integer digits + 1 decimal, max 999.9. A value >= 1000 raises Postgres `numeric field overflow` (SQLSTATE 22003). Salaries at lines 213-214 are `integer` (max ~2.1B), so a foreign-denomination salary (e.g. tens of millions of yen/rupiah) can overflow integer range too.

2. Tool schema: src/lib/ai/claude.ts:177 declares `years_experience_total: { type: 'number' }` with no bound; salaries are `integer` with no maximum (lines 171-172). Output passed verbatim via parseCV() return.

3. No validation: src/lib/db/candidate-cvs.ts:347 builds `patch[col] = parsedValue` with zero range/clamp check. src/lib/ai/cv-extract.ts is text-extraction only — no normalization layer exists anywhere.

4. Inngest swallow: src/lib/inngest/functions/parse-cv.ts:249 calls `await markCandidateFieldsFromCV(...)` WITHOUT checking the returned DbResult. The helper returns `{ ok: false, code: 'internal' }` on update error rather than throwing (candidate-cvs.ts:416-421). So inside step.run('write-extracted') the failed merge produces no throw, the step succeeds, parsing_status stays 'complete', and the embed step proceeds. Candidate page silently shows no merged fields — only a Sentry capture inside the helper. Exactly as described.

5. Accept-all surfaces it: src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/actions.ts:370-392 checks mergeResult.ok and on failure returns the opaque toast "Couldn't merge CV fields onto the candidate (internal)." with no hint that a single out-of-range number was the cause.

6. Atomic blast radius: the whole patch is one UPDATE, so a single overflowing value drops ALL fields the CV would have populated, not just the offending one.

Severity is honestly low: it requires the model to misextract a number past a tight-but-real bound, the failure is non-destructive (extracted_data is preserved on candidate_cvs, retriable), there is no security or cross-tenant impact. For a live client demo it would only bite if a demo CV happened to trigger an overflow — unlikely but not impossible given the 999.9 cap is genuinely tight. Confirmed at the claimed severity.

title: Cards pagination Next/Previous always appends view=cards, flipping a list-view user into cards on page change
description: candidate-cards.tsx pageHref hardcodes `params.set('view', 'cards')` on every Previous/Next link (line 49). The CandidatesShell renders CandidateCards on mobile regardless of the URL ?view= param, so on a narrow viewport the page links inject view=cards into the URL. If the user later widens the window (or shares the URL), they are now pinned to cards view even though they never chose it — the URL state diverges from their intent. The table's pageHref (candidate-table.tsx:79) correctly omits the view param.
userImpact: A user who paginates on mobile (or whose layout collapses to cards) ends up with view=cards stuck in their URL; reopening on desktop shows cards instead of the list they prefer.
evidence: candidate-cards.tsx pageHref: `params.set('view', 'cards')` unconditionally. Shell: `if (isMobile || desktopView === 'cards') return <CandidateCards .../>` — cards render even when ?view= is list/absent.
suggestedFix: Drop the forced `view=cards` from the cards pageHref (mirror the table's pageHref), or only set it when the user explicitly selected cards on desktop. Let the shell's mobile detection decide the view, not the URL.
verifierReasoning: The claim is verified against the actual code. candidate-cards.tsx line 49 (`params.set('view', 'cards')`) unconditionally appends view=cards to every Previous/Next pagination href, while candidate-table.tsx pageHref (lines 79-86) correctly omits view — confirming the described asymmetry. candidates-shell.tsx line 36 (`if (isMobile || desktopView === 'cards')`) renders CandidateCards on any sub-768px viewport regardless of the URL ?view= param (useIsMobile returns true below 768px). view-toggle.tsx line 9 maps 'cards' -> 'cards', so once view=cards lands in the URL, page.tsx line 60/117 sets desktopView='cards', which on desktop also forces the cards branch in the shell. There is no client-side cleanup that strips view=cards when the viewport widens (grep confirmed). So the full chain holds: a user who paginates while collapsed to cards injects a sticky view=cards into the URL, pinning them to cards on a later desktop load or shared link, diverging from their intent. The asymmetry between the two pageHref functions confirms this is an oversight, not deliberate. Severity is genuinely low: it produces only a stuck URL/view-preference state after mobile pagination — no data loss, no broken functionality, no crash. A desktop user can recover via the ViewToggle List button (which does usp.delete('view')). For a live (typically desktop) client demo it rarely triggers and is cosmetic when it does. The reviewer's suggested fix is sound, with the refinement that the cleanest version only re-emits view=cards when the user explicitly selected cards (to avoid flipping a deliberate desktop-cards user back to list on page change).

title: parse-cv embed step bundles the Voyage call with the DB write, so a write failure re-charges ai_usage on step retry
description: In parseCVOnUpload the 'embed-candidate' step.run wraps THREE operations in a single step: getCandidateForEmbedding -> embed() (Voyage API + record_ai_usage) -> bumpCandidateEmbedding. Inngest retries a step when its body throws. If embed() succeeds (Voyage charged + ai_usage row written) but the subsequent bumpCandidateEmbedding throws, the entire step body re-executes on retry, calling embed() again — a second Voyage charge AND a duplicate ai_usage row for the same candidate. The sibling embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts avoids this by isolating the Voyage call in its own step ('voyage-embed') separate from 'persist-embedding', so the embed output is memoised across retries. parse-cv does not follow that pattern.
userImpact: Per-tenant AI cost figures in /settings/usage over-report Voyage spend for any candidate whose embedding write transiently fails and retries. Skews the cost-per-tenant data the product relies on for SaaS pricing. Not visible to the recruiter directly but corrupts the cost ledger.
evidence: await step.run('embed-candidate', async () => { ... const { vectors } = await embed({ organizationId: organization_id, ... purpose: 'candidate_embed', inputs: [embeddingText] }) ... await bumpCandidateEmbedding(supabase, { candidateId: candidate_id, embedding: vector, ... }) })
suggestedFix: Split the embed-candidate step into separate steps mirroring embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts: one step.run('voyage-embed') that returns the vector (memoised after success), and a separate step.run('persist-embedding') for bumpCandidateEmbedding. Then a persist failure replays only the persist step, not the paid Voyage embed.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed the structural facts. In src/lib/inngest/functions/parse-cv.ts the single step.run('embed-candidate', ...) (lines 286-325) bundles three operations in one Inngest step: getCandidateForEmbedding (288), embed() (308-314), and bumpCandidateEmbedding (320-324). src/lib/ai/voyage.ts confirms embed() both makes the billable Voyage API call (112-118) and writes a record_ai_usage row (127-136). Inngest memoises step output only at the step boundary, so any throw inside that step body AFTER a successful embed() forces the whole body — including the paid embed() — to re-execute on retry (retries: 3, line 99), producing a second Voyage charge plus a duplicate ai_usage row. The sibling src/lib/inngest/functions/embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts genuinely avoids this by isolating the Voyage call in step.run('voyage-embed') (123-131) separate from step.run('persist-embedding') (143-153), so the embed output is memoised across persist-step retries. The inconsistent pattern and the suggested fix (split the steps) are both accurate. So the claim is real in materially-equivalent form. However, two facts narrow the real-world reachability well below what the claim's specific mechanism implies, justifying low (not higher) severity: (1) The claim states 'bumpCandidateEmbedding throws' and triggers the retry — but in parse-cv that call's return value is NOT checked (no if(!result.ok) guard, unlike the LinkedIn version at 150-152). bumpCandidateEmbedding returns { ok:false } on DB error (candidates.ts:558-563) rather than throwing, so the most likely failure (a transient write error) does NOT re-run the step and does NOT re-charge. (2) The only explicit post-embed throw inside the step (the empty-vector guard, 316-318) is effectively unreachable after a successful charged embed. So a re-charge requires an unexpected non-result throw (network/serialization) in the narrow window between embed() returning and step completion. Impact is bounded to cost-ledger over-reporting in /settings/usage — not user-facing, no data corruption, no tenant leak — and the documented embed-candidates-batch sweep is the failure fallback. Genuine pattern-inconsistency/cost-accuracy bug, low severity.

title: listCandidates semantic branch double-reads org and is effectively dead/confused — /candidates never uses it and it duplicates /search logic
description: The semantic branch of listCandidates (mode === 'semantic') performs the Voyage query embed inline, resolves current_organization_id() via RPC, then resolves it AGAIN a few lines later (line 148: orgRpcAgain) because the first org value is scoped inside the embed try-block. The /candidates page (page.tsx:63) never passes mode, defaulting to trigram, and the /search page does its OWN inline embed + hybridSearchCandidates rather than calling this branch. So this branch is unreachable from the shipped UI yet still contains a synchronous Voyage embed in an RSC path plus a redundant SECURITY DEFINER RPC round-trip. It is a maintenance/correctness trap: a future caller wiring /candidates?mode=semantic gets a second, divergent semantic implementation that can drift from /search.
userImpact: No direct user impact today (branch is not exercised). Risk is future divergence: two semantic-search code paths with different thresholds (this branch uses minCosineSimilarity 0.3 hardcoded vs /search MIN_COSINE 0.3 — currently same, but independently maintained) producing inconsistent results, and an extra paid query-embed if accidentally enabled.
evidence: const orgResult = await supabase.rpc('current_organization_id') ... // later: const orgRpcAgain = await supabase.rpc('current_organization_id')  // 'Reading the RPC twice is harmless'
suggestedFix: Either delete the semantic branch from listCandidates and make /search the single semantic entry point, or refactor so org is resolved once at the top and the branch delegates to the same helper /search uses. Do not ship two semantic implementations.
verifierReasoning:
Every factual claim in the report is verified against the code:

1. Dead branch confirmed. The semantic branch in src/lib/db/candidates.ts:85 requires `mode === 'semantic'`. Grepping all callers of listCandidates: /candidates page.tsx:63 calls it with no `mode` (defaults to undefined → never semantic). /search page.tsx calls listCandidates only at lines 99-106 and 178-185, BOTH with `mode: 'trigram'`. A repo-wide grep for `mode: 'semantic'` passed to listCandidates returns zero matches. So the semantic branch is unreachable from the shipped UI.

2. /search has its own implementation confirmed. search/page.tsx:135-163 does its own inline Voyage embed (current_organization_id RPC at line 137 → auth.getUser → embed → hybridSearchCandidates at 157) rather than calling the listCandidates semantic branch. This is a fully duplicated semantic path.

3. Double org read confirmed. candidates.ts:106 (`orgResult = await supabase.rpc('current_organization_id')`, scoped inside the embed try-block) and candidates.ts:148 (`orgRpcAgain = await supabase.rpc('current_organization_id')`). The code's own comment at lines 143-147 admits "Reading the RPC twice is harmless."

4. Duplicated/divergent threshold confirmed. candidates.ts:160 hardcodes `minCosineSimilarity: 0.3`; search/page.tsx:26 defines `MIN_COSINE = 0.3`. Identical now, independently maintained.

So the issue genuinely exists as described: an unreachable second semantic implementation that duplicates /search, performs a redundant SECURITY DEFINER RPC round-trip, and carries an independently-maintained threshold constant. It is a real maintainability/correctness trap.

On severity: the report self-grades 'low' and explicitly concedes 'No direct user impact today (branch is not exercised).' I agree. For a live client demo there is zero user-facing impact — no crash, no silent-fail, no data leak, no cross-tenant risk. The redundant RPC read only fires if the branch is ever invoked, which it never is. The risk is purely future divergence if someone later wires /candidates?mode=semantic. Low is the correct grade; this is real dead/divergent code, not a non-issue, but it does not rise above low.

title: Hybrid embedding silently degrades to structured-fields-only for any candidate re-embedded by the batch sweep (raw CV text dropped)
description: The design intent (embed-text.ts D2-01) is a HYBRID embedding: structured summary + raw CV text. The inline parse-cv path achieves this because the extracted CV text is still in memory (parse-cv.ts:299 passes `text`). But the scheduled embed-batch sweep calls candidateEmbeddingText(r, null) (embed-batch.ts:133) — structured fields only, no CV body. And getLatestCVTextForCandidate ALWAYS returns null (line 635: 'Returning null here means no extra CV-body text'), because raw CV text is never persisted (only the structured extracted_data JSON is). So whenever a candidate's embedding is invalidated by an edit (the invalidate_candidate_embedding trigger NULLs it) and later re-embedded by the sweep, the embedding loses the rich CV-text half and becomes structured-summary-only. The same candidate's search relevance silently differs depending on whether they were last embedded inline (full hybrid) or by the sweep (degraded).
userImpact: Semantic search quality is inconsistent and silently degrades over time. A candidate whose CV described 'offshore wind turbine commissioning' in prose (but not in the structured skills tags) is findable right after upload, then becomes much harder to find after any profile edit triggers a sweep re-embed. The recruiter has no signal this happened — the core 'find the right candidate in seconds' value prop quietly weakens.
evidence: // embed-batch.ts: const inputs = batch.map((r) => candidateEmbeddingText(r, null))  ... // candidates.ts getLatestCVTextForCandidate: 'Returning null here means no extra CV-body text — the embedding input is the structured summary only'
suggestedFix: Persist the extracted CV plain text (e.g. a candidate_cvs.extracted_text column or a dedicated field) at parse time, and have getLatestCVTextForCandidate return it so the batch sweep can rebuild the full hybrid embedding. Otherwise re-embeds are not equivalent to the original embed, violating 'regenerate only on material change' with a quality regression.
verifierReasoning:
Every load-bearing factual assertion in the claim is verified against the code:

1. HYBRID design intent is real and documented: embed-text.ts:6-11 (D2-01) and candidateEmbeddingText (lines 51-67) append `\n\n---\n\n${cvText.slice(0, 30000)}` only when cvText is non-null.

2. Inline parse path achieves full hybrid: parse-cv.ts:299 calls `candidateEmbeddingText(candidate, text)` where `text` is the in-memory extracted CV plain text from Step 2 (lines 189-193). Comment at 296-298 confirms intent.

3. Batch sweep degrades to structured-only: embed-batch.ts:133 literally calls `candidateEmbeddingText(r, null)`. Moreover, the sweep's SELECT (lines 108-110) only fetches structured columns (full_name, current_role_title, skills, sector_tags, etc.) — it never reads any CV text, so there is nothing to pass even if it wanted to.

4. getLatestCVTextForCandidate ALWAYS returns null: candidates.ts:626 and 635 unconditionally return `{ ok: true, data: null }`, with the comment (632-634) explicitly stating the embedding input is structured-summary only. Additionally, this helper is DEAD CODE — grep shows zero callers anywhere in src/. And even if it were called, it selects `extracted_data` (the structured ParsedCV tool output, confirmed via claude.ts:213-242), which contains no raw CV prose to begin with.

5. Raw CV text is never persisted: only candidate_cvs.extracted_data (jsonb, structured) and the candidates structured fields are written (parse-cv.ts:235-274). The extracted `text` lives only in memory for the duration of the parse-cv run. Migration grep confirms no extracted_text/raw_text/cv_text column exists.

6. The invalidation→re-embed loop is real: invalidate_candidate_embedding (migration 20260519092951:38-63) NULLs candidate_embedding when any of 8 structured fields (full_name, current_role_title, current_company, location, skills, seniority_level, years_experience, sector_tags) changes on UPDATE. The sweep (embed-batch.ts:111 `.is('candidate_embedding', null)`) then re-embeds those rows structured-only.

Net effect: a candidate first embedded inline (full hybrid incl. up to 30k chars of CV prose) permanently loses the CV-text half the first time any watched structured field is edited. Search relevance for concepts that appear only in CV prose (the cited 'offshore wind turbine commissioning in prose but not in skills tags' example) silently degrades and never recovers. The asymmetry is genuine and undocumented as a regression.

Severity adjusted to LOW rather than medium because: (a) the comment at candidates.ts:632-634 frames structured-only as a known, accepted interim state, not an undetected defect; (b) it only fires AFTER a structured-field edit, and only matters when CV prose carries searchable signal absent from skills/sector tags; (c) for a live demo, fresh uploads still produce full hybrid embeddings — the degradation surfaces only over time after edits, so it does not break the headline demo. It is a real, quiet quality-consistency regression worth fixing (persist extracted_text and feed it to the sweep) but not a demo-breaking or data-integrity issue. Relevant files: /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/ai/embed-text.ts, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/inngest/functions/embed-batch.ts (line 133), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/inngest/functions/parse-cv.ts (line 299), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/candidates.ts (lines 607-636), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/supabase/migrations/20260519092951_invalidate_embeddings_triggers.sql.

title: Sending a check-in never clears the client's Dormant flag (the whole feature loop is broken)
description: When the recruiter sends a check-in, sendOutreachAction does NOT insert a new activity — it UPDATEs the existing 'email_draft' row to kind='email' (lines 297-314). The only trigger that bumps companies.last_contacted_at is `activities_bump_last_contacted`, which is declared `after insert on public.activities` (supabase/migrations/20260517215957_bump_last_contacted_at.sql:43-46). An UPDATE does not fire an AFTER INSERT trigger, so last_contacted_at is never written. The dormant_clients RPC selects companies WHERE last_contacted_at < now() - 60 days (20260520031200_phase3_dormant_clients_rpc.sql:75-76), so the client the recruiter just emailed stays in the dormant list and keeps showing the amber Dormant badge.
userImpact: In the live demo, the recruiter opens a Dormant client, sends the AI check-in successfully, and the client STILL shows as Dormant in the widget, the /clients badge, and the detail header. The core 'we reached out, clear it' loop visibly does nothing — it looks like the send did not register.
evidence: outreach-actions.ts: `await service.from('activities').update({ kind: 'email', body: subject, metadata: {...} }).eq('id', existing.id)...` — an UPDATE, not an insert. Trigger: `create trigger activities_bump_last_contacted after insert on public.activities ... execute function public.bump_last_contacted_at();` (bump fn only runs on INSERT, and only for kind in ('call','email','meeting','note')).
suggestedFix: On successful send, after flipping the draft row, explicitly bump the company: update companies set last_contacted_at = now() where id = clientId (scoped to profile.organization_id under service role), OR add an AFTER UPDATE branch to bump_last_contacted_at that fires when NEW.kind='email' AND OLD.kind='email_draft'. Simplest: add the companies update inside sendOutreachAction's success path. Then revalidatePath('/') and '/clients' (already present) reflect the cleared state.
verifierReasoning: Verified end-to-end. (1) sendOutreachAction (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts:285-314) finds the existing email_draft row and UPDATEs it to kind='email' on a successful Outlook send; it only INSERTs a fresh email row in the defensive else branch (line 320) when no prior draft exists. (2) The only mechanism that bumps companies.last_contacted_at is the bump_last_contacted_at() function, attached via `create trigger activities_bump_last_contacted after insert on public.activities` in 20260517215957 and re-created identically (still AFTER INSERT) in the hardened migration 20260518211702. A full grep of every migration confirms NO AFTER UPDATE trigger on activities exists. An UPDATE therefore never fires the bump, so last_contacted_at is never written on send. (3) The Inngest draft insert (draft-outreach-email.ts:145-160) writes kind='email_draft', which is excluded from the trigger's ('call','email','meeting','note') whitelist, so the only event that should bump (the email_draft->email transition) is exactly the UPDATE that doesn't fire. (4) Dormancy is computed solely from last_contacted_at in three surfaces: isDormant() (clients.ts:43-48) used by getClient->enrichWithActiveJobs (line 152) for the /clients badge AND the detail-header badge (page.tsx:60), and the dormant_clients RPC `where c.last_contacted_at < now() - interval` (20260520031200:75-76) for the dashboard widget. Since the column is never updated, all three keep showing Dormant after a successful send. (5) The modal's handleSend success path (send-checkin-modal.tsx:190) just calls onSent(); revalidatePath('/') and revalidatePath('/clients/${clientId}') (outreach-actions.ts:343-344) re-render from the still-stale value, so there is no optimistic masking. The migration 20260520031300 header even documents the intended "flips the same row to kind='email'" design, confirming the UPDATE is deliberate and the bump was simply never wired for that transition. The claimed line numbers, trigger semantics, RPC predicate, and suggested fix are all accurate. Severity adjusted from critical to high: it is a consistently-reproducing, demo-visible break of the advertised core loop, but it is a stale-flag/UX correctness bug (no data loss, no security/tenant impact, email actually sends, activity row is logged) and a one-line companies update or an AFTER UPDATE trigger branch fully fixes it.

title: Re-opening the check-in modal fires a fresh Sonnet draft every time — duplicate AI cost + duplicate draft rows
description: SendCheckinModalBody's mount effect unconditionally calls requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId }) on every open (line 148), and handleRetryDraft does it again (line 204). requestOutreachDraftAction fires an `outreach-draft/requested` Inngest event each time (outreach-actions.ts:84-92), which runs draftOutreachEmail (a Sonnet 4.6 call that logs ai_usage and writes a new email_draft activity row in draft-outreach-email.ts:141-167). There is no check for an existing recent draft and no disable-on-pending guard at the request layer.
