Opening the modal, closing it, and re-opening — or clicking Retry a few times during the demo — spawns N Sonnet calls and N email_draft activity rows for the same client.
userImpact: Each modal open silently spends ~0.55p of Claude budget and litters the client's activity timeline with multiple 'email_draft' entries. Per-tenant AI cost (a non-negotiable tracking concern) inflates with no user-visible reason, and the activity feed shows stacked draft noise.
evidence: send-checkin-modal.tsx:147-148 `seedTimeRef.current = Date.now(); async function go() { const requested = await requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId }) ...` runs on every mount; draft-outreach-email.ts inserts a brand new row each invocation with no dedup/upsert.
suggestedFix: Before dispatching a new draft request, call getLatestOutreachDraftAction and reuse a draft created within the last N minutes instead of always re-requesting. Or have requestOutreachDraftAction short-circuit when a fresh email_draft (< e.g. 10 min old) already exists for the client. At minimum, debounce/guard so rapid re-opens don't fan out multiple Sonnet calls.
verifierReasoning:
The mechanism described is fully confirmed by the code. In send-checkin-modal.tsx the body is conditionally rendered only when open===true (line 79: `{open ? <SendCheckinModalBody .../> : null}`), so closing unmounts it and reopening remounts it. The body's mount useEffect (lines 143-181) unconditionally calls `requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId })` on line 148 with no existing-draft check and no per-clientId guard; the effect's only cleanup is `cancelled = true`. handleRetryDraft (line 204) fires another `requestOutreachDraftAction` on each Retry click.

requestOutreachDraftAction (outreach-actions.ts:84-92) unconditionally calls `inngest.send({ name: 'outreach-draft/requested', ... })` every invocation — no dedup, no recent-draft short-circuit. The Inngest function draftOutreachEmailFn (draft-outreach-email.ts) declares only `concurrency` (limit 5) and `retries: 2` — there is NO `idempotency` key — so every event runs draftOutreachEmail (a real claude-sonnet-4-6 call via runWithLogging, which logs ai_usage through record_ai_usage — confirmed in claude.ts:82-85) and then inserts a brand-new activities row with kind='email_draft' (lines 141-167, plain `.insert`, no upsert/dedup). I grepped for idempotency/dedup/debounce across all three files and found none.

Confirming the design intent: the polling loop uses seedTimeRef and only accepts drafts whose created_at is >= seedTime-5s (lines 164-172). It actively ignores any pre-existing draft, so the feature is built to always request a fresh draft per open rather than reuse one — exactly the wasteful behavior the claim describes.

So opening→closing→reopening, or repeated Retry clicks, do each spawn an additional Sonnet call (~0.55p each, per the wrapper's own cost comment in outreach-draft.ts:21) and an additional email_draft activity row for the same client. Both claimed impacts (duplicate AI spend + stacked draft rows) are real and repeatable.

Severity adjustment: the per-event cost is sub-penny (~0.55p), so cost inflation is real but small in absolute terms; it would take many reopens to matter financially. There is no functional/correctness breakage — getLatestOutreachDraftAction orders by occurred_at desc limit 1, so the modal always surfaces the newest draft and the flow works. The tangible downsides are (1) redundant per-tenant AI spend on a non-negotiable-tracking metric and (2) timeline clutter from accumulating stale email_draft rows, both of which will surface in a live demo where the modal is reopened. That is a genuine idempotency gap worth fixing, but the tiny unit cost and absence of any correctness bug make 'high' an overstatement. Medium is the honest grade. The suggested fix (short-circuit when a fresh email_draft exists, or guard against rapid re-opens) is appropriate.

title: Service-role activity flip discards its error — email sends but timeline can silently keep showing a draft
description: Inside sendOutreachAction's post-send block, the service-role UPDATE that flips email_draft->email returns a PostgREST result whose `error` is never read (lines 297-314 destructure nothing). The wrapping try/catch only catches thrown exceptions, but supabase-js does NOT throw on a query error — it resolves with { error }. So a failed update (e.g. RLS/constraint/connectivity) resolves successfully, the function returns { ok: true }, and the activity row stays kind='email_draft'.
userImpact: After a successful Outlook send, the activity log can still show the message as an un-sent AI draft. The recruiter has no signal that the timeline is out of sync with reality — they may re-send thinking it never went out.
evidence: outreach-actions.ts: `await service.from('activities').update({...}).eq('id', existing.id).eq('organization_id', profile.organization_id)` — the awaited result (and its `.error`) is discarded; only an outer try/catch exists which supabase resolved-with-error will never trigger.
suggestedFix: Capture and check the result: `const { error: flipErr } = await service.from('activities').update({...})...; if (flipErr) Sentry.captureException(...)`. The send already succeeded so don't fail the action, but log the divergence so it's diagnosable and consider returning a soft warning.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed at /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts:297-314. The service-role UPDATE that flips kind='email_draft' -> 'email' is awaited but its result is fully discarded (no `{ error }` destructure, no check). The wrapping try/catch (lines 282-341) only catches thrown exceptions.

I verified the load-bearing premise against the actually-installed postgrest-js 2.105.4 source (node_modules/.pnpm/@supabase+postgrest-js@2.105.4/.../src/PostgrestBuilder.ts): `shouldThrowOnError` defaults to false (line 82); `.throwOnError()` is NOT chained on this query; server-side errors (RLS denial, constraint, any non-2xx PostgREST response) only throw when shouldThrowOnError is true (line 534) and otherwise resolve with `{ error, data: null }` (line 539); network/fetch errors are caught at line 372 and converted into a *resolved* `{ error }` object. So a failed flip resolves successfully, the try/catch never fires, no Sentry event is emitted, and the action returns { ok: true } (line 345).

Downstream consequence confirmed: the consuming modal (src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx:190-192) treats ok:true as success, calls onSent() and closes. revalidatePath runs regardless, re-rendering the timeline with the still-`email_draft` row. So after a genuinely-sent Outlook email, the activity timeline can keep showing it as an un-sent AI draft, with zero error signal.

Severity calibration: I confirmed via supabase/migrations/20260520031300_phase3_activity_kind_email_draft.sql and the Phase 1 schema that both 'email' and 'email_draft' are valid enum values and the `.eq('organization_id', profile.organization_id)` predicate scopes to the caller's own org, so this is NOT a deterministic every-time failure — it only manifests on transient connectivity/outage or a missing row, making per-send probability low. But when it does fire it is fully silent (Sentry never gets it) and the user-visible failure mode is a recruiter seeing a 'draft' for an email that already went out, prompting a re-send = duplicate outreach email to a real client contact. That client-facing duplicate-send risk plus the complete absence of any diagnostic signal (the exact silent-fail class CLAUDE.md prioritizes) keeps this at medium rather than low. The suggested fix (capture { error }, Sentry.captureException on divergence, don't fail the action since the send succeeded) is correct.

title: Send flips the most-recent draft row, not the one the recruiter actually reviewed
description: sendOutreachAction re-queries for the latest email_draft row (order by occurred_at desc limit 1, lines 285-294) and flips that, with no freshness guard and no linkage to the specific draft activity_id the modal displayed. The modal applies a 5s freshness check on READ (send-checkin-modal.tsx:166), but the SEND path has none. Combined with the no-idempotency issue above (multiple draft rows can exist), or if a newer draft lands after the recruiter starts editing, the flipped row may be a different draft than the body the recruiter reviewed. The body actually emailed comes from the editable form state, so the email content is correct — but the timeline's flipped row and its metadata (subject/body_html) can correspond to a different draft.
userImpact: The activity timeline's 'sent' entry can show subject/body that differs from what the recruiter saw, and stray older email_draft rows are left dangling un-flipped, cluttering the audit trail of a client relationship.
evidence: outreach-actions.ts:284-294 selects `kind='email_draft' ... order('occurred_at', { ascending: false }).limit(1)` with no occurred_at lower-bound and no draft id passed from the client. getLatestOutreachDraftAction returns activity_id (line 179) but the modal never sends it back to sendOutreachAction.
suggestedFix: Pass the reviewed draft's activity_id from the modal into sendOutreachAction and flip that exact row (still scoped to organization_id). This also ties the sent row to the content the user approved and avoids flipping the wrong draft when duplicates exist.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the code facts. sendOutreachAction (outreach-actions.ts:285-294) does re-query the newest email_draft by occurred_at desc limit 1, with no activity_id linkage and no freshness lower-bound. The modal (send-checkin-modal.tsx) does have activity_id available from getLatestOutreachDraftAction (lines 116/179) but never plumbs it into handleSend (lines 183-199 send only clientId/subject/body_html). The 5s freshness check exists only on the READ path (lines 165-166), not on SEND. And draft-outreach-email.ts always INSERTs a new email_draft row (no upsert/dedup), so repeated opens/retries accumulate multiple draft rows; only the newest flips to kind='email', leaving older ones as 'Drafted an email' entries that DO render in activity-timeline.tsx (line 70) and recent-activity-feed.tsx (line 34). That dangling-row / audit-clutter portion of the claim is real.

HOWEVER, the claim's headline impact is factually wrong. It asserts the timeline's flipped 'sent' row can show subject/body that DIFFERS from what the recruiter reviewed. It cannot: the flip update at lines 297-310 recomposes metadata as { ...prevMeta, subject, body_html, sent_at, sent_to } where subject and body_html are the validated FORM-STATE values the recruiter approved (parsed.data, lines 207/303-304), spread AFTER prevMeta so they always win. Whatever row gets flipped, its displayed subject/body is exactly the reviewed/edited content — never a stale draft's content. The 'body' column is also set to the form subject. So no wrong-content can surface on the sent row.

For the TOCTOU 'newer draft lands mid-edit' to even pick a different physical row, two concurrent draft requests for the same client are needed (two tabs/modals); a single modal instance requests exactly once. Writes are org-scoped (lines 291, 314), so no cross-tenant risk. The emailed body is from form state (claim concedes this). Net real-world residual is cosmetic: stray 'Drafted an email' rows cluttering the timeline when a recruiter retries/reopens — an audit-tidiness nit rooted in non-idempotent draft creation, not in the send-flip targeting. No data corruption, no wrong sent-content, no demo-breaking behaviour.

title: Client `website` is rendered as a clickable href with no URL-scheme validation (stored XSS / dangerous-link)
description: The `website` field is validated only as a trimmed string up to 2000 chars (clientFormSchema / updateClientSchema use `optionalString` with no `.url()` and no scheme allowlist). On the client detail page it is rendered directly as `<a href={client.website} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">`. A user can save `javascript:fetch('/api/...')` (or any non-http scheme) as a website. Any teammate in the same org who clicks the website link on that client's detail page executes the attacker-controlled script in their authenticated session — a stored client-side injection. The form's create path runs through `createClientAction` -> `createClient()` which writes the raw value; the edit path (`updateClientAction`) is identical. The card/list view is NOT affected (it only renders the website as text via `prettyWebsite`), so the vector is the detail-page anchor specifically.
userImpact: A malicious or compromised user (or anyone who can get a crafted website value into a company row) can plant a link that runs script in a colleague's browser when clicked — session theft, silent cross-tenant action under the victim's identity, or data exfiltration. For a 2-3 person agency this is internal, but the same codebase is sold to multi-user SaaS tenants where this becomes a real privilege/abuse path.
evidence: page.tsx: `<a href={client.website} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" ...>`; schema.ts: `const optionalString = z.string().trim().max(2000,'Too long').optional()` and `website: optionalString` (no `.url()`, no http(s) scheme check).
suggestedFix: Validate the scheme at the schema boundary: require the value to start with `https://` (or `http://`) e.g. `z.string().trim().url().refine(v => /^https?:\/\//i.test(v))`, and additionally guard at render time by refusing to render the anchor unless `client.website` starts with `http://` or `https://` (fall back to plain text otherwise). Apply the same scheme check to both create and edit schemas.
verifierReasoning:
The core mechanism is real. Verified each link in the chain:

1. Schema (src/app/(app)/clients/new/schema.ts:7-21): `website: optionalString` where `optionalString = z.string().trim().max(2000).optional()` — no `.url()`, no scheme allowlist. The edit-path schema (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/actions.ts:179) is identical: `website: z.string().trim().max(2000).optional()`. Confirmed.

2. Storage: `createClientAction` -> `createClient()` (src/lib/db/clients.ts:213-218) inserts the raw `website` value unchanged. Confirmed.

3. Render: src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/page.tsx:78-88 renders `<a href={client.website} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">` with zero sanitization. Confirmed.

4. React 19.2.4 (per package.json) does NOT strip `javascript:` hrefs — it emits only a dev-mode console warning and renders the attribute verbatim. So a stored `javascript:...` value would execute on click. The DOM-injection vector is technically valid.

5. The card/list view (client-cards.tsx:86) renders `prettyWebsite(row.website)` as plain text, not an anchor — so the claim is correct that only the detail-page anchor is the vector.

6. A correct allowlist sanitiser already exists in the repo — `sanitiseUrl()` in src/lib/email/escape.ts:22-28 collapses `javascript:`/`data:`/etc. to `#` — but it is NOT imported by the detail page. So no mitigation is applied at the render site.

So the bug genuinely exists: stored, unsanitised, `javascript:`-capable value rendered as a clickable href.

Corrections to the claim's evidence/impact: (a) `updateClientAction` is currently DEAD CODE — a grep for callers returns only its own definition, and its own comment states it is "not implemented as a separate page in this plan; left as an exported action so a future inline edit Sheet can call it." So the edit path the claim presents as an active vector is unreachable today; only the create form is live (which is still sufficient for the bug). (b) The threat model is constrained: `companies` is RLS-scoped per org, so there is NO cross-tenant vector. Exploitation requires a malicious/compromised authenticated user in the same org to plant the payload AND a same-org colleague to click the link. For the anchor customer (a 2-3 person trusted internal agency) this is essentially self-inflicted and very low impact. It only matters in the hypothetical multi-user SaaS future the claim itself flags. (c) The form field uses `type="url"` which is a soft browser hint only and does not block `javascript:` server-side or in all browsers.

Net: real defensive-coding gap (stored DOM XSS via unsanitised href) with a trivial fix (apply the existing `sanitiseUrl()` at render and add a scheme check to the schema), but realistic exploitability for the actual product is low — not the claimed "high".

title: deleteContactAction deletes a contact by id without verifying it belongs to the route's company (same-org cross-company delete)
description: `deleteContactAction(companyId, contactId)` validates that both ids are UUIDs, then calls `deleteContact(supabase, contactId)` which runs `delete from contacts where id = contactId` with no `company_id` predicate. RLS on `contacts` scopes the delete to the caller's organization (so this is NOT cross-tenant), but it does NOT enforce that `contactId` belongs to `companyId`. The contact-edit page deliberately performs this exact check (`if (contact.data.company_id !== id) notFound()` in contacts/[contactId]/edit/page.tsx) and comments that RLS only gates cross-tenant, so a same-org cross-company stale/forged id must be rejected. The delete action omits that defence: a user on `/clients/A` can delete a contact that actually belongs to client B in the same org by passing B's contactId. The post-delete `revalidatePath('/clients/'+companyId)` then revalidates the wrong client's page, so the deletion is also invisible on the page the user was viewing.
userImpact: A recruiter (or a crafted request) can delete a contact attached to a different client in the same agency, and the UI won't reflect it on the page they were on — confusing data loss that looks like a ghost. In a larger multi-recruiter tenant this is an integrity/authorization gap, not just cosmetic.
evidence: actions.ts deleteContactAction: validates ids then `await deleteContact(supabase, contactIdResult.data)` with no company match; contacts.ts deleteContact: `await supabase.from('contacts').delete().eq('id', id)` (only id, no company_id). Compare to edit/page.tsx which guards `if (contact.data.company_id !== id) notFound()`.
suggestedFix: Scope the delete to the company: either pass `companyId` to `deleteContact` and add `.eq('company_id', companyId)` to the delete, or fetch the contact first and assert `contact.company_id === companyId` before deleting (mirroring the edit page). Return a not_found/error otherwise.
verifierReasoning: Verified every load-bearing fact. deleteContactAction (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/actions.ts:103-121) validates companyId+contactId as UUIDs but only passes contactId to deleteContact(supabase, contactIdResult.data); companyId is used solely for revalidatePath. deleteContact (src/lib/db/contacts.ts:105-116) runs `supabase.from('contacts').delete().eq('id', id)` with no company_id predicate. RLS confirms the gap: the contacts `tenant delete` policy (phase1 schema migration:463-464) uses only `organization_id = current_organization_id()` — org-scoped, NOT company-scoped. The cross_tenant_fk_guards trigger (20260517204500:49-51) fires only `before insert or update of company_id, organization_id`, never on delete, so no DB layer enforces the company relationship either. The sibling edit page (contacts/[contactId]/edit/page.tsx:39) deliberately performs `if (contact.data.company_id !== id) notFound()` with a comment that RLS gates cross-tenant only and this defence-in-depth catches same-org cross-company stale URLs — establishing the company-ownership check as the project's own expected pattern, which the delete action omits. The revalidatePath('/clients/'+companyId) mismatch is also real: it revalidates the route's company, not the deleted contact's actual company, producing the described 'ghost' invisibility. So the claim is accurate as written. Severity calibration: the rendered UI path (contact-table.tsx:68) always supplies the route's correct companyId together with target.id drawn from that company's own rows, so a normal user clicking the UI cannot trigger a cross-company delete — exploitation requires a crafted/forged server-action invocation or a stale id, and is confined entirely to the same organization (same agency, where every user already has org-wide contact-delete rights per the RLS policy). RLS fully prevents the worst-case cross-tenant deletion, so the project's stated nightmare bug is absent. This is a genuine defence-in-depth / authorization-consistency gap and an integrity nit (wrong-page revalidation), but it is not reachable through the product UI, not cross-tenant, and not a live-demo blocker — hence low rather than medium.

title: Teammate email addresses are shipped to the browser for every timeline entry even though only the name is displayed
description: The client detail page fetches the full timeline (100 rows) including `actor_full_name` and `actor_email` from the `client_activity_timeline` view, and `toActivityEntries` builds `actor = { full_name: row.actor_full_name, email: row.actor_email }` into the serialized props handed to the `<ActivityTimeline>` Client Component. The actor email is internal-team PII (the recruiter's own email) and is same-org, so it is not a cross-tenant leak, but it is sent in the page's serialized RSC payload for every entry while the UI only needs the display name. This is unnecessary PII surface in the client bundle. It is in-tenant data so severity is low, but worth trimming given the project's audit/PII discipline rules.
userImpact: Org member emails are present in the page source / network payload of the client detail page even when only names render. Low risk for the anchor agency; tightens up for SaaS tenants and aligns with the 'never expose more PII than needed' posture.
evidence: client-management-tabs.tsx: `const actor = row.actor_user_id !== null ? { full_name: row.actor_full_name, email: row.actor_email } : null` passed into ActivityEntry props for a Client Component; view migration 20260518211530 selects `u.email as actor_email`.
suggestedFix: Only pass `full_name` (and `actor_user_id`) into the client-facing ActivityEntry unless the email is actually rendered. If ActivityTimeline needs email for a tooltip, confirm that is intentional; otherwise drop `actor_email` from the mapped entry.
verifierReasoning:
The factual core of the claim is verified. getClientTimeline in src/lib/db/clients.ts does `.select('*')` from the client_activity_timeline view (line 307), and migration 20260518211530 (line 44) defines that view to expose `u.email as actor_email`. So actor_email is fetched on all 100 rows. src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/page.tsx passes the full `timeline` array into <ClientManagementTabs> (line 110), which is a 'use client' Client Component (line 1 of client-management-tabs.tsx). React serializes that component's props into the RSC/Flight payload, so actor_email reaches the browser on every entry — both via the raw `timeline` prop AND via toActivityEntries building `actor = { full_name, email: row.actor_email }` (lines 64-67). These emails are correctly characterised by the claim as same-org internal team emails (recruiters' own addresses), not cross-tenant candidate PII, so there is no leakage bug.

Two corrections to the claim's framing, neither of which negates it but both of which justify keeping severity at the floor: (1) The email is NOT purely unused display data. In src/components/app/activity-timeline.tsx, actorName (line 94) renders `actor.email` as a fallback when full_name is null, and actorInitials (line 88) derives the avatar/aria-label initial from email when full_name is null. So the field has a legitimate rendering purpose for entries whose actor has no full_name. (2) The suggested fix (trim actor_email from the mapper) would not fully achieve the stated goal anyway, because the raw `timeline: ClientTimelineEntry[]` prop already carries actor_email to the client independently of the mapper; you'd also have to strip it in page.tsx before passing it down.

Net: a true but minor hardening observation about in-tenant PII surface in the client bundle, correctly self-graded as low. It is a tidy-up, not a functional bug or a security/compliance violation.

title: List-view "Edit" dropdown links to /clients/[id]/edit which does not exist (404)
description: In the clients table (list view) every row's actions dropdown renders an "Edit" item that links to `/clients/${row.id}/edit`. There is no `edit/page.tsx` under `src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/` — the only edit route in the tree is `contacts/[contactId]/edit`. I confirmed via `find` and `grep`: the only `/edit` page that exists is the contact edit page. The `updateClientAction` server action exists in actions.ts but is explicitly documented as "not implemented as a separate page in this plan" (actions.ts line 171-173). So clicking Edit on any client in the list view navigates to a Next.js 404. The card view (client-cards.tsx) has no Edit affordance at all, and the detail page header (page.tsx) only has Check-in + Delete — there is genuinely NO working way to edit a client's name/industry/website/notes anywhere in the UI, and the one entry point that looks like it works hard-404s.
userImpact: During the demo, if the recruiter opens a client's "..." menu in the list and clicks Edit (the obvious thing to do to fix a typo in a company name), they hit a 404 'page not found' screen. Worse, there is no other route to edit a client at all, so a wrong company name / website can never be corrected through the product.
evidence:
client-table.tsx:98-100
  <DropdownMenuItem asChild>
    <Link href={`/clients/${row.id}/edit`}>Edit</Link>
  </DropdownMenuItem>

`find src/app/(app)/clients -path '*edit*'` returns only `[id]/contacts/[contactId]/edit/page.tsx`. No `[id]/edit` directory exists.
suggestedFix: Either (a) build the missing `/clients/[id]/edit/page.tsx` that renders a client form wired to the already-existing `updateClientAction`, or (b) for the demo, remove the Edit DropdownMenuItem from client-table.tsx so there is no dead link. Option (a) is preferable since the action and validation schema already exist.
verifierReasoning: Verified directly in the code. client-table.tsx line 99 renders `<Link href={`/clients/${row.id}/edit`}>Edit</Link>` inside every row's actions dropdown. The route does not exist: `ls` of `src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/edit` returns "No such file or directory", and the only `/edit` page in the entire clients subtree is `[id]/contacts/[contactId]/edit/page.tsx` (contact edit, not client edit). No catch-all/[...slug] route exists. A root `src/app/not-found.tsx` is present, so visiting the non-existent `/clients/[id]/edit` renders the Next.js 404 page. There is no alternative edit affordance: the card view (client-cards.tsx) wraps the whole card in a single Link to `/clients/${row.id}` with no Edit option, and the detail page header (`[id]/page.tsx`) renders only ClientCheckinButton and ClientDeleteButton. `updateClientAction` is defined in `[id]/actions.ts:188` but its own comment (lines 171-173) states it is "not implemented as a separate page in this plan," and grep across all of src confirms it has zero callers — it is dead code never wired to any UI. So a recruiter has no working way to edit a client's name/industry/website/notes, and the one affordance that appears to do so hard-404s. Every specific claim (the link, the missing route, the unused action, the comment, the absent card/detail edit) checks out exactly as described.

title: Check-in email is sent to an auto-picked contact the recruiter never sees or chooses
description: The SendCheckinModal lets the recruiter edit Subject and Body and click "Send via Outlook", but never displays a To: / recipient. The recipient is resolved entirely server-side in sendOutreachAction as 'the first contact on the company that has a non-null email, ordered by created_at ascending' (limit 1). The modal UI (send-checkin-modal.tsx) renders only Subject + Body fields — there is no recipient display and no recipient picker. For a client with multiple contacts, the email silently goes to whichever contact happens to be oldest-with-an-email, which is frequently NOT the right hiring manager.
userImpact: The recruiter sends a real outbound email to a client contact without ever being shown who it is going to, and with no ability to choose among multiple contacts. They could email the wrong person at the client (e.g. an old junior contact instead of the current decision-maker) and not realise it. This is a relationship-damaging action firing blind — exactly what the CLAUDE.md 'recruiters' professional relationships are on the line' note warns against.
evidence:
outreach-actions.ts:237-256 resolves recipient server-side:
  .from('contacts').select('id, email, full_name').eq('company_id', clientId).not('email','is',null).order('created_at',{ascending:true}).limit(1).maybeSingle()
send-checkin-modal.tsx form (lines 283-330) shows only `dormant-subject` and `dormant-body` inputs — no recipient field rendered anywhere.
suggestedFix: Surface the resolved recipient (full_name + email) in the modal — either as a read-only 'To: Jane Smith <jane@acme.com>' line returned by getLatestOutreachDraftAction / a new resolve action, or a recipient dropdown when the client has more than one contact with an email. At minimum show the To: line before the Send button is enabled.
verifierReasoning: Every factual assertion in the claim is verified against the code. (1) sendOutreachAction (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts:237-244) resolves the recipient entirely server-side as the first contact on the company with a non-null email, ordered by created_at ascending, limit 1 — exactly as quoted. (2) The modal (src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx) renders only a Subject input (id=dormant-subject) and a Body textarea (id=dormant-body) inside its form (lines 283-330). There is NO To:/recipient line, no recipient display, and no contact picker anywhere in the component. The DialogTitle shows only the company name, not the contact person. (3) No recipient identity ever reaches the client: getLatestOutreachDraftAction returns only {activity_id, subject, body_html, created_at}; both entry points (ClientCheckinButton at clients/[id]/page.tsx and DormantClientRow on the dashboard) pass only clientId + clientName. The contact's email is written to activity metadata (sent_to) only AFTER the send succeeds (lines 308, 327) — never surfaced beforehand. (4) The contacts table (migration 20260513152244, lines 178-194) has company_id as a non-unique FK with a plain index, so a company can have many contacts each optionally with an email — meaning 'oldest-with-an-email' is a real, frequent ambiguity. (5) sendMail genuinely transmits a real outbound email via Microsoft Graph (outlook.ts:693). So the recruiter sends a real relationship-bearing email to an auto-picked client contact they are never shown and cannot choose among. This violates the CLAUDE.md note that recruiters' professional relationships are on the line.

title: Draft poll freshness window compares server timestamp to client Date.now() — clock skew can make the draft never appear
description: When the modal polls for the AI draft it only accepts a draft whose `created_at` is fresh: `if (draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current)`. `seedTimeRef.current` is set to the browser's `Date.now()` at mount (client clock), while `draftTime = new Date(got.data.created_at).getTime()` is the DB-written `occurred_at` (server clock, the activities default `now()`). If the client clock runs more than ~5s ahead of the server clock (common on laptops with drifted clocks), a draft that the Inngest function just wrote will have `draftTime` well behind `seedTimeRef.current`, the condition fails on every poll, and after 10 attempts the modal shows the 'taking longer than expected' timeout — even though the draft row exists and is correct. Retrying just repeats the same comparison and times out again.
userImpact: On some machines (and unpredictably), the 'Send check-in' modal will spin 'Drafting an email with AI…' and then show a timeout/Retry every time, never surfacing the generated email — while the AI cost was already incurred. The recruiter perceives the headline AI feature as broken during the demo, with no way to recover.
evidence:
send-checkin-modal.tsx:145 `seedTimeRef.current = Date.now()` (client clock)
send-checkin-modal.tsx:165-166 `const draftTime = new Date(got.data.created_at).getTime(); if (draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current)` compares server-written occurred_at against the client clock seed.
suggestedFix: Don't gate on absolute time comparison across two clocks. Instead capture the latest draft activity_id BEFORE firing the request (or have requestOutreachDraftAction return a request id / the new draft's id) and accept the first draft whose id differs from the pre-existing one. Alternatively widen the window dramatically or drop the freshness check and rely on the fire→poll ordering, since the modal already fired the request itself on mount.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is mechanically accurate and I confirmed every link in the chain against the actual code.

1. send-checkin-modal.tsx:145 sets `seedTimeRef.current = Date.now()` at mount — the BROWSER clock.
2. send-checkin-modal.tsx:165-166 computes `draftTime = new Date(got.data.created_at).getTime()` and accepts a draft only if `draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current`.
3. `got.data.created_at` is sourced in outreach-actions.ts:182 from `data.occurred_at` (the SELECT at line 146-154 reads `occurred_at`, mapped into `created_at`). It is NOT the browser clock.
4. The `activities.occurred_at` column defaults to `now()` (Postgres/server clock) per migration 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:339, and the Inngest writer (draft-outreach-email.ts:141-167) inserts the row without supplying `occurred_at`, so it takes the server-default `now()`.

So the comparison is genuinely cross-clock: client `Date.now()` at mount vs server `now()` at insert. Working the algebra with skew = clientClock - serverClock and Δ = draft latency (Inngest dispatch + one Sonnet call + insert), the accept condition reduces to `skew <= Δ + 5000ms`. If the client clock runs ahead of the server by more than roughly (Δ + 5s), every one of the 10 polls fails and the modal lands in the `timeout` state showing "taking longer than expected" — even though a correct draft row exists and the AI cost was already spent.

The retry path is worse, not a recovery: handleRetryDraft (line 202) re-stamps `seedTimeRef.current = Date.now()` to an even-later client time while still polling the same draft, so under client-ahead skew it deterministically times out again — matching the "retry just repeats and times out" narrative.

The modal is reachable from both the dashboard dormant-clients widget (dormant-client-row.tsx) and the client detail page (client-checkin-button.tsx), so the user-impact narrative about the demo CTA is the real path.

Where I diverge from the report is severity. The bug only fires when the client clock runs AHEAD of the server by more than ~6-12s. A client running behind makes the freshness check pass trivially. Modern macOS/Windows/Linux laptops (including the anchor customer's) keep NTP enabled by default and stay within sub-second of true time, so the triggering condition is uncommon, not "common." When it does hit it is deterministic, unrecoverable from the UI, and burns AI spend while presenting the headline feature as broken — but it is a visible failure with a Retry affordance, not silent data loss or cross-tenant leakage. That profile is a real, fixable, design-fragility bug of medium severity rather than high. The suggested fix (gate acceptance on a new activity_id rather than a cross-clock timestamp comparison) is correct and removes the clock dependency entirely.

title: Send check-in opens and generates a paid AI draft even when the client has no contact email — fails only at Send
description: The detail-page ClientCheckinButton always renders (for any client, dormant or not) and is not gated on whether the client has any contact with an email. SendCheckinModalBody fires requestOutreachDraftAction immediately on mount, which dispatches the Inngest event and runs a Sonnet draft (a real ai_usage cost). Only later, when the recruiter clicks Send, does sendOutreachAction discover there is no contact email and return 'No contact with an email on file for this client. Add a contact first.' So for a freshly-created client with zero contacts, the AI spend happens up-front and the recruiter only learns it was futile after editing and clicking Send.
userImpact: On a brand-new or contact-less client, clicking 'Send check-in' burns an AI draft (cost) and walks the recruiter all the way through editing an email, only to dead-end at Send with 'Add a contact first.' Wasted time and spend, and a confusing flow where the AI clearly worked but sending is impossible.
evidence:
client-checkin-button.tsx:25-34 renders the button unconditionally (no contact check).
send-checkin-modal.tsx:147-148 `const requested = await requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId })` runs on mount before any recipient check.
outreach-actions.ts:251-256 only here does it return the no-email error, after the draft has already been generated.
suggestedFix: Resolve a sendable recipient BEFORE drafting: have requestOutreachDraftAction (or a cheap pre-check) verify a contact-with-email exists and short-circuit with the 'Add a contact first' message before firing the Inngest/Sonnet draft. Optionally disable/hide the Send check-in button when the client has no contact email.
verifierReasoning: Verified end-to-end; the claim is accurate. (1) ClientCheckinButton (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/client-checkin-button.tsx:25-34) renders unconditionally for every client — its own comment says "Always-on … visible whether the client is dormant or not"; client/[id]/page.tsx:70-74 passes isDormant only to switch the button variant, not to gate it, and there is no contact-email check. (2) On modal open, SendCheckinModalBody's mount useEffect (send-checkin-modal.tsx:143-148) immediately calls requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId }). (3) That action (outreach-actions.ts:84-92) dispatches the outreach-draft/requested Inngest event with NO contact check; draft-outreach-email.ts gathers only company name + last placement (no contacts query) and calls draftOutreachEmail → claude-sonnet-4-6 via runWithLogging, which writes a real ai_usage row (purpose='dormant_outreach_draft', ~0.55p per draft per the wrapper's own comment). (4) The ONLY contact-email lookup in the whole flow is in sendOutreachAction (outreach-actions.ts:237-256), returning 'No contact with an email on file for this client. Add a contact first.' at line 254 — i.e. at Send, after the paid draft already exists. A grep across the request action, Inngest function, and AI wrapper confirms no earlier pre-check. (5) The dormant_clients RPC (migration 20260520031200) filters on last_contacted_at + existence of a prior placement but does NOT require a contact-with-email, and placement (applications/jobs) vs contact email (contacts) are independent — so even the dashboard dormant path can hit the dead-end, and the detail-page button is open to any client whatsoever. Severity downgraded from medium to low: the bug is genuine but its impact is sub-penny wasted AI spend plus a confusing edit-then-dead-end flow; it does not break tenancy, corrupt data, or fail silently. The suggested fix (cheap contact-with-email pre-check in requestOutreachDraftAction before firing Inngest, and/or hiding the button when no contact email exists) is correct.

title: Re-opening the check-in modal (or Retry) writes a new email_draft activity each time, cluttering the Activity timeline
description: Every mount of SendCheckinModalBody (i.e. each time the modal opens) and every handleRetryDraft call fires requestOutreachDraftAction, which dispatches outreach-draft/requested; the Inngest function then INSERTS a fresh activities row with kind='email_draft' (draft-outreach-email.ts step 'write-activity', plain insert, no upsert/dedupe). There is no idempotency guard. The Activity tab timeline (client-management-tabs.tsx) does not filter out email_draft, and ActivityTimeline renders email_draft as 'Drafted an email'. So opening the modal 3 times, or hitting Retry a couple times, leaves 3+ 'Drafted an email' entries in the client's permanent activity history even though nothing was sent.
userImpact: The recruiter's clean activity timeline for a client fills up with repeated 'Drafted an email' system entries every time they so much as open (or retry) the check-in modal without sending. The audit/relationship timeline — pitched as a key feature — looks noisy and untrustworthy.
evidence:
draft-outreach-email.ts:141-167 step 'write-activity' does `.from('activities').insert({ kind: 'email_draft', ... })` with no dedupe.
send-checkin-modal.tsx:147 and :204 both call requestOutreachDraftAction on every open / Retry.
client-management-tabs.tsx:181-187 Activity tab passes the full timeline (which includes email_draft kinds) to ActivityTimeline.
suggestedFix: Either filter kind='email_draft' out of the Activity timeline (only show 'email' once sent), or make the draft write an upsert that replaces the most recent unsent email_draft for the company rather than inserting a new row per request. Dedupe on (organization_id, entity_type, entity_id, kind='email_draft', unsent).
verifierReasoning: I traced the full chain and the claim holds end-to-end. (1) send-checkin-modal.tsx mounts SendCheckinModalBody only when open=true (lines 79-85), and its useEffect (143-181) calls requestOutreachDraftAction({clientId}) unconditionally on every mount; handleRetryDraft (201-204) does the same. (2) requestOutreachDraftAction in outreach-actions.ts always fires inngest.send('outreach-draft/requested') (lines 84-92) with no check for an existing recent/unsent draft. (3) draft-outreach-email.ts step 'write-activity' (141-167) does a bare supabase.from('activities').insert({ kind:'email_draft', ... }) — no upsert, no dedupe, no update-most-recent logic. (4) There is no unique constraint guarding this: the only indexes on activities are activities_organization_id_idx and a non-unique activities_entity_idx (phase1_domain_schema.sql:346-347). So N opens / N retries => N rows. (5) The client_activity_timeline view (both 20260517215956 and 20260518211530) selects a.kind with NO WHERE filter excluding email_draft, and client-management-tabs.tsx Activity tab (181-187) passes the entire timeline through toActivityEntries to ActivityTimeline unfiltered; KIND_META.email_draft renders 'Drafted an email' (activity-timeline.tsx:70-74). Every cited line number matches. The impact is actually broader than claimed: getRecentActivity (dashboard.ts:84-88) also selects '*' with no kind filter and recent-activity-feed.tsx renders email_draft as 'Drafted an email', so repeated drafts also clutter the dashboard 'Recent activity' widget, not just the client Activity tab. The send path (sendOutreachAction) flips the most recent draft to kind='email', but only the most recent one — earlier duplicate drafts remain as 'Drafted an email' rows regardless. Severity held at medium: it is real, user-facing, and triggered by the most mundane action (merely opening or retrying the modal without sending), and it undermines the audit/relationship timeline that is pitched as a key feature — but it is cosmetic clutter, not data corruption, cross-tenant leakage, or a silent-fail write, so it is not high/critical.

title: Disabled Previous/Next pagination buttons render with no disabled styling (look clickable)
description: The pagination uses `<Button asChild variant="outline" size="sm" disabled={!hasPrev}>` wrapping either a Link (enabled) or a <span> (disabled). When asChild is true the Button renders Slot and spreads `disabled` onto the child. The child on the disabled branch is a plain <span>, which does not support the `disabled` attribute, and the Tailwind disabled styles (`disabled:opacity-50 disabled:pointer-events-none`) are :disabled pseudo-class rules that only match real form controls — so the disabled span gets neither dimming nor pointer-events-none. The button is functionally inert (it's a span, not a link) but visually looks identical to an active button.
userImpact: On page 1 (or the last page), Previous/Next still look like normal clickable buttons. The recruiter clicks them and nothing happens, with no greyed-out cue explaining why — mildly confusing but not blocking, since the buttons genuinely do nothing rather than misnavigating.
evidence: page.tsx:139-145 button.tsx:8 disabled styles are `disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50` (:disabled pseudo, no-op on span); :51-59 spreads `{...props}` (incl. disabled) onto Slot.
suggestedFix: On the disabled branch, render a span with explicit muted classes (e.g. add `aria-disabled="true"` and `opacity-50 pointer-events-none cursor-not-allowed`) instead of relying on the `disabled` prop, which is a no-op for non-form children under asChild.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed every link in the claim against the actual code. page.tsx lines 139-145 render two pagination buttons exactly as cited: `<Button asChild variant="outline" size="sm" disabled={!hasPrev/hasNext}>` wrapping `<Link>` (enabled) or plain `<span>` (disabled branch). button.tsx line 8 has `disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50` in the base class string. button.tsx lines 51/59 set `Comp = Slot.Root` when asChild and spread `{...props}` (including `disabled`) onto it; radix-ui ^1.4.3 Slot.Root merges those props onto the single child. The CSS reasoning is correct: Tailwind's `disabled:` variant compiles to the `:disabled` pseudo-class, which per spec matches only form-associated elements (button/input/select/etc.), never a `<span>`. So the `disabled` boolean attribute on the span is inert for styling — no opacity dimming, no pointer-events removal. The disabled Previous/Next button therefore renders at full opacity, visually identical to an active outline button. It is functionally inert (span has no href/handler, so clicking does nothing — no misnavigation), matching the claimed impact. Note this only manifests when total > 25 (pageSize), since the pagination nav is gated behind `total > pageSize`. Real, cosmetic, mild-confusion defect; no functional/navigation/data breakage. Low severity is honest.

title: Single-character client search silently returns the full unfiltered list
description: listClients only runs the search RPC when `q && q.length >= 2`. For a 1-character query it falls through to the plain `select('*')` and returns ALL clients ignoring the query. The page derives `isNoMatch = total === 0 && q.length > 0`, but since the 1-char query returns the full list, total > 0 and the entire client list renders while the search box visibly contains the typed character. There is no feedback that the single character is being ignored.
userImpact: A recruiter typing one letter to filter sees the whole client list rather than a filtered set or a 'keep typing' hint. Minor — most searches are longer — but momentarily confusing that the first keystroke appears to do nothing.
evidence:
clients.ts:62 `if (q && q.length >= 2) { ...search RPC... }` — falls through to plain select for length-1 queries.
page.tsx:56 `const isNoMatch = total === 0 && q.length > 0` — never true for a 1-char query because the full list is returned.
suggestedFix: Either run the search for length-1 queries too, or render a subtle 'Type at least 2 characters to search' hint when q.length === 1 so the no-op is explained.
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code. clients.ts:62 gates the pg_trgm search RPC behind `if (q && q.length >= 2)`. For a 1-character query the trimmed `q` has length 1, so the condition is false and execution falls through to the "No query" branch (lines 96-119), which runs a plain `supabase.from('companies').select('*', { count: 'exact' })` with sort + pagination — i.e. it returns ALL tenant companies, ignoring the typed character. The page (page.tsx:54-56) then computes `total` from that unfiltered count, so `isNoMatch = total === 0 && q.length > 0` is false (total > 0 whenever the tenant has any clients) and `isEmpty` is also false. The full client list therefore renders via ClientsShell while the search box still visibly contains the typed character. search-input.tsx confirms the single char IS pushed to the URL (commit() sets ?q= for any next.length > 0), so the discrepancy is real and observable. I grepped the entire clients feature: the only length>=2 guard is clients.ts:62, and there is no client-side minLength attribute, no length-1 short-circuit, and no "type at least 2 characters" hint anywhere — so the no-op genuinely has no feedback. The same >=2 threshold appears in candidates.ts (lines 85, 194), confirming the minimum-length is an intentional codebase-wide pattern, just one that lacks a user-facing hint. Severity stays low: the affected window is a single keystroke, the 300ms debounce in SearchInput means the user is usually already typing toward a 2+ char query before the URL updates, and once a second character is entered the search fires correctly. No data loss, no broken flow — purely momentary cosmetic confusion, negligible for a live client demo.

title: Sending a check-in to a dormant client does not clear the Dormant flag or update Last contacted
description: The headline dormant-outreach flow flips the existing email_draft activity row to kind='email' via an UPDATE (outreach-actions.ts lines 297-314). But the bump_last_contacted_at trigger that maintains companies.last_contacted_at fires `after insert on public.activities` only (migration 20260517215957_bump_last_contacted_at.sql line 43-46) — it never runs on UPDATE. The original email_draft insert by the Inngest function also does not bump, because the trigger's `kind in ('call','email','meeting','note')` filter excludes 'email_draft'. Net result: after a successful check-in send, companies.last_contacted_at is unchanged. The client stays >60 days stale, so isDormant() (clients.ts line 43-48) still returns true and the dormant_clients RPC still lists it. The ONLY path that would bump is the rare fallback insert branch (lines 320-328, when no draft row existed) — so behaviour is inconsistent: the common path fails, the unlikely path works.
userImpact: The recruiter sends an AI check-in email to a dormant client during the demo, the email genuinely sends, but the client stays flagged 'Dormant' (amber badge), stays in the 'N dormant' header badge and the dashboard dormant widget, and 'Last contacted' never updates. The core 'wake up dormant clients' loop appears broken — the action they just took has no visible effect.
suggestedFix: In sendOutreachAction, after a successful Outlook send, explicitly bump companies.last_contacted_at (and ideally the chosen contact's last_contacted_at) to now() within the same service-role write, scoped to profile.organization_id. Do not rely on the activity flip to propagate the timestamp, since the bump trigger only runs on INSERT. Also revalidatePath('/clients') so the list reflects the change.
verifierReasoning: Verified the full chain against the actual code. (1) The common send path in src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts:297-314 flips the existing email_draft row to kind='email' via .update(...), not an insert. (2) The active trigger definition (supabase/migrations/20260518211702_harden_bump_last_contacted_at.sql:20-42, which CREATE OR REPLACEs the body but the trigger object from 20260517215957 fires AFTER INSERT on public.activities only). Grep confirms there is no AFTER UPDATE trigger on activities anywhere; the other 'insert or update' triggers are set_org / cross-tenant FK guards that never touch last_contacted_at. (3) The original Inngest insert uses kind='email_draft' (draft-outreach-email.ts:150), which is excluded by the trigger's kind in ('call','email','meeting','note') filter, so it also does not bump. (4) Dormancy is computed purely from companies.last_contacted_at: dormant_clients RPC filters c.last_contacted_at < now() - p_dormant_days (20260520031200 lines 75-76) and isDormant() uses a 60-day window (clients.ts:43-48). Net: after a successful send via the common path, companies.last_contacted_at is never updated, so the client stays Dormant in the badge, header count, and dashboard widget, and Last contacted never moves. (5) Confirmed the inconsistency: the rare fallback INSERT branch (lines 320-328) inserts kind='email' with occurred_at defaulting to now() (activities.occurred_at default now() at phase1 schema line 339), which DOES fire the trigger and bump — so the unlikely path works while the common path silently fails. (6) The only application-level writer of last_contacted_at is candidates.ts:454 (candidates, unrelated to companies). The suggested fix (explicitly bump companies.last_contacted_at within the service-role write scoped to org, plus revalidatePath) is correct.

title: Contact emails are stored with original casing but matched case-sensitively, dropping inbound email activity
description: createContactAction stores email via coerceOptional() which only trims — it does NOT lowercase (actions.ts lines 31-35, 54-57; same for updateContactAction). However findContactByEmail (contacts.ts lines 130-151) lowercases the search input and does an exact `.eq('email', normalised)` match, with a comment asserting 'contacts follow the same convention' of being lowercased at write-time. That assertion is false for the contact create/edit path in this domain. So a contact saved as 'Jane@Acme.com' will never match an inbound Outlook email normalised to 'jane@acme.com'.
userImpact: Emails to/from a client contact whose address was typed with any uppercase letters silently fail to attach to that contact's timeline (D2-19 drops unmatched emails). The recruiter sees an incomplete activity history for the client and never knows entries were lost.
suggestedFix: Lowercase the contact email at the write boundary in createContactAction/updateContactAction (e.g. coerceOptional(parsed.data.email)?.toLowerCase()), matching the candidate convention findContactByEmail depends on. Alternatively make findContactByEmail case-insensitive with a functional lower(email) index. Backfill existing rows to lowercase.
verifierReasoning:
The claim holds. Verified end to end:

WRITE PATH (case preserved): src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/actions.ts:31-35 coerceOptional() only .trim()s; createContactAction (line 55) and updateContactAction (line 88) store email via it. The contact form schema (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/contacts/new/schema.ts:20-28) is .trim()+.refine() only — no .toLowerCase()/.transform(). src/lib/db/contacts.ts createContact/updateContact (lines 55-103) insert/update the email verbatim. No DB normalization: contacts table (supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:178-191) is plain `email text`; the set_organization_id() trigger only sets org; there is NO lower(email)=email CHECK on contacts (contrast org_invitations which DOES have org_invitations_email_lower_check in 20260524000100). So contact emails persist with original casing.

READ PATH (case-sensitive): src/lib/db/contacts.ts findContactByEmail (lines 130-151) computes normalised = email.toLowerCase().trim() then queries .eq('email', normalised). Postgres .eq is exact/byte comparison, so a stored 'Jane@Acme.com' will not equal 'jane@acme.com'. The code comment (lines 126-128) asserts "contacts follow the same convention [lowercased at write-time]" — this is provably false for the contact CRUD path. Additionally migration 20260519120000_contacts_email_idx.sql indexes lower(email) "for the ilike path", but the M1 fix swapped .ilike for .eq('email'), so the query is now both case-sensitive AND can no longer use that functional index.

IMPACT PATH confirmed: src/lib/inngest/functions/sync-outlook-history.ts:188-194 — findContactByEmail is one of two lookups; if neither candidate nor contact matches, the message is skipped (continue, the D2-19 orphan-skip). So an inbound/outbound email to a contact whose address was stored with any uppercase letter silently fails to attach to that contact's timeline.

Contrast confirming the divergence: the candidate apply-form schema (src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/schema.ts:49) explicitly .toLowerCase()s email, so candidates from the primary intake ARE lowercased — exactly the convention findContactByEmail depends on but contacts do not honor.

Severity downgraded from high to medium: the bug is genuine and silent (worst kind), but it only fires when (a) the Outlook history sync feature is active AND (b) a recruiter typed a contact email containing uppercase letters. Most email addresses are typed/pasted lowercase, so the realistic hit rate is a minority of contacts, and the failure mode is missing-timeline-entries (data completeness gap) rather than a crash or visibly broken demo flow. Real correctness bug, conditional impact — medium, not high.

title: List/detail 'Dormant' badge and the 'N dormant' count use different definitions
description: isDormant() (clients.ts lines 43-48) flags a client dormant whenever last_contacted_at is set and older than 60 days — regardless of placement history. This drives the amber 'Dormant' badge in client-table.tsx, client-cards.tsx and the detail header. But the DormantBadge count and dashboard widget call the dormant_clients RPC (migration 20260520031200), which additionally requires `exists (placement with stage='placed')` (lines 77-83). The two populations differ: a client contacted 90 days ago with no prior placement shows the amber Dormant badge but is NOT counted in the header '+N dormant' badge and never appears in the dormant widget.
userImpact: On the same Clients page the recruiter sees rows tagged 'Dormant' that aren't reflected in the 'N dormant' header count, and clicking through to the dashboard widget those clients are missing. It reads as a counting bug and erodes trust in the dormancy feature during the demo.
suggestedFix: Pick one definition. Either drop the placement requirement from the badge/widget count, or have isDormant()/the row badge also require placement history, so the per-row badge and the aggregate count always agree. Document which definition is canonical.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed in code. The per-row "Dormant" badge is driven by isDormant() in src/lib/db/clients.ts:43-48, which returns true whenever last_contacted_at is non-null and older than 60 days (DORMANT_AFTER_MS), with NO placement requirement. This `dormant` boolean is set in enrichWithActiveJobs (clients.ts:152) and consumed by client-table.tsx:66 (row.dormant), client-cards.tsx:62 (row.dormant), and the detail header [id]/page.tsx:60 (client.dormant). Separately, the "N dormant" header badge (clients/dormant-badge.tsx:18-30) and the dashboard widget both call getDormantClients (src/lib/db/dormant-clients.ts), which invokes the dormant_clients RPC. That RPC (migration 20260520031200_phase3_dormant_clients_rpc.sql:74-84) requires BOTH last_contacted_at older than the threshold AND `exists (select 1 from applications a join jobs j on j.id=a.job_id where j.company_id=c.id and a.stage='placed')`. The migration header comment even explicitly states the placement requirement was a deliberate design choice (lines 17-22, RESEARCH §M6: "We don't widget every cold lead with a stale contact timestamp"). So the two populations genuinely differ: a company contacted >60 days ago with no placed application renders the amber "Dormant" row badge but is excluded from both the header count and the dashboard widget. Both surfaces appear on the same /clients page (page.tsx:93 renders <DormantBadge/> in the same header as the table). The claim's file/line citations, RPC SQL, and UI wiring all verify exactly. Note one nuance the claim glosses over: this divergence was an intentional design decision (documented in the migration), not an accidental oversight — but the per-row badge was never updated to match, so the inconsistency the reviewer describes is real. Severity adjusted to low: it is a display-only count/badge mismatch contingent on a narrow data shape (stale-contact company with zero placements), causes no incorrect action or data issue, and the anchor agency's demo data may not even trigger it.

title: Client search returns results ordered by last-contacted, not by match relevance
description: The page always defaults sort to 'last_contacted_at' (page.tsx parseSort line 17-20, used line 39) and SearchInput only ever sets ?q= (search-input.tsx commit() lines 23-34) — it never sets sort=similarity. listClients passes that sort straight into search_clients as p_sort (clients.ts lines 70-77), and the RPC only orders by similarity when p_sort='similarity' (migration 20260517215958 lines 70-76). So a name/industry search is ordered by last_contacted_at desc, not by trigram similarity. The closest match can be buried below less-relevant but more-recently-contacted clients.
userImpact: The recruiter types a client name in search and the best match is not at the top — it may be on page 2 — making search feel broken for the exact scenario (fast lookup) the product sells.
suggestedFix: When a query is present and the user hasn't explicitly chosen a sort, default to similarity ordering (e.g. in listClients: when q is set and args.sort is undefined, pass p_sort='similarity'), or have SearchInput set sort=similarity alongside q.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the full chain against the actual code; every link in the claim holds.

1) src/app/(app)/clients/page.tsx lines 17-20: parseSort() returns 'last_contacted_at' whenever sp.sort is absent or invalid; used at line 39. Confirmed.

2) src/app/(app)/clients/search-input.tsx lines 23-34: commit() builds params from existing entries, sets/deletes only 'q', and deletes 'page'. It never sets 'sort'. So a freshly typed search lands on the page as ?q=foo with no sort param, which parseSort resolves to 'last_contacted_at'. Confirmed.

3) src/lib/db/clients.ts: sort defaults to 'last_contacted_at' (line 57); in the search branch (q present, len>=2) it calls search_clients with p_sort: sort === 'similarity' ? 'similarity' : sort (line 73) — i.e. it forwards 'last_contacted_at' straight through. There is NO "switch to similarity when a query is present" logic anywhere. Confirmed.

4) supabase/migrations/20260517215958_search_clients_rpc.sql lines 69-76: the ORDER BY only ranks by f.similarity when p_sort='similarity'; otherwise (p_sort='last_contacted_at') it orders by f.last_contacted_at desc nulls last, then f.id. Confirmed.

Net effect: a name/industry search is ordered by last_contacted_at desc, not by trigram similarity. The strongest match can be buried below weaker-matching but more-recently-contacted clients.

Decisive corroboration: the analogous candidates flow does it the RIGHT way and documents the intended design. src/lib/db/candidates.ts lines 194-237 calls search_candidates with only p_query/p_limit/p_offset (no p_sort), and the inline comment states: "the RPC has its own ordering (similarity desc, then full_name asc) and ignores sort/dir from the caller. This is intentional — search results are most useful ranked by relevance. Sort headers should be disabled in the UI while a query is active." The clients implementation regressed from this established, documented pattern.

I also checked the UI for any escape hatch: client-table.tsx has no sortable column headers (only Links to detail/edit pages), and 'similarity' appears in clients code ONLY as an allowlisted VALID_SORTS value (page.tsx:14) — nothing in the UI ever emits sort=similarity. So the relevance-ranked path is unreachable for a searching recruiter.

Severity: medium is correct, not high. The RPC pre-filters with the pg_trgm % operator and similarity >= 0.2 (lines 46-50), so results are still genuinely relevant rows — the bug is mis-ORDERING among matches, not returning junk. But for common-token searches (e.g. multiple companies sharing a word, or partial-name lookups) the best/exact match can be pushed off page 1 (page size 25), which directly undermines the product's headline "find in seconds" promise. Real, user-facing, but bounded by the trigram gate.

Relevant files: /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/clients/page.tsx (lines 17-20, 39), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/clients/search-input.tsx (lines 23-34), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/clients.ts (lines 57, 62-77), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/supabase/migrations/20260517215958_search_clients_rpc.sql (lines 14, 69-76). Contrast reference: /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/candidates.ts (lines 194-237).

Suggested fix is sound: in listClients, when q is present and args.sort is undefined/not explicitly chosen, pass p_sort='similarity' (and ideally disable/avoid emitting non-similarity sort while searching, mirroring the candidates pattern). Alternatively have the RPC default-order by similarity when a query is active regardless of p_sort.

title: Outreach draft freshness check compares client clock to server timestamp, can spuriously time out
description: The modal seeds seedTimeRef = Date.now() on the browser clock (line 145) and accepts a polled draft only if `draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current` (lines 165-166). draftTime comes from activities.occurred_at, which defaults to the database clock (now(), migration 20260513152244 line 339) and is returned via created_at. If the user's laptop clock is more than ~5s ahead of the server (common), a freshly generated, correct draft is rejected as 'stale', the loop exhausts MAX_POLL_ATTEMPTS, and the recruiter sees the 'taking longer than expected' timeout despite a valid draft already existing.
userImpact: On a machine with even minor clock drift, the AI check-in modal shows a timeout/Retry instead of the draft that was actually produced — the recruiter retries (spending another Sonnet call) and may still hit the same skew, making the AI feature look unreliable in the demo.
suggestedFix: Don't gate freshness on a client-vs-server timestamp comparison. Capture the activity_id returned by requestOutreachDraftAction's Inngest flow (or return the new draft's id) and poll for that specific id, or have getLatestOutreachDraftAction return only drafts created after a server-issued cursor. At minimum widen the skew tolerance well beyond 5s.
verifierReasoning:
The mechanism is real and the code references check out. In src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx, seedTimeRef.current = Date.now() (line 145, browser clock) is compared against the draft timestamp at lines 165-166: `const draftTime = new Date(got.data.created_at).getTime()` then `if (draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current)`. The draft timestamp comes from the activities row: getLatestOutreachDraftAction (outreach-actions.ts:182) returns `created_at: data.occurred_at`, i.e. the value is actually occurred_at. Both occurred_at and created_at on activities default to server `now()` (migration 20260513152244 lines 339 and 341), and the Inngest insert (draft-outreach-email.ts:141-167) does NOT set occurred_at, so it takes the DB server clock. So the comparison is genuinely server-clock-timestamp + 5000ms >= browser-clock-timestamp — a cross-clock comparison, exactly as claimed. There is no skew correction anywhere. The same flawed comparison is duplicated in handleRetryDraft (lines 202, 218-219). So on a machine whose clock runs fast relative to the Supabase server, a freshly generated valid draft can be rejected as 'stale', exhausting MAX_POLL_ATTEMPTS and showing the 'taking longer than expected' / Retry timeout. The suggested fix is also sound and notably cheap: getLatestOutreachDraftAction ALREADY returns activity_id and the Inngest function returns activity_id — the modal just ignores it and gates on timestamps instead, so polling for the specific id is a small, available change.

However the claimed magnitude is overstated on two counts. (1) The draft row is always created AFTER the seed in real wall-clock time — there is a server-action round-trip + Inngest dispatch + Sonnet generation latency (typically ~2-5s) between seeding seedTimeRef and the row's occurred_at. That real elapsed time adds headroom in the SAME direction, so a fresh draft is wrongly rejected only when the browser is ahead of the server by more than ~5000ms PLUS the generation latency (roughly 8s+ in practice), not 'more than ~5s' as stated. (2) The description calls >5s clock drift 'common'; in reality macOS/Windows are NTP-synced to sub-second by default, so this only bites a machine with broken/disabled time sync. It is a genuine edge-case robustness bug worth fixing, but it does not fire on a normally-configured demo laptop.

title: Notes and Activity tabs silently cap at 100 entries; older notes disappear
description: The detail page fetches getClientTimeline(supabase, id, 100) (page.tsx line 36). client-management-tabs.tsx then both renders the Activity tab from this 100-row slice and derives the Notes tab by filtering the same 100 rows (lines 196-205). For a long-running client relationship with >100 activity entries, the oldest notes silently vanish from the Notes tab with no 'showing latest 100' indicator and no pagination.
userImpact: On an established client, a recruiter scrolling the Notes tab for an older note won't find it and has no signal that the list is truncated — they may conclude the note was never saved.
suggestedFix: Either paginate/lazy-load the timeline, query notes separately without the shared 100 cap, or render a clear 'showing the 100 most recent activities' affordance so truncation is visible.
verifierReasoning: Verified at the code level. src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/page.tsx line 36 calls getClientTimeline(supabase, id, 100). getClientTimeline (src/lib/db/clients.ts lines 280-317) orders by occurred_at DESC and applies .limit(100). The client_activity_timeline view (supabase/migrations/20260517215956_client_activity_view.sql) aggregates ALL activity kinds (notes, calls, emails, status changes) across company, contact, AND job entities for the client. client-management-tabs.tsx renders the Activity tab (lines 181-187) from props.timeline and derives the Notes tab (lines 189-213) by filtering that same 100-row array on kind === 'note' && entity_type === 'company' (lines 196-197, 206-208). No pagination, no 'load more', and no 'showing latest 100' indicator exist anywhere in the clients/[id] directory (grep confirmed zero matches). So for an established client exceeding 100 combined activity entries, older company notes silently drop from the Notes tab with no signal. The claim is in fact understated: because the 100 cap is applied across all kinds/entities BEFORE the Notes filter, a client with many calls/job activities could lose even relatively recent company notes, not just notes beyond the 100th. This is not a silent-fail mutation or data-loss bug — notes ARE persisted and ARE queryable; the data layer works correctly up to the cap. It is purely a missing-truncation-affordance edge case. For the anchor 2-3 person agency, reaching 100+ combined activities on a single client is plausible only for very long-running relationships, so real-world incidence is low and it would not break a live demo. The originally-claimed low/edge-case grade is accurate.

title: One-character search silently returns the full client list instead of matches
description: listClients only routes to the search RPC when `q && q.length >= 2` (clients.ts line 62). For a single-character query the branch is skipped and it falls through to the plain select, returning ALL clients while the URL still shows ?q=a. The page's isNoMatch flag is `total === 0 && q.length > 0` (page.tsx line 56), which is false here, so no 'no matches' messaging appears and the count line reads e.g. '1–25 of 40' even though the user is mid-search.
userImpact: Typing one letter shows the entire client list with no indication the search term was ignored, which is confusing while typing a longer query.
suggestedFix: When q is present but below the 2-char minimum, either show a 'keep typing to search' hint or treat it consistently (don't render full-list counts as if unfiltered). Pass the minimum-length state to the page so messaging matches the actual filter applied.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the claim against the actual code and it holds exactly as described.

1. src/lib/db/clients.ts:62 gates the search RPC behind `if (q && q.length >= 2)`. For a 1-char query this is false, so execution falls through to the plain select at line 101 (`.from('companies').select('*', { count: 'exact' })`) which returns ALL clients for the tenant plus the full `count`. The query `q` is completely ignored in this branch.

2. src/app/(app)/clients/search-input.tsx commits any non-empty value to the URL with no minimum-length guard: line 25 `if (next.length > 0) params.set('q', next)`. So typing one character does put `?q=a` in the URL. There is no "keep typing" hint or min-length check anywhere (confirmed by grep — no such string exists in the clients route).

3. src/app/(app)/clients/page.tsx:38 reads `q = sp.q?.trim() ?? ''` (a single non-whitespace char survives the trim, length 1). Line 56 `isNoMatch = total === 0 && q.length > 0` evaluates to false because `total` is the full unfiltered count (e.g. 40), not 0. Line 127 therefore renders the full `ClientsShell` with all rows, and the count line (lines 107-109) reads e.g. `1–25 of 40 clients`. No "no matches" or "still searching" messaging appears.

Net: with `?q=a` showing in the URL, the user sees the entire unfiltered client list and a full count, with zero indication the search term was dropped. Every link in the cited evidence chain is correct.

This is a consistent pattern (same 2-char floor in jobs.ts:112 and candidates.ts:85/194), so it's a deliberate threshold with no matching UI affordance, not an accidental typo.

Severity assessment for a live demo: real but minor. It's transient (the search input is 300ms-debounced and self-corrects the instant a second character is typed), causes no error, no data/tenant issue, and no incorrect persisted state. The only impact is a brief, mildly confusing flash of the full list with a non-filtered count during the one-character window (or if a user pauses at one char, or searches for a genuinely single-character company name). Low is the correct grade — keeping it as claimed.

title: Sending an AI check-in never clears the client's Dormant status (last_contacted_at not bumped)
description: The whole point of the dormant-outreach AI loop is: a client has gone quiet, the recruiter sends an AI-drafted check-in, the client drops off the dormant list. But sending does not update companies.last_contacted_at. The flow: (1) the Inngest draft function INSERTs an activities row with kind='email_draft' (draft-outreach-email.ts:145) — and the bump_last_contacted_at trigger explicitly excludes 'email_draft' (it only fires on 'call','email','meeting','note', see 20260517215957_bump_last_contacted_at.sql:25). (2) On send, sendOutreachAction UPDATEs that same row to kind='email' (outreach-actions.ts:299-310). The bump trigger is AFTER INSERT only (migration line 43-46) — there is no AFTER UPDATE trigger, and sendOutreachAction does no explicit last_contacted_at write. So after a successful send, last_contacted_at is unchanged.
userImpact: On the live demo, the recruiter opens a dormant client, sends the AI check-in successfully (email actually goes out via Outlook), and the client STILL shows the amber 'Dormant' badge and STILL appears in the dashboard 'Dormant clients' widget and the '/clients' dormant count. It looks like the action did nothing. The core re-engagement feature appears broken to the buyer.
evidence: outreach-actions.ts flips kind via UPDATE: `.update({ kind: 'email', body: subject, metadata: {...} })` with no last_contacted_at set. bump_last_contacted_at trigger: `create trigger activities_bump_last_contacted after insert on public.activities` — UPDATE never fires it; and the function guards `if new.kind in ('call','email','meeting','note')` so even the original email_draft INSERT was skipped.
suggestedFix: In sendOutreachAction, after a successful send, explicitly bump the company: `await service.from('companies').update({ last_contacted_at: new Date().toISOString() }).eq('id', clientId).eq('organization_id', profile.organization_id)`. (Or add an AFTER UPDATE trigger on activities that fires the same bump when kind transitions into 'email'.) Then the revalidatePath('/') + revalidatePath('/clients/[id]') already present will refresh the now-cleared dormant state.
verifierReasoning:
Every load-bearing fact in the claim checks out against the code.

1. sendOutreachAction (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts) does NOT write companies.last_contacted_at on a successful send. The post-send block (lines 297-314) UPDATEs the existing email_draft activity row, setting only { kind: 'email', body, metadata: {subject, body_html, sent_at, sent_to} } scoped by .eq('id', existing.id).eq('organization_id', ...). No companies update exists anywhere in the function. revalidatePath('/') and revalidatePath(`/clients/${clientId}`) run (lines 343-344) but re-render against unchanged data.

2. The bump is INSERT-only. Trigger activities_bump_last_contacted is `after insert on public.activities` (20260517215957_bump_last_contacted_at.sql:43-46). The later hardening migration (20260518211702) only does `create or replace function` (adds search_path) and does not add an AFTER UPDATE trigger. So flipping kind from email_draft to email via UPDATE never fires the bump. Confirmed no other AFTER UPDATE trigger on activities exists.

3. The original email_draft INSERT from the Inngest function (draft-outreach-email.ts:150 inserts kind='email_draft') is also skipped: the trigger function guards `if new.kind in ('call','email','meeting','note')` (line 26), which excludes email_draft.

4. Dormant state is driven entirely by companies.last_contacted_at on all surfaces:
   - Detail page amber badge: getClient -> isDormant(r.last_contacted_at) = now - last_contacted_at > 60d (clients.ts:43-48,152; rendered page.tsx:60).
   - Dashboard widget + /clients count badge: dormant_clients RPC filters c.last_contacted_at < now() - p_dormant_days (20260520031200_phase3_dormant_clients_rpc.sql:75-76).

Net: a successful send leaves last_contacted_at untouched, so the client keeps the amber Dormant badge and stays in the dormant list — exactly the 'looks like nothing happened' symptom on a live demo. Notably the fallback branch (lines 320-328, fired only when no prior draft row exists) INSERTs a fresh kind='email' activity, which WOULD trigger the bump — confirming the normal draft-then-send path is the inconsistent/broken one. The suggested fix (explicit org-scoped companies.last_contacted_at update after a successful send, or an AFTER UPDATE trigger) is correct and matches existing service-client patterns in the file.

One honest grading nuance: the dashboard widget and /clients count RPC also require `exists(placement for this company)`, so those two surfaces only apply to clients with a prior placement. The detail-page badge has no such gate and stays amber for any client contacted >60d ago. So the claim's three-surface impact is fully correct only for previously-placed clients; the detail-page symptom is universal.
