This is a minor over-statement, not a refutation — the core defect holds.

title: AI-draft polling compares browser clock to DB clock — clock skew causes spurious 'taking longer than expected' timeouts
description: The modal seeds seedTimeRef.current = Date.now() (the BROWSER clock) at mount (line 145) and accepts a polled draft only if `new Date(got.data.created_at).getTime() + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current` (line 166). created_at is activities.occurred_at, defaulted server-side to Postgres now() (DB/server clock). A 5s slack is the only tolerance for the difference between two unsynchronised clocks. If the user's browser clock runs more than ~5s ahead of the Supabase server clock (laptop clock drift is common), a perfectly fresh draft will have occurred_at < seedTime - 5000 and will be rejected on every poll, so the modal always falls through to the 'timeout' state even though Sonnet succeeded and the draft row exists.
userImpact: During the demo, on a machine whose clock is a few seconds fast, the recruiter clicks 'Send check-in', waits 10 seconds, and is told 'The AI draft is taking longer than expected' with a Retry button — even though the AI draft was generated fine. Retrying just burns another paid Sonnet call and hits the same wall. The feature looks flaky/broken intermittently and per-machine.
evidence: go(): `seedTimeRef.current = Date.now()` then match guard `if (draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current)`. draftTime derives from got.data.created_at which is activities.occurred_at (`occurred_at timestamptz not null default now()` in phase1 schema).
suggestedFix: Don't compare a server timestamp against a client Date.now(). Either: (a) have requestOutreachDraftAction return a server-side request timestamp / a draft request id and match the returned draft against that, or (b) widen the freshness comparison to use the draft's age relative to a server-derived 'now' (e.g. include now() from the DB in getLatestOutreachDraftAction and compare server-to-server), or (c) tag the email_draft row with the request's correlation id and poll for that exact id rather than a time window.
verifierReasoning:
Every factual claim checks out in code. send-checkin-modal.tsx:145 sets seedTimeRef.current = Date.now() (browser clock) at mount, and lines 165-166 (and the duplicate retry path at 218-219) accept the polled draft only if `new Date(got.data.created_at).getTime() + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current`. got.data.created_at is sourced from activities.occurred_at (outreach-actions.ts:182: `created_at: data.occurred_at`), and the Inngest writer (draft-outreach-email.ts:141-167) inserts the activities row WITHOUT setting occurred_at, so it falls back to the schema default `occurred_at timestamptz not null default now()` (phase1_domain_schema.sql:339) — a DB/server clock. So the modal genuinely compares a browser clock (seedTime) against a DB clock (draftTime) with only a fixed 5s tolerance. The reject condition for a fresh draft is draftTime < seedTime - 5000. That CAN happen and would falsely fall through to the 'timeout' state, exactly as described — the mechanism, direction, and schema basis are all correct, so the claim cannot be refuted.

However, the claim materially OVERSTATES the threshold and likelihood. (1) The real reject condition is browserSkew > elapsed + 5s, where elapsed is the wall-clock time between mount and the insert: Inngest event dispatch + queue pickup + gather-context query + a full Sonnet 4.6 generation (outreach-draft.ts, max_tokens 800 — typically several seconds) + the insert. Since occurred_at is stamped only AFTER that whole pipeline, draftTime is in real time several seconds LATER than seedTime, which the Sonnet latency actively works in the gate's favour. So a browser clock must be roughly 7-13+ seconds fast to trip it, not the '~5s' the claim states. (2) Cloud Supabase is NTP-synced (dbSkew ~0) and modern client OSes NTP-sync to within ~1-2s, so a 10s+ fast browser clock is uncommon (requires NTP disabled / drifting VM / manually-wrong clock). (3) The seed comparison is intentional and serves a real purpose — the poll query orders by occurred_at desc limit 1 and would otherwise pick up a STALE prior email_draft row for the same client; the freshness gate is the (imperfect) guard against that. (4) Impact is non-destructive and recoverable: it shows 'taking longer than expected' + Retry, the draft row exists, and Retry re-seeds Date.now() and re-dispatches. The suggested fix (correlate by request id / draft row id, or compare server-derived now) is the correct architecture. Net: a genuine latent clock-skew bug and a real anti-pattern, but lower probability on a typical NTP-synced demo machine than the medium grade implies — hence low.

title: Every modal open (and every Retry) fires a fresh paid Sonnet draft — no caching/dedup of recent drafts
description: CLAUDE.md core principle 4 is 'Cache AI outputs aggressively. Regenerate only on material change.' The check-in modal does the opposite: SendCheckinModalBody mounts whenever open=true and its useEffect unconditionally calls requestOutreachDraftAction (line 148), which fires the Inngest event and a full Sonnet 4.6 draft. There is no check for an existing recent email_draft for this client. So: closing and reopening the modal, or clicking 'Send check-in' from both the dashboard widget AND the client detail page, or hitting Retry on a clock-skew timeout (handleRetryDraft, line 204), each generates another billable Sonnet call and another email_draft activity row. The same client accumulates many orphan email_draft rows; getLatestOutreachDraftAction only surfaces the newest, so the rest are pure waste.
userImpact: Cost leak that scales with recruiter fidgeting (reopen the modal three times = 3x the Sonnet spend for one email) and a cluttered activity timeline full of never-sent email_draft entries on the client. On the per-tenant cost ledger the founder is using to price the SaaS, dormant-outreach cost looks inflated and noisy.
evidence: useEffect on mount: `const requested = await requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId })` with no prior-draft lookup; handleRetryDraft repeats the same. Each call lands `await inngest.send({ name: 'outreach-draft/requested', ... })` → draftOutreachEmail → runWithLogging (paid Sonnet) → new activities insert.
suggestedFix: Before firing a new draft, call getLatestOutreachDraftAction first; if a recent (e.g. < 1h old) unsent email_draft exists, load it into the editor instead of regenerating. Only fire requestOutreachDraftAction when no fresh cached draft exists or the recruiter explicitly clicks a 'Regenerate' button. Consider de-duping the email_draft rows (update-in-place per client rather than insert-each-time).
verifierReasoning:
The claim is structurally accurate and I verified every load-bearing fact in the code.

1. src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx:79 — SendCheckinModalBody is only rendered/mounted when open===true. Closing and reopening the modal remounts it.
2. Lines 143-148 — on every mount the useEffect unconditionally calls requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId }) with NO prior-draft lookup. The polling logic at line 166 (draftTime + 5_000 >= seedTimeRef.current) explicitly requires a draft NEWER than this mount's seed time, so even an existing fresh draft is deliberately ignored — the inverse of caching.
3. handleRetryDraft (line 204) fires requestOutreachDraftAction again.
4. Two entry points both open the same modal for the same clientId: dashboard _dashboard/dormant-client-row.tsx:52,57 and client-detail clients/[id]/client-checkin-button.tsx:30,35. Opening from both = two billable drafts.
5. outreach-actions.ts:85 inngest.send({ name:'outreach-draft/requested', data:{...} }) passes NO idempotency id; draft-outreach-email.ts has no dedup. Each event → step 'claude-draft' (paid Sonnet 4.6 via runWithLogging) → step 'write-activity' which does a fresh INSERT (line 145, not update-in-place) of a new kind='email_draft' activity row.
6. getLatestOutreachDraftAction (outreach-actions.ts:146-154) orders by occurred_at desc limit 1, so only the newest draft is ever surfaced — older drafts are orphan waste on the company timeline.

This violates CLAUDE.md core principle 4 ('Cache AI outputs aggressively. Regenerate only on material change.'). The behavior is real and reproducible.

Severity downgraded from medium to low: the action is gated behind an explicit recruiter click (not automatic/loop), the documented cost is ~0.55p per draft (outreach-draft.ts:21-22) so even 3x fidgeting is sub-2p, and there is zero correctness, security, tenant-isolation, or demo-breaking impact. The most visible symptom is timeline clutter (orphan email_draft rows), which is cosmetic. The per-tenant ai_usage ledger noise the founder cares about is a legitimate but minor concern. The suggested fix (lookup a recent unsent draft before regenerating; update-in-place per client; gate regeneration behind an explicit Regenerate button) is sound and worth doing, but this is a low-priority efficiency/hygiene issue, not a medium-severity defect.

title: When Anthropic is down the modal shows a generic 'taking longer than expected', not the required 'AI temporarily unavailable' degradation
description: CLAUDE.md error-handling rule: 'AI errors degrade gracefully — if Claude is down, the feature shows "AI temporarily unavailable" but the rest of the app works.' When Anthropic is genuinely unavailable, draftOutreachEmail throws inside the Inngest function, which retries twice and fails; no email_draft row is ever written. The modal cannot distinguish 'AI is down' from 'AI is slow' — it just exhausts its 10 polls and renders 'The AI draft is taking longer than expected.' with a Retry button (line 248-253). Retrying re-fires the whole pipeline (and another billable attempt sequence) into the same outage.
userImpact: During an AI outage the recruiter sees a vague 'slow' message and keeps retrying into a dead pipeline, with no signal that this is an AI availability problem they should wait out. Contradicts the product's stated graceful-degradation contract.
evidence: Modal only has a 'timeout' status (`The AI draft is taking longer than expected`) reached purely by exhausting MAX_POLL_ATTEMPTS; there is no signal channel from the failed Inngest run back to the modal, so a hard AI failure is indistinguishable from latency.
suggestedFix: Surface the Inngest run failure to the poller (e.g. write a lightweight 'draft_failed' marker row / status the action can read) so the modal can show 'AI temporarily unavailable — please try again shortly' distinctly from the slow path, and avoid implying a retry will help mid-outage.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code; the claim holds on every load-bearing point.

1. send-checkin-modal.tsx (lines 45-54, 155-174, 247-253): the modal polls getLatestOutreachDraftAction every 1s for MAX_POLL_ATTEMPTS=10. Its only non-success terminal state when no draft row appears is `{ kind: 'timeout' }`, which renders exactly "The AI draft is taking longer than expected." plus a Retry button. There is no `unavailable`/`failed` status variant.

2. draft-outreach-email.ts: the `claude-draft` step (line 128) calls draftOutreachEmail. The function has retries: 2. Its onFailure handler (lines 51-66) ONLY does Sentry.captureException — it writes no email_draft activity row and no failure marker. The write-activity step (the row the modal polls for) never runs when claude-draft throws.

3. outreach-draft.ts -> claude.ts (lines 98-128): on a real Anthropic outage (429/529/5xx) runWithLogging retries then `throw lastError`. This propagates through draftOutreachEmail -> the Inngest step -> two Inngest retries -> failure. No row is written.

4. getLatestOutreachDraftAction (outreach-actions.ts lines 126-185): only queries for a kind='email_draft' activity row; on AI failure none exists, so it returns { ok: true, data: null } for all 10 polls -> modal lands in `timeout`. Therefore AI-down and AI-slow are genuinely indistinguishable; there is no signal channel from the failed Inngest run back to the poller, exactly as claimed.

5. grep confirms the required string "AI temporarily unavailable" does not exist anywhere in src/ (only an unrelated turnstile.ts comment). This directly violates CLAUDE.md's error-handling contract.

6. handleRetryDraft (lines 201-228) re-fires requestOutreachDraftAction, re-triggering the whole pipeline (and another billable retry sequence) into the same outage — also as claimed.

The claim's mechanism, evidence, and impact are all accurate.

Severity: 'low' is correct. The failure is contained to one optional dormant-client outreach widget; the rest of the app keeps working, so the graceful-degradation goal (app stays up) is partially met. The defect is a vague message + wasted billable retries during an outage and a documented-contract violation — a feedback/polish gap, not data loss, security, or a demo-blocker.

title: Adding a candidate to a job shows a success toast but the candidate never appears until a manual page reload
description: AddCandidateForm is a client island rendered on the RSC job-detail page. On success it shows toast.success('Candidate added to pipeline.') and closes the popover, but it never calls router.refresh(). The server action (addCandidateToJobAction) only calls revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}`) — that marks the route cache stale but does NOT re-render the currently-mounted client page. The ApplicationsList right below it is server-rendered from props passed at page load and will not update. The recruiter is told the candidate was added but the Applications table stays empty/unchanged.
userImpact: During the live demo: recruiter searches a candidate, clicks them, sees 'Candidate added to pipeline', but the Applications list does not show the new row. Looks broken / like the add silently failed. They may add the same candidate again (the DB unique guard then rejects it, producing a confusing 'may already be on this job' error for what looked like a fresh add).
suggestedFix: Import useRouter from next/navigation and call router.refresh() inside handleSelect after toast.success, so the RSC page (and its ApplicationsList) re-fetches. This is the same pattern already used correctly in ApplicationRowActions (router.refresh() on success).
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code. add-candidate-form.tsx is a 'use client' island (line 1) rendered by the RSC JobDetailPage (page.tsx:72) directly above <ApplicationsList rows={applications} jobId={id}/> (page.tsx:74), whose rows come from server props captured at page load (page.tsx:35-36). On success (add-candidate-form.tsx:92-95) it only toasts, closes the popover, and resets search — grep confirms no useRouter import and no router.refresh() anywhere in the file. The server action addCandidateToJobAction (actions.ts:53-54) only calls revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}`) + revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}/pipeline`). revalidatePath marks the route cache stale but does NOT re-render the already-mounted client page; without router.refresh() the RSC tree (and ApplicationsList) is not re-fetched, so the new application row does not appear until a manual reload. The codebase's own sibling component ApplicationRowActions confirms the required pattern: its actions also rely on internal revalidatePath, and it explicitly calls router.refresh() on success (lines 78, 97, 147, 159) with a comment (lines 30-32) stating it does so 'to re-fetch the table.' AddCandidateForm omits exactly this. The confusing re-add follow-on is also real: a second add hits the unique guard and createApplication returns formError 'Could not add candidate. They may already be on this job.' (actions.ts:50). The DB write itself succeeds (success toast is truthful), so this is a stale-UI / missing-client-refresh defect, not data loss — but for a live client demo it reads as a silent failure and produces a contradictory error on retry. The suggested fix (import useRouter, call router.refresh() in handleSelect after toast.success) is correct and matches the established pattern.

title: Shortlist 'Remove' and 'Convert to application' succeed but the row stays on screen
description: ShortlistList is a client component rendering `rows` passed from the RSC shortlist page. Both remove() and promote() show a success toast but neither calls router.refresh(). removeFromShortlistAction and convertShortlistToApplicationAction only call revalidatePath — which does not re-render the already-mounted page. After a successful remove the candidate row is still visible; after a successful promote (which should drop the row out of the shortlist view) the row also remains.
userImpact: Recruiter clicks 'Remove' on a shortlisted candidate, gets 'Removed from shortlist', but the candidate is still in the list — looks like the action failed. Same for 'Convert to application': toast says promoted, but the row stays on the shortlist, so the recruiter cannot tell it worked and may click again.
suggestedFix: Add useRouter and call router.refresh() after both success toasts (or optimistically drop the row from local state). Mirror the working pattern in application-row-actions.tsx.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual code. shortlist-list.tsx (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/shortlist/shortlist-list.tsx) is a client component that renders the `rows` prop passed from the RSC page.tsx. Both remove() (line 48) and promote() (line 33) await the server action, clear pending state, and show a success toast, but neither calls router.refresh(), mutates local `rows`, nor uses optimistic state. useRouter/next/navigation is not imported at all (grep count 0). The two server actions only call revalidatePath: removeFromShortlistAction (actions.ts lines 178-181) and convertShortlistToApplicationAction (shortlist-actions.ts lines 124-128). page.tsx is a pure RSC that fetches rows once via listShortlistForJob and passes them as a static prop, so the already-mounted client tree keeps rendering the original rows after a direct-call server action. The codebase's own established convention refutes any 'revalidatePath is sufficient' defense: the cited reference application-row-actions.tsx sits in the SAME jobs/[id] directory, its actions also use revalidatePath, and its author wrote an explicit comment (lines 26-32) 'Removal + decline both rely on revalidatePath inside the actions, so after success we just router.refresh() to re-fetch the table' then calls router.refresh() on lines 78 and 97. The same router.refresh()-after-mutation pattern recurs in 11+ components (candidate-applications, float-row-actions, saved-ad-row-actions, spec-delete-button, log-activity-form, etc.). The shortlist component is the lone mutation surface that omits it. Net effect: after Remove the row stays visible despite the success toast (looks failed); after Convert the row should drop out of the shortlist filter (application_type flips shortlist->standard) but stays, so the recruiter cannot tell it worked. The underlying writes are correct (verified the actions), and a second Convert click is caught server-side by the defensive application_type !== 'shortlist' guard (returns an error toast, no corruption), so this is a stale-view UX bug, not data corruption.

title: Saving a generated job ad shows 'Saved to job ads' but the Saved ads section does not update
description: AdPanel lives inside a Sheet overlay on the job-detail RSC page. onSave/onSaveScore call saveJobAdAction, which only calls revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}`). There is no router.refresh() and the Sheet does not navigate, so the SavedAdsList rendered on the underlying page (from listJobAdsForJob at page load) does not re-render. The user gets 'Saved to job ads.' but sees no new entry under 'Saved ads' until a manual reload.
userImpact: Recruiter generates an ad, clicks 'Save to job ads', is told it saved — but closing the panel shows the Saved ads list unchanged. They cannot tell whether it saved and may save duplicates (D3-33 explicitly allows multiple rows, so each retry actually creates another row).
suggestedFix: Call router.refresh() after toast.success in onSave and onSaveScore so the parent page re-fetches listJobAdsForJob and the new ad appears. Because each save is a new row with no dedup, the missing refresh also masks accidental duplicate saves.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed real. In src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/ad-panel/ad-panel.tsx, onSave (line 164) and onSaveScore (line 272) call saveJobAdAction inside a useTransition, then only toast.success(...). The file imports no useRouter and contains no router.refresh() (grep-confirmed). saveJobAdAction (ad-panel/actions.ts:239) only calls revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}`). AdPanel is mounted inside a client <Sheet> (ad-panel-trigger.tsx) overlaying the job-detail RSC page; the Sheet does not navigate, and SavedAdsList is server-rendered once at page load from listJobAdsForJob (page.tsx:39/79). The save genuinely persists (createJobAd writes the row; listJobAdsForJob orders newest-first), so the recruiter sees 'Saved to job ads.' but the Saved ads section on the underlying page does not visibly gain the new row until a manual reload. The decisive corroboration is the codebase's own convention: the sibling file in the SAME directory, saved-ad-row-actions.tsx, calls deleteJobAdAction (which likewise only revalidatePaths) and then explicitly calls router.refresh() on line 70 to re-render the very same SavedAdsList. application-row-actions.tsx carries an explicit comment (lines 31-32): 'Removal + decline both rely on revalidatePath inside the actions, so after success we just router.refresh() to re-fetch the table.' Every client-triggered mutation in jobs/** pairs revalidatePath with router.refresh() — the two ad-save handlers are the sole exception. That asymmetry (Delete refreshes the list, Save does not) confirms revalidatePath alone is not refreshing this list in the live UI, and the missing refresh both hides the successful save and masks the duplicate rows D3-33 allows on retry. Downgraded from high to medium: data is never lost, a reload corrects the view, and the fix is the one-line router.refresh() the rest of the codebase already uses — but it is a genuine, demo-visible staleness bug and a duplicate-row enabler.

title: Placement modal collects 'Notes' but they are silently discarded — never sent or stored
description: PlacementModal renders a Notes textarea and tracks it in state (setNotes), but handleConfirm never includes notes in the moveApplicationAction payload. moveApplicationAction has no placementNotes field, the move_application RPC has no placement-notes parameter, and there is no placement_notes column on the applications table (migration 20260523160000 added only placement_type + placement_currency; decline_notes exists but is only written for rejected/withdrawn). So anything the recruiter types into Placement Notes is thrown away with no error or warning.
userImpact: When recording a placement (the revenue event), a recruiter typing fee context / IR35 / start-date notes into the Notes field will lose all of it. They believe it was captured because the modal accepts it and shows 'Placement recorded.' This is data loss on the single most important record in the CRM.
suggestedFix: Either remove the Notes field from PlacementModal (so it doesn't promise capture it can't deliver), or add a placement-notes path: add a migration column applications.placement_notes, extend move_application RPC + moveApplicationAction with placementNotes, and pass notes from the modal. Until then, drop the field to avoid silent data loss.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is mechanically accurate in every particular. In src/components/app/placement-modal.tsx, `notes` state (line 78) is bound to the textarea (lines 200-201) and cleared in reset() (line 85), but handleConfirm (lines 102-134) never references it. The moveApplicationAction payload (lines 112-121) passes only applicationId, toStage, placementFeePence, placementDate, placementType, placementCurrency, jobId, candidateId — no notes.

Tracing the full chain confirms there is nowhere for the notes to go:
- src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts moveSchema (lines 153-187) has no placementNotes field; only declineNotes (line 170), which the action passes through to declineNotes (line 233), not a placement field.
- src/lib/db/applications.ts MoveApplicationArgs (lines 348-359) has no placementNotes; moveApplication's RPC call (lines 376-386) sends p_placement_* params but no p_placement_notes.
- The RPC migration 20260523160100_move_application_with_placement_fields.sql has a 9-param signature with no notes param. Notably, decline_notes IS written but ONLY `when p_to_stage in ('rejected','withdrawn')` (lines 103-106), so even routing notes through declineNotes would be discarded on a 'placed' move.
- The schema migration 20260523160000 adds only placement_type and placement_currency columns; fee_pence/placed_at pre-exist. There is no placement_notes column.
- grep across all of src and supabase for placement_notes / placementNotes / p_placement_notes returns ZERO hits.

On the success path the modal shows toast.success('Placement recorded.') (line 129) regardless, so the recruiter gets a positive confirmation while their typed notes are silently dropped. This is a genuine silent-fail / data-loss bug.

I downgraded from high to medium: the structured, revenue-critical fields (fee, date, type, currency) ARE persisted correctly and appear in the source-attribution report. Only the optional free-text Notes field (labeled 'Notes (optional)') is lost. The original claim overstates impact by implying fee/IR35/start-date capture is lost wholesale — it isn't; only ad-hoc free text in the optional box vanishes. Still a real, user-visible silent loss of entered data with a misleading success toast, so not a non-issue.

title: createApplication detects the duplicate-application case but returns the same generic code, so the duplicate message is guesswork
description: createApplication branches on Postgres code 23505 (unique violation = candidate already on this job) but both the 23505 branch and the generic branch return the identical { ok: false, code: 'internal' }. The distinction is computed and thrown away. The caller addCandidateToJobAction therefore cannot tell a duplicate from a real DB failure or a cross-tenant FK guard fire, and shows the hedged message 'Could not add candidate. They may already be on this job.' for every failure — including genuine errors.
userImpact: On any add-candidate failure (transient DB error, FK guard), the recruiter is told the candidate 'may already be on this job', which is misleading and sends them looking for a duplicate that doesn't exist.
suggestedFix: Add a 'conflict' / 'duplicate' variant to DbResult's code union and return it from the 23505 branch; have addCandidateToJobAction map only that to 'This candidate is already on this job.' and a different generic message otherwise (this is exactly what addToShortlistAction already does correctly).
verifierReasoning: Confirmed verbatim in code. In src/lib/db/applications.ts createApplication, the error block (lines 286-296) branches on pgErr.code === '23505' at line 292 but BOTH the 23505 branch (line 293) and the generic fall-through (line 295) return the identical { ok: false, code: 'internal' }. The duplicate distinction is computed and thrown away. src/lib/db/types.ts confirms DbResult<T>'s failure union is only 'not_found' | 'internal' — there is no conflict/duplicate variant, so the caller structurally cannot tell the cases apart. The caller addCandidateToJobAction (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts:45-51) only sees result.ok === false and unconditionally returns formError: 'Could not add candidate. They may already be on this job.' — its own comment (lines 46-49) admits the failure could be a unique violation OR a cross-tenant FK guard fire. That string is surfaced directly to the user via toast.error(res.formError) at add-candidate-form.tsx:89. The suggested-fix premise is also verified: addToShortlistAction (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/shortlist/actions.ts:72-86) does the insert inline (not via the helper that erases the code), checks 23505 itself, and returns a precise 'already on the shortlist' message vs a separate generic 'Could not add to shortlist.' — so the correct pattern already exists in this codebase. Severity stays low: the action fails loudly (toast, no silent success, no data issue); the message is hedged ('may already be') rather than a false assertion; and the unique-violation case (re-adding a candidate) is in fact the most common trigger, so the wording is usually correct. The misleading case (transient/FK error) is rarer. It is a UX wording polish item, not a functional defect for a client demo.

title: Precomputed match scores never persist: service-role insert into ai_summaries fails at the set_org trigger (NULL org)
description: precompute-matches-for-job (Inngest) runs under createServiceClient() and calls upsertMatchSummary(), whose insert deliberately OMITS organization_id (header comment line 14-16: 'organization_id is filled by the ai_summaries_set_org BEFORE INSERT trigger — callers MUST NOT pass it'). That trigger runs set_organization_id() (20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:86), which does `new.organization_id := public.current_organization_id()`. current_organization_id() (20260513151021:61) is `select organization_id from public.users where id = auth.uid()`. Under the service-role client there is NO auth session, so auth.uid() is NULL, current_organization_id() returns NULL, and the trigger hits line 95: `raise exception 'organization_id is required and could not be resolved from auth context'`. Every precompute match-score write therefore errors. The Inngest function swallows it: upsertMatchSummary returns {ok:false}, and precompute's per-candidate step.run treats !upsertResult.ok as a no-op `return` (precompute-matches-for-job.ts:288-294, commented 'Unique-violation = concurrent worker already inserted; no-op'), so the function reports success. Note the asymmetry: bumpJobEmbedding/bumpCandidateEmbedding are UPDATEs (the BEFORE INSERT trigger never fires) so embeddings DO persist under service-role — only the ai_summaries INSERT path is broken.
userImpact: The headline AI feature — 'top matches with strengths/gaps/screening questions appear automatically ~30s after a job is created' — is dead in the demo. The matches page literally promises 'Matches will appear within ~30 seconds — refresh shortly' (matches/page.tsx:140-142) and shows every candidate as 'Not scored yet'. The client must click 'Explain match' on each card one-by-one (the authenticated on-demand path works because it runs with the recruiter's session). For a client call selling AI-first matching, the automatic scoring never demonstrates.
evidence:
.insert({
  kind: 'match_score',
  candidate_id: input.candidateId,
  job_id: input.jobId,
  candidate_embedding_version: input.candidateEmbeddingVersion,
  job_embedding_version: input.jobEmbeddingVersion,
  content: input.content,
  model: input.model,
  cost_pence: input.costPence,
})  // no organization_id; set_org trigger calls current_organization_id() which is NULL under service-role and RAISES
suggestedFix: Give upsertMatchSummary an explicit organizationId param and include it in the insert payload (the precompute function already has organization_id in scope and verifies it against the job). The ai_summaries cross-tenant FK guard will still validate candidate_id/job_id belong to that org, so caller-supplied org is safe here. Alternatively, make set_organization_id() a no-op when organization_id is already provided (it already is) — the real fix is to stop relying on the auth-context trigger for the service-role write path. Add an integration test that asserts a precompute run produces a persisted ai_summaries row.
verifierReasoning: Tried to refute but every link in the chain is confirmed in code. (1) upsertMatchSummary (src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:130-143) inserts into ai_summaries directly via .from().insert() with NO organization_id. (2) The ai_summaries_set_org BEFORE INSERT trigger (20260519092944_ai_summaries.sql:88-90) calls set_organization_id(); its active definition (20260517204504_harden_set_organization_id.sql:9-23, body identical to phase1 :86-99) sets new.organization_id := current_organization_id() when NULL then RAISES 'organization_id is required...' if still NULL. (3) current_organization_id() (20260513151021:61-69) is `select organization_id from public.users where id = auth.uid()`. (4) createServiceClient() (src/lib/supabase/service.ts:14-18) uses SERVICE_ROLE_KEY with persistSession:false and injects no JWT/set_config — confirmed no set_config/request.jwt.claims anywhere in the service path — so auth.uid() is NULL, current_organization_id() returns NULL, and the trigger raises. BEFORE INSERT triggers fire even though service-role bypasses RLS, so the insert genuinely errors. (5) upsertMatchSummary returns {ok:false}; precompute (precompute-matches-for-job.ts:288-294) treats !upsertResult.ok as a no-op `return` and the function returns a success object — silent fail confirmed. (6) Asymmetry confirmed: bumpJobEmbedding (jobs.ts:363-368) is .update() so the BEFORE INSERT trigger never fires and embeddings persist. (7) On-demand explainCandidateMatchAction (matches/actions.ts:74) uses authenticated createSupabaseClient() with the recruiter session, so auth.uid() resolves and the same insert succeeds — precisely why on-demand 'Explain match' works but auto-scoring never appears. (8) Auto-trigger wired: embed-job-on-jd-change.ts:116 fires job/score-top-candidates → precomputeMatchesForJob (registered route.ts:32). (9) UI text confirmed: matches/page.tsx:140-141 and match-card.tsx:85. No later migration redefines the trigger/function to tolerate service-role. The only nuance vs the claim's line numbers: the binding set_organization_id definition is the hardened one at 20260517204504 (functionally identical raise), not the phase1 :86 reference cited — does not change the outcome. User-facing impact for a live AI-first matching demo is severe: automatic top-matches never persist; every candidate shows 'Not scored yet' until manually explained one-by-one. High severity stands.

title: Match-spend cost ceiling is bypassed when the spend lookup fails (fail-open)
description: explainCandidateMatchAction gates the Sonnet call behind getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth, but only blocks when `spendResult.ok && spendResult.data >= ceiling`. If the aggregate read returns {ok:false} (transient DB error, or the cost helper's PostgREST cast breaks), the guard is skipped entirely and the Sonnet call proceeds unmetered. The precompute path makes the same fail-open choice deliberately (precompute-matches-for-job.ts:124-140, 'better to over-spend by one batch'), but the on-demand Explain button is user-triggered and repeatable — a recruiter hammering Explain while ai_usage reads are flaking could exceed the £100/month per-tenant ceiling that CLAUDE.md treats as a SaaS-pricing control.
userImpact: Edge case: if the cost-aggregation query errors, per-tenant AI spend tracking stops enforcing its cap on the recruiter-facing Explain button. Not a tenant leak, but undermines the 'track cost per tenant / cost visibility for pricing' guarantee under failure.
evidence:
const spendResult = await getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth(supabase, organizationId)
if (spendResult.ok && spendResult.data >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE) {  // skipped entirely when spendResult.ok === false
suggestedFix: On the user-triggered path, treat a failed spend lookup conservatively for the interactive button (e.g. allow but log a Sentry warning, or surface 'try again') rather than silently spending. At minimum, capture a Sentry warning when spendResult.ok === false so the fail-open is observable.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is factually accurate. At src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/actions.ts:131-132 the cost ceiling guard reads `if (spendResult.ok && spendResult.data >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE)`. Because the condition requires `spendResult.ok === true`, a failed spend lookup (`{ok:false, code:'internal'}`) short-circuits the condition to false, the guard is skipped, and execution falls through to the synchronous Sonnet call at line 157. Confirmed against the helper: getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth (src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:225-249) returns `{ok:false, code:'internal'}` whenever the PostgREST `.eq().eq().gte()` read errors, captured to Sentry at the DB layer (line 242). So the on-demand, recruiter-triggered, repeatable Explain button genuinely fails open under a transient ai_usage read failure. The precompute Inngest path (precompute-matches-for-job.ts:124-140) makes the same fail-open choice deliberately AND logs an action-level Sentry capture for the failed lookup (lines 128-138); the on-demand action does NOT — it relies only on the DB helper's Sentry capture and has no action-scoped warning when it skips the gate, so the reviewer's "at minimum capture a Sentry warning when spendResult.ok===false" suggestion targets a real observability gap.

Severity is correctly low and arguably borderline non-issue: (1) it only triggers when the cost-aggregation query is actively erroring — a transient/edge condition; (2) ai_usage is still written authoritatively by runWithLogging on every Sonnet call, so per-tenant cost remains *tracked/visible* (the CLAUDE.md "cost visibility for pricing" guarantee is intact) — only the cap *enforcement* is bypassed; (3) at ~1 pence/call against a £100 (10,000 pence) ceiling, the financial exposure during a transient flake is negligible — meaningful overspend would require the aggregation to flake persistently while a recruiter clicks thousands of times; (4) not a tenant leak, not a crash, not demo-breaking. Real bug, real but minor impact, correctly graded low.

title: createApplication maps the unique-violation (duplicate) to a generic 'internal' code, so the duplicate message in the action never fires
description: createApplication explicitly checks for pgErr.code === '23505' (duplicate application) but returns the SAME { ok:false, code:'internal' } for both the duplicate case and any other error — the if/else are identical (lines 292-295). addCandidateToJobAction then surfaces a hardcoded combined string 'Could not add candidate. They may already be on this job.' regardless. Compare addToShortlistAction (shortlist/actions.ts:79-85) which DOES distinguish 23505 and returns a precise 'already on the shortlist' message. Functionally the add-candidate toast is acceptable, but the dead branch means a genuine internal failure and a duplicate are indistinguishable to the caller and to logs.
userImpact: Minor: a real transient DB failure when adding a candidate to a job is reported to the recruiter as 'they may already be on this job', which is misleading and could cause the recruiter to give up rather than retry.
suggestedFix: Return a distinct code for the duplicate (e.g. 'conflict') and let addCandidateToJobAction map it to the 'already on this job' message while mapping 'internal' to a retryable 'Something went wrong, please try again.'
verifierReasoning: Every concrete factual assertion in the claim is verified against the code. In src/lib/db/applications.ts:286-296, createApplication captures the error to Sentry, then checks `if (pgErr.code === '23505') return { ok: false, code: 'internal' }` followed immediately by `return { ok: false, code: 'internal' }` — both branches return the byte-identical value, so the 23505 (unique-violation/duplicate) check is genuinely dead code with no observable effect on the return value. The caller addCandidateToJobAction (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts:45-50) then returns a single hardcoded string 'Could not add candidate. They may already be on this job.' for ANY !result.ok, so a genuine transient/internal DB failure or a cross-tenant FK guard fire is reported to the recruiter as a duplicate. The cited contrast is also accurate: addToShortlistAction (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/shortlist/actions.ts:79-85) DOES distinguish 23505 and surfaces a precise 'already on the shortlist' message, proving the better pattern exists in-repo. Two nuances temper but do not refute the claim: (1) the DbResult<T> type in src/lib/db/types.ts only permits codes 'not_found' | 'internal', so the dead branch cannot currently carry a distinct 'conflict' code without a type change — the if is harmless-but-misleading; (2) the action's own comment shows the combined message is partly a deliberate choice to avoid leaking which org a candidate lives in (cross-tenant FK guard case), not purely an oversight. Net: a real, low-severity code-smell + minor-UX issue. It does not break functionality, corrupt data, or affect tenancy/security, and the duplicate-add toast is functionally acceptable as the claim concedes. Severity confirmed as low (not raised) for a live demo — the only realistic harm is a misleading message on a rare transient DB failure causing a recruiter to not retry.

title: deleteJobAction relies on substring-matching raw RPC error messages to classify failures
description: deleteJobAction classifies the delete_job RPC outcome via error.message.includes('job_has_applications') and error.message.includes('job not found'). The migration raises 'job_has_applications' and 'job not found' as exact strings (20260603130000:44, 38/62), so today this works. But it is brittle: any rewording of the RPC's raise text (a future append-only migration), or Postgres wrapping the message, silently degrades the helpful 'remove candidates first' message into the generic 'Couldn't delete this job' fallback. The block guard itself (RPC re-asserts organization_id = current_organization_id() and blocks on applications) is correctly implemented and tenant-safe — this is only about the client-side message classification.
userImpact: If the RPC message ever changes, a recruiter trying to delete a job that still has pipeline candidates would see a generic 'please try again' instead of the actionable 'remove all candidates from the pipeline first' — they'd retry fruitlessly. Not a security issue.
evidence:
if (error.message.includes('job_has_applications')) { ... }
if (error.message.includes('job not found')) { ... }
suggestedFix: Have delete_job raise with a SQLSTATE/errcode (e.g. `raise exception using errcode = 'P0001', message = 'job_has_applications'`) or return a structured code, and branch on error.code rather than substring-matching free-text message.
verifierReasoning: Verified directly in code. src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts lines 377 and 384 classify the delete_job RPC failure via error.message.includes('job_has_applications') and error.message.includes('job not found'). The migration 20260603130000_delete_job_and_company_rpcs.sql raises those exact strings: line 44 `raise exception 'job_has_applications'` (with a hint), and lines 38 and 62 `raise exception 'job not found'`. So the substring-coupling the claim describes is exactly what the code does, and it works correctly today (PostgREST surfaces RAISE EXCEPTION text verbatim in the JSON message field for P0001 errors). The same free-text-matching pattern is used consistently across all three sibling delete RPCs — delete_candidate (20260603120100, raising 'candidate_has_applications' / 'candidate not found') and delete_company (raising 'company_has_jobs', matched at clients/[id]/actions.ts:253/260). None of these RPCs assign a custom SQLSTATE, so every custom raise defaults to P0001. That has two consequences: (1) the brittleness is genuine — a future `create or replace` migration that reworded the text would silently fall through to the generic 'Couldn’t delete this job' fallback while still hiding the actionable 'remove candidates first' guidance; (2) the suggested fix is partly off — branching on error.code alone would NOT disambiguate, because all three raises share code P0001; the fix only works in its first form (raise ... using errcode = '<distinct code>' per case, then branch on the distinct codes). Importantly, there is no current user-facing defect: the message fires correctly, and the migration is in the same repo as the action, so a reword would naturally be co-edited. The block guard itself (re-asserts organization_id = current_organization_id(), blocks on applications, cleans polymorphic orphans, audit row) is correctly implemented and tenant-safe, as the claim concedes. Sentry logging on the fallback path (line 387) is also present.

title: PlacementModal "Notes" field is collected but never saved (dead input + silent data loss)
description: The Mark-as-placed modal renders a 'Notes (optional)' Textarea bound to a `notes` useState. On confirm, handleConfirm() calls moveApplicationAction with placementFeePence, placementDate, placementType, placementCurrency and jobId/candidateId — but NOT notes. The server action's move schema has no placement-notes field, and the underlying move_application RPC (migration 20260523160100) has no p_placement_notes parameter (only p_decline_notes exists). So whatever the recruiter types into the placement notes box is silently discarded — never sent, never stored, and never shown again.
userImpact: During the demo, a recruiter records a placement and types context into the Notes box (e.g. 'Started 1 July, 20% fee, contract via X'). They click Confirm, see 'Placement recorded.', and assume the note is on record. It is gone forever. The field looks fully functional, so the loss is invisible until they go looking for the note later.
evidence: PlacementModal: `const [notes, setNotes] = useState('')` … `<Textarea id="placement-notes" value={notes} onChange={...} />` but handleConfirm sends `await moveApplicationAction({ applicationId, toStage: 'placed', placementFeePence: feePence, placementDate: placementDateIso, placementType, placementCurrency: 'GBP', jobId, candidateId })` — `notes` is absent. The move_application RPC signature is (uuid, application_stage, decline_reason, text/*decline_notes*/, uuid, bigint, timestamptz, placement_type, text) — no placement-notes param.
suggestedFix: Either (a) remove the Notes field from PlacementModal so it doesn't promise capture it can't deliver, or (b) add a p_placement_notes text param to move_application (new migration), thread placementNotes through MoveApplicationArgs + the move schema in jobs/[id]/actions.ts, and persist it (e.g. on applications or in the activity metadata).
verifierReasoning:
The claim is verified end-to-end across all four layers; I could not refute it.

1. Modal (src/components/app/placement-modal.tsx): line 78 declares `const [notes, setNotes] = useState('')`; lines 198-204 render `<Textarea id="placement-notes" value={notes} onChange={(e) => setNotes(e.target.value)} />`. handleConfirm() (lines 102-134) calls moveApplicationAction with exactly { applicationId, toStage: 'placed', placementFeePence, placementDate, placementType, placementCurrency: 'GBP', jobId, candidateId } — `notes` is genuinely absent from the payload. The reset()/onOpenChange just clear it.

2. Server action (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts): moveSchema (lines 153-187) has `declineNotes` (line 170) but no placementNotes/placementCurrency-adjacent notes field. Zod safeParse would strip any unknown key anyway. The destructured fields (lines 200-211) and the moveApplication() call (lines 231-241) carry no placement notes.

3. DB helper (src/lib/db/applications.ts): MoveApplicationArgs (lines 348-359) has declineNotes but no placement notes; the rpc('move_application', ...) call (lines 376-386) passes only p_decline_notes, p_placement_fee_pence, p_placement_date, p_placement_type, p_placement_currency.

4. RPC (supabase/migrations/20260523160100_move_application_with_placement_fields.sql): the recreated 9-param signature (lines 39-49) has p_decline_notes but NO p_placement_notes. The activity metadata jsonb (lines 152-162) stores decline_notes but no placement notes column/key. A repo-wide grep across src and supabase for placement_notes/p_placement_notes/placementNotes returns NO MATCHES ANYWHERE, and no later migration adds it.

Behaviour: handleConfirm fires toast.success('Placement recorded.') (line 129) unconditionally on res.ok, so the recruiter gets positive confirmation while the typed note is silently dropped at the modal layer (never even sent). This is a genuine dead-input / silent-data-loss defect exactly as described.

Severity adjustment: I downgrade from high to medium. The lost field is an explicitly OPTIONAL free-text note ("Notes (optional)"). All structured, business-critical placement data — fee_pence, placed_at, placement_type, placement_currency — is captured and persisted correctly (verified in the UPDATE at lines 94-128 and the metadata jsonb). The source-attribution/revenue reporting the modal promises is therefore intact. No crash, no corruption, no cross-tenant leak, and the placement itself succeeds. Impact is confined to silent loss of an optional annotation, which is a real UX/trust defect worth fixing but not high-severity.

title: 'Withdrawn' terminal stage has no UI path — candidates can be Rejected but never Withdrawn
description: The application_stage enum and the move-action validation both support 'withdrawn' as a terminal stage (with a required decline reason), but PIPELINE_STAGES deliberately excludes both 'rejected' and 'withdrawn'. Every move control — the PipelineCard dropdown, the ApplicationRowActions dropdown, and the mobile Sheet — iterates PIPELINE_STAGES.filter(s => s !== currentStage), so 'withdrawn' never appears as a destination. The only terminal action exposed is 'Reject…' (which moves to 'rejected'). There is no way for a recruiter to mark a candidate as having withdrawn themselves.
userImpact: When a candidate pulls out of a process (a very common recruitment event), the recruiter has no 'Withdrawn' option. They must mis-record it as a Rejection with decline_reason='candidate_withdrew', which muddies pipeline analytics (rejections vs. self-withdrawals) and reads oddly to the candidate-facing record.
evidence: PIPELINE_STAGES = ['applied','screening','cv_submitted','first_interview','second_interview','offer','placed'] — no 'withdrawn'. application-row-actions.tsx and pipeline-card.tsx both render `PIPELINE_STAGES.filter(s => s !== currentStage)`. grep for 'withdrawn' in src/app/(app) and src/components/app finds only validation/display references, never a move control.
suggestedFix: Either expose a 'Withdraw…' action (analogous to Reject, with decline_reason='candidate_withdrew' or a dedicated reason) in the card/row dropdowns, or document that withdrawal is intentionally folded into Reject so it isn't perceived as a missing feature.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed in code. The `application_stage` enum (migration 20260513152244 lines 50-51) and the move-action validation both support `withdrawn` as a terminal stage requiring a decline reason: actions.ts:150 lists it in APPLICATION_STAGES and actions.ts:216 enforces a reason for `rejected || withdrawn`. The DB CHECK constraint (lines 318-319) treats both equally. However, `PIPELINE_STAGES` (pipeline-stages.ts:17-25) deliberately excludes both terminal stages, with an explicit comment that they are 'triggered by the Reject action, not rendered as columns.' All four stage-move UI surfaces iterate `PIPELINE_STAGES.filter(s => s !== currentStage)` and offer only a 'Reject…' terminal action: pipeline-card.tsx:124/139, application-row-actions.tsx:120/135, pipeline-mobile-list.tsx:269/300, candidate-applications.tsx:152/231. The only terminal modal, DeclineModal, hard-codes `toStage: 'rejected'` (decline-modal.tsx:76). A grep for any control setting `toStage: 'withdrawn'` finds zero hits — every `withdrawn` reference is validation, display (badge/label), or analytics filtering, never a move control. The analytics-muddying impact is real: `candidate_withdrew` is offered as a decline_reason (decline-reasons.ts:24), so withdrawals get recorded as stage=`rejected` with that reason rather than stage=`withdrawn`. The claim is accurate in every particular. Severity stays low: nothing errors, there's a working workaround (Reject + 'Candidate withdrew'), and the only cost is record semantics / rejection-vs-withdrawal analytics fidelity — no blocked workflow, no data leakage, and nothing that would visibly break in a client demo.

title: Placement fee uses parseFloat — '7,500' silently becomes £7, locale/typo corrupts revenue
description: parsedFeePence() does `const parsed = parseFloat(feeGbp); if (!isFinite(parsed) || parsed < 0) return null; return Math.round(parsed * 100)`. parseFloat stops at the first non-numeric character, so a recruiter typing a UK-formatted figure like '7,500' parses to 7 (the comma terminates the parse) → stored as 700 pence (£7). '7500abc', '£7500', '7 500' all mis-parse or truncate. The server action only validates `z.number().int().min(0).max(100_000_000)` on the already-computed pence, so the corrupted 700 passes every guard. There is no thousands-separator stripping, no reject-on-trailing-garbage, and the `inputMode="decimal"` field invites comma entry.
userImpact: On the live demo, a recruiter who types a placement fee with a comma (the natural UK way to write 7,500) records a £7 placement. The fee feeds the source-attribution revenue report and recruiter rollups, so the headline revenue number is silently wrong — the worst possible bug to surface in front of a client evaluating the product as their system of record.
suggestedFix: Strip thousands separators and reject trailing garbage before parsing: e.g. `const cleaned = feeGbp.replace(/[\s,£]/g, ''); if (!/^\d+(\.\d{1,2})?$/.test(cleaned)) return null; const n = Number(cleaned); return Math.round(n * 100)`. Show an inline validation hint when the field can't be parsed rather than silently disabling the button.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed in src/components/app/placement-modal.tsx:89-93. parsedFeePence() does `const parsed = parseFloat(feeGbp); if (!isFinite(parsed) || parsed < 0) return null; return Math.round(parsed * 100)`. No thousands-separator stripping, no trailing-garbage rejection. Empirically verified with Node: '7,500' -> parseFloat 7 -> 700 pence (£7), a 1000x understatement; '7 500' -> £7; '75,000' -> £75; '1,250.50' -> £1; '7500abc' silently accepts £7500. The field is type="text" inputMode="decimal" (line 159-160), which invites comma/decimal entry, with placeholder 'e.g. 7500' that does not warn against separators. The server action (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts:178) validates only `z.number().int().min(0).max(100_000_000)` against the ALREADY-truncated pence integer — 700 is a valid non-negative int <= 100M, so it passes every guard. The DB RPC/CHECK constraint (20260523160100, placement_fields_present_when_placed) only checks presence, not magnitude. No layer re-parses the raw string. Confirmed revenue propagation: placement_fee_pence (bigint) -> moveApplication (src/lib/db/applications.ts:382) -> DB -> source_attribution_summary RPC's total_fee_pence aggregate (src/lib/db/source-attribution.ts:29) and buyer-value report. So a comma-typed fee silently understates the headline revenue figure on the source-attribution report. The bug is real, the truncation values are exactly as claimed, and the suggested fix (regex-clean separators + reject trailing garbage + inline hint) is correct.

title: Explain match succeeds but the card never updates — toast tells the user to manually refresh
description: explainCandidateMatchAction does the synchronous 3-6s Sonnet call, upserts the summary, and calls revalidatePath('/jobs/[id]/matches'), then returns { ok: true }. But ExplainButton only fires toast.success('Match explained — refresh to see details') and never calls router.refresh(). Because the matches page is an RSC, revalidatePath invalidates the server cache but does NOT cause the already-rendered client tree to re-render. The recruiter waits 3-6 seconds, gets a success toast, yet the card still shows 'Not scored yet' with the Explain button — until they manually reload the page.
userImpact: In the demo this looks broken: click Explain, wait 6 seconds, see 'done', and nothing changes on screen. The recruiter assumes the AI failed and either re-clicks (burning another Sonnet call + cost) or loses trust in the headline AI feature the product is sold on.
suggestedFix: Import useRouter and call router.refresh() after the success toast so the RSC re-fetches and the card upgrades in place. Change the toast copy to 'Match explained.' once the auto-refresh is wired.
verifierReasoning: The claim holds. In src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/explain-button.tsx the onClick handler (lines 29-38) awaits explainCandidateMatchAction, and on success only fires toast.success('Match explained — refresh to see details') at line 36. The component does not import useRouter and never calls router.refresh(). In actions.ts the action calls revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}/matches`) at line 185 then returns { ok: true }. A revalidatePath() call inside a programmatically-invoked server action (it is called as a plain async function inside startTransition, not bound to a <form action>) invalidates the server-side route cache for the NEXT fetch but does not force the currently-mounted client router tree to re-fetch and re-render. The matches page (page.tsx) is a pure RSC, MatchCard is an RSC, and ExplainButton is the only client component in the subtree — a grep of the entire matches/ directory shows no useRouter, no router.refresh, no useEffect, and no polling. So after the 3-6s Sonnet call completes the card stays on its 'Not scored yet' branch (match-card.tsx lines 84-87) with the Explain button still shown (line 176) until the recruiter manually reloads. The strongest corroboration is the codebase's own universal pattern: ApplicationRowActions, SpecDeleteButton, FloatRowActions, LogActivityForm and LogNoteForm all use the identical useTransition + server-action-with-revalidatePath structure and every one of them calls router.refresh() after the success toast. application-row-actions.tsx lines 31-32 even documents 'rely on revalidatePath inside the actions, so after success we just router.refresh() to re-fetch the table', and float-row-actions.tsx does this for the same route it is displayed on. ExplainButton is the lone deviation, and its toast copy ('refresh to see details') is itself an admission that the card does not auto-update. The suggested fix (import useRouter, call router.refresh() after success, simplify the toast copy) is correct and matches the existing convention. I down-grade from high to medium: the bug is real and on the headline AI feature, but the summary IS persisted (upsertMatchSummary runs), a manual reload surfaces it, and there is no data loss; the cache lookup branch (actions.ts lines 99-108) also makes a re-click cheap (it returns from cache without a second Sonnet call), so the 'burns another Sonnet call' impact is partially mitigated. Net: a clearly-broken, demo-embarrassing UX gap on the flagship feature with a trivial one-line fix.

title: Placement notes are collected in the modal but never sent to the server — they vanish on save
description: The PlacementModal renders a Notes textarea bound to `notes`/`setNotes`, but handleConfirm builds the moveApplicationAction payload with only applicationId, toStage, placementFeePence, placementDate, placementType, placementCurrency, jobId, candidateId. `notes` is never included. moveApplicationAction has no placement-notes parameter and the move_application RPC has no notes column for placement, so anything typed there is discarded with no warning.
userImpact: A recruiter records context about the placement ('starts in 4 weeks, fee invoiced on day 1', IR35 status, etc.), clicks Confirm, sees 'Placement recorded', and the note is gone forever. Data-loss that the user has no way to detect.
suggestedFix: Either remove the Notes field from the modal (so users don't expect persistence), or thread it through: add placementNotes to MoveApplicationArgs/moveSchema, store it in the activities metadata (the RPC already builds a jsonb metadata object) or in decline_notes-equivalent placement column.
verifierReasoning: Verified the full chain. (1) src/components/app/placement-modal.tsx:78 declares `const [notes, setNotes] = useState('')` and lines 197-205 bind it to a Textarea labeled "Notes (optional)". (2) handleConfirm (lines 112-121) builds the moveApplicationAction payload with exactly applicationId, toStage, placementFeePence, placementDate, placementType, placementCurrency, jobId, candidateId — `notes` is never passed. (3) The action's Zod `moveSchema` (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/actions.ts:153-187) has a `declineNotes` field but NO placement-notes/notes field; z.object strips unknown keys, so even an attempt to pass it would be silently dropped. (4) The db helper `moveApplication` (src/lib/db/applications.ts:352-358) has no placement-notes arg. (5) The RPC move_application (supabase/migrations/20260523160100_move_application_with_placement_fields.sql) persists decline_notes only on rejected/withdrawn, and its activity metadata jsonb (lines 152-162) carries decline_notes but no placement notes. A repo-wide grep confirms `placement_notes`/`placementNotes` exists nowhere in supabase/ or src/. So the typed note is discarded at the modal layer, and there is no DB column or RPC param that could receive it. The success toast 'Placement recorded.' fires regardless, giving the user no signal of the loss. This matches the claim's silent-fail/data-loss description exactly. Severity held at medium rather than higher because fee/date/type (the actual revenue-critical fields) all persist correctly, the lost field is explicitly labeled '(optional)', and the placement itself succeeds; the only harm is loss of free-text context — real and undetectable, but not a broken core flow.

title: Monthly match-spend ceiling silently under-counts beyond PostgREST's 1000-row default — cost guard can be bypassed
description: getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth fetches every ai_usage row for the org with purpose='match_score' this month and sums cost_pence client-side. PostgREST caps result rows (default 1000) unless a range/limit is set; this query sets none. The helper's own comment acknowledges '~5k rows/month worst case'. Once an org exceeds ~1000 match_score rows in a month, the select returns only the first 1000, the client-side sum under-reports month-to-date spend, and the `spend >= MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE` check in both precompute-matches-for-job and explainCandidateMatchAction never trips — the cost ceiling that protects the £100/month budget is defeated.
userImpact: Not visible in a single demo, but it undermines the per-tenant cost cap the SaaS pricing story depends on. A busy org can blow well past the configured monthly match-scoring budget while the guard reports it as under-limit.
suggestedFix: Replace the client-side sum with a Postgres sum() RPC (sum_match_spend_this_month(org)) — the comment already flags this is needed at scale. The aggregate is exact, single-round-trip, and immune to the row cap.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is mechanically correct and verified against the actual code.

1. getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth (src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:234-239) issues an unbounded `.select('cost_pence')` with `.eq(organization_id)`, `.eq(purpose='match_score')`, `.gte(created_at, monthStart)` and NO `.limit()`/`.range()`. The sum is computed client-side at line 247.

2. PostgREST DOES cap unbounded selects. supabase/config.toml:10 explicitly sets `max_rows = 1000` (also the Supabase Cloud default for db-max-rows). So the query returns at most the first 1000 ai_usage rows regardless of how many exist for that org/month. The helper's own JSDoc (lines 220-224) admits PostgREST has no sum() without an RPC and flags ~5k rows/month worst case — i.e. the author knew rows can exceed the cap.

3. Both guard call-sites compare against the under-counted sum: the Inngest precompute path (precompute-matches-for-job.ts:124-167, `context.spendPence >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE`) and the on-demand action (jobs/[id]/matches/actions.ts:131-154). Neither passes any range. So once >1000 match_score ai_usage rows accumulate in a calendar month, both guards see only the first 1000 rows' cost and under-report month-to-date spend.

4. Economics confirm the cap can defeat the £100 ceiling. env.ts:83 default MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE = 10_000 pence. cost_pence is `Math.ceil(...)` of Sonnet pricing (claude.ts:44-46), so ~1-3 pence per match_score row (max_tokens 800 output, ~few-k input). To reach 10,000 pence TRUE spend you need ~3,300-10,000 rows; the capped query reports only the first 1000 rows (~1,000-3,000 pence), so `>= 10,000` never trips. The £100/month per-tenant cost ceiling is therefore bypassable at high volume — exactly as described. The suggested fix (a Postgres sum() RPC) is correct and the code comment already anticipates it.

Note: the worst-case overshoot is bounded — actual spend keeps climbing only while the recruiter keeps triggering scores, and ai_usage remains the authoritative ledger (the usage page shows the real total via its own query), so an operator watching the dashboard would still see overspend. The bug defeats the automatic *enforcement*, not the *visibility*.

Severity downgrade rationale: This is a real correctness defect in a cost-protection guard, but for the live anchor customer (a 2-3 person UK agency) it is invisible in a demo and requires ~3k-10k match_score AI calls in a single calendar month before it bites — sustained, atypical volume for a tiny agency. It is not a data-loss, security, or cross-tenant bug, and ai_usage still records true cost. It matters for the SaaS-scale cost-cap story but has near-zero impact on a single-org demo or near-term anchor usage. Hence 'low' rather than the claimed 'medium', and certainly not demo-breaking.

title: Moving a card out of 'placed' (or out of a terminal decline) leaves stale fee/placement/decline data on the row
description: move_application only SETS placement fields when moving INTO 'placed' and decline fields when moving INTO 'rejected'/'withdrawn'; every other transition uses `else fee_pence` / `else decline_reason` (i.e. keeps the existing value). So moving a placed candidate back to 'offer' (fully supported via the dropdown 'Move to stage', which lists all 7 stages for any card) leaves fee_pence, placed_at, placement_type, placement_currency populated on a row that is no longer placed. Likewise un-rejecting leaves decline_reason/declined_at set. Source-attribution is protected because its RPC filters `and a.stage = 'placed'`, but the row data is now internally inconsistent: e.g. a non-terminal row carries a decline_reason, and a re-placed candidate could retain an old fee unless re-entered.
userImpact: Mostly an integrity/hygiene issue rather than a visible demo break. The risk is a future report or export that reads fee_pence/decline_reason without re-checking stage will show ghost revenue or a phantom decline reason for an active candidate.
evidence:
fee_pence = case when p_to_stage = 'placed' then p_placement_fee_pence else fee_pence end,
placed_at = case when p_to_stage = 'placed' then p_placement_date else placed_at end,
decline_reason = case when p_to_stage in ('rejected','withdrawn') then p_decline_reason else decline_reason end
suggestedFix: In the UPDATE, clear placement fields when moving to any non-'placed' stage and clear decline fields when moving to any non-terminal stage (else-branch sets them to NULL rather than preserving). This keeps the row consistent with its stage.
verifierReasoning:
The migration logic is exactly as claimed. In supabase/migrations/20260523160100_move_application_with_placement_fields.sql (the latest/only definition of move_application), lines 99-127 set placement fields only when p_to_stage='placed' and decline fields only when moving into 'rejected'/'withdrawn'; every other transition uses `else <existing column>`, preserving stale values. The reverse transition is reachable: src/components/app/pipeline-card.tsx:124 renders a "Move to stage" submenu over PIPELINE_STAGES.filter(s => s !== card.stage), and 'placed' IS a rendered kanban column (groupByStage at src/lib/db/applications.ts:159 only drops 'rejected'/'withdrawn'). So a placed card can be moved to offer/interview, leaving fee_pence, placed_at, placement_type, placement_currency populated on a non-placed row. The row is therefore internally inconsistent with its stage, exactly as described.

Two mitigating facts: (1) All revenue/source consumers re-check stage — source_attribution_rpc, both buyer_value_rpcs variants, and the commission RPC all filter `and a.stage = 'placed'` — so ghost revenue does NOT surface in any current report or export (the claim acknowledges this). (2) The per-job applications-list.tsx:109 gates decline_reason display behind isTerminal, so stale decline reasons don't show there.

However the claim slightly under-weights one VISIBLE path: src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/candidate-applications.tsx:188 renders `app.decline_reason` UNCONDITIONALLY (no isTerminal gate, unlike applications-list), so a row moved back out of a terminal stage would show a phantom decline reason next to a live stage badge. That said, producing an un-rejected row is hard in normal flow: the candidate page disables the move dropdown for terminal rows (line 205) and the kanban never renders terminal cards, so the decline-side stale data is largely unreachable via UI today. The placement-side stale data (the headline scenario) is trivially reproducible but currently invisible.

Net: a genuine data-integrity/hygiene defect that is latent today (no consumer surfaces ghost fee data, decline path is hard to trigger), with real future risk if a report/export reads fee_pence or decline_reason without re-checking stage. The suggested fix (NULL the fields in the else-branch) is correct and append-only-compatible via a new migration.

title: precompute-matches counters (scored/cache_hits) under-report after any Inngest step retry
description: cacheHits and scored are plain closure variables mutated inside per-candidate step.run() blocks. Inngest memoizes completed step.run outputs across retries: on a retry/replay, already-completed score-<id> steps return their memoized result WITHOUT re-executing the closure body, so the ++ never runs for those candidates. After any single transient failure that triggers a function retry, the returned { scored, cache_hits } will be lower than reality.
userImpact: No user-facing impact — these counters only appear in the Inngest function return value used for observability. But anyone using them to confirm 'did precompute actually score N candidates' will be misled after a retry.
suggestedFix: Return per-candidate outcome ('hit'|'scored'|'skipped') from each step.run and aggregate the memoized return values after the loop, or accept the counters are best-effort and document them as non-authoritative.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed accurate by reading src/lib/inngest/functions/precompute-matches-for-job.ts. `let cacheHits = 0` / `let scored = 0` are declared at lines 205-206, and `cacheHits++` (line 258) / `scored++` (line 295) are mutated INSIDE per-candidate `step.run(`score-${candidate.id}`, ...)` closures. The final return at line 299 reads these closure variables. The function is configured with `retries: 3` (line 73). Inngest's execution model (v4, confirmed in package.json) replays the function on each attempt: completed step.run outputs are memoized and returned WITHOUT re-executing the closure body. Concretely: if candidates A and B score successfully but C throws a transient error (e.g. Anthropic 529 from scoreCandidateForJob at line 272), the error propagates out of step C, is caught at line 300 and re-thrown at line 308, triggering a full-function retry. On the retry pass, `cacheHits`/`scored` re-initialize to 0, and steps score-A/score-B return memoized results so their `++` never runs — only score-C's closure executes. The returned `{ scored, cache_hits }` then undercounts everything completed in the prior attempt. The diagnosis, the line reference, and the proposed fix (return per-candidate outcomes and aggregate the persisted step return values) are all correct.</reasoning>
</invoke>

title: Matches page falls back to empty matches when current_organization_id() returns null but otherwise shows 'Couldn't resolve org' error
description: The matches page hard-errors ('Couldn't resolve your organisation. Please refresh.') if orgRpc.data isn't a string. getJob succeeded (so RLS resolved the org for the SELECT), which means a null here implies a transient RPC hiccup or a user whose users-row org is unexpectedly null. The page treats this as a full-page error even though the job header already loaded — inconsistent with the rest of the page which degrades to empty/`errored` banners. Minor, but the all-or-nothing early return discards the successfully-fetched job context.
userImpact: Edge case: a user/session whose org lookup flakes sees a red full-page error on the matches tab even though the job itself loaded fine elsewhere. Rare, low blast radius.
suggestedFix: Log the unexpected null to Sentry (org resolution failed despite getJob succeeding) and render the existing 'Couldn't load matches' banner inline within the normal page chrome rather than replacing the whole page.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the code at src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/page.tsx:56-65. It matches the evidence exactly: after getJob succeeds, the page calls supabase.rpc('current_organization_id'), and if orgRpc.data isn't a string it does a full-page early return ("Couldn't resolve your organisation. Please refresh.") with no Sentry logging (confirmed no Sentry import/call in the file). The rest of the page degrades gracefully instead: the top-N match fetch failure sets `errored` (line 81) and renders an inline banner inside the normal page chrome (line 132-135), while still showing the job header. So the inconsistency the claim describes is real.

The claim's logical premise also checks out. current_organization_id() is `select organization_id from public.users where id = auth.uid()` (migration 20260513151021, lines 61-69). The jobs RLS policy is `organization_id = current_organization_id()`. If the org were genuinely null, that predicate is null/not-true for every row, so getJob would return zero rows -> code 'not_found' -> notFound() (page.tsx:44) BEFORE ever reaching line 56. Therefore a null at line 58, given a successful getJob, can only arise from a transient RPC flake on the second sequential call (sharing the same session/token as getJob) or an extraordinarily inconsistent users-row state — not a normal condition. This makes the early-return path almost unreachable.

Net: the observation is accurate, but the user-facing impact is a pure UX-consistency nit (full-page red text vs inline banner) on a near-unreachable path. No correctness, security, data-leak, or multi-tenancy concern. For a live client demo the blast radius is negligible. The reviewer's own self-grade of 'low' is appropriate; it sits at the floor of real.

title: Ad generation has no cache check — every "Generate ad" click re-bills a fresh Sonnet call
description: generateAdAction calls generateAdWithInclusivity (a Sonnet 4.6 call) unconditionally on every button press. There is no lookup of a previously saved/generated ad for the job before spending tokens. CLAUDE.md core principle 4 is "Cache AI outputs aggressively. Regenerate only on material change." The match-score path correctly caches in ai_summaries keyed on embedding versions; the ad path does not cache at all. The plan note (D3-33: "every save is a new row, no dedup") justifies multiple SAVED rows but does not justify skipping a cache/regenerate guard on the expensive Sonnet GENERATE call. A recruiter clicking Generate repeatedly during the demo bills ~1.8p each time with no ceiling on this purpose.
userImpact: Demo/usage cost accrues every time the recruiter regenerates an ad, even if nothing about the job changed. Unlike match scoring, there is no per-org monthly spend guard on the ad_generate purpose, so a recruiter hammering the button burns tokens unbounded.
evidence: const result = await generateAdWithInclusivity({ organizationId: job.organization_id, ... }) — called with no prior cache/dedup lookup. Compare with match path which does getMatchSummary(...) cache lookup before scoreCandidateForJob.
suggestedFix: Acceptable for the demo as-is (generation is intentionally a fresh-draft action), but before charging customers add: (a) reuse the most recent saved job_ad for the job if the job's embed-relevant columns are unchanged, and (b) apply the same getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth-style monthly ceiling to purpose='ad_generate' so a runaway loop is bounded.
verifierReasoning:
Every checkable assertion in the claim holds against the actual code.

1. generateAdAction (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/ad-panel/actions.ts:79) calls generateAdWithInclusivity unconditionally inside a Sentry span. There is no prior lookup of saved job_ads or any cache before the Sonnet call. Confirmed.

2. generateAdWithInclusivity (src/lib/ai/ad-generate.ts:259-306) is a claude-sonnet-4-6 call with purpose 'ad_generate', logged to ai_usage via runWithLogging. The file's own comment states ~1.8p per ad+score call. Confirmed.

3. The match path the claim compares against is real and behaves exactly as described. src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/actions.ts does getMatchSummary(...) (lines 99-108) and returns early on cache hit, AND applies getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth + env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE ceiling (lines 131-154) before the Sonnet call. So the claimed asymmetry is genuine.

4. The spend guard is purpose-scoped to match_score only: src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:238 filters .eq('purpose', 'match_score'). It does NOT bound 'ad_generate' spend. There is no equivalent ceiling on the ad path. Confirmed.

5. D3-33 ('every save is a new row, no dedup') governs saved rows, not the generate call — the claim's distinction is correct.

6. UI (ad-panel.tsx GenerateTab) only sets disabled={isPending} (debounces the in-flight request); no client-side dedup or reuse of an existing ad. Confirmed.

On severity: the reviewer self-graded low and explicitly noted generation is intentionally a fresh-draft action, which is correct — caching an ad by job columns would be a UX regression (a recruiter clicking 'Generate' again wants new copy, unlike the deterministic candidate-job match summary). The genuinely missing piece is a spend ceiling on purpose='ad_generate'. In practice each click is ~1.8p and a 3-6s synchronous round-trip, so a runaway loop is human-rate-limited and bounded to pennies, not a real cost-blowout vector for a 2-3 person demo. low is the honest grade.

title: Explain-match success toast tells the recruiter to refresh manually; the card does not auto-update
description: explainCandidateMatchAction performs the Sonnet call, upserts the summary, and calls revalidatePath('/jobs/[id]/matches'). But the client ExplainButton runs the action inside useTransition WITHOUT calling router.refresh(), and on success shows toast.success('Match explained — refresh to see details'). revalidatePath on its own does not re-render the current client view — it only marks the cache stale for the NEXT navigation. So after a 3-6s wait the recruiter sees a success toast that explicitly instructs them to manually reload the page to see the strengths/gaps/screening questions they just paid Sonnet to generate.
