userImpact: In the live demo, clicking "Explain match" appears to do nothing visible — the card stays on "Not scored yet" and the toast asks the user to refresh. Looks broken / unpolished for an AI-first product whose headline feature is exactly this match explanation.
evidence: explain-button.tsx: startTransition(async () => { const result = await explainCandidateMatchAction(...); ... toast.success('Match explained — refresh to see details') }) — no router.refresh(). The card upgrade depends on a manual reload.
suggestedFix: Import useRouter and call router.refresh() after a successful action (it re-fetches the RSC tree using the revalidated cache), then change the toast to 'Match explained.' so the card upgrades in place without a manual reload.
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code. src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/explain-button.tsx line 36 shows toast.success('Match explained — refresh to see details') and the file imports only useTransition, lucide icons, toast, Button, and the action — there is NO useRouter import and NO router.refresh() call anywhere in the component. The action (actions.ts:185-186) calls revalidatePath(`/jobs/${jobId}/matches`) then returns { ok: true }, exactly as the claim states. The action is invoked IMPERATIVELY inside startTransition (await explainCandidateMatchAction(...)), not bound to a <form action> on the current route. In Next.js 16.2.6 (confirmed installed) this imperative pattern means revalidatePath only marks the server cache stale for the next navigation; it does NOT reconcile the currently-mounted client tree. page.tsx is an RSC whose `summary` prop is what flips the card from the 'Not scored yet' badge (match-card.tsx:84-87) to the strengths/gaps/screening-questions view (match-card.tsx:101-159), so without a refresh the card visibly stays on 'Not scored yet' after a successful 3-6s Sonnet call. Strong corroboration: 10+ other components in this same repo (application-row-actions.tsx, cv-review-panel.tsx, saved-ad-row-actions.tsx, etc.) DO call router.refresh() after a revalidatePath-using Server Action — this button is the lone exception, and its own toast copy ('refresh to see details') is an admission the card won't auto-update. The cache-hit branch (actions.ts:105-108) has the same defect. Impact is exactly as described and hits the product's headline AI feature in a live demo; it is UX/feedback, not data-loss or security, so medium is the honest grade. Suggested fix (import useRouter, call router.refresh() on success, change toast to 'Match explained.') is correct and matches the codebase's existing convention.

title: Stale match cards still render the OLD Sonnet explanation while only labelling it "Refreshing…"
description: When the cached summary's candidate_embedding_version or job_embedding_version no longer matches the live versions (i.e. the CV or JD materially changed and was re-embedded), the card sets isStale=true and shows a small amber 'Refreshing…' badge, but it STILL renders the full prior summary.content (score, strengths, gaps, screening questions) generated against the previous version of the data. The recruiter reads strengths/gaps that were computed against an out-of-date CV/JD with only a subtle badge to signal it.
userImpact: After a candidate's CV is updated, the matches page can present confidently-worded AI strengths/gaps that reference the old CV. A recruiter could relay stale, now-incorrect screening questions to a client. The 'Refreshing…' badge is easy to miss.
evidence: const isStale = summary !== null && ((summary.candidate_embedding_version ?? 0) !== candidateEmbeddingVersion || (summary.job_embedding_version ?? 0) !== jobEmbeddingVersion) — but the JSX below still maps summary.content.strengths/gaps/screening_questions in full when summary is present, regardless of isStale.
suggestedFix: When isStale, either visually de-emphasise the body (e.g. opacity + a clear 'This explanation is out of date — click Explain to refresh' banner) or hide the strengths/gaps and show only the staleness prompt, so stale AI content can't be mistaken for current.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is accurate at the code level. In src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/match-card.tsx, isStale is computed (lines 59-62) but the JSX gates the body purely on `summary ?` (truthy), not on isStale. When a summary is present, the card unconditionally renders the full prior content: strengths (lines 107-117), gaps (lines 124-138), and screening_questions (lines 145-149), with no opacity, no de-emphasis, and no "out of date" banner. The only things isStale changes are: showing the small amber "Refreshing…" badge (lines 88-96) and surfacing the ExplainButton in the footer (lines 175-177). So stale AI content is presented as current with only a subtle badge — exactly as described.

The path is reachable: page.tsx fetches summaries via listMatchSummariesForJob (src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:158-176), which filters only by kind='match_score' and job_id — it does NOT filter on the embedding-version columns. So stale-versioned rows are returned and rendered. (Contrast with getMatchSummary at lines 92-100, the precompute cache-read path, which DOES filter on both version columns. The cleanup-stale-summaries comment "stale rows are simply ignored on reads" is true only for that precompute path, not for the list view that drives this UI.)

The scenario is actually slightly worse than the claim implies. A candidate CV update → re-embed happens via embed-batch (10-min cron) which bumps candidates.embedding_version but does NOT fire job/score-top-candidates. Re-scoring (precompute-matches-for-job) is only triggered on job creation and on a job JD change (embed-job-on-jd-change). So a candidate-side staleness is never auto-regenerated — the stale row persists until either the recruiter clicks Explain or the weekly cleanup-stale-summaries cron (Monday 04:00, cleanup-stale-summaries.ts:27) deletes it. That means the full stale strengths/gaps/screening questions can linger on the matches page for up to ~7 days. The card's own comment (lines 28-30) claiming "the next precompute run will replace it" is misleading for the candidate-stale case, and the "Refreshing…" badge copy implies active work that isn't happening on that path.

Severity: genuinely low, not higher. The content was valid when generated, there IS a visible signal (badge + tooltip + Explain affordance), and it is not a data-integrity or tenancy bug. But it is a real presentation-correctness issue: a recruiter could read AI strengths/gaps/screening questions computed against an outdated CV and relay them, with only a subtle badge as warning.

title: Monthly match-spend ceiling can silently under-count via PostgREST's default 1000-row cap
description: getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth selects ai_usage.cost_pence rows for the org/month and sums them client-side with no explicit range/limit. PostgREST returns at most 1000 rows per request by default. The function's own comment estimates '~5k rows/month worst case'. Once an org exceeds ~1000 match_score ai_usage rows in a month, the sum silently truncates to the first 1000, undercounting spend — so the cost ceiling (used by both the precompute Inngest path and explainCandidateMatchAction) would fail to trip even when real spend exceeds the limit.
userImpact: The per-tenant AI spend guard — the safety net protecting against runaway match-scoring cost — can fail to fire for an active org, allowing spend to exceed the configured monthly ceiling without pausing.
evidence: await asAiUsageClient(supabase).from('ai_usage').select('cost_pence').eq('organization_id', orgId).eq('purpose', 'match_score').gte('created_at', monthStart) — no .limit() / range, and a client-side reduce(). Comment: 'At anchor scale (~5k rows/month worst case) the round-trip is fine'.
suggestedFix: Replace the row-fetch+JS-sum with a Postgres aggregate via an RPC (sum(cost_pence)), or at minimum pass an explicit high range. The comment already flags 'at SaaS scale this should become an RPC' — do it before onboarding a busy tenant.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the claim against the actual code. src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:234-248: getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth does `.from('ai_usage').select('cost_pence').eq('organization_id', orgId).eq('purpose','match_score').gte('created_at', monthStart)` with NO `.limit()` or `.range()`, then sums client-side with `.reduce()`. The PostgREST row cap is not just a default assumption — it is explicitly configured: supabase/config.toml:10 sets `max_rows = 1000` (the PostgREST db-max-rows ceiling; the Supabase cloud platform default is also 1000). So this query returns at most 1000 rows regardless of how many match_score rows exist, and with no explicit ORDER BY the truncated subset is arbitrary.

Economics confirm the truncation bites well before the ceiling trips. Each match_score call uses Sonnet 4.6 (240p/1200p per MTok, src/lib/ai/claude.ts:40) with max_tokens 800 and small prompts (src/lib/ai/match.ts:104), so cost_pence per row is roughly 1-3 pence (calcCostPence uses Math.ceil, min 1p). The default ceiling MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE is 10,000 pence / £100 (src/lib/env.ts:83). Reaching it requires thousands of rows — consistent with the function's own comment ("~5k rows/month worst case"). Thus 1000 rows corresponds to only ~£10-30 of reported spend; beyond that, the sum silently caps and under-counts.

Both consumers gate on this value as a hard stop: the Inngest precompute path compares `context.spendPence >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE` (precompute-matches-for-job.ts:150, returns stopped:'cost-ceiling') and explainCandidateMatchAction does the same (jobs/[id]/matches/actions.ts:132). So once an active org exceeds ~1000 match_score rows in a calendar month, the under-counted sum can stay below the ceiling forever and the spend guard never fires — exactly as the reviewer described. The settings usage page (settings/usage/page.tsx) reads spend via a different aggregation (byPurpose map), so the display path differs, but the guard logic is genuinely affected.

Impact is real but bounded: it is a degradation of a cost safety net (overspend on a £100/month cap), not data loss, cross-tenant leakage, a crash, or anything user-visible in a demo. It requires a genuinely high-volume tenant (>1000 match scores in one month) before it manifests, and the failure mode is graceful (recruiter still gets results, spend just isn't paused). The author already flagged the RPC fix as future work. Severity 'low' is accurate.

title: Match scoring degrades silently when the spend ceiling is hit — no UI signal that AI is paused
description: When the precompute Inngest function detects the org is at/over the monthly spend ceiling it returns early with a Sentry warning and leaves the cache unpopulated, so the matches page just shows 'Not scored yet' cards. The on-demand explainCandidateMatchAction does surface a clear toast ('Match scoring is paused this month…'), but the precompute path's silent exit means a recruiter who never clicks Explain sees only blank 'Not scored yet' cards with no explanation that scoring was intentionally paused for cost reasons. There is graceful degradation (the app still works), but the WHY is invisible until they click Explain.
userImpact: If an org hits its match-scoring budget, the top-matches page looks like the AI simply failed to run, with no on-screen indication that scoring was deliberately paused. Confusing for the recruiter and bad for a demo if the cap were low.
evidence: precompute-matches-for-job.ts: if (context.spendPence >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE) { Sentry.captureMessage(...); return { stopped: 'cost-ceiling', ... } } — exits without any user-facing signal; matches/page.tsx has no 'scoring paused' state, only 'Not indexed yet' and per-card 'Not scored yet'.
suggestedFix: Surface a banner on the matches page when month-to-date spend is at the ceiling (the page can read getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth) explaining scoring is paused for the month, matching the toast wording already used in explainCandidateMatchAction.
verifierReasoning: Every cited fact checks out against the code. In src/lib/inngest/functions/precompute-matches-for-job.ts:150-167, when context.spendPence >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE the function emits only a Sentry.captureMessage (level 'warning') and returns { stopped: 'cost-ceiling', spend_pence } — no cache write, no user-facing signal. The cache therefore stays unpopulated for any (candidate, job) pair not already scored. In src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/page.tsx the page has exactly three render states: an 'errored' box, the 'Not indexed yet' Alert (matches.length === 0), and the cards grid. It never calls getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth and has no 'scoring paused' state. In src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/match-card.tsx:84-86 a null summary renders a 'Not scored yet' outline badge plus body text 'This candidate is a strong vector match for the job but hasn't been scored yet. Click Explain…'. By contrast explainCandidateMatchAction (actions.ts:131-153) does the same ceiling check and returns the explicit message 'Match scoring is paused this month — monthly spend limit reached. Contact the org owner to lift the limit.' So a recruiter who never clicks Explain sees only blank 'Not scored yet' cards with no on-screen reason the AI paused. The claim is materially accurate. Severity stays low: this is a missing-feedback / UX-polish gap, not a functional, data, or security defect. Graceful degradation is real (vector top-N still renders, Explain still works and surfaces the toast on click), the WHY is discoverable via the existing settings/usage ceiling indicator (settings/usage/page.tsx:113) and by clicking Explain, and the default ceiling is £100/month (env.ts:83 default 10_000 pence), so a fresh demo is very unlikely to trip it. The demo-risk framing is contingent on an artificially low cap. Confirmed at the claimed low severity, with the suggested fix (a banner on the matches page when month-to-date spend is at the ceiling, reusing the existing getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth helper and the explain toast wording) being a sound, low-effort improvement.

title: Approve action marks draft approved, then returns ok:false on Inngest failure — recruiter sees an error toast for a draft that IS already approved, and the documented recovery (re-click Approve) re-fires job creation
description: approveSpecDraftAction performs the three DB writes (company_id, structured_data, markSpecDraftApproved -> status='approved') BEFORE attempting inngest.send('spec-draft/approved'). If the Inngest send throws, the action returns { ok:false, error:'Approved, but job creation could not be queued. Try again in a moment.' }. The form (spec-review-form.tsx handleApprove) treats any !result.ok as a hard failure: it shows toast.error and does NOT navigate. So the row is now status='approved' but the user is told it failed. The comment explicitly says the recruiter should 're-click Approve which will re-fire the event' — but re-clicking runs markSpecDraftApproved again (harmless) and re-sends the event, which is the trigger for the duplicate-job risk below.
userImpact: During the live demo, if the background queue hiccups (cold start / transient), the recruiter approves a draft, sees a red error toast, the job silently does get created moments later (or not), and re-clicking creates confusion or a duplicate job. The success/failure signalling contradicts the actual DB state.
suggestedFix: Either (a) fire the Inngest event BEFORE marking approved so a send failure leaves the draft un-approved and re-clickable cleanly, or (b) keep ordering but return a distinct ok:true-with-warning shape so the form navigates to /jobs and shows a warning toast (e.g. 'Approved — job is being created') instead of a destructive error that implies nothing happened. The current half-committed state is the worst case.
verifierReasoning:
The core mechanism of the claim is verified in code. In src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/actions.ts the approve action performs all three DB writes first — updateSpecDraftCompanyId (l.98), updateSpecDraftStructuredData (l.108), and markSpecDraftApproved which sets status='approved' + approved_at (l.118, helper at spec-drafts.ts:214-238) — and only THEN attempts inngest.send (l.127-135). On a send throw it returns { ok:false, error:'Approved, but job creation could not be queued...' } (l.150-153). The consuming form spec-review-form.tsx:81-84 treats any !result.ok as a hard failure: toast.error + early return, with the success path (router.push('/jobs')) skipped. So a transient Inngest dispatch failure leaves the row genuinely status='approved' while the recruiter sees a destructive red error toast and is stranded on the review page — a real contradictory-state / silent-fail UX bug, and exactly the cold-start/transient profile that bites during a live demo. The documented recovery (re-click Approve, comment l.146-149) is confirmed.

However, the claim's headline 'duplicate-job risk' is largely overstated. create-job-from-spec.ts has two guards: status!=='approved' skip (l.117-122) and, decisively, a created_job_id-is-set skip (l.124-128, 'already-created'). In the primary failure path (first send THROWS so no event is delivered, created_job_id stays NULL), the re-click fires exactly one event and creates exactly one job — no duplicate. A duplicate would require the narrow race where the first send actually delivered-then-rejected on the await, job #1 got created, and a re-click fired a second event before patch-draft-job-id landed (l.199-209) — a thin window the idempotency guard mostly closes. So data integrity (one job, eventually) is substantially preserved.

Net: the misleading-error-on-already-approved-draft bug is real and user-facing, but the unmitigated harm is UX/signalling confusion rather than data corruption or routine duplicate jobs, and it only triggers on an Inngest dispatch failure (not the happy path). That is a solid medium, not high. The suggested fix (b) — return an ok:true-with-warning shape so the form navigates to /jobs and shows a non-destructive 'Approved — job is being created' toast — is the correct remedy.

title: No unique guard prevents create-job-from-spec inserting TWO jobs for one spec draft when the approved event is delivered/fired twice before created_job_id is patched
description: The function's only idempotency guard is reading draft.created_job_id and skipping if set. created_job_id is patched in a LATER step (patch-draft-job-id) AFTER the jobs insert. The approve action's documented recovery path (re-click Approve after an Inngest-send error) re-fires 'spec-draft/approved'. Concurrency is keyed on organization_id with limit 5, so two deliveries for the same draft can run in parallel; both read created_job_id=null, both pass the guard, both run insert-job. There is no DB unique constraint on jobs tying a job to spec_draft_id (jobs table has no spec_draft_id column at all), so nothing at the DB layer prevents the duplicate. Result: two identical jobs rows from one approval.
userImpact: A recruiter who approves once but hits the retry path (or whose event is delivered twice by Inngest's at-least-once delivery) ends up with the same role listed twice in /jobs — embarrassing in a client demo and corrupts the pipeline (duplicate applications, double-counted placements).
suggestedFix: Make creation atomically idempotent: either insert the job with an `on conflict` against a unique (organization_id, spec_draft_id) — add spec_draft_id to jobs — or do a conditional update `update spec_drafts set created_job_id = <new> where id=? and created_job_id is null` and only keep the job if that update affected a row (otherwise delete the just-inserted job / skip). At minimum set created_job_id in the SAME step as the insert via a returning+conditional-update so a second concurrent run sees it.
verifierReasoning: The claim is verified against the actual code. In src/lib/inngest/functions/create-job-from-spec.ts: (1) the sole idempotency guard is reading draft.created_job_id in the `read-draft` step and skipping if set (lines 124-128); (2) created_job_id is only written in the LATER `patch-draft-job-id` step (lines 199-209) which runs AFTER `insert-job` (lines 157-197); (3) that patch is UNCONDITIONAL — `update spec_drafts set created_job_id = newJob.id where id=? and organization_id=?` with NO `where created_job_id is null` clause. The concurrency config is `{ limit: 5, key: 'event.data.organization_id' }` (line 73), keyed at org level not draft level, so two deliveries of `spec-draft/approved` for the SAME draft within one org can run in parallel. Both runs' `read-draft` can complete before either `patch-draft-job-id`, so both see created_job_id=null and status='approved', both pass the guards, both run insert-job. The jobs table (supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql lines 266-290) has NO spec_draft_id column and no unique constraint tying a job to a spec draft; no later migration adds one (grep confirmed). So nothing at the DB layer blocks the duplicate. The trigger is real: the approve action (src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/actions.ts lines 127-154) explicitly documents and instructs the re-click-Approve recovery path on inngest.send failure, AND Inngest delivers at-least-once with no event `id` set so no event-level dedup. Notably, the action's own comment (line 149) claims the function is 'idempotent on created_job_id via a `where created_job_id is null` write' — that guard does NOT exist in the function, so the documented safety net is fictional. Result: a single approval can produce two identical jobs rows. I downgrade from 'high' to 'medium' because the duplicate requires either a specific send-failure-then-retry path or an Inngest double-delivery landing inside a narrow read-window (two cheap reads), making per-approval probability low; but the consequence (duplicate job visible in /jobs, downstream duplicate applications/placements) is real and user-visible, and the claimed safety mechanism is genuinely absent.

title: Approve navigates to /jobs then router.refresh() after a fixed 2s — on cold start the new job is not yet inserted, so the recruiter lands on a /jobs list missing the job they just created
description: After a successful approve, the form does router.push('/jobs') and schedules a single setTimeout(() => router.refresh(), 2000). The actual jobs row is inserted asynchronously by the create-job-from-spec Inngest function. On an Inngest cold start or under load, insert-job commonly takes well over 2 seconds, so the refresh fires before the row exists and the recruiter sees a /jobs list WITHOUT the job. There is no further poll/refresh, so the list stays stale until a manual reload.
userImpact: Recruiter approves a spec call, gets a success toast, lands on Jobs, and their brand-new job is not there. They assume it failed and may re-approve (feeding the duplicate-job bug) or lose confidence in the tool mid-demo.
evidence:
toast.success('Draft approved — creating job…')
router.push('/jobs')
// Inngest inserts the jobs row asynchronously ...
setTimeout(() => router.refresh(), 2000)
suggestedFix: Don't rely on a single fixed delay. Either keep the recruiter on the review page showing a 'creating job…' state that polls the draft's created_job_id and links through once set, or have /jobs poll briefly for very-recently-approved drafts. A fixed 2s refresh will routinely lose the race.
verifierReasoning:
I attempted to refute this and could not — the race is real and mechanically confirmed.

1. The job row IS created asynchronously, after the action returns. In src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/actions.ts:128 the action only `await inngest.send({ name: 'spec-draft/approved', ... })` — that just enqueues the event. The actual insert happens in a SEPARATE Inngest run, createJobFromSpec (src/lib/inngest/functions/create-job-from-spec.ts), which executes three sequential step.run round-trips (read-draft → insert-job → patch-draft-job-id), each a separate HTTP call to the Inngest executor, plus potential function cold start. This routinely exceeds 2s on cold start / under load.

2. The form's only recovery mechanism is a single fixed delay. spec-review-form.tsx:86-90 does router.push('/jobs') then setTimeout(() => router.refresh(), 2000). There is exactly one refresh. I grepped jobs/ and spec/ for setInterval, realtime channels, repeated refresh, and created_job_id polling — none exist on the jobs list or review page (the only setInterval is the unrelated mic-recorder timer). So if the row isn't inserted within ~2s, the list stays stale until a manual reload.

3. The jobs list shows only real jobs rows. jobs/page.tsx renders solely from listJobs against the jobs table; it does NOT surface approved-but-not-yet-created spec drafts. So a not-yet-inserted job is simply absent. The action's revalidatePath('/jobs') (actions.ts:157) invalidates the cache but cannot fix data-not-yet-written — the refresh fires before the row exists, re-fetches the same empty result, and there is no further re-fetch.

This is a genuine cache/timing race with no self-healing path. The recruiter gets a 'Draft approved — creating job…' success toast (spec-review-form.tsx:85), lands on Jobs, and on the cold-start/loaded path the new job is missing with no recovery short of manual reload.

One sub-claim is slightly overstated: the 'may re-approve, feeding the duplicate-job bug' concern is mostly mitigated — createJobFromSpec is idempotent on created_job_id (lines 124-128 return 'already-created'; status guard at 117-122), so a second approve would NOT create a duplicate job. But the core problem — a stale jobs list missing the just-created job, with no automatic recovery — is fully real.

Severity 'medium' is fair: not data loss or corruption, recoverable by reload, but a visible, confidence-eroding failure on a flagship AI-first flow (spec call → job) precisely during the kind of live demo this product is built to win. It is not 'high' because it self-recovers on any subsequent navigation/reload and creates no bad data; it is clearly above 'low' because it routinely manifests under realistic cold-start/load conditions and the UI actively tells the user the action succeeded.

title: List-page delete button is a one-click destructive action with no confirmation — sets status=rejected + deleted_at and is the trigger for permanent 30-day hard-delete + audio purge
description: SpecDeleteButton fires deleteSpecDraftAction -> markSpecDraftRejected on a single click with no window.confirm() guard (the in-review Reject button DOES confirm). markSpecDraftRejected sets status='rejected', rejected_at=now, deleted_at=now. This both removes the draft from the list and starts the 30-day clock after which spec-draft-cleanup-sweep hard-deletes the row and spec-audio-retention-sweep purges the recording. A single mis-click on the small trash icon silently discards a transcribed spec call.
userImpact: On a card with a tight trash icon next to the status badge, an accidental tap deletes the spec call and its audio with no undo prompt. The user only sees a 'Draft deleted.' toast after the fact.
evidence:
const onClick = () => { startTransition(async () => { const result = await deleteSpecDraftAction({ specDraftId: draftId }) ... }) }
// no confirm(); markSpecDraftRejected sets status='rejected', rejected_at, deleted_at = now()
suggestedFix: Add a window.confirm() (or an AlertDialog) before deleteSpecDraftAction, matching the Reject button's pattern in spec-review-form.tsx ('Reject this draft? The recording will be deleted in 30 days.').
verifierReasoning:
Every link in the claim is verified against the actual code:

1. spec-delete-button.tsx:21 — onClick immediately calls startTransition(async () => deleteSpecDraftAction({ specDraftId: draftId })) with NO window.confirm() guard. The trash icon is rendered at page.tsx:101 inside a flex row right next to the status Badge (small size-8 icon), exactly as described.

2. actions.ts:32 — deleteSpecDraftAction calls markSpecDraftRejected (after only a uuid zod check + auth check). It does revalidatePath('/spec') on success.

3. spec-drafts.ts:244-253 — markSpecDraftRejected sets status='rejected', rejected_at=now, deleted_at=now (single nowIso for both). This both hides the draft from the default list (listSpecDrafts filters .is('deleted_at', null)) and starts both retention clocks.

4. spec-review-form.tsx:95 — the in-review Reject button DOES guard with confirm('Reject this draft? The recording will be deleted in 30 days.'). So the inconsistency the claim alleges is real and confirmed.

5. spec-draft-cleanup-sweep.ts — nightly cron hard-deletes rows where deleted_at < now-30d. Triggered by the deleted_at set in step 3.

6. spec-audio-retention-sweep.ts — nightly cron removes the spec-audio Storage object where status in ('approved','rejected') AND status_changed_at < now-30d. markSpecDraftRejected sets status='rejected', and the spec_drafts_bump_status_changed_at trigger (migration 20260520003437) bumps status_changed_at, so the audio purge clock starts too.

7. Schema: migration 20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql defines audio_storage_path, status_changed_at, rejected_at, deleted_at — all columns the claim depends on exist. No phantom-column problem.

The action's own comment explicitly states it is 'distinct from the in-review Reject button (which keeps confirm())' — confirming the missing confirm was a deliberate but inconsistent design choice, which strengthens (not refutes) the inconsistency point.

Mitigating nuance (doesn't refute, just bounds severity): the delete is a SOFT delete with a 30-day window. The row and audio remain physically present for 30 days, so an engineer could recover via direct DB access. There is, however, no in-app undo, and the only user feedback is a post-hoc 'Draft deleted.' toast. For a live client demo, a mis-tap on the small trash icon silently removes a high-value AI-transcribed spec call from view. Medium is the correct grade — genuine destructive-action-without-confirmation UX gap, inconsistent with its sibling control, but recoverable within the soft-delete window rather than instant irreversible loss.

title: Approve shows full success ('Draft approved — creating job…') even though the actual jobs row can still fail to be created downstream (missing company set to failed, insert error) with no path back to the recruiter
description: Once approveSpecDraftAction returns ok:true (Inngest send succeeded), the form shows success and navigates to /jobs. But create-job-from-spec can still NonRetriably fail after that: e.g. the missing-company branch flips the row to status='failed' with parse_error, or insert-job errors. In those cases the recruiter has already been told it succeeded and navigated away; the only surfacing is the draft reverting to 'failed' back on /spec, which they are no longer looking at. The approve action pre-checks effectiveCompanyId so missing-company is unlikely from the UI, but any insert-job error (e.g. job_type/hiring_context enum drift, FK guard) produces a silent post-success failure.
userImpact: Recruiter is told the job was created and moves on; the job never appears and they have no notification. They discover it missing later, with no error to explain why.
evidence:
if (!draft.company_id) { ... update({ status: 'failed', parse_error: 'Pick a client before approving — jobs require a company.' }) ... throw new NonRetriableError('spec-draft:missing-company') }
... insert-job throws new Error(`insert-job: ${error.message}`)
suggestedFix: This is the cost of fire-and-forget create-on-approve. Mitigate by keeping the recruiter on a 'creating job…' state that polls the draft until created_job_id is set OR status flips to failed, then either links to the job or shows the parse_error. Don't declare success at send-time.
verifierReasoning:
The core mechanism is real and verified in code. The form (src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/spec-review-form.tsx:81-91) declares full success (toast.success('Draft approved — creating job…'), router.push('/jobs')) the instant approveSpecDraftAction returns ok:true. That ok:true is returned at actions.ts:159 immediately after inngest.send succeeds (line 128) — i.e. at send-time, NOT at job-creation-time. The real jobs INSERT happens later, asynchronously, inside create-job-from-spec.ts (step 'insert-job', lines 157-197). If that step throws after the user has navigated to /jobs, the recruiter has already been told it worked and is no longer looking at /spec, where the only surfacing lives (page.tsx:98-109 renders the failed badge + parse_error). There is no toast, notification, or polling back on /jobs. So the fire-and-forget create-on-approve genuinely can produce a silent post-success failure with no path back to the recruiter — claim confirmed in materially the form described.

However, the realistic trigger probability is much lower than 'medium' implies, which drags the live-demo impact down: (1) The missing-company branch (lines 136-151) is effectively dead from the UI: approveSpecDraftAction pre-checks effectiveCompanyId (actions.ts:86-93), the Approve button is disabled unless companyId is set (form line 326), and the <select> is required. (2) The insert-job enum-drift concern is not live: the function hardcodes 'perm'|'contract'|'temp', 'new_role'|'backfill', 'open' with ?? defaults, and these EXACTLY match the DB enums (migration 20260513152244 lines 36/38/40); the company_id FK is on delete restrict and pre-validated. So a real insert failure would be transient (DB connectivity), and the function has retries: 2, so most transient failures self-heal. (3) Only a hard, repeated insert failure surfaces the gap — and notably that path leaves the row at status='approved' with created_job_id null and NO parse_error (only the missing-company branch writes 'failed'+parse_error), so even returning to /spec shows 'Approved' with nothing wrong. onFailure only logs to Sentry. Net: a genuine missing-feedback gap exists, but it fires only on rare hard failures, loses no data (the draft is recoverable/re-approvable), and is unlikely to manifest in a live client demo. Honest grade: low.

title: Whisper always returns durationSeconds >= 1 (Math.max(1, ...)), so the pipeline's 'could not read the audio file' guard (durationSeconds <= 0) is dead code — a corrupt/empty decode falls through to the empty-transcript path instead
description: transcribe() computes durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0)), so it can never return <= 0. The Inngest pipeline checks `if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0) return { ..., durationSeconds: -1, ... }` then maps -1 to the user message 'Could not read the audio file. Please re-upload.' Because the source can never be <=0, this branch is unreachable. A genuinely unreadable/0-byte-after-recompress file will instead either error in recompressToOpus or produce an empty transcript and surface the generic 'Transcription returned no text. The recording may be silent.' message, which is misleading for a corrupt (not silent) file.
userImpact: When a recording is corrupt rather than silent, the recruiter is told it 'may be silent' and re-records good audio that still fails for the same reason, instead of being told the file couldn't be read.
evidence:
whisper.ts: const durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0))
transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts: if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0) { return { transcriptText: '', durationSeconds: -1, whisperCostPence: 0 } }
suggestedFix: Either drop the Math.max(1, ...) floor in whisper.ts (return the real rounded duration, including 0) so the <=0 guard can fire, or remove the now-dead -1 branch and improve the empty-transcript message to cover corrupt-decode cases.
verifierReasoning:
The mechanical core of the claim is verified true. At src/lib/ai/whisper.ts:154, `const durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0))` floors to 1, so transcribe() can never return durationSeconds <= 0. The sole caller, src/lib/inngest/functions/transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts (grep confirms no other callers), reads that value directly at line 196 (`if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0)`), so lines 197 (durationSeconds: -1) and the downstream block at lines 210-217 — including the user message 'Could not read the audio file. Please re-upload.' — are genuinely unreachable dead code. A stale docstring at whisper.ts:93 ('durationSeconds <= 0 → throws') compounds this; it describes pre-refactor ffprobe behavior that no longer exists.

However, the claim's stated USER IMPACT is mostly wrong. It asserts a corrupt file falls through to the 'may be silent' message. Tracing the real failure modes through src/lib/ai/ffmpeg.ts: (1) a 0-byte buffer throws at ffmpeg.ts:84; (2) undecodable/corrupt audio fires ffmpeg's 'error' event and rejects at ffmpeg.ts:106; (3) empty output throws at ffmpeg.ts:114. All three exceptions propagate to the outer try/catch in the Inngest function and route to markSpecFailed with FAILED_USER_MESSAGE ('Transcription failed. Try uploading again, or contact support.') — NOT the 'may be silent' message. The 'may be silent' branch (line 230) is only reached when ffmpeg fully succeeds AND Whisper decodes AND returns empty/whitespace text, i.e. an actually-silent (but valid) recording — for which the message is correct. The corrupt-file-mislabeled-as-silent scenario the claim describes requires ffmpeg to silently succeed on a corrupt file, which its explicit guards prevent.

So: dead code exists (real, cosmetic/maintainability + stale doc), but the functional/UX harm the claim hangs its severity on does not occur in practice. Severity stays low; for a live client demo this is invisible — no user-facing misbehavior.

title: MicRecorder can produce a 0-byte file (onstop with no chunks); the upload action rejects size===0 with a generic 'Choose an audio file' error that doesn't match the user's mental model (they did record)
description: rec.onstop builds a Blob from chunksRef.current unconditionally. If ondataavailable never fired with data>0 (very short recording, device glitch), chunks is empty and the resulting File is 0 bytes. The recorder transitions to 'preview' and calls onRecording(file) regardless of size, so the parent enables submit. On submit, submitSpecCallAction returns 'Choose an audio file before uploading.' (audioRaw.size === 0 branch) — confusing because the user just recorded and sees a preview player.
userImpact: A recruiter records (maybe a 1-second test), sees a 'Recording captured' preview, clicks upload, and is told to choose an audio file — contradicting what's on screen.
evidence:
rec.onstop = () => { ... const blob = new Blob(chunksRef.current, { type: baseType }) ... const file = new File([blob], ...) ... setState({ kind: 'preview', file, ... }); onRecording(file) }
// upload action: if (!(audioRaw instanceof File) || audioRaw.size === 0) return { ok:false, error:'Choose an audio file before uploading.' }
suggestedFix: In rec.onstop, if blob.size === 0 (or chunks empty), don't transition to preview — show an inline 'No audio captured, try again' and reset to idle. Optionally give the upload action a distinct 'That recording was empty' message for the recorded-File case.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is mechanically accurate and verified end-to-end across three files.

1) mic-recorder.tsx:74-75 — ondataavailable only pushes chunks when e.data.size > 0, so chunksRef.current can be empty if no non-empty data event ever fires (very short start→stop, muted/no-input mic, encoder that never flushed, or device glitch; the 60-min cap path at :122-125 also routes through stop()).

2) mic-recorder.tsx:81-88 — rec.onstop builds `new Blob(chunksRef.current, ...)` and the File UNCONDITIONALLY, then calls setState({kind:'preview', file, ...}) and onRecording(file) with NO size/empty-chunk guard. There is no `if (blob.size === 0)` check anywhere. The preview UI (:174 "Recording captured", :176 shows duration · "0 KB", :191 an <audio> player) renders even for a 0-byte recording.

3) spec-upload-form.tsx:47-50 sets the (possibly 0-byte) File into React state. The submit button at :109 uses disabled={!file || isPending}; a File object is always truthy regardless of byte length, so a 0-byte File does NOT disable submit. handleSubmit (:30) only checks `if (!file) return`, which a truthy 0-byte File passes.

4) actions.ts:84-85 — `if (!(audioRaw instanceof File) || audioRaw.size === 0) return { ok:false, error:'Choose an audio file before uploading.' }`. The 0-byte File passes instanceof but trips size===0, surfacing the file-picker-oriented message via toast (spec-upload-form.tsx:37). This contradicts the on-screen "Recording captured" preview the user just saw.

No guard, no minimum-duration check, and no alternate consumer short-circuits this anywhere in src/app/(app)/spec/new. The described user-facing contradiction is real.

Severity: empty-chunks-on-stop is a genuine but uncommon edge case (with no timeslice argument MediaRecorder normally emits at least one chunk at stop even for sub-second clips), and when it triggers the outcome is a confusing-but-recoverable error message — no data loss, crash, or tenant leak, and it does not impair a normal demo flow. 'low' is the correct grade, matching the original claim.

title: Whisper transcribes (and bills) the full recording BEFORE the >60-minute duration guard rejects it
description: The 60-minute cap is only enforced inside the MicRecorder client component (HARD_CAP_MS) and is a UI hint on the upload form. The server-side guard lives in the Inngest function AFTER the audio is already downloaded, recompressed with ffmpeg, and fully transcribed by Whisper. The duration check reads transcript.durationSeconds (which only exists because Whisper already decoded and billed the whole file): `if (transcript.durationSeconds > MAX_DURATION_SECONDS) { return { ... durationSeconds: -2 ... } }`. A user can upload any file up to the 100 MiB cap regardless of length — Whisper at ~0.48p/min means a 3-hour file is fully paid for before being thrown away. There is NO pre-transcription duration probe (the code comment explains ffprobe was removed because it is unreliable on Vercel, so duration is only known post-transcription). This is a direct per-tenant cost-abuse vector and the CLAUDE.md AI-cost-visibility constraint depends on cost being bounded.
userImpact: A recruiter (or a malicious user in a SaaS tenant) can upload arbitrarily long audio and run up the org's Whisper bill; the cost is incurred and logged to ai_usage even though the draft is then rejected for being over the limit. The owner cannot bound spend per spec call.
suggestedFix: Probe duration BEFORE the Whisper call. ffprobe against the /tmp file (not a stream) is reliable per ffmpeg.ts's own /tmp-file insight — run ffprobe on inPath inside recompressToOpus (or a sibling) and reject >MAX_DURATION_SECONDS before calling transcribe(). Alternatively cap the bitrate*duration via file-size heuristic, but a real duration probe on the written temp file is the correct fix and is already feasible given the /tmp-file pipeline.
verifierReasoning: The claim is accurate in every material respect. In src/lib/inngest/functions/transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts the process-audio step runs: download (L169) -> recompressToOpus/ffmpeg (L179) -> transcribe() which calls OpenAI Whisper (L188). The MAX_DURATION_SECONDS=3600 guard is at L199, strictly AFTER transcribe() returns. There is NO pre-transcription duration probe: the ffprobe step was deliberately removed (whisper.ts L78-82 and transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts L183-187 both explain ffprobe is unreliable on Vercel), so durationSeconds is only known from Whisper's verbose_json response after the full file is decoded and billed. Critically, inside whisper.ts the Whisper API call (L135) completes, then costPence is computed (L155) and record_ai_usage is written (L161-172) BEFORE the result is returned — so OpenAI has billed the full audio AND the cost is logged to ai_usage with the real duration, regardless of the over-limit guard. The over-limit path returns whisperCostPence:0 to the function (L200), which only zeroes the denormalised spec_drafts.whisper_cost_pence column; the canonical ai_usage row still carries the full charge. Server-side guards are size+mime only: actions.ts MAX_AUDIO_BYTES=100*1024*1024 (L31, L95) with a comment (L29) that 100 MiB explicitly 'covers ~3-hour outliers', and the mime allowlist — no duration check. The 60-minute cap (mic-recorder.tsx L13 HARD_CAP_MS) is a client-only UI cap on the in-browser recorder; a file-picker upload bypasses it entirely. The suggested fix (ffprobe on the written /tmp inPath inside recompressToOpus, which is the reliable seekable-file path per ffmpeg.ts's own insight) is technically sound and feasible. I down-grade from high to medium: the cost per abusive upload is bounded by the 100 MiB cap to roughly £0.85-£1.15 (≈3-4h at voice bitrate × 0.48p/min), the anchor customer is a trusted 2-3 person agency rather than adversarial, and it is neither a crash nor a data-leak for a live demo — but it is a genuine unbounded-per-call-spend gap that violates the CLAUDE.md AI-cost-visibility/boundedness constraint and matters for the multi-tenant SaaS future.

title: Spec-call transcript (contains names, salaries, client details) is retained indefinitely on approved drafts with no retention sweep
description: The 30-day retention sweep only deletes the Storage audio object and NULLs audio_storage_path; it never clears the spec_drafts.transcript column. The transcript is the full verbatim text of the call and routinely contains PII: hiring-manager names, candidate names mentioned in passing, salary figures, company internal detail. For APPROVED drafts the row is never hard-deleted (only rejected/soft-deleted rows are vacuumed by spec-draft-cleanup-sweep after 30 days). So an approved draft keeps its raw transcript forever even though the policy stated intent (D3-10) is to remove the recording after 30 days. CLAUDE.md mandates audit-ready PII handling and bounded retention. The audio is treated as PII-sensitive (deleted at 30d) but the transcript — which is the same content in text form — is not.
userImpact: GDPR / data-minimisation exposure: an approved spec call's verbatim transcript with named individuals and salaries lives in the DB indefinitely with no deletion path, contradicting the stated 30-day audio retention promise. In a SaaS context this is a data-portability and compliance liability the buyer will scrutinise.
evidence:
.select('id, organization_id, audio_storage_path')
.in('status', ['approved', 'rejected'])
// ...
.update({ audio_storage_path: null })  // transcript column never touched
suggestedFix: In the retention sweep, also NULL the transcript column (and optionally structured_data) when removing the audio for rows past the 30-day window, OR document a deliberate decision to retain transcripts and add it to the audit/retention policy. At minimum, transcripts on approved drafts need a defined retention boundary, not unbounded persistence.
verifierReasoning:
Attempted to refute but the code confirms every load-bearing element of the claim.

1) spec-audio-retention-sweep.ts: the select at line 72 only fetches `id, organization_id, audio_storage_path`; the only mutation (line 113) is `update({ audio_storage_path: null })`. The transcript column is never read or written by this sweep. The file's own header comments (lines 10, 27) state its purpose is solely deleting the Storage audio object and nulling the path.

2) The `transcript` column exists and holds verbatim PII. Migration 20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql:66 defines `transcript text check(...)` (50k char cap, D3-11). transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts:267 writes the full Whisper transcript into it (`transcript: transcriptText`), and review/page.tsx:131 renders it verbatim. A spec-call transcript routinely contains hiring-manager names, salary figures, and incidentally-mentioned candidate names.

3) Approved drafts are never hard-deleted. markSpecDraftApproved (spec-drafts.ts:214-238) sets status='approved' + approved_at but does NOT set deleted_at. The cleanup sweep (spec-draft-cleanup-sweep.ts:62-64) only `.delete()`s rows where deleted_at is non-null and older than 30 days. Only markSpecDraftRejected (line 244) sets deleted_at. So an approved draft's row — and its transcript — persists indefinitely.

4) Repo-wide grep found zero code anywhere (no migration trigger, no DB helper, no action, no Inngest function) that ever NULLs or clears the transcript column.

Net effect is exactly as described: at 30 days an approved draft loses its audio file (audio_storage_path nulled) but retains the verbatim transcript — the same PII content in text form — forever. This contradicts the stated D3-10 30-day recording-retention intent and the CLAUDE.md 'audit-ready / bounded retention' mandate. The category (pii-logging / data-retention) is correct.

Severity: medium is appropriate. It is a genuine GDPR data-minimisation gap and a SaaS-portability liability a buyer would scrutinise. However it is a background-cron / retention-policy gap with zero user-facing or demo-time impact — nothing breaks in a live client demo — so it does not rise above medium. The suggested fix (also NULL transcript, and optionally structured_data, when the audio is removed past the 30-day window, OR explicitly document a transcript retention decision) is sound and minimally invasive.

title: getSpecDraft has no deleted_at filter — soft-deleted (rejected) drafts remain directly fetchable by URL
description: listSpecDrafts excludes soft-deleted rows by default (`if (!args.includeDeleted) query.is('deleted_at', null)`), but getSpecDraft does not apply the same filter. The status poller page (/spec/[id]) and review page (/spec/[id]/review) both call getSpecDraft with no deleted_at guard, so a rejected/soft-deleted draft is still rendered if the user navigates directly to its URL (e.g. browser back button after rejecting, or a bookmarked link). The status page only special-cases pending/transcribing/failed/approved/ready_for_review; a status='rejected' row falls through to a bare 'This draft was rejected' card, and the review page would still render the editable form for a rejected draft, letting the user re-approve a draft they just deleted. This is same-tenant only (RLS still blocks cross-org), so not a leak — but it is a stale-state UX trap and lets a 'deleted' draft be resurrected via Approve.
userImpact: A recruiter who rejects a draft, then hits back, can land on the review page for the deleted draft and click Approve, re-creating a job from a draft they intended to discard. The 'deleted' state is not honoured on the detail/review routes.
evidence:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('spec_drafts')
  .select('*')
  .eq('id', id)
  .maybeSingle()  // no .is('deleted_at', null)
suggestedFix: Add an optional `includeDeleted` arg to getSpecDraft defaulting to false and apply `.is('deleted_at', null)`, returning not_found for soft-deleted rows on the poller/review pages; or have the review page redirect away when draft.status === 'rejected' || draft.deleted_at.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the full path; the claim holds and the resurrection-via-Approve consequence is real, not just a stale-UX trap.

1) getSpecDraft (src/lib/db/spec-drafts.ts:70-85) does a bare `.eq('id', id).maybeSingle()` with NO `.is('deleted_at', null)`, while listSpecDrafts (line 52-54) excludes soft-deleted rows by default. Confirmed verbatim.

2) Schema (supabase/migrations/20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql:55-106): spec_drafts has `deleted_at timestamptz` and `rejected_at timestamptz`. The RLS "tenant select" policy is purely org-scoped (`organization_id = current_organization_id()`) — no deleted_at filter — so a soft-deleted row IS still selectable by the same tenant via direct id lookup. RLS still blocks cross-org, so this is NOT a leak (claim agrees).

3) markSpecDraftRejected (spec-drafts.ts:244-267) soft-deletes: sets status='rejected', rejected_at=now, deleted_at=now. The list page (/spec, page.tsx:56) calls listSpecDrafts with no includeDeleted, so rejected drafts vanish from the list — the only way back to the URL is browser-back or a bookmark, exactly as claimed.

4) Status poller (/spec/[id]/page.tsx): special-cases pending/transcribing/failed and redirects ready_for_review/approved; a 'rejected' row falls through to a passive "This draft was rejected." card (lines 123-125). Stale-state but harmless on its own.

5) Review page (/spec/[id]/review/page.tsx): only redirects pending/transcribing (lines 44-47). A 'rejected' draft renders the full editable SpecReviewForm with an "Approve & create job" button. No deleted_at / rejected check.

6) RESURRECTION CONFIRMED end-to-end. approveSpecDraftAction (review/actions.ts:62-160) never checks draft.status or draft.deleted_at — it re-reads, persists company/structured data, calls markSpecDraftApproved, and fires the Inngest event. markSpecDraftApproved (spec-drafts.ts:214-238) sets status='approved' but does NOT clear deleted_at. createJobFromSpec (create-job-from-spec.ts) only guards on `status !== 'approved'` (line 117) and `created_job_id` (line 124) — there is NO deleted_at guard. So a rejected draft (status='rejected', deleted_at set, created_job_id=null) → re-approve flips status to 'approved' (deleted_at still set, created_job_id still null) → Inngest passes both guards → inserts a real job (line 188). The "discarded" spec call becomes a live job. Worse, the draft is now in a contradictory approved+deleted_at state, and the 30-day spec-draft-cleanup-sweep (filters on deleted_at, not status) would later hard-delete the draft row out from under the live job it spawned.

Severity assessment for a live client demo: correctly graded low. It is same-tenant-only (no security/tenant-leak dimension), requires a specific multi-step user action (reject → navigate back to a cached/bookmarked URL → click Approve), and the rejected draft is not surfaced in any normal navigation (list page hides it). But it is a genuine data-integrity bug, not merely cosmetic: it lets a discarded draft create a job and leaves an inconsistent row the retention sweep will later orphan. The suggested fix (add deleted_at guard to getSpecDraft and/or redirect when draft.status==='rejected' || draft.deleted_at on the poller/review routes, and ideally a status/deleted_at guard in the approve action and Inngest function) is appropriate.

title: Audio object is left orphaned in Storage when inngest.send fails (no rollback), and stays until the 30-day sweep
description: The action removes the orphaned Storage object only when updateSpecDraftAudioPath fails (line 163). When inngest.send fails, it marks the draft status='failed' and returns an error but does NOT remove the uploaded audio object. Because the retention sweep anchors on status_changed_at and status in ('approved','rejected') — NOT 'failed' — a failed draft's audio is never picked up by spec-audio-retention-sweep (the .in('status', ['approved','rejected']) filter excludes 'failed'). So the audio for an upload that failed at the Inngest dispatch step persists in Storage indefinitely, accruing storage cost and retaining PII audio past any retention window.
userImpact: Audio files from failed uploads accumulate in the spec-audio bucket forever (never swept, never rolled back), costing storage and keeping recorded PII audio beyond the intended retention boundary. Invisible to the owner.
suggestedFix: On inngest.send failure, remove the uploaded object (await supabase.storage.from('spec-audio').remove([storagePath])) before returning, mirroring the updateSpecDraftAudioPath rollback. Additionally include 'failed' rows in the audio retention sweep so orphaned audio from any failure path is eventually reclaimed.
verifierReasoning:
Verified in code. In src/app/(app)/spec/new/actions.ts the inngest.send catch block (lines 180-199) sets status='failed' and returns an error but does NOT call supabase.storage.from('spec-audio').remove([storagePath]) — unlike the updateSpecDraftAudioPath failure branch at line 163 which DOES roll back. At this point the audio was already uploaded successfully (lines 137-139) and audio_storage_path was recorded (updateSpecDraftAudioPath succeeded at lines 155-159), so the row carries a non-NULL audio_storage_path with status='failed'.

No reclamation path ever picks this up:
- spec-audio-retention-sweep.ts line 73 filters .in('status', ['approved','rejected']) — 'failed' is excluded, confirmed against the spec_draft_status enum in migration 20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql lines 46-53 which lists 'failed' as a distinct value.
- spec-draft-cleanup-sweep.ts only acts on rows with deleted_at set (line 63-64) and only deletes the DB row, never the Storage object. A 'failed' draft has no deleted_at (deleted_at is the soft-delete/rejection anchor per the migration comment lines 74-77), so it is never matched, and even if it were, the audio file would not be removed.

So the Storage object persists indefinitely. The claim's facts all check out.

Notably the orphaning is BROADER than the claim states: transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts marks status='failed' via markSpecFailed (lines 62-85) and its onFailure handler (lines 96-116) on the realistic pipeline failures (no-duration, over-60-min, empty/silent transcript, generic transcription failure) without ever removing the audio either. Those paths are far more likely to fire than an inngest.send dispatch failure, so failed-draft audio orphaning is a recurring real condition, not a rare one.

Severity remains low: zero functional/UI breakage, storage cost negligible at anchor volume (~120 spec calls/year), and invisible to operators. The legitimate concern is PII (recorded audio) retained past the intended 30-day boundary — which matters given the project's explicit 'audit-ready by default' / retention-window principle, but it is a minor compliance hygiene issue, not a demo-blocking or data-leak bug. The suggested fix is sound: remove the object on the inngest.send failure path (mirroring line 163) and/or include 'failed' rows in the retention sweep so all failure paths are eventually reclaimed.

title: MicRecorder can emit a 0-byte recording on stop-with-no-chunks, producing a confusing client-side dead end
description: rec.onstop builds the File from chunksRef.current regardless of whether any data chunks arrived. If the user grants the mic, hits Stop almost immediately (or the device produces no data), chunksRef is empty and the resulting Blob/File is 0 bytes. The preview UI still shows 'Recording captured' with '0 KB', and onRecording(file) hands the empty file to the parent, which enables the Upload button (disabled={!file}). submitSpecCallAction rejects size===0 with 'Choose an audio file before uploading.' — a confusing message for someone who just recorded — but the UX is the dead-end the surface inventory already flagged. There is no client-side guard against the empty-chunks case.
userImpact: A recruiter who records and stops too quickly sees a 'Recording captured' preview, clicks Upload, and gets a misleading 'Choose an audio file' error with no explanation that the recording was empty — they cannot tell what went wrong.
suggestedFix: In rec.onstop, if blob.size === 0 (or chunksRef.current.length === 0), do not transition to 'preview' — set an inline error state ('Recording was empty, please try again') and call onRecording(null) so the Upload button stays disabled.
verifierReasoning: Verified the full chain and the claim holds mechanically. In src/app/(app)/spec/new/mic-recorder.tsx, ondataavailable (lines 74-75) only pushes chunks when e.data.size > 0, so an empty recording leaves chunksRef.current = []. rec.onstop (lines 77-89) then builds the Blob/File unconditionally with NO guard on chunksRef.current.length or blob.size, transitions to the 'preview' state, and calls onRecording(file) with a 0-byte File. The preview UI (lines 169-193) renders "Recording captured" and "(state.file.size / 1024).toFixed(0)" -> "0 KB". In spec-upload-form.tsx, onRecordingChange (lines 47-49) does setFile(next) with the empty (truthy, non-null) File, and the submit button is disabled={!file || isPending} (line 109) — a non-null empty File is truthy, so the button IS enabled. submitSpecCallAction in actions.ts (lines 84-85) rejects audioRaw.size === 0 with "Choose an audio file before uploading." — the misleading message the recruiter sees after having just recorded. So the dead-end and confusing error are real, and there is no client-side guard for the empty-chunks case. On real-world likelihood: rec.start() is called without a timeslice argument (line 91), so MediaRecorder buffers and fires a single dataavailable on stop; even a ~1s real recording yields a non-empty blob. The genuinely-empty case requires a near-synchronous stop before any media is captured, or a broken/muted device — a narrow degenerate path. The component already handles unsupported/denied states well; this is the residual edge where the preview lies about a 0-byte capture. Impact is a confusing-but-recoverable one-off (the user can re-record); no data loss, no security/tenancy issue. Matches the reporter's own low/edge-case grading. The suggested fix (guard blob.size === 0 in onstop: set an inline error and call onRecording(null) so Upload stays disabled) is correct and small.

title: Per-card delete button on the spec list is a one-click destructive action with no confirmation
description: The trash-icon button on each card in the spec list calls deleteSpecDraftAction immediately on click with no confirm() / dialog guard. deleteSpecDraftAction -> markSpecDraftRejected sets status='rejected' AND deleted_at=now(), which (a) removes the draft from the list and (b) starts the 30-day hard-delete clock. By contrast the Reject button inside the review form (spec-review-form.tsx line 95) DOES use window.confirm(). So the same destructive soft-delete is gated in one place and one-click in another. In a live demo, a mis-click on the small ghost trash icon silently destroys a transcribed/structured draft (the recruiter's reviewed work) with only a success toast.
userImpact: Recruiter accidentally taps the trash icon next to a draft they wanted to keep; the draft vanishes from the list instantly with a 'Draft deleted.' toast and no undo affordance. The recording and parsed JD are scheduled for permanent deletion.
suggestedFix: Add a confirmation step before calling deleteSpecDraftAction — either a window.confirm() mirroring the review form's reject ('Delete this draft? The recording will be deleted in 30 days.') or a shadcn AlertDialog. The project rule requires destructive actions to have a confirmation step.
verifierReasoning: Claim verified in full against the code. spec-delete-button.tsx:21 — onClick calls deleteSpecDraftAction({specDraftId}) immediately inside startTransition with no confirm()/dialog; on success shows toast.success('Draft deleted.') and router.refresh(), no undo. page.tsx:101 renders this as a per-card ghost trash icon (size-8, text-muted-foreground hover:text-destructive) beside the status badge for every draft. actions.ts:32 routes deleteSpecDraftAction -> markSpecDraftRejected; the action file's own comment (lines 9-12) explicitly notes it is 'distinct from the in-review Reject button (which keeps confirm())', confirming the asymmetry is known. spec-drafts.ts:244-267 markSpecDraftRejected sets status='rejected', rejected_at=now(), AND deleted_at=now(); listSpecDrafts filters deleted_at is null so the row vanishes. spec-draft-cleanup-sweep.ts:54-66 hard-deletes rows with deleted_at older than 30 days (nightly cron), so the one-click does schedule permanent destruction. spec-review-form.tsx:95 DOES gate the same soft-delete path behind confirm('Reject this draft? The recording will be deleted in 30 days.'). Schema columns status/rejected_at/deleted_at all exist (migration 20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql). Severity adjusted from high to medium: the action has correct error handling (returns ok:false, error toast, no false-success on 4xx) and there is a 30-day recovery window, but recovery is entirely invisible — rejected/deleted rows are hidden from the default list and no page passes includeDeleted, so there is no surfaced restore/undo UI. Combined with the user's global rule that destructive actions require confirmation and the demonstrated inconsistency with the sibling Reject button, this is a genuine missing-confirmation issue that can bite a non-coding operator in a live demo, but the soft-delete + intact error handling keep it below high.

title: Approving a draft silently wipes the AI 'verify with client' ambiguities and confidence flags
description: updateSpecDraftStructuredData overwrites the entire structured_data JSON with only the fields in approveSchema. That schema (lines 38-52) has no confidence_per_field and no ambiguities keys, and handleApprove (spec-review-form.tsx lines 65-79) does not send them. So on approve, structured_data is replaced and the AI-generated ambiguities array + confidence_per_field map are lost. Approved drafts remain reachable at /spec/[id]/review (the list links ready_for_review AND approved drafts there — page.tsx line 88), and that page renders the 'Verify with client' card from structured.ambiguities (review/page.tsx lines 57, 110-122). After approval the card disappears because the data was overwritten.
userImpact: A recruiter approves a draft, then re-opens it from the list to check the 'things to verify with the client' checklist before the client call — but it is now blank, because approval erased it. The low-confidence 'verify this' badges also vanish.
suggestedFix: Merge the edited fields over the existing structured_data instead of replacing it (read draftResult.data.structured_data and spread it under the edited fields), or carry confidence_per_field/ambiguities through approveSchema so they survive the write.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the full data chain — the claim holds mechanically.

1. The AI DOES produce both keys into structured_data. src/lib/ai/jd-extract.ts lines 90-104 define confidence_per_field and ambiguities in the tool schema; both are listed in `required` (line 105) and returned by extractJdFromTranscript (lines 207-208). transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts line 268 persists the entire jdDraft object (including both keys) into spec_drafts.structured_data at status='ready_for_review'.

2. The review page renders them. review/page.tsx line 57 reads structured.ambiguities and lines 110-122 render the "Verify with client" card; the form drives "verify this" badges off confidence_per_field (spec-review-form.tsx lines 39-46, 54, 102, 123, 164, 215).

3. Approve overwrites the whole blob with a 13-field subset. handleApprove (spec-review-form.tsx lines 65-79) constructs structuredData containing only the editable fields — neither ambiguities nor confidence_per_field is included. approveSchema.structuredData = structuredJdSchema (actions.ts lines 38-59) likewise has neither key (and Zod strips unknowns by default), so even if sent they'd be dropped. updateSpecDraftStructuredData (spec-drafts.ts lines 187-207) does `update({ structured_data: args.structuredData })` — a full replace, not a merge. Both AI keys are therefore erased on approve.

4. Approved drafts stay reachable at /spec/[id]/review. spec/page.tsx lines 88-90 link both ready_for_review AND approved to the review page, and review/page.tsx only redirects pending/transcribing (lines 45-47), not approved. So re-opening an approved draft shows the card/badges gone.

5. Nothing restores the data: create-job-from-spec reads structured_data but only consumes the 13 core fields and never re-writes structured_data (lines 130-209), so the lost AI metadata is not recoverable.

The suggested fix (merge edited fields over the existing structured_data, or carry the two keys through approveSchema) is correct.

Severity adjustment: this is a genuine null-trap/data-loss bug but the impact is bounded. The ambiguities checklist and low-confidence badges are present and correct during the review-before-approve step — which is the moment they actually matter and the path a demo would exercise. The loss only surfaces on the secondary, less-common path of re-opening an ALREADY-approved draft to re-consult the verify list. No crash, no corruption of the core JD fields, no tenancy issue. For a live client demo, practical impact is low.

title: Approve navigates to /jobs and relies on a 2s setTimeout refresh that loses the race on cold start
description: After a successful approve, the form pushes to /jobs then schedules router.refresh() after a hard-coded 2000ms. The actual jobs row is inserted asynchronously by the create-job-from-spec Inngest function (event spec-draft/approved). On an Inngest cold start or any queueing delay this takes well over 2 seconds, so the single refresh fires before the row exists and the recruiter lands on a /jobs list that does NOT contain the job they just created — with a 'Draft approved — creating job…' toast implying it is there. There is no further polling or 'still creating' indicator.
userImpact: Recruiter clicks 'Approve & create job', is taken to the jobs list, sees their new job is missing, and assumes the feature failed — likely during the live demo where Inngest has been cold.
suggestedFix: Instead of a fixed timeout, navigate to the new job once created_job_id is populated (poll the draft, or have the action await job creation if fast enough), or land on the spec draft's status view that shows 'creating job…' until created_job_id is set, then deep-link to /jobs/[id]. At minimum poll a few times rather than a single 2s refresh.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed in code. spec-review-form.tsx:86-90 pushes to /jobs then fires a SINGLE router.refresh() at a hard-coded 2000ms. The server action (actions.ts:127-135) only awaits inngest.send() — it queues the spec-draft/approved event and returns; it does NOT await job creation. The jobs row is inserted asynchronously by create-job-from-spec.ts (read-draft step -> insert-job step -> patch-draft-job-id), which runs in a separate Inngest invocation reached via a remote dispatch + serverless callback. jobs/page.tsx is a dynamic Server Component (cookie-based Supabase auth forces dynamic render; no use-cache/unstable_cache), so router.refresh() WILL show the job once it exists — meaning the defect is purely a timing race, not a stale-cache problem. There is NO compensating mechanism: grep confirms no realtime subscription on jobs, no polling of the jobs list, no repeated refresh, and no 'still creating' indicator. The toast 'Draft approved — creating job…' implies presence with no follow-up. On an Inngest cold start or queue delay (the cited live-demo scenario), the round-trip to even begin insert-job routinely exceeds 2s, so the single refresh loses the race and the recruiter lands on a /jobs list missing the new job until a manual reload. Notably the codebase already uses a status-poller pattern for the same draft lifecycle (review/page.tsx:44-47 bounces pending/transcribing drafts to a /spec/[id] poller) and create-job-from-spec.ts:203 patches spec_drafts.created_job_id when the job exists — so the suggested fix (poll created_job_id then deep-link to /jobs/[id]) is both feasible and idiomatic. Tempering the severity: the job IS created correctly and idempotently (no data loss), and the condition self-heals on any reload, so this is a UX/perception defect rather than a correctness bug. The claim's category tag 'ai-sync-blocking' is a mild misnomer (nothing blocks synchronously; it is an async-completion race), but the substantive defect described is accurate.

title: Review page is a dead-end when the tenant has no clients yet — approve is permanently disabled with no way to add a client
description: Client selection is required to approve (button disabled when !companyId, line 326; the select is required). When the tenant has zero companies the form shows the helper text 'No clients yet — add one in Clients first, then return here.' but provides no link to the Clients page and no way to create a client inline. The recruiter is stranded on the review screen: they can edit fields and read the transcript but cannot complete the only forward action.
userImpact: A brand-new agency (the exact anchor/demo scenario) records and transcribes their first spec call, reaches review, and finds the only enabled button is 'Reject draft'. They must manually discover the Clients section, create a client, and navigate back — no guidance link is offered.
suggestedFix: Turn the helper text into an actionable link to /clients/new (or render a secondary 'Add a client' button), and/or open the new-client flow in a dialog so the recruiter can add one without losing the review form state.
verifierReasoning: Verified directly against the code. spec-review-form.tsx line 326: the approve submit button is disabled={busy || !form.title.trim() || !companyId}. companyId starts as initialCompanyId ?? '' (line 51) and is only ever set by the <select> (line 141). When the tenant has zero companies, the select renders only the empty placeholder option (line 146), so companyId is permanently '' (falsy) and the approve button is permanently disabled. The empty-state helper (lines 153-157) is plain text with no Link or inline-create. The review page header (page.tsx) offers only a '← All spec calls' link, no link to Clients. This is not merely a cosmetic client-side block: approveSpecDraftAction (actions.ts) also hard-rejects a null company with 'Pick a client below before approving — jobs need to be linked to a company,' so a client genuinely is required. Net effect: a tenant that reaches the review screen with no clients can only Reject; the only forward action is impossible and the page gives no navigation affordance. A /clients/new route does exist (src/app/(app)/clients/new/), so the suggested fix (link or inline dialog) is feasible. The form state is client-side, so navigating away to create a client and returning also discards the recruiter's edits. The issue is real and matches the claim in every detail. It is bounded (recoverable by self-navigating to Clients, no data corruption or security impact, and exact trigger requires recording a spec call before any client exists), so medium — not higher — is the honest grade; the brand-new-agency demo scenario keeps it at medium rather than low.

title: Spec list silently shows the 'first spec call' empty state when the DB query errors
description: listSpecDrafts returns a DbResult discriminated union. The page does `const drafts = result.ok ? result.data : []`, so any internal error (RLS hiccup, connection failure) collapses to an empty array, which then renders the EmptyState 'Record your first spec call'. There is no error UI and no rethrow to the (app)/error.tsx boundary. A user who has 20 spec drafts but hits a transient error is told they have none.
userImpact: If the spec_drafts query fails, the recruiter sees 'Record your first spec call' as if all their drafts disappeared, with no error message — alarming and misleading mid-demo.
evidence:
const result = await listSpecDrafts(supabase, { limit: 50 })
const drafts = result.ok ? result.data : []
suggestedFix: On !result.ok, throw (let (app)/error.tsx handle it) or render a distinct inline error state ('Couldn't load your spec calls — refresh to try again') rather than the empty state, so a fetch failure is never mistaken for 'no data'.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed at the code level. src/app/(app)/spec/page.tsx:57 does `const drafts = result.ok ? result.data : []`. listSpecDrafts (src/lib/db/spec-drafts.ts:59-64) returns { ok: false, code: 'internal' } on any Postgres/RLS/connection error. That collapses to [], and line 73's `drafts.length === 0` then renders the EmptyState 'Record your first spec call' — the exact onboarding prompt a brand-new tenant sees. There is no distinct error UI and no rethrow to the (app)/error.tsx boundary (which does exist). The failure is logged (Sentry.captureException in the helper) but invisible to the recruiter.

The claim is also reinforced by an in-codebase convention this page violates: for a page's PRIMARY list query, two sibling pages branch on !ok with a real error state — pipeline/page.tsx:42-51 ('Couldn\'t load the pipeline. Please refresh.') and jobs/[id]/matches/page.tsx:81,132-135 ('Couldn\'t load matches. Please refresh.', distinct from the empty state). Those same pages deliberately collapse only their AUXILIARY queries (e.g. pipeline:60 jobs dropdown) to []. The spec list IS its primary query, so it should follow the pipeline/matches pattern. The reviewer's suggested fix (throw to boundary or render a distinct inline error) is directly supported by the existing error.tsx and the matches-page precedent.

Downgraded from medium to low because: it triggers only on an actual DB/RLS/connection error (not a normal-path event), it is fully captured to Sentry, it is non-destructive (data intact, recoverable by refresh), and a few other pages (floats:18, reports/source-attribution:100) use the same collapse-to-[] pattern, so it is not a clear-cut universal violation. Still a real, worth-fixing silent-fail per the project's own conventions.

title: Status page uses a meta-refresh poller; on the failed-without-JD path the page bounces but offers only 'Upload another' — losing the recording reference
description: Polling is implemented as <meta http-equiv='refresh' content='4'> which forces a full document reload every 4s while pending/transcribing. This is functional but causes a visible full-page white flash every 4s (no skeleton, no in-place update) during the 30-60s transcription window — jarring in a demo. Separately, when status='failed' with no parsed JD the only CTA is 'Upload another recording' (/spec/new); the failed draft is not auto-deleted, so it lingers in the list as a 'Failed' card the recruiter must manually trash.
userImpact: While the recruiter waits for transcription the page hard-reloads and flashes every 4 seconds. On a hard failure they are sent to re-upload and additionally left with a stale 'Failed' card cluttering the list.
suggestedFix: Replace the meta-refresh with a small client poller (useEffect + router.refresh on an interval, or revalidate on an interval) so the page updates in place without a full flash; on the failed path consider linking back to /spec rather than only /spec/new.
verifierReasoning:
Both halves of the claim are confirmed in the actual code.

(1) Meta-refresh full-page flash — CONFIRMED. `src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/page.tsx:57-59` renders `<meta httpEquiv="refresh" content="4" />` whenever `status === 'pending' || status === 'transcribing'`. Combined with `dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` and `revalidate = 0` (lines 21-22), this forces a full HTTP document reload every 4 seconds with no skeleton, no in-place hydration update — a real full-navigation white flash. The page itself documents the transcription window as 30-60s (line 84), so the recruiter watches ~8-15 hard reloads while waiting. This is functional but visibly jarring in a live demo, exactly as described.

(2) Failed-without-JD path — CONFIRMED. When `status === 'failed'` and there is no parsed JD (`hasParsedJd` false, lines 47-50), the only action button is `<Link href="/spec/new">Upload another recording</Link>` (line 118); there is no link back to /spec in the failed body.
