The failed draft is NOT auto-deleted: `markSpecFailed` in transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts:62-85 only sets `status:'failed'` + `parse_error`, never `deleted_at`. The cleanup sweep (spec-draft-cleanup-sweep.ts) only hard-deletes rows where `deleted_at` is non-null (i.e. rejected drafts), so a failed draft is never swept. `listSpecDrafts` (spec-drafts.ts:33-65) filters only `deleted_at IS NULL` and does not exclude `failed`, so the failed draft renders on the /spec list with a destructive 'Failed' badge (statusVariant line 34, statusLabel line 25). The recruiter must clear it manually via the SpecDeleteButton trash icon.

Two minor softeners that do not refute the claim: the status page header DOES include a secondary '← All spec calls' link to /spec (line 67), so the recruiter is not fully stranded on the failed path — only the primary CTA points at /spec/new. And the list does expose a manual delete trash button per card (the claim itself acknowledges 'must manually trash'). Neither contradicts the substance: the flash is real and the failed card does linger until manually deleted.

Severity: low is correct. Nothing breaks, no data loss, no security/tenancy issue. The impact is purely cosmetic/UX (flicker during waiting; a stale Failed card needing one click to remove). For a live client demo the 4s full-page flash during the 30-60s transcription wait is the more noticeable of the two and is worth fixing per the suggested client-side poller, but it does not rise above low.

title: create-job-from-spec idempotency is read-then-check, not atomic — a double-delivered event before patch can insert two jobs
description: The function guards duplicate job creation with `if (draft.created_job_id) return skipped` (read in the read-draft step) and only writes created_job_id in a later step (patch-draft-job-id, line 199). The insert itself has no `where created_job_id is null` guard. If spec-draft/approved is delivered twice (Inngest at-least-once, or the recruiter re-clicks Approve after the 'queued' error path in actions.ts line 150) and both runs read the draft before either patches created_job_id, both will pass the guard and insert a separate jobs row. The approve action's own comment (actions.ts line 149) claims idempotency 'via a where created_job_id is null write', but that conditional write does not exist in this function.
userImpact: Rare, but a retried/re-clicked approval could create two duplicate jobs for one spec call, which the recruiter then has to spot and delete manually.
suggestedFix: Make the claim true: gate the spec_drafts patch with `.is('created_job_id', null)` and check the affected-row count, or use an Inngest idempotency key keyed on spec_draft_id so duplicate event deliveries collapse to one run.
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code. In /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/inngest/functions/create-job-from-spec.ts the duplicate guard is a read-then-check, not atomic: the read-draft step (lines 95-115) loads created_job_id, the guard `if (draft.created_job_id) return { skipped: 'already-created' }` is at line 124, the jobs insert at lines 188-190 is unconditional (plain .insert(payload), no precondition), and created_job_id is only written later in the patch-draft-job-id step (lines 199-209) which filters on .eq('id', ...).eq('organization_id', ...) with NO .is('created_job_id', null) guard. So nothing serialises two concurrent runs. The approve action comment at /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/actions.ts lines 148-149 explicitly claims idempotency 'via a `where created_job_id is null` write' — grepping the whole codebase, that conditional write does not exist; the claim's quote of the comment is exact. No backstop exists: the Inngest function config (lines 69-91) has no idempotency/singleton/debounce key (grep confirmed), concurrency is { limit: 5, key: organization_id } (allows overlapping same-event runs), and there is no DB unique constraint on jobs tied to a spec draft (only applications has a unique constraint per migration 20260513152244 / 20260520010419; created_job_id in migration 20260520003437 line 73 is a plain nullable FK). Both trigger paths are real: Inngest at-least-once redelivery, and the re-click path in actions.ts (draft is already status='approved' at line 118 before the send at line 128; on a send error the action returns 'Try again' at line 150-153, and a retry re-fires spec-draft/approved). If two deliveries both execute read-draft before either completes patch-draft-job-id, both pass the guard and insert separate jobs rows. The race window spans a synchronous insert so it is narrow but genuine. Impact: a rare duplicate jobs row the recruiter must manually delete — recoverable, no data loss, no cross-tenant leak. Low severity is correct.

title: MicRecorder can produce a 0-byte recording (no chunks) that looks valid in the preview but fails on upload
description: rec.onstop builds the File from chunksRef regardless of whether any data arrived. If MediaRecorder fires onstop with no chunks (very short tap of record→stop, or a device that never emits ondataavailable), the result is a 0-byte Blob -> 0-byte File. The preview UI shows 'Recording captured' with '0 KB' and a playable (silent) audio element, and the parent's submit button becomes enabled because file is non-null. submitSpecCallAction rejects size===0 with a toast, so no bad data is persisted — but the user experiences record→looks-fine→upload→rejected, with no guidance that the recording was empty.
userImpact: If a recording captures no audio, the recorder still presents a 'Recording captured' card; the recruiter clicks Upload and gets a generic 'Choose an audio file before uploading' error that does not match what they see on screen.
suggestedFix: In onstop, if blob.size === 0 (or below a small floor), transition to an error/idle state with a 'No audio captured — try recording again' message instead of going to 'preview', so the empty case is caught client-side before upload.
verifierReasoning: Every link in the claim is verified in the actual code. In src/app/(app)/spec/new/mic-recorder.tsx, ondataavailable (lines 74-75) only pushes chunks with size > 0, and rec.onstop (lines 77-89) builds the Blob/File from chunksRef.current unconditionally with no size guard, then unconditionally calls setState({kind:'preview', file, ...}) (line 86) and onRecording(file) (line 87). If no dataavailable event with data ever fires (instant record→stop tap, or a device that emits nothing), chunksRef is [] → a 0-byte Blob → 0-byte File. The preview UI (lines 169-193) renders 'Recording captured', shows (state.file.size/1024).toFixed(0) KB → '0 KB' (line 176), and a playable <audio> element. In spec-upload-form.tsx, onRecordingChange sets file to the non-null (but 0-byte) File (line 49); the submit button is disabled={!file || isPending} (line 109), and a non-null File is truthy → button enables. On submit, submitSpecCallAction (actions.ts:84-86) rejects with size===0 → returns error 'Choose an audio file before uploading.', surfaced via toast.error (spec-upload-form.tsx:37). So: no bad data is persisted (server guard fires), but the user sees record→'Recording captured'→Upload→generic 'choose a file' toast that contradicts the on-screen card. The claim is accurate in every detail including the mismatched messaging and the no-persistence safety net.

title: Free-text currency from spec call can crash the job detail page (Intl RangeError)
description: The spec review form collects currency as free text (Input, placeholder 'GBP') validated only as z.string().trim().max(8) in src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/actions.ts:45. create-job-from-spec writes that value verbatim to jobs.currency (create-job-from-spec.ts:184 `currency: jd.currency ?? 'GBP'`). The job detail header then formats salary via `new Intl.NumberFormat('en-GB', { style: 'currency', currency: currency || 'GBP' })`. Intl.NumberFormat throws a RangeError for any string that is not a valid ISO-4217 code (verified: currency 'pounds' or '£' throws 'RangeError: Invalid currency code'). Sonnet's jd-extract schema (jd-extract.ts:54) only *describes* a three-letter code; it is not enum-constrained, so it can emit '£', 'GBP ', 'pound', etc., and a recruiter can type anything. The `currency || 'GBP'` guard only catches empty string, not a non-empty invalid code.
userImpact: A recruiter approves a spec call, a job is created, and clicking into that job throws and shows the error screen instead of the job. The job is effectively un-viewable until the bad currency is fixed in the DB. Very plausible during a live demo because the value originates from AI transcription of a verbal salary mention.
evidence: const fmt = new Intl.NumberFormat('en-GB', { style: 'currency', currency: currency || 'GBP', maximumFractionDigits: 0 })   // throws RangeError for currency='£' or 'pounds'
suggestedFix: Constrain currency to ISO-4217 (uppercase, 3 letters) in structuredJdSchema with a regex/enum, default to 'GBP' when invalid at job-creation time, AND make formatSalary defensive: wrap the NumberFormat construction in try/catch and fall back to 'GBP' (or a plain number + code) so a single bad value can never crash the page.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is verified end-to-end against the actual code.

1. Free-text currency input: src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/spec-review-form.tsx:247-253 — currency is a plain shadcn <Input> with placeholder "GBP". A recruiter can type "pounds", "£", "GBP ", etc. The Sonnet schema (jd-extract.ts:54) only describes "Three-letter currency code" in prose; it is NOT enum-constrained, so the model can also emit a non-ISO value.

2. Weak validation: actions.ts:45 — currency: z.string().trim().max(8).nullable().optional(). Any string of length 1–8 passes (trim removes trailing spaces, but "pounds" and "£" are both ≤8 and survive). No regex/enum/uppercasing.

3. Verbatim DB write: create-job-from-spec.ts:184 — currency: jd.currency ?? 'GBP'. The ?? only substitutes when null; a non-empty invalid string is written as-is to jobs.currency.

4. No DB guard: supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:215 — currency text not null default 'GBP', with no CHECK constraint validating ISO-4217.

5. The throw: job-detail-header.tsx:28-32 — new Intl.NumberFormat('en-GB', { style: 'currency', currency: currency || 'GBP', maximumFractionDigits: 0 }). The currency || 'GBP' guard only catches empty string. I empirically ran node: 'pounds', '£', and 'GBP ' (trailing space) all throw RangeError: Invalid currency code. (Note: lowercase 'gbp' does NOT throw — Intl uppercases it — so the trim().max(8) lets the dangerous cases through but not all of them.)

6. Reachability: formatSalary is only invoked when salary_min or salary_max is non-null (line 27). During a spec review a recruiter typically enters a salary, so the formatter is constructed and the throw fires. JobWithCompany (jobs.ts:137) is Tables<'jobs'> & {...}, so the real DB currency value flows straight into the header. The page (jobs/[id]/page.tsx:67) renders JobDetailHeader in a Server Component, so the RangeError throws during RSC render.

Net effect: an invalid currency makes the specific job detail page un-renderable; the job is effectively un-viewable until the value is corrected in the DB. The recruiter has no in-app way to edit currency back (the manual job form jobs/new/* has no currency field at all, and there is no job-edit form exposing it), so recovery requires a DB edit — matching the claimed impact.

Severity adjustment: there IS an (app)-group error boundary at src/app/(app)/error.tsx, so the throw is contained to the job detail route subtree and shows "Something went wrong" while preserving the TopNav — it is NOT a full white-screen app crash as a naive reading of "high" might imply. Also the only realistic injection vector is the spec-review free-text input (seed data and manual jobs always use 'GBP'/default), narrowing exposure. The outcome (poisoned currency -> un-viewable job needing manual DB fix) is real and demo-plausible, and the fix is cheap, so this is a legitimate medium, not high.

title: Contract/temp day rates from spec calls are stored in the annual salary columns and shown as a salary
description: jd-extract.ts:48 explicitly tells Sonnet that salary_range_min/max is 'annual for perm, daily rate for contract'. But create-job-from-spec.ts unconditionally writes salary_range_min/max into jobs.salary_min/salary_max regardless of job_type, and never populates jobs.day_rate_min/day_rate_max (which exist in the schema, phase1_domain_schema.sql:279-280). The job detail header (job-detail-header.tsx:40) only ever formats job.salary_min/max as currency and never reads day_rate_*. So a contract role with an extracted day rate of e.g. 500 is persisted as salary_min=500 and rendered as '£500' next to a 'Contract' badge.
userImpact: A contract job created from a spec call shows a day rate of £500 as if it were a £500 annual salary. For UK recruitment this is a glaring domain error a client/recruiter will immediately notice — it makes the AI extraction look broken and undermines the demo's credibility around day rate / pay rate / charge rate handling.
evidence:
salary_min: jd.salary_range_min ?? null,
salary_max: jd.salary_range_max ?? null,   // written for every job_type; day_rate_min/day_rate_max never set
suggestedFix: Branch on jd.job_type in the insert payload: for job_type 'contract'/'temp', write salary_range_min/max into day_rate_min/day_rate_max and leave salary_min/max null (or vice-versa). Ensure the job detail/header renders day rate (with a /day suffix) for contract/temp jobs.
verifierReasoning: Verified every link in the chain. (1) jd-extract.ts:48 explicitly documents salary_range_min/max as 'annual for perm, daily rate for contract', so Sonnet returns a day rate in that field for contract specs. (2) create-job-from-spec.ts:182-183 writes salary_min: jd.salary_range_min / salary_max: jd.salary_range_max unconditionally; the insert payload type (lines 159-187) has no day_rate fields at all, so jobs.day_rate_min/max are never populated regardless of jd.job_type. (3) Schema phase1_domain_schema.sql:279-280 confirms day_rate_min/day_rate_max columns exist. (4) job-detail-header.tsx:40 calls formatSalary(job.salary_min, job.salary_max, ...) which uses Intl currency formatting with no /day suffix and never reads day_rate. A grep across src/ shows 'day_rate' appears ONLY in the migration and generated database.ts types — never read or written by any application code, i.e. the columns are dead. The recruiter review form (spec-review-form.tsx lines 214/231) labels the inputs 'Salary min'/'Salary max' unconditionally, so the human-in-the-loop step does not relabel or redirect the value for contract roles. The same salary_min/max is also fed into the AI job-ad generator (ad-panel/actions.ts:87-88), compounding the wrong presentation. Net effect exactly as claimed: a contract role with a 500/day rate is stored as salary_min=500 and shown as '£500' next to a 'Contract' badge. Adjusted from high to medium because it is a presentation/labeling defect on a specific path (contract spec with a stated day rate, approved without manual correction), it is recruiter-visible and editable at review, and there is no data loss, correctness corruption, or cross-tenant leakage. It stays above low because day-rate-vs-annual-salary is a core UK recruitment distinction the product explicitly claims to handle, so it is genuinely demo-embarrassing.

title: Approving wipes confidence_per_field and ambiguities, breaking the retry-from-failed review
description: updateSpecDraftStructuredData persists parsed.data.structuredData, which is built only from the visible form fields (no confidence_per_field, no ambiguities). Because it overwrites the whole structured_data jsonb, the Sonnet-produced confidence map and ambiguities checklist are deleted on first approve. If job creation later fails (e.g. missing company) and the recruiter returns to /spec/[id]/review to retry, the review page (review/page.tsx:56-57) reads confidence_per_field ?? {} and ambiguities ?? [] from the now-empty data, so the 'verify this' badges and the 'Verify with client' sidebar card silently disappear.
userImpact: On any recover-from-failed retry, the recruiter loses the AI's low-confidence flags and the client-verification checklist — the exact AI value-add that differentiates the product — even though the original recording captured it.
evidence:
const structured = (draft.structured_data ?? {}) as StructuredJd
const ambiguities = structured.ambiguities ?? []   // empty after first approve overwrote structured_data
suggestedFix: Merge the recruiter edits over the existing structured_data (read-modify-write) instead of replacing it, preserving confidence_per_field and ambiguities; or persist those two fields separately so they survive an approve/retry cycle.
verifierReasoning: The core mechanism is real and verified end to end. updateSpecDraftStructuredData (src/lib/db/spec-drafts.ts:187-207) does a full jsonb column overwrite (`structured_data: args.structuredData`), no read-modify-write. approveSpecDraftAction (src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/actions.ts:108-111) persists only parsed.data.structuredData, which (a) is built by the form from visible fields only — spec-review-form.tsx:65-79 never sends confidence_per_field or ambiguities, and (b) is further stripped by Zod's structuredJdSchema (actions.ts:38-52) which omits both keys and strips unknown keys by default. The original full object (incl. confidence_per_field + ambiguities) is written once by the Inngest transcribe-and-structure-spec function (transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts:264-273) and is never re-extracted on any approve/retry path (extractJdFromTranscript is called only in that one Inngest step). The review page reads ambiguities ?? [] (review/page.tsx:57) and confidence_per_field ?? {} (review/page.tsx:102), so after the first approve both the 'verify this' badges and the 'Verify with client' sidebar card silently disappear. A retry path genuinely exists: after approve, status becomes 'approved'; the spec list (page.tsx:88-89) and detail poller (spec/[id]/page.tsx:38-39) both route an 'approved' draft to /review, the review page renders for any non-pending/transcribing status (review/page.tsx:44-47), and the Approve button has no status-based disable, so re-approve is possible. The loss is irreversible short of re-running the whole audio pipeline. So the null-trap is confirmed.

title: No min<=max validation and unbounded integer can fail job insert with a generic error
description: salary_range_min/max are validated as z.number().int().min(0) with NO upper bound and NO cross-field min<=max check. A recruiter can submit min=80000, max=60000 (approved cleanly, then displayed as '£80,000 – £60,000'), or a value above the Postgres integer max (2,147,483,647). jobs.salary_min/max are `integer` columns (phase1_domain_schema.sql:277-278); an out-of-range value passes Zod but the insert in create-job-from-spec.ts throws, which only surfaces async by flipping the draft to 'failed' with the generic 'Transcription failed' message — the recruiter has no idea the salary number was the cause.
userImpact: Reversed or oversized salary ranges either render nonsensically on the job, or cause an opaque background failure where the approve appeared to succeed (toast 'Draft approved — creating job…') but no job ever appears and the error is misattributed to transcription.
evidence:
salary_range_min: z.number().int().min(0).nullable().optional()...
salary_range_max: z.number().int().min(0).nullable().optional()...  // no .max(), no refine(min<=max)
suggestedFix: Add .max(2_000_000_000) (or a realistic cap) to both fields and a .superRefine that, when both present, asserts min <= max with a clear message surfaced inline on the form before approval.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code. The two underlying defects are real:

1. No cross-field min<=max check AND no upper bound. At src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/review/actions.ts:43-44, salary_range_min/max are z.number().int().min(0).nullable().optional() with no .max() and no .superRefine. By contrast, the sibling manual job-creation schema at src/app/(app)/jobs/new/schema.ts:35-46 DOES enforce min<=max, and its coerceSalary() caps at 10,000,000. So the spec-review path is genuinely inconsistent with the established pattern. A reversed range (min=80000, max=60000) is accepted and would render via formatSalary (src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/job-detail-header.tsx:33) as the nonsensical "£80,000 – £60,000".

2. Unbounded integer -> opaque insert failure. jobs.salary_min/max are plain `integer` columns (supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:277-278), no CHECK constraint. A value > 2,147,483,647 passes Zod, flows into structured_data JSON, and the create-job-from-spec Inngest insert at create-job-from-spec.ts:182-183/188-192 throws "value out of range for type integer". The salary inputs (spec-review-form.tsx:217-243) are type=number with no max attribute and Number(e.target.value), so the value is reachable via manual entry.

HOWEVER the claim's described symptom is materially WRONG. The claim says the insert failure "flips the draft to 'failed' with the generic 'Transcription failed' message." It does not. create-job-from-spec's onFailure handler (create-job-from-spec.ts:75-90) ONLY calls Sentry.captureException — it never sets status='failed' nor writes parse_error. The only place that code path sets status='failed' with a friendly message is the explicit missing-company branch (lines 141-150), which is unrelated to salary. So after an out-of-range insert, the draft stays status='approved'; src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/page.tsx:38 redirects approved drafts to /review, and the "Transcription failed" heading (page.tsx:70, gated on status==='failed') never renders. The real outcome is even more silent than claimed: success toast "Draft approved — creating job…", redirect to /jobs, no job ever appears, no user-facing error anywhere — only a Sentry event. The misattribution-to-transcription detail in the claim is incorrect.

Severity: both are edge cases needing deliberately malformed manual input (a reversed range, or a salary above ~2.1 billion). AI extraction from a spec call would essentially never produce either. The reversed-range issue is cosmetic display; the out-of-range issue is a silent failure but extremely unlikely to fire organically and harmless to the rest of the app. Worth fixing for consistency with jobs/new (add .max + superRefine min<=max surfaced inline), but low impact for a live client demo.

title: no-duration failure path is dead code because Whisper duration is clamped to >=1
description: whisper.ts:154 computes durationSeconds as Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0)), so transcribe() can never return a value <= 0. The Inngest pipeline's `if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0)` branch (and the corresponding -1 / 'Could not read the audio file. Please re-upload.' user message at line 210-217) is therefore unreachable. The whisper.ts docstring also claims 'durationSeconds <= 0 → throws' which is false — it clamps instead. A genuinely undecodable/zero-length file would instead surface (correctly) via the empty-transcript guard, so behaviour is acceptable, but the intended dedicated message never fires and the contract is misdocumented.
userImpact: Minor: users who upload a truly unreadable file get the 'Transcription returned no text. The recording may be silent.' message rather than the more accurate 'Could not read the audio file.' The dead branch also hides intent from future maintainers.
suggestedFix: Either have transcribe() return the raw (un-clamped) duration and let the caller decide, or remove the dead <=0 branch and update the docstring so the only real guards are over-60-min and empty-transcript.
verifierReasoning: Verified every assertion against the actual code. whisper.ts:154 computes `const durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0))` and returns it directly at line 183, so transcribe() can structurally never return durationSeconds <= 0 (floor is 1). grep confirms transcribe() has exactly one caller — transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts — so no other path can feed a non-clamped value. Therefore the consumer guard `if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0)` at line 196 is dead code; the `durationSeconds: -1` sentinel it sets is never produced, and the dedicated `if (durationSeconds === -1)` block at lines 210-217 with message 'Could not read the audio file. Please re-upload.' is unreachable. The whisper.ts:93 docstring 'durationSeconds <= 0 → throws (probe returned malformed/no duration)' is doubly wrong: the code clamps (does not throw), and the 'probe' reference is stale since ffprobe was eliminated (duration now comes from Whisper's verbose_json, as documented at lines 76-85 and 183-187). The over-60-min branch (-2, lines 199/218) IS reachable, so only the -1 path is dead. Behavior remains acceptable: a truly silent/undecodable file is still caught by the empty-transcript guard (lines 230-238), which marks the draft failed with 'Transcription returned no text. The recording may be silent.' — no crash, no cross-tenant leak, no silent success. Impact is confined to a marginally less accurate error message in a rare edge case plus a misleading docstring/dead branch that obscures intent for maintainers.

title: Approve relies on a 2s setTimeout refresh that loses the race on Inngest cold start
description: After approve, the form navigates to /jobs and schedules `setTimeout(() => router.refresh(), 2000)`. The job row is inserted asynchronously by create-job-from-spec (Whisper/Sonnet already done, but Inngest dispatch + cold start can easily exceed 2s). If the job hasn't landed within 2s, the single refresh shows a /jobs list without the new job and there is no further retry or 'creating…' indicator, so the recruiter believes approval silently failed.
userImpact: Recruiter approves a spec call, lands on /jobs, and the just-created job is missing with no spinner or message; they must manually reload. Likely to happen on the first approve of a session (cold Inngest) — exactly the demo scenario.
evidence:
router.push('/jobs')
setTimeout(() => router.refresh(), 2000)  // single fixed-delay refresh, no guarantee the Inngest insert has completed
suggestedFix: Show an explicit 'Creating job…' pending state on /jobs (or poll for the created_job_id on the draft) instead of a one-shot 2s refresh; or keep the recruiter on the review page showing live status until created_job_id is set, then navigate.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the cited code at src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/spec-review-form.tsx:86-90: handleApprove does router.push('/jobs') then setTimeout(() => router.refresh(), 2000) — a single fixed-delay, one-shot refresh with no retry, no polling, no 'creating…' placeholder.

The latency reasoning in the claim is sound. Whisper transcription + Sonnet JD extraction run earlier in transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts (confirmed: transcribe() + extractJdFromTranscript() with persist-draft, all pre-approval). The post-approval create-job-from-spec.ts contains NO AI calls — it does an Inngest event dispatch (approveSpecDraftAction line 128) plus three sequential step.run DB writes (read-draft, insert-job, patch-draft-job-id). On a warm Inngest run this is well under 2s, but on a cold start (the first approve of a session — exactly the live-demo case) Inngest dispatch + function scheduling/boot can plausibly exceed 2s, so the single refresh at 2000ms can fire before the jobs row lands.

No compensating feedback exists: jobs-table/jobs-cards/jobs-shell have no 'creating'/'pending'/spinner strings; the /jobs page has no optimistic placeholder. The /spec list page does NOT surface created_job_id, so an approved draft card just links back to /review — the recruiter has no easy in-app confirmation that the job was created. There is no realtime channel or setInterval polling in the spec/jobs flows. revalidatePath('/jobs') is called in the server action (actions.ts:157) but executes before the async Inngest insert, so it does not close the race.

Mitigating facts that keep this at low (not higher): data is never lost — the Inngest function is idempotent on created_job_id and always inserts the row; the /jobs page is dynamically rendered (RSC reading cookies, no static cache export found), so any subsequent navigation or manual reload reliably shows the job once it lands; and the success toast reads 'Draft approved — creating job…', which sets the expectation that creation is in progress. So the worst case is a transient 'where did my job go' moment that self-resolves on the next refresh, not a silent failure or lost write.

The claim's suggested fixes (explicit 'Creating job…' pending state, or poll created_job_id, or stay on review page until created_job_id is set) are all reasonable and would eliminate the race. The issue is genuine missing-feedback during an Inngest cold start; original 'low' severity is accurate.

title: List-page delete is a one-click destructive action with no confirmation
description: SpecDeleteButton fires deleteSpecDraftAction -> markSpecDraftRejected (status=rejected, deleted_at=now) immediately on a single icon click with no window.confirm() guard, unlike the in-review Reject button which does confirm (spec-review-form.tsx:95). It soft-deletes any draft regardless of status, including a 'ready_for_review' draft the recruiter spent effort on. Recovery is only possible within the 30-day cleanup window via DB, not via UI.
userImpact: A misclick on the small trash icon next to a draft immediately removes it from the list during a demo, with only a toast and no undo affordance in the UI.
evidence: const onClick = () => { startTransition(async () => { const result = await deleteSpecDraftAction({ specDraftId: draftId }) ... }) }  // no confirm()
suggestedFix: Add a confirmation (window.confirm or an AlertDialog) before calling deleteSpecDraftAction, matching the in-review Reject button, and/or offer an Undo action on the success toast.
verifierReasoning: Tried to refute but the claim holds on every checkable point. src/app/(app)/spec/spec-delete-button.tsx:21-31 fires deleteSpecDraftAction on a single ghost icon-button click with no window.confirm() and no AlertDialog — verified verbatim. The action (src/app/(app)/spec/actions.ts:20-39) calls markSpecDraftRejected, which in src/lib/db/spec-drafts.ts:244-267 sets status='rejected', rejected_at, deleted_at=now() unconditionally — it does soft-delete any draft regardless of status, including a ready_for_review draft. By contrast the in-review Reject button DOES guard: src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/review/spec-review-form.tsx:95 has `if (!confirm('Reject this draft? The recording will be deleted in 30 days.'))`. (The claim cited this as spec-review-form.tsx:95; actual path is [id]/review/spec-review-form.tsx:95 — same line/content, only the directory prefix was abbreviated; not material.) Recovery: listSpecDrafts excludes deleted_at IS NOT NULL by default and includeDeleted is only a DB-helper arg — grep across src found NO restore/undo/trash UI surface, only the 30-day spec-draft-cleanup-sweep cron. The success path shows only `toast.success('Draft deleted.')` with no Undo action. This is also the LONE unguarded destructive delete in the app: candidates use an AlertDialog, clients/jobs/contacts/saved-ads use window.confirm — so it's genuinely inconsistent with the codebase's own established pattern. Note the action comment (actions.ts:11-12) shows the missing confirm was a deliberate choice ('list-level tidy up affordance'), not an oversight, but the inconsistency and misclick risk are real. Severity stays low: it's a soft delete with a 30-day recovery window (so no permanent data loss), affects only spec-call drafts (not candidates/placements), and the button is a small icon — annoying on a misclick during a demo but not catastrophic. The suggested fix (AlertDialog matching the candidate pattern, and/or an Undo toast) is appropriate and cheap.

title: Extracted/edited seniority_level is never persisted onto the created job
description: The review form captures seniority_level and the approve action saves it into spec_drafts.structured_data, but create-job-from-spec's insert payload (lines 159-187) never writes seniority anywhere — jobs has no seniority_level column (it lives on candidates, phase1_domain_schema.sql:216) and composeDescription (lines 53-66) omits seniority from the description text too. So the recruiter's seniority selection silently evaporates on the resulting job.
userImpact: A job created from a spec call shows no seniority even though the recruiter explicitly set Senior/Lead/etc. on the review form — a small but visible 'I entered that, where did it go?' gap.
evidence: function composeDescription(jd: SpecJd, ...) { ... if (jd.culture_notes) ...; if (jd.reporting_line) ...; if (jd.urgency) ... }  // no seniority_level; insert payload also omits it
suggestedFix: Include seniority_level in composeDescription (e.g. 'Seniority: senior') so it survives on the job description, or add it to a job field if/when one exists.
verifierReasoning: Verified the full chain. (1) The review form captures seniority: spec-review-form.tsx:163-169 renders a Seniority field bound to form.seniority_level. (2) The approve action persists it into the draft: handleApprove includes seniority_level (spec-review-form.tsx:67), it is validated by structuredJdSchema (actions.ts:40, seniorityEnum), and updateSpecDraftStructuredData (actions.ts:108-111) writes the whole structuredData (including seniority_level) into spec_drafts.structured_data. (3) create-job-from-spec.ts reads structured_data as jd (line 130) and its SpecJd type even declares seniority_level (line 36), but it goes nowhere: composeDescription (lines 53-66) renders must_haves, nice_to_haves, culture_notes, reporting_line, urgency and recruiterNotes only — no seniority — and the insert payload (lines 159-187) writes title, location, job_type, hiring_context, status, description, salary_min/max, currency, created_by, owner_user_id — no seniority field. (4) Confirmed jobs has no seniority column: phase1_domain_schema.sql:266-290 (CREATE TABLE jobs) has none; the only seniority_level column in the schema is on candidates (line 216). Grep across all 50+ migrations shows no ALTER TABLE jobs adding seniority. So the recruiter's seniority selection survives on the draft but never appears on the created job, neither as a column nor in the description text. Claim is accurate as written. Severity correctly graded low: the data is not lost (it persists on spec_drafts.structured_data), there is no crash, no silent mutation failure, no tenant leakage; it is a cosmetic 'where did my entry go' gap on the resulting job, and seniority is usually also encoded in the job title.

title: 60-minute / cost-control cap is enforced AFTER Whisper has already transcribed (and billed) the whole file
description: The function comment (lines 42-44) states the MAX_DURATION_SECONDS=3600 cap exists 'instead of paying Whisper to transcribe noise.' But the duration is only known from Whisper's own verbose_json response, which is returned by transcribe() AFTER the full audio has been sent and billed. The over-60-min branch (durationSeconds === -2) runs after transcribe() has already incurred and logged the real Whisper cost to ai_usage. There is no pre-transcribe duration probe (ffprobe was deliberately removed per the whisper.ts comment) and the action only enforces a 100 MiB byte cap, which at ~64kbps AAC corresponds to ~3.5 hours of audio. So a 3-hour recording is transcribed and paid for in full, then rejected to the user. The cap protects the downstream Sonnet step and the UI, not the Whisper spend.
userImpact: A founder uploading or accidentally recording a very long file (e.g. 2-3 hours) is still billed the full Whisper minute-cost, then told 'Recording is over 60 minutes. Split into chunks and re-upload.' They pay for transcription they cannot use, and the per-tenant cost ledger shows spend with no resulting draft.
suggestedFix: Probe duration from the recompressed /tmp WebM file (ffprobe works on a seekable file, unlike a stream) BEFORE calling transcribe(), and reject over-60-min audio there. Alternatively tighten the byte cap or estimate duration from bitrate*size and reject early. At minimum, document that the cap is a UX guard, not a cost guard, so the cost-control comment is not misleading.
verifierReasoning: The claim is accurate and fully confirmed by the code. Flow: (1) whisper.ts:135-146 makes the actual paid OpenAI Whisper API call. (2) whisper.ts:154-172 computes cost and writes it to ai_usage via record_ai_usage AFTER the call returns — so the real Whisper spend is both incurred and logged to the per-tenant ledger before any duration check. (3) In transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts the MAX_DURATION_SECONDS=3600 check is on line 199, AFTER the awaited transcribe() call on line 188; the over-60-min branch (durationSeconds === -2, lines 199-201) only fires post-transcription, then marks the draft failed with the user-facing "Recording is over 60 minutes" message (lines 218-226). No pre-transcribe duration probe exists: the inngest comment at lines 183-187 explicitly says "we don't need ffprobe at all," and whisper.ts:77-85 documents that ffprobe-on-stream was deliberately removed. The ffprobe binary IS still installed (ffmpeg.ts:36-39) and the recompressed audio DOES land on a seekable /tmp file (ffmpeg.ts:90-91), so the suggested fix (probe /tmp before transcribe) is feasible — but it is not currently done. The only pre-spend guard is the 100 MiB byte cap in spec/new/actions.ts:31, whose own comment (lines 28-30) admits "100 MiB covers ~3-hour outliers" — confirming multi-hour files pass the gate, get fully billed, then are rejected. The misleading comment (inngest function lines 41-44: cap exists "instead of paying Whisper to transcribe noise") is real and contradicts the actual ordering. One nuance: the -2 branch returns whisperCostPence=0 so spec_drafts.whisper_cost_pence shows 0, but record_ai_usage inside whisper.ts already wrote the true cost to the ai_usage table independently — so the claim that the per-tenant cost ledger shows spend with no resulting draft is correct. Severity adjusted from medium to low: the leak is real but bounded by the byte cap (truly catastrophic files >~3.5h are blocked pre-spend), only triggers on edge-case recordings between 60min and ~3.5h, and per-event cost is small (~80p for a 3h transcription at whisper-1's 0.48p/min). It breaks no working flow and is not demo-facing; it is a genuine cost-control gap plus a misleading comment.

title: Whisper transcript is discarded if the Sonnet JD step fails — forces a full re-upload and re-pay for transcription
description: The expensive Whisper output (transcript) is held only in memory and is written to spec_drafts.transcript inside the 'persist-draft' step, which runs AFTER 'sonnet-structure-jd'. If extractJdFromTranscript fails all its retries (Sonnet 5xx/overload/timeout), the function throws, onFailure/markSpecFailed sets status='failed', and the transcript is never persisted. CLAUDE.md core principle 4 is 'Cache AI outputs aggressively; regenerate only on material change' — here the costly transcription is regenerated from scratch (re-uploading audio, re-running ffmpeg + Whisper, re-billing) just because the cheaper, later Sonnet step failed. The status page's recovery path only works when structured_data.title exists, which it never does if Sonnet failed before producing output.
userImpact: If Claude is briefly overloaded during the JD-structuring step, the recruiter's whole spec call is marked failed with no transcript saved. They must re-upload the audio and pay the Whisper minute-cost again, even though transcription succeeded the first time.
suggestedFix: Persist the transcript to spec_drafts.transcript in its own step immediately after Whisper succeeds (before the Sonnet step). On Sonnet failure, mark status='failed' but keep the transcript so the recruiter can retry JD extraction (or manually proceed) without re-transcribing. This also lets a 'retry extraction' path call only Sonnet, not the full audio pipeline.
verifierReasoning:
The claim holds against the actual code. In src/lib/inngest/functions/transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts the transcript is computed inside the `process-audio` step (lines 161-208) but only ever persisted to spec_drafts.transcript inside the `persist-draft` step (line 267), which runs AFTER `sonnet-structure-jd` (line 240). extractJdFromTranscript throws a generic Error on Sonnet failure (jd-extract.ts:178 / underlying runWithLogging), not a NonRetriableError, so on final failure the catch block (line 297-303) calls markSpecFailed (status='failed', parse_error only) and `persist-draft` never executes — spec_drafts.transcript stays NULL.

The recovery path is confirmed broken for this exact case: the status page (spec/[id]/page.tsx lines 46-50) gates its retry CTA on `hasParsedJd`, which requires structured.title to be a non-empty string. After a Sonnet failure structured_data is empty, so hasParsedJd is false and the only CTA shown is "Upload another recording" (lines 117-119). The review page would render "(no transcript captured)" (review/page.tsx:131). There is no action or Inngest path anywhere in src/app/(app)/spec or src/lib/inngest that re-runs only Sonnet against a stored transcript — grep found none. So the recruiter must re-upload the audio and re-run the full download→ffmpeg→Whisper→re-bill pipeline (spec/new/actions.ts fires spec/uploaded fresh). This violates CLAUDE.md core principle 4 (cache AI outputs; regenerate only on material change), since the expensive Whisper output is discarded because the cheaper, later Sonnet step failed.

One refinement to the claim's framing (not enough to refute): the function has `retries: 2`, and Inngest memoizes the completed `process-audio` step across those automatic retries, so Whisper does NOT re-run on each Sonnet retry — only after all 3 Sonnet attempts are exhausted AND the recruiter manually re-uploads. So the wasted Whisper billing is a re-upload-time cost, not a per-retry cost. The net user impact (lost transcript + forced re-record/re-upload + re-pay) is exactly as described.

Severity medium is fair: it requires Sonnet to fail across all 3 attempts on one call (sustained overload — plausible but uncommon), and the cost is pennies plus the friction/data-loss of having to re-record a live spec call that may no longer be available. Real, fixable as the reviewer suggests (persist transcript in its own step right after Whisper succeeds), but conditional and non-catastrophic — not high (no data corruption / no cross-tenant leak) and clearly above non-issue.

title: Sonnet outage produces a generic 'Transcription failed' message instead of graceful 'AI temporarily unavailable' degradation
description: The lens requires AI errors to degrade gracefully with an 'AI temporarily unavailable' style message while the rest of the app keeps working. When the Sonnet JD-extraction step fails (Claude overloaded/down), the only failure path sets parse_error to the generic FAILED_USER_MESSAGE 'Transcription failed. Try uploading again, or contact support.' This is misleading: transcription actually succeeded; it was the AI structuring step that was unavailable. It also tells the user to re-upload (re-paying Whisper) rather than to retry shortly. The transcribe and JD-extract failure modes are conflated under one message.
userImpact: During a brief Claude outage the recruiter is told transcription failed and to re-upload, when in fact only the AI drafting step was temporarily down. They take the wrong corrective action (re-upload + re-pay) instead of simply retrying a minute later.
suggestedFix: Distinguish the Sonnet-step failure from the transcribe-step failure. When the JD-extraction step throws (especially after the transcript is persisted per the related finding), set a distinct parse_error like 'AI is temporarily unavailable — your transcript is saved, please retry in a moment.' and offer a retry-extraction CTA rather than re-upload.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code. In src/lib/inngest/functions/transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts the pipeline order is: mark-transcribing -> process-audio (Whisper transcribe, transcript held only in the step output, NOT persisted) -> validation gates -> sonnet-structure-jd (line 240, calls extractJdFromTranscript) -> persist-draft (line 247, which is the ONLY place transcript + structured_data are written to the DB, and runs only after Sonnet succeeds).

When Sonnet is overloaded/down, extractJdFromTranscript (src/lib/ai/jd-extract.ts) throws a plain Error (e.g. retries exhausted, or 'Sonnet did not return tool_use block' on line 178). That is not a NonRetriableError, so after Inngest's 2 retries the catch block at line 297 (and the onFailure handler at line 114) calls markSpecFailed with FAILED_USER_MESSAGE = 'Transcription failed. Try uploading again, or contact support.' Because persist-draft never ran, draft.transcript and structured_data are both null.

On the status page (src/app/(app)/spec/[id]/page.tsx) the heading hardcodes 'Transcription failed' for any failed status (line 70), shows the generic parse_error (line 91), and because hasParsedJd is false (structured.title is empty, lines 47-50) the ONLY CTA is 'Upload another recording' -> /spec/new (lines 117-119). A grep confirms there is no 'AI temporarily unavailable' message and no retry-extraction action anywhere in the spec flow.

So every material claim is accurate: (1) the message blames transcription when transcription actually succeeded and only the Sonnet structuring step failed; (2) the only corrective action offered is re-upload, which re-runs Whisper (~1-2p per call) instead of a wait-and-retry; (3) the transcribe and JD-extract failure modes are conflated under one message; (4) this contradicts the project's documented error-handling lens in CLAUDE.md: 'AI errors degrade gracefully — if Claude is down, the feature shows AI temporarily unavailable but the rest of the app works.' The suggested-fix premise (persist transcript before the Sonnet step so a distinct retry-extraction path can exist) is correctly identified as not yet implemented.

Severity stays low: this only fires during an actual transient Claude outage, the lost work is a cheap Whisper re-run rather than data corruption or cross-tenant leakage, and it does not break the rest of the app. It is a genuine missing-feedback / graceful-degradation polish gap on a rare path, not a demo-breaker.

title: Backfill / Build-index buttons never refresh the page — count stays stale and button is permanently disabled
description: triggerCandidateBackfillAction and triggerHnswBuildAction (integrations/actions.ts) fire an Inngest event and return { ok: true } with NO revalidatePath('/settings/integrations'). The client buttons then set done=true permanently (disabled forever for this render). After the Inngest job actually embeds the candidates, the page's 'N candidates not yet embedded' count (page.tsx:81-82, server-rendered) does not update until a full manual reload. Worse, because done is local component state and there is no revalidation, the operator gets no signal that anything completed — the button just says 'Queued' indefinitely. If they reload and the count is unchanged (job still running or failed), there is no way to retry without another reload.
userImpact: After clicking 'Backfill N candidates' the page keeps showing the same N and a dead 'Queued' button. The operator cannot tell whether embeddings are progressing and cannot re-trigger. Looks stuck.
suggestedFix: These are async background jobs so an immediate count change isn't expected, but the UI should let the operator re-check: either call router.refresh() after the toast, or replace the permanent done lock with a timed re-enable, or render a 'Refresh status' affordance. At minimum document on screen that the count updates on reload, and don't permanently disable the button on a single fire.
verifierReasoning:
Core mechanics confirmed in the actual code. actions.ts (triggerCandidateBackfillAction L20-60, triggerHnswBuildAction L66-118) fire `inngest.send` and return `{ ok: true }` with NO revalidatePath/revalidateTag — confirmed by grep. The sibling outlook-actions.ts in the SAME directory DOES call revalidatePath('/settings/integrations') (L100, L126), so the omission is a genuine inconsistency, not a deliberate codebase norm. integration-buttons.tsx (L19, L24, L30, L48, L53, L57) confirms `done` is local useState set true on success, with `disabled={pending || done}` and no reset/timer/router.refresh — so the button is locked at "Queued" until the component unmounts (navigate away or full reload). The count is server-rendered: page.tsx L81-82 calls countCandidatesWithoutEmbedding (lib/db/embeddings.ts L155-170) server-side and passes it to BackfillButton, so it only changes on a fresh render. So the described state — stale count + dead "Queued" button until manual reload — is real and reproducible.

However the claim overstates impact in two ways. (1) It frames revalidatePath as the fix for the stale count, but the action queues an ASYNC Inngest job; at return time no embeddings have been written, so even with revalidation the re-rendered count would be identical. "Count stays stale" is therefore inherent to background processing, not a missing-revalidation bug — the claim's own suggested-fix section concedes "an immediate count change isn't expected." (2) Nothing is functionally broken: the job fires, the toast accurately says "queued" (not "done"), the backfill sweeps all unembedded org candidates so re-triggering is rarely needed, and any reload fully recovers the button and refreshes the count. The residual real issue is purely UX polish: a permanently-disabled button with no in-page "refresh status" affordance and no on-screen note that the count updates on reload. For a live client demo this is cosmetic, not a blocker — hence low, not medium.

title: Outlook reconnect via service-role UPDATE may write a row whose set_org trigger context is absent (relies on prior insert having set organization_id)
description: On first connect, upsertOutlookCredentials inserts via service-role and relies on the set_org BEFORE INSERT trigger to fill organization_id. But service-role has no auth session, so current_organization_id() returns null inside the trigger. The insert helper (outlook-credentials.ts:152) does NOT pass organization_id explicitly. If the set_organization_id() trigger raises when it can't resolve the org under service-role (as set_invited_by does on org_invitations), the very first Outlook connect would fail and fall into the UPDATE branch — which matches zero rows (no row exists yet) and silently no-ops with updErr=null, then redirects with outlook=connected. The card would show 'connected' from the in-memory redirect param while no credentials row exists, and the subscription-create Inngest event references a user with no row. Whether this fires depends on how set_organization_id() behaves under a null session (hardened in 20260517204504_harden_set_organization_id.sql).
userImpact: If the org-id trigger rejects service-role inserts, a first-time Outlook connect shows success but persists nothing; the integration silently does not work and the operator has no error.
suggestedFix: In the callback, pass the already-resolved organizationId (read at route.ts:146) explicitly into upsertOutlookCredentials so the row never depends on a session-less trigger. After the UPDATE branch, verify rowCount > 0 (use .select('id') and check data) and redirect with persist_failed if zero rows were affected, instead of treating a no-op UPDATE as success.
verifierReasoning:
Every link in the claimed causal chain is confirmed in the actual code, and the failure is NOT an edge case — it affects every first-time Outlook connect.

1. Trigger raises on null org: supabase/migrations/20260517204504_harden_set_organization_id.sql lines 14-20 — `set_organization_id()` does `new.organization_id := current_organization_id()`, and if still null, `raise exception 'organization_id is required and could not be resolved from auth context'`. So it is NOT the lenient kind of trigger that silently leaves null.

2. Trigger fires on outlook_credentials INSERT: 20260519092945_outlook_credentials.sql lines 112-114 attach `outlook_credentials_set_org BEFORE INSERT ... EXECUTE FUNCTION set_organization_id()`.

3. Service-role has no session: src/lib/supabase/service.ts creates the client with the service-role key and persistSession:false — no JWT, so auth.uid() is null. current_organization_id() (20260513151021 lines 61-69) is `select organization_id from users where id = auth.uid()` → `where id = null` → returns null inside the trigger.

4. upsertOutlookCredentials omits organization_id: src/lib/db/outlook-credentials.ts lines 150-161 inserts user_id + microsoft fields + tokens + scopes but NOT organization_id. The callback (src/app/api/outlook/callback/route.ts) resolves organizationId at line 146, throws if missing, but then calls upsertOutlookCredentials at lines 151-160 WITHOUT passing it. Result on first connect: the BEFORE INSERT trigger raises → error → writeResult.ok === false.

5. UPDATE fallback no-ops silently: route lines 166-178 run `.update({...}).eq('user_id', user.id)` with NO `.select()`/`.single()`. PostgREST treats an UPDATE matching zero rows as success (error:null), so updErr is null, the persist_failed branch (lines 179-186) is skipped, the inngest subscription-create event fires (lines 192-206), and the handler redirects with ?outlook=connected (line 209).

6. User impact: connect-outlook-card.tsx lines 67-70 fire toast.success('Outlook connected.') purely from the ?outlook=connected param — a false success signal. create-outlook-subscription.ts lines 61-68 then load the (nonexistent) row and bail (returns null). No credentials row is ever persisted; Outlook silently does not work.

Corroborating evidence that this is a real oversight, not by design: every OTHER service-role insert helper in this codebase explicitly accepts/passes organizationId for exactly this reason — candidate-cvs.ts line 81 ('with no session, which MUST pass organizationId explicitly'), spec-drafts.ts line 115, activities.ts line 106, candidates.ts line 674, candidates-linkedin.ts. upsertOutlookCredentials is the sole exception, and its docstring even claims 'organization_id is filled by the set_org trigger' — which is false under the service-role caller it actually has.

Minor correction to the original claim: the persistent card status is derived from a DB read (integrations/page.tsx lines 99-102: disconnected when no row), so on reload it shows 'disconnected', NOT 'connected'. Only the transient success toast is misleading. This does not change the substance: the operator sees a success toast while nothing persisted.

title: Outlook card tells recruiter "Read-only, we never send email on your behalf" while the connect flow actually requests Mail.Send
description: The Connect-Outlook card renders a fixed reassurance line: "Read-only access (Mail.Read). We never send email on your behalf." (connect-outlook-card.tsx:108-110). But the OAuth scope set the app requests at connect time is OUTLOOK_SCOPES = ['offline_access','Mail.Read','Mail.Send','User.Read'] (src/lib/integrations/outlook.ts:55-60), and getAuthorizationUrl builds the authorize URL with scope: OUTLOOK_SCOPES.join(' ') plus prompt:'consent' (outlook.ts:174-183). So when the recruiter clicks Connect, Microsoft's consent screen will explicitly ask them to grant "Send mail as you" — directly contradicting the in-app copy. The app genuinely uses this: sendMail() posts to /me/sendMail (outlook.ts:697-745). For a live client demo this is a visible trust/consent integrity problem: the recruiter is told one thing by the app and shown another by Microsoft, and the agency is granting send-as permission they were told they were not granting.
userImpact: During the demo, the recruiter clicks "Connect Outlook" trusting the on-screen promise of read-only access, then Microsoft asks them to approve sending email as them. The mismatch reads as either a bug or a dark pattern, undermining confidence in a CRM whose entire pitch is handling their candidate relationships responsibly. It is also a GDPR/consent-transparency weakness (the stated basis for the permission is false).
evidence:
connect-outlook-card.tsx:108-110 →

Read-only access (Mail.Read). We never send email on your behalf.

outlook.ts:55-60 → export const OUTLOOK_SCOPES = ['offline_access','Mail.Read','Mail.Send','User.Read'] as const outlook.ts:179 → scope: OUTLOOK_SCOPES.join(' ') (inside getAuthorizationUrl, with prompt:'consent')
suggestedFix: Either (a) update the card copy to reflect reality — e.g. "Reads inbound/outbound email to build candidate timelines, and (with a separate consent) can send check-in emails you explicitly trigger. We never auto-send." — or (b) if Mail.Send is meant to be incremental-consent-only (as the buildIncrementalConsentUrl comment at outlook.ts:44-54 suggests), remove 'Mail.Send' from the base OUTLOOK_SCOPES used by getAuthorizationUrl so the initial connect truly requests only read scopes, and rely on buildIncrementalConsentUrl for the first send. Pick one and make the copy and the requested scopes agree.
verifierReasoning: Every cited fact is confirmed in the actual code. connect-outlook-card.tsx:108-110 renders the literal string "Read-only access (Mail.Read). We never send email on your behalf." — and this paragraph is rendered UNCONDITIONALLY (it sits outside the status conditional, after ConnectedState/DisconnectedState, inside CardContent). OUTLOOK_SCOPES (outlook.ts:55-60) is exactly ['offline_access','Mail.Read','Mail.Send','User.Read'] — Mail.Send IS in the base set. getAuthorizationUrl (outlook.ts:166-184) builds the authorize URL with scope: OUTLOOK_SCOPES.join(' ') (line 179) AND prompt:'consent' (line 181), with no scope filtering. I traced the full button chain: the "Connect Outlook" button (connect-outlook-card.tsx:199) calls startOutlookOAuthAction, which (outlook-actions.ts:47) calls getAuthorizationUrl(state) and returns that URL for the browser to navigate to. There is no read-only-only alternate path on initial connect — buildIncrementalConsentUrl exists but only serves users who connected under an earlier phase; it does NOT gate a fresh connect. The module's own comment (outlook.ts:50-54) explicitly admits: "Adding Mail.Send here means the standard OAuth getAuthorizationUrl also asks for it on a fresh connect." With prompt=consent forcing the full consent screen, Microsoft will display "Send mail as you," directly contradicting the in-app copy. sendMail() does POST to /me/sendMail (outlook.ts:736), so the scope is genuinely used. The claim is materially and precisely correct.

title: outlook_credentials schema default + migration header document read-only scopes; runtime persists Mail.Send
description: The outlook_credentials migration header states the integration uses "Mail.Read + offline_access + User.Read scopes" (lines 4-5) and the scopes column default is '{Mail.Read,offline_access,User.Read}' (line 56). But the OAuth callback persists scopes: [...OUTLOOK_SCOPES] which now includes Mail.Send (callback/route.ts:159 + 175, OUTLOOK_SCOPES at outlook.ts:55-60). hasMailSendScope() then reads creds.scopes to decide whether send is allowed (outlook.ts:628-630). The DB default is now actively misleading about what a connected row is capable of, and any reviewer auditing the table for 'what can this integration do' will reach the wrong conclusion. This is the data-layer twin of the UI mismatch above — the whole stack disagrees with itself about whether send is enabled.
userImpact: Not directly user-visible, but it is the kind of documentation-vs-reality drift that causes the founder (or a future buyer's technical reviewer) to misrepresent the integration's permissions to the agency or in a data-processing record — a compliance footgun for a product whose selling point is audit-readiness.
evidence:
migration line 4-5: "...single-tenant Entra app, Mail.Read + offline_access + User.Read scopes..."
migration line 56: scopes text[] not null default '{Mail.Read,offline_access,User.Read}'
callback/route.ts:159: scopes: [...OUTLOOK_SCOPES]  (and :175 on the re-connect UPDATE)
outlook.ts:628-630: return Array.isArray(creds.scopes) && creds.scopes.includes('Mail.Send')
suggestedFix: Reconcile in one direction. If send-on-trigger is a shipped feature, add a new migration updating the column default + header comment to include Mail.Send and document the send capability + consent flow. If send is meant to be off until incremental consent, remove Mail.Send from the base scope set persisted on connect (see previous finding). Do not leave the schema asserting read-only while the code stores send.
verifierReasoning:
All factual claims verified. Migration 20260519092945_outlook_credentials.sql header (lines 3-5) documents "Mail.Read + offline_access + User.Read scopes" and line 56 sets `scopes text[] not null default '{Mail.Read,offline_access,User.Read}'`. No later migration alters this default (the only follow-up, 20260519120001, adds last_renewal_error/last_renewal_attempt_at columns only). Runtime OUTLOOK_SCOPES (src/lib/integrations/outlook.ts:55-60) includes 'Mail.Send', and both callback write paths persist it: upsertOutlookCredentials with scopes:[...OUTLOOK_SCOPES] (callback/route.ts:159 -> db helper outlook-credentials.ts:160) and the reconnect UPDATE (callback/route.ts:175). hasMailSendScope (outlook.ts:628-630) reads creds.scopes. So the DDL default literal and header comment genuinely disagree with what the code stores — the schema-mismatch is factually real.

However the severity is overstated. The column default is effectively dead code: EVERY real row is written with an explicit scopes value (the insert helper always passes input.scopes; the reconnect UPDATE always sets scopes:[...OUTLOOK_SCOPES]). The `default '{...}'` only fires on a bare INSERT omitting the scopes column, which occurs nowhere in src/. Therefore no connected row ever actually carries the stale read-only set, hasMailSendScope reads the correctly-persisted array, and the running data layer + send-gating logic are mutually consistent. The report's framing that "the whole stack disagrees about whether send is enabled" is inaccurate — only the never-exercised DDL DEFAULT clause and the prose header comment are stale. The send design is itself coherent and documented (D3-20 incremental consent, no auto-send). The compliance-footgun narrative is speculative: a migration comment and an unused default are not a data-processing record, and any real audit of integration capability would read the persisted scopes arrays + the sendMail/OAuth code, all of which correctly reflect Mail.Send. Zero functional, data-correctness, or demo impact.

title: Candidate-embedding backfill can be fired by any recruiter, repeatedly, with no role gate or server-side idempotency
description: triggerCandidateBackfillAction authenticates the user and resolves their org via current_organization_id() (good — org-scoped, no cross-tenant leak), then fires inngest.send('embed/backfill-org') for the whole org. Unlike its sibling triggerHnswBuildAction — which was explicitly hardened with an owner-only check (actions.ts:80-95, comment 'Phase 2 review H3 fix — restrict to owners') — the backfill action has NO role check. Any recruiter in the org can trigger an org-wide re-embed of every unembedded candidate, which spends real Voyage API budget. The only abuse brake is the client button's local `done` state (integration-buttons.tsx:18-19,31) which disables after one click; that is purely client-side and resets on reload, and the server action itself has no idempotency guard, so a recruiter can reload and re-fire arbitrarily, or call the action directly. There is no per-tenant cost ceiling on embeddings the way there is for match-scoring (env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE).
userImpact: A non-owner recruiter (or anyone who can reach the action) can repeatedly queue org-wide embedding backfills, inflating the agency's AI bill. Not a cross-tenant leak, but it is an authorization inconsistency: the project chose owner-only for the comparable HNSW trigger and left the cost-bearing backfill open.
evidence:
actions.ts:20-60 — triggerCandidateBackfillAction: getUser() → current_organization_id() → inngest.send('embed/backfill-org'); no users.role check.
Contrast actions.ts:84-95 — triggerHnswBuildAction: '...restrict to owners...' if (me.role !== 'owner') return { ok:false, error:'Only owners...' }.
integration-buttons.tsx:18-19,31 — setDone(true) is the only repeat-fire guard, and it is client-only.
suggestedFix: Mirror the owner-check from triggerHnswBuildAction in triggerCandidateBackfillAction (SELECT role FROM users WHERE id = user.id; reject non-owners). Optionally add a short server-side de-dupe (e.g. skip if an embed/backfill-org for this org ran in the last N minutes) so reload-spam can't re-queue, and consider extending the per-tenant spend ceiling to embedding cost.
verifierReasoning: The authorization inconsistency is genuinely present and verified in code. triggerCandidateBackfillAction (src/app/(app)/settings/integrations/actions.ts:20-60) authenticates the user, resolves org via current_organization_id() RPC, and fires inngest 'embed/backfill-org' with NO users.role check. Its sibling triggerHnswBuildAction (lines 85-95) explicitly gates to owners with the comment "Phase 2 review H3 fix — restrict to owners," and four other settings actions are owner-gated (apply-form-actions.ts:38, settings/actions.ts:77, team/actions.ts:105/230/276). The integrations page (page.tsx:79) only checks for an authenticated user, so any recruiter can see and click BackfillButton, whose `done` state (integration-buttons.tsx:19,30) is client-only and resets on reload. The match-scoring path has a per-tenant spend ceiling (env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE, default £100, env.ts:83) while the embedding path has no equivalent ceiling — also accurate. HOWEVER, the claimed cost-abuse impact is substantially overstated. The downstream embed-batch function (embed-batch.ts:111,187) only selects rows WHERE candidate_embedding/job_embedding IS NULL, and bumpCandidateEmbedding writes the vector + stamps embedded_at (candidates.ts:546-547), so embedded rows become non-NULL and are skipped on every subsequent fire. Embeddings only revert to NULL via the invalidate_*_embedding triggers on a genuine material data change (migration 20260519092951). This means reloading and re-firing does NOT re-embed already-embedded candidates — after the first successful sweep, repeat fires embed nothing and incur zero incremental Voyage cost. The work is bounded by the count of genuinely-unembedded rows, which is exactly what the 10-minute cron already does. The function also runs with concurrency:{limit:1} and PER_RUN_ROW_CAP=256. So the reviewer's framing that a recruiter can "repeatedly queue org-wide re-embeds, inflating the bill" is inaccurate: the IS NULL filter is a de-facto data-level idempotency guard that the claim does not account for. The real issue is a defense-in-depth authorization gap (the cost-bearing action should mirror the owner-gate its siblings have), not an exploitable cost-inflation vector. For a live client demo this is benign — a non-owner triggering a backfill of their own org's unembedded candidates just runs the intended background work early.

title: Profile email field is editable and silently overwrites the displayed email, while telling the user it is "Display only"
description: The email <Input> in ProfileForm is fully editable (no readOnly / disabled), and updateProfileAction -> updateProfile() writes whatever the user types into public.users.email (profiles.ts line 44-48). The helper text under the field says "Display only. Changing the email used to sign in is a Phase 2 feature." So a user can edit the field, click Save, get a green "Profile saved" toast, and have their displayed email permanently changed to a value that no longer matches the email they actually sign in with. That divergent public.users.email then propagates everywhere it is shown: the TopNav, the Team page Members list (team/page.tsx line 113 shows row.email), and the invite-email "from" label (team/actions.ts line 165 uses me.email as the inviter name fallback).
userImpact: A recruiter updates their profile, sees "Profile saved", and believes their email changed. In reality their login email is unchanged but the email shown across the app and on outgoing team invites is now wrong/inconsistent. During a demo this looks like a broken or untrustworthy save.
evidence:
// no readOnly

Display only. Changing the email used to sign in is a Phase 2 feature.

// actions.ts -> updateProfile(supabase, user.id, { full_name, email: parsed.data.email }) // profiles.ts -> .update({ full_name: ..., email: patch.email })
suggestedFix: Make the email field readOnly (mirror OrganizationForm's readOnly pattern) and drop `email` from updateProfileSchema/updateProfile so the action only saves full_name. If the displayed email must track the auth email, seed it from user.email and never write it. Either way the field must not accept edits that the copy says are ignored.
verifierReasoning: The claim is verified end-to-end against the actual code. (1) profile-form.tsx:83 renders `<Input type="email" autoComplete="email" {...field} />` with no readOnly/disabled — fully editable. (2) The helper text at profile-form.tsx:85-87 says "Display only. Changing the email used to sign in is a Phase 2 feature." (3) schema.ts:23 validates and passes `email` through; actions.ts:39-42 calls updateProfile with `email: parsed.data.email`; profiles.ts:43-46 writes `.update({ full_name, email: patch.email })` into public.users.email. (4) The write genuinely succeeds: the `email text not null` column exists (migration 20260513151021 line 26) and the "self update" RLS policy (line 108-113) permits `id = auth.uid()` updates including email. (5) On success, profile-form.tsx:45 fires `toast.success('Profile saved')`. So a user can edit the field, click Save, see green success, and permanently set public.users.email to a value diverging from their auth login email. Propagation confirmed: layout.tsx:34 passes `userEmail={profile.data.email ?? user.email ?? ''}` (prefers the editable public copy) into TopNav (top-nav.tsx:91, shown when userName is null); team/page.tsx:113 renders row.email and line 111 uses it as the name fallback; team/actions.ts:165 uses me.email as the inviter-name fallback that lands in outbound invite email subject/body (lines 176,184). The suggested fix is sound and has a sibling precedent: organization-form.tsx:79,97 already uses `readOnly={!isOwner}`. Severity adjusted from high to medium: the bug is real and the copy directly contradicts behavior, but it is self-inflicted (requires the user to deliberately edit a field labelled display-only), causes no auth lockout (auth.users.email untouched) and no tenant/security boundary breach (RLS with-check still pins organization_id), and most propagation paths only surface when full_name is empty. Impact is data-consistency / misleading-UX and a demo-trust dent rather than data loss or security.

title: "Invitation sent" / "Invitation resent" toast fires even when no email is actually delivered
description: inviteMemberAction and resendInviteAction return { ok: true } as long as the DB row is written, regardless of whether the Resend email succeeded. The email is best-effort: if RESEND_API_KEY is unset (env.ts line 108 marks it optional) the send returns { ok:false, reason:'no_api_key' } and is swallowed silently (only http_error is logged). resolveOrigin() can also return null (no NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL, no origin header) in which case the email is skipped entirely and the action still returns ok. The client (team-invites.tsx line 217 / 113) then shows "Invitation sent" / "Invitation resent". MEMORY flags custom SMTP as a launch-blocker because the built-in path throttles, so a real invite tonight may produce a pending row and a success toast but no email in the teammate's inbox.
userImpact: Owner invites a colleague during the demo, sees a confident green "Invitation sent", but the teammate never receives anything. The owner has no signal that delivery failed — they will assume the colleague is ignoring it.
suggestedFix: Distinguish 'row created, email queued' from 'email not sent'. Return a soft flag (e.g. { ok: true, emailDelivered: boolean }) so the toast can say "Invitation created — but we couldn't email it (check email settings)" when no_api_key / skipped-origin / http_error occurred. At minimum, verify RESEND_API_KEY + NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL are set before the demo.
verifierReasoning:
I attempted to refute the claim and could not — the code confirms it on every point.

1. inviteMemberAction unconditionally returns { ok: true } at src/app/(app)/settings/team/actions.ts:206, regardless of email outcome. The send sits in a best-effort try block (lines 148-204); the sendResendEmail result.ok is read only to decide whether to log Sentry, never propagated to the caller. The !origin early-return (line 155) also returns { ok: true }. resendInviteAction is identical (lines 331, 380).

2. sendResendEmail (src/lib/email/resend.ts) returns { ok:false, reason:'no_api_key' } when RESEND_API_KEY is unset (lines 32-35), and the action only Sentry-logs when reason === 'http_error' (actions.ts:191-198, 366-372). The no_api_key case produces ZERO signal — no Sentry, no return-flag change, nothing user-facing.

3. resolveOrigin() can return null (no NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL, no origin/host headers — actions.ts:58-71). In that case the email is skipped, a server-side Sentry *warning* is logged, and the action still returns { ok: true } (lines 150-156). No user-facing signal.

4. env.ts confirms both vars are optional: RESEND_API_KEY at line 108 (z.string().min(1).optional()) and NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL at line 135 (z.string().url().optional()). Comments explicitly state production MUST set them but the schema does not enforce it.

5. The client (team-invites.tsx) fires toast.success('Invitation sent') at line 217 and toast.success('Invitation resent') at line 113 whenever result.ok is true — which it always is on the happy path. There is no emailDelivered flag for the toast to distinguish 'row created, email not sent' from 'row created, email delivered'.

6. The MEMORY context (custom SMTP flagged as launch-blocker; Resend domain still being settled) makes the no_api_key / unverified-domain scenario a realistic state at demo time, not a theoretical one.

The mechanism, the line references, the swallowed failure modes, and the user impact are all accurate. The DB row IS canonical (the invite appears in the pending list and a teammate could still accept if they happened to get the link by other means), and two of three failure modes do emit a server-side Sentry warning — but none of that surfaces to the owner, who sees an unambiguous green success. For a live client demo where Resend may not be fully wired, this is a genuine silent communication failure.

title: Backfill / Build-index buttons never refresh the page, leaving a stale count and an enabled duplicate button
description: triggerCandidateBackfillAction and triggerHnswBuildAction fire an Inngest event and return { ok:true } with no revalidatePath (actions.ts). The buttons set a local done=true so they show "Queued" and disable — but only for the current client render. The surrounding RSC (integrations/page.tsx) still shows the same "N candidates not yet embedded" count and, after any client navigation/refresh, renders a fresh enabled BackfillButton. There is no progress indicator for the async embedding either. The HNSW "Build" button is even more confusing: the toast says "Build queued" but the actual CREATE INDEX is a manual DDL step (per the card copy) so nothing visibly changes.
userImpact: Owner clicks "Backfill 40 candidates", gets a toast, but the page still says "40 candidates not yet embedded" and the button can be clicked again on reload, looking like the action didn't work or double-firing the job.
suggestedFix: After a successful queue, either call router.refresh() in the button or have the action revalidatePath('/settings/integrations'). Better: surface a small "Embedding in progress" state so repeated clicks are clearly idempotent and the count visibly moves.
verifierReasoning:
The mechanical facts in the claim are all verified in the actual code:

1. actions.ts — both triggerCandidateBackfillAction (returns {ok:true} at line 59) and triggerHnswBuildAction (returns {ok:true} at line 117) fire an Inngest event and have NO revalidatePath call anywhere. Confirmed by grep: revalidatePath only appears in the sibling outlook-actions.ts, never in actions.ts.

2. integration-buttons.tsx — BackfillButton (lines 17-40) and BuildIndexButton (lines 42-71) use only local useState(done) and useTransition. The file does NOT import useRouter and never calls router.refresh(). So the "Queued"/disabled state is purely client-local and resets on any navigation or reload.

3. page.tsx — the RSC reads unembeddedCount from countCandidatesWithoutEmbedding (lines 81-82) and renders both the "{N} candidates not yet embedded" text and a fresh enabled BackfillButton (lines 129-137). Because the action runs asynchronously via Inngest, the count won't move immediately even on a manual reload, and the button re-enables. There is no progress/in-progress indicator.

4. The HNSW card copy (lines 162-168) explicitly states the real CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY is a manual DDL step (docs/hnsw-build-runbook.md, which exists), while the BuildIndexButton toast says "Build queued" — so nothing visible changes after clicking, matching the claim.

The asymmetry is real: outlook-actions.ts in the same directory DOES call revalidatePath('/settings/integrations') on lines 100/126, and connect-outlook-card.tsx uses useRouter — so the omission in the backfill/build path is a genuine inconsistency, not the project pattern.

Where the claim overstates: (a) Severity. This is a settings/admin page gated to a signed-in owner, not a customer-facing or demo-critical flow; the action genuinely fires and succeeds, this is purely missing UI feedback. (b) The "double-firing the job" risk is largely benign — the embed/backfill-org event drives embed-batch which sweeps candidates WITHOUT an embedding, so a duplicate enqueue is effectively idempotent (already-embedded rows are skipped). And revalidatePath alone would NOT fix the perceived problem because the count moves only after the async job writes embeddings, which the claim itself correctly acknowledges. So the durable real issue is narrower than the title implies: no reload-surviving feedback and a misleading HNSW toast — a low-severity missing-feedback/clarity issue, not a correctness bug.

title: Resend on an already-accepted invite is only caught server-side after the optimistic 'Resending…' badge appears, with a confusing error
description: handleResend optimistically marks the row 'Resending…' then calls resendInviteAction.
