If the invite was accepted in another tab/session between page load and click, the action returns formError 'That invitation has already been accepted.' (actions.ts line 298-300) and the toast shows that message. The pending list itself is only refreshed on revalidate, so the now-accepted invite can still be visible with Resend/Revoke buttons until the next revalidation. This is an edge case (small team) but produces a momentarily wrong UI and a jarring error toast.
userImpact: In the rare case a teammate accepts while the owner is on the Team page, clicking Resend shows an error toast for a row that should no longer be actionable.
suggestedFix: On resend/revoke failure, call router.refresh() so the stale accepted row drops out of the pending list, and downgrade the accepted-case copy to an informational toast rather than an error.
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code. handleResend (src/app/(app)/settings/team/team-invites.tsx:109-119) calls addOptimistic({ type: 'resending', id }) synchronously inside startTransition, which renders the "Resending…" badge (lines 149-153), then awaits resendInviteAction over a network round-trip. resendInviteAction (src/app/(app)/settings/team/actions.ts:298-300) returns { ok: false, formError: 'That invitation has already been accepted.' } when existing.accepted_at is set. Line 117 surfaces that string via toast.error(...), styled as an error. The accepted_at column genuinely exists (migration 20260524000100_org_invitations.sql:79) and the accept RPC sets it. The pending list in page.tsx:63 filters .is('accepted_at', null), so an accepted invite only drops out on a fresh server render — the client component holds initialInvites in useOptimistic and never calls router.refresh() (confirmed: no useRouter import, no refresh call anywhere in the file). On the failed resend the optimistic 'resending' flag reverts (initialInvites unchanged), so the stale accepted row reappears with its Resend/Revoke buttons still actionable until the next revalidation. So the sequence the claim describes — badge flash, then jarring error toast, then a row that shouldn't be there lingering — is exactly what the code does. The trigger is a genuine multi-tab race: requires a multi-member org, the owner parked on the Team page, a teammate accepting in that window, and the owner clicking Resend. The server guard correctly prevents any wrong action (no duplicate email, no data change), so impact is purely cosmetic UX. Severity 'low' as claimed is accurate; this is polish, not a functional or security bug.

title: Apply-form toggle holds local state that can desync from the server after revalidation
description: ApplyFormToggle seeds useState(initialEnabled) and only ever mutates `enabled` from its own onToggle. revalidatePath('/settings') re-runs the RSC and passes a fresh initialEnabled prop, but because the checkbox is driven by useState (not the prop), the component does not re-sync to the server value. For a single owner this is fine, but if the value changes elsewhere (second owner, or an optimistic revert path) the checkbox can show a value that disagrees with the server. The optimistic revert in onToggle (setEnabled(!nextChecked)) is correct, so impact is limited to multi-actor drift.
userImpact: Two owners editing the same org could see the apply-form toggle in a state that no longer matches the saved value until a full reload.
evidence: const [enabled, setEnabled] = useState(initialEnabled)  // never reconciled with prop changes
suggestedFix: Either lift the toggle to a server-action <form> with the server value as the source of truth, or add a useEffect that resyncs `enabled` when initialEnabled changes (keyed render). Low priority for a single-owner demo.
verifierReasoning:
The code matches the claim exactly. In /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/settings/apply-form-toggle.tsx line 27, the component seeds `const [enabled, setEnabled] = useState(initialEnabled)` and never reconciles `enabled` with later `initialEnabled` prop changes — there is no `useEffect` keyed on `initialEnabled` and no `key` prop on the parent (page.tsx lines 136-140) to force a remount. React's `useState` only reads its initializer on the first mount, so subsequent prop changes are ignored. This is verifiably true. The server action (apply-form-actions.ts line 59) does call `revalidatePath('/settings')`, which re-runs the RSC and passes a fresh `initialEnabled`, but the mounted client component will not pick it up. So the latent code defect the claim describes is genuinely present and accurately stated.

However, the user-facing impact is minimal and the claim already self-grades it correctly as low. (1) Single-owner case (the actual 2-3 person anchor scenario): the optimistic `setEnabled(nextChecked)` already sets local state to the saved value, and the revert path (line 40, `setEnabled(!nextChecked)`) is correct, so no visible desync ever occurs. The claim concedes this. (2) Multi-actor case: I confirmed there is NO realtime subscription anywhere in src (grep for `.channel(`/`postgres_changes`/`realtime` found nothing). `revalidatePath` only affects the acting user's own render cache. Therefore two owners already would not see each other's live changes regardless of this hook — Owner A's tab stays stale until a reload no matter what, because nothing pushes Owner B's change to it. The `useState`-not-resyncing behavior is the sole cause of staleness only in the narrow sub-case of a soft client navigation back to /settings that re-runs the RSC without remounting the component. Even then, the impact is a momentarily stale non-critical boolean: the server is always authoritative (the action re-checks owner role and current state; the public /apply page reads live DB state at apply-form-actions/page.tsx for the 404 gate). No data-integrity consequence.

Net: real code-quality pattern, accurately described, low severity, leaning toward the non-issue boundary for the single-owner demo. The suggested fix (useEffect resync or lift to a server-action form) is reasonable but low priority.

title: Editing the email on the Profile form silently does nothing useful and can desync the sign-in identity from the displayed identity
description: The Profile form exposes a fully-editable Email input bound to updateProfileSchema, and updateProfileAction -> updateProfile writes whatever the user types straight into public.users.email. The auth email (auth.users.email, used to actually sign in / receive magic links) is never touched. The small grey note says 'Display only', but the field is a normal editable input that shows a green 'Profile saved' toast on submit. A non-technical owner will reasonably believe they changed their email. From then on the app displays one email (public.users.email) while the account still signs in with the original auth email — and the magic-link / invite-acceptance flow keys on the auth email. There is no constraint forcing public.users.email to equal the auth email, so they drift permanently with a success toast confirming the wrong mental model.
userImpact: Owner 'changes' their email in Settings, sees 'Profile saved', then can't understand why login links still go to the old address and why teammates see a stale address. In a live demo, the founder editing the email and getting a success toast that does nothing real looks broken.
evidence: <Input type="email" autoComplete="email" {...field} /> with only a grey note 'Display only. Changing the email used to sign in is a Phase 2 feature.' — and updateProfile() does `.update({ full_name: ..., email: patch.email })` on public.users with no link back to auth.users.email.
suggestedFix: Until Phase 2 ships real auth-email change, make the email field readOnly/disabled (like the non-owner org fields) so the user cannot edit a value that won't take effect, OR drop `email` from updateProfileSchema and the action so it cannot be written. A disabled field with the note is honest; an editable field with a success toast is a silent no-op.
verifierReasoning:
Verified every load-bearing assertion against the actual code; the claim holds.

1. The email field at src/app/(app)/settings/profile-form.tsx:83 is a normal editable <Input type="email" autoComplete="email" {...field} /> with only a grey note (lines 85-87: "Display only. Changing the email used to sign in is a Phase 2 feature."). It is NOT disabled/readOnly — contrast organization-form.tsx:79,98 where the project already uses readOnly={!isOwner} for fields the user shouldn't change.

2. updateProfileAction (actions.ts:39-50) -> updateProfile (src/lib/db/profiles.ts:41-49) writes `email: patch.email` straight into public.users via `.update({ full_name, email })` and returns ok. On success the form fires a green toast.success('Profile saved') (profile-form.tsx:45). auth.users.email is never touched.

3. The auth email is what actually drives identity: sign-in is signInWithOtp({ email }) (sign-in-form.tsx:102) and the magic link goes to the registered auth address; invite-acceptance keys on invitation.email re-checked in the /auth/callback RPC (accept-invite/[token]/route.ts + handle_new_user). public.users.email plays no role in either.

4. Display drift confirmed in three surfaces, all of which prefer public.users.email: top nav layout (src/app/(app)/layout.tsx:34 `profile.data.email ?? user.email`), settings page (page.tsx:53), and the Team page which selects and renders public.users.email for every teammate (team/page.tsx:58,111,114). So an owner's edit visibly "sticks" and propagates to colleagues' view while magic links keep going to the old address.

5. No reconciliation exists: handle_new_user (migration 20260513151021:160-169) copies new.email into public.users only on `after insert on auth.users`; grep across all migrations found no `after update` trigger, no on_auth_user_updated, and no constraint linking the two columns. They drift permanently.

So the mechanism is exactly as described: editable field + success toast + display precedence for public.users.email + no link back to the sign-in identity = a confident-looking no-op that desyncs displayed identity from sign-in identity.

Severity re-grade to medium (down from claimed high): the impact is genuine user confusion and a stale teammate-visible email, plus a demo-embarrassing moment, with a clean fix (disable the field or drop email from updateProfileSchema, mirroring the org-form readOnly pattern). But it crosses no security boundary — RLS/tenancy intact, login still works on the real address, no data loss, no cross-tenant leakage, no auth bypass. There is also an existing (if ambiguously worded) disclaimer, which partially mitigates the 'silent' framing. That bounded blast radius is medium, not high.

title: Outlook reconnect UPDATE path leaves a stale organization_id and never re-fires the subscription-create event
description: On first connect, upsertOutlookCredentials inserts a row (organization_id filled by the set_org trigger). On RECONNECT the insert collides on the UNIQUE user_id, writeResult.ok is false, and the code falls into a manual service-role UPDATE that sets tokens/email/scopes and clears revoked_at — but it does NOT reset subscription_id / subscription_client_state / subscription_expires_at / delta_link. revokeOutlookCredentials nulls all of those on disconnect, so after a reconnect the row has revoked_at=null and subscription_id=null. Then the code unconditionally fires 'outlook/subscription-create-requested' (line 193) for BOTH the insert and update branches, which is correct — but only because the update branch falls through to the same send. The real risk: organization_id is read once (line 146) for the event, and createOutlookSubscription guards `result.data.organization_id !== data.organization_id`. If the user changed orgs between the original connect and the reconnect (accept_invitation moves users between orgs and even deletes the old org), the credential row still carries the OLD organization_id from the original insert trigger, but the event carries the freshly-read NEW organization_id. createOutlookSubscription then throws NonRetriableError('credentials not in claimed organization') and the subscription is never created — Outlook silently never ingests mail, with no user-visible error (the callback already redirected with outlook=connected).
userImpact: A recruiter who switched organisations (e.g. accepted an invite to a different agency) reconnects Outlook, sees 'Outlook connected', but emails never appear on candidate timelines. No error is shown anywhere the user can see it.
evidence: Update branch sets `scopes: [...OUTLOOK_SCOPES], revoked_at: null` but never updates organization_id; createOutlookSubscription: `if (result.data.organization_id !== data.organization_id) throw new NonRetriableError('credentials not in claimed organization')`.
suggestedFix: In the reconnect UPDATE branch, also set organization_id to the freshly-resolved organizationId (and null subscription_* + delta_link for cleanliness). Better: replace the insert-then-manual-update dance with a single upsert onConflict('user_id') that always writes organization_id from the resolved value.
verifierReasoning:
I attempted to refute the claim and could not — the bug is real in a materially equivalent form, and the recovery path the claim assumed exists is also broken.

Verified mechanics:
1. accept_invitation RPC (supabase/migrations/20260524000100_org_invitations.sql:188-205) does move a user between orgs (update users set organization_id = v_invite.organization_id) and deletes the old org only when no other users reference it.
2. outlook_credentials.organization_id is `references organizations(id) on delete cascade` (migration 20260519092945:44). This creates TWO distinct cases:
   - Case A (old org deleted, user was sole member): the cascade removes the credential row, so on reconnect the INSERT in upsertOutlookCredentials succeeds with the set_org trigger filling the NEW org. No bug.
   - Case B (old org survives — other recruiters remain, the normal 2-3 person agency case): the credential row persists carrying the OLD organization_id.
3. In Case B the reconnect INSERT collides on UNIQUE(user_id), writeResult.ok is false, and the manual UPDATE branch (route.ts:166-178) sets tokens/email/scopes and revoked_at:null but does NOT set organization_id. The set_organization_id() trigger is `before insert` only (migration ...092945:112-114) and its body only assigns when null (migration 20260517204504), so it never corrects the org on UPDATE.
4. The subscription-create event fires with the freshly-read NEW organizationId (route.ts:146 → 197).
5. createOutlookSubscription (create-outlook-subscription.ts:70-72) throws NonRetriableError('credentials not in claimed organization') when result.data.organization_id !== data.organization_id. NonRetriableError bypasses the retries:3, so the subscription is never created.
6. The callback already redirected ?outlook=connected (route.ts:209) before the async Inngest event runs, so the user sees success.
7. The settings UI (integrations/page.tsx:99-103) derives status ONLY from revoked_at — it never inspects subscription_id — so it shows green "connected" with no error.

Additionally, the claim UNDERSTATES the problem: the 6-hourly cron does NOT provide recovery. listExpiringSubscriptions filters `.is(revoked_at,null).lt(subscription_expires_at,cutoff)`. After reconnect the row has subscription_expires_at=NULL (revoke nulled it; UPDATE branch didn't reset it), and in Postgres NULL < cutoff is NULL/not-true, so the cron never selects the row. There is no automatic recovery path — the only fix is the user disconnecting and reconnecting again, which still hits the same UPDATE branch and fails identically.

End-to-end impact is exactly as described: a recruiter who switched agencies via invite reconnects Outlook, sees "connected", and emails silently never ingest, with no user-visible error.

Severity downgraded from high to medium for the live-demo/anchor-customer context: the anchor is a single 2-3 person agency where org-switching via invite-accept is a rare administrative event, and even rarer combined with a prior Outlook connect on the same user. It is a genuine silent total-failure when triggered, but the trigger sequence (connect Outlook in org A → accept invite to org B while A still has other users → reconnect Outlook) is an uncommon edge case, not a daily flow. The suggested fix (write organization_id + reset subscription_*/delta_link in the UPDATE branch, or use a single onConflict('user_id') upsert) is correct and cheap.

title: Usage 'this month' boundary is UTC but per-row timestamps render in local time, dropping/duplicating calls at month edges
description: The month filter uses Date.UTC(year, month, 1) as monthStart and queries `created_at >= monthStart`. But the per-row 'When' column renders new Date(row.created_at).toLocaleString('en-GB'), i.e. the operator's LOCAL timezone. For a UK operator in BST (UTC+1) on the 1st of the month, an AI call made at 00:30 local time on the 1st was made at 23:30 UTC on the LAST day of the previous month — it is excluded from 'this month' totals yet, if it scraped in, would render with a local date of the 1st. Symmetrically, calls in the last local-evening of a month (e.g. 30 June 23:30 BST = 30 June 22:30 UTC) are correctly included, but a call at 00:30 BST on 1 July (23:30 UTC 30 June) is wrongly attributed to June's window. The headline total, the per-purpose breakdown, AND the match-scoring ceiling progress all silently use the UTC window while the recruiter reads local dates, so the numbers won't reconcile with what they see in the table around month boundaries.
userImpact: On/near the 1st of the month the spend total and the match-scoring ceiling bar can look wrong vs the listed calls; a founder reconciling 'why does it say £4 but I count £5 of calls' has no explanation. Worse near the ceiling, the cap could appear not-yet-hit when UTC says it is (or vice versa).
evidence: monthStart = new Date(Date.UTC(now.getUTCFullYear(), now.getUTCMonth(), 1)).toISOString(); ... query .gte('created_at', monthStart) ... but rows render `new Date(row.created_at).toLocaleString('en-GB')`.
suggestedFix: Pick one timezone basis. For a UK product, compute monthStart in Europe/London (or document that the window is UTC and render the table timestamps in UTC too) so the boundary and the displayed dates agree.
verifierReasoning:
The code facts in the claim are accurate. In src/app/(app)/settings/usage/page.tsx:71-72 the month window is computed in UTC (`Date.UTC(now.getUTCFullYear(), now.getUTCMonth(), 1)`), the query filters `.gte('created_at', monthStart)` (line 95), and per-row timestamps render via `new Date(row.created_at).toLocaleString('en-GB')` (line 243) with no explicit timezone. `created_at` is `timestamptz` (migration 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:380). So there genuinely IS a basis mismatch: the window edge is UTC, the displayed date is locale-formatted with the ambient timezone. At a month boundary a call near midnight could appear in the table with a date that disagrees with which month the totals attribute it to.

However, the claim materially overstates the impact in two ways:

1. This is a React Server Component (`async function UsagePage()`, no 'use client'). `toLocaleString('en-GB')` therefore executes on the SERVER, not "the operator's LOCAL timezone" as the claim asserts. On Vercel the server runs in UTC by default, so in the actual deployed environment the displayed per-row date and the UTC window would AGREE, and no discrepancy would be visible. The mismatch only surfaces if the server's TZ is non-UTC.

2. Even granting a TZ-offset environment (e.g. UTC+1), the window is internally consistent — the headline total, per-purpose breakdown, and match-scoring ceiling all use the SAME UTC window, so they reconcile with each other. The only thing that can look off is a per-row displayed date for calls timestamped within the ~1-hour offset band on the single day at a month edge. That is a narrow cosmetic edge-case affecting at most a couple of rows, one or two days a month, and only if someone is actively reconciling at midnight on the 1st.

For a live client demo this is effectively invisible: it requires a non-UTC server deployment, a month-boundary date, and a call timestamped in the offset window simultaneously. There is no data loss, no double-counting in the totals (they share one window), and no security/tenancy impact. The suggested fix (pick one timezone basis, render table timestamps in the same basis as the window) is reasonable polish but not demo-blocking.

title: Resend refreshes expiry via service-role but the displayed 'Expires' date does not update, and a non-stale invite shows nothing changed
description: resendInviteAction only refreshes expires_at when the invite is within 24h of expiry (isStale). When the invite is NOT stale, no UPDATE happens and the email is re-sent with the same accept URL — which is correct. But the optimistic UI marks the row 'Resending…' then reverts to the original initialInvites row on settle; for the stale-refresh case the service-role UPDATE did change expires_at, yet revalidatePath('/settings/team') re-reads the page so the new expiry should appear. The subtle issue: if the service-role UPDATE fails (updateErr), the code deliberately falls through and still returns ok, re-sending the email with the OLD (about-to-expire) expiry while the toast says 'Invitation resent'. The recipient then gets a fresh email whose link may expire within hours, with no signal to the owner that the refresh failed.
userImpact: Owner resends an almost-expired invite, sees success, but the teammate's link still dies within a day — and the owner has no way to know the refresh silently failed.
evidence: if (updateErr) { Sentry.captureException(...) // Fall through — re-firing the email with the old (still-valid) expiry is acceptable } ... then returns { ok: true } with the email sent against `existing.token` whose expiry was not extended.
suggestedFix: If the stale-refresh UPDATE fails, either retry once or return a soft warning so the UI can tell the owner 'resent, but the link still expires soon — try again'. At minimum, do not show an unqualified 'Invitation resent' success when the expiry extension you intended did not happen.
verifierReasoning:
The substantive defect is real and confirmed end-to-end. In src/app/(app)/settings/team/actions.ts:307-321, resendInviteAction only attempts the service-role expires_at refresh when isStale (within 24h of expiry, or already expired). On the refresh path, if the service-role UPDATE returns updateErr, the code captures it to Sentry then deliberately falls through (lines 314-320) and still re-fires the email against existing.token, ultimately returning { ok: true } at line 380. The caller (team-invites.tsx:113-114) shows an unqualified toast.success('Invitation resent') for any result.ok===true, with no warning that the intended extension failed.

This genuinely matters because expires_at IS enforced at accept time in TWO places: (1) the pre-sign-in usability check isInvitationUsable() returns reason:'expired' when expires_at <= now() (src/lib/invitations/lookup.ts:46), redirecting to /sign-in?error=expired-invite; and (2) the canonical accept_invitation() RPC rejects with reason 'invalid' when v_invite.expires_at <= now() (migration 20260524000100_org_invitations.sql:171). So if the stale-refresh UPDATE silently fails, the owner sees 'Invitation resent', the recipient gets a fresh email, but the link can die within hours and neither party gets any signal. The suggested fix (surface a soft warning / avoid unqualified success when the extension you intended did not happen) is valid.

Severity 'low' is correct: the swallowed-error path is narrow. It requires (a) the invite to be within 24h of expiry, (b) the service-role UPDATE to actually fail — createServiceClient (src/lib/supabase/service.ts) uses the service-role key which bypasses RLS and there is intentionally no UPDATE policy, so a failure here is only a transient DB error or a misconfigured service-role key, not a routine occurrence, and (c) the recipient not to click within the remaining hours. It is not security or data-loss, and the happy path (UPDATE succeeds) works fine.

title: Backfill/Build buttons never refresh the page, so the unembedded count and 'Ready to build' state stay stale until a manual reload
description: Both BackfillButton and BuildIndexButton fire an Inngest event and, on success, set local done=true (disabling the button) and toast. Neither the action (triggerCandidateBackfillAction / triggerHnswBuildAction) nor the button calls revalidatePath or router.refresh. The IntegrationsPage computes unembeddedCount and the HNSW eligibility server-side. After a backfill is queued, the '<N> candidates not yet embedded' line keeps showing the old N (the button is the only thing that changes, to 'Queued'). If the user reloads before Inngest finishes they may see the same N and re-trigger; if they reload after, it updates. Since done=true blocks re-fire within the same render, the main harm is a confusing stale count, not a duplicate job.
userImpact: After clicking Backfill, the page still says e.g. '37 candidates not yet embedded' indefinitely, making the founder think nothing happened. They may reload repeatedly looking for movement.
evidence: onClick fires triggerCandidateBackfillAction() then `setDone(true)` + toast; the action returns { ok: true } with no revalidatePath. The page reads `unembeddedCount` from countCandidatesWithoutEmbedding(supabase) at request time only.
suggestedFix: After a successful queue, call router.refresh() in the client button (or revalidatePath('/settings/integrations') in the action) so the count/eligibility re-reads, with copy that embeddings take a moment to appear.
verifierReasoning:
Verified all three load-bearing assertions directly in the code.

1. src/app/(app)/settings/integrations/integration-buttons.tsx: BackfillButton (lines 17-40) and BuildIndexButton (lines 42-71) use useTransition + a local `done` boolean. On res.ok they call toast.success + setDone(true) only. There is NO useRouter import and NO router.refresh() call. useTransition only governs the action's pending flag; it does not revalidate or re-render the server component tree.

2. src/app/(app)/settings/integrations/actions.ts: triggerCandidateBackfillAction (line 59) and triggerHnswBuildAction (line 117) both fire an inngest.send and return { ok: true }. There is NO revalidatePath/revalidateTag import or call. Grep confirms revalidatePath('/settings/integrations') exists ONLY in the sibling outlook-actions.ts (lines 100/126) — so the team knows and uses the pattern for the Outlook flow but did not apply it to backfill/build.

3. src/app/(app)/settings/integrations/page.tsx: unembeddedCount is read at render time via countCandidatesWithoutEmbedding(supabase) (lines 81-82, helper in src/lib/db/embeddings.ts:155). The 'N candidates not yet embedded' line (lines 129-137) and the HNSW 'Ready to build' eligibility (eligible = rowCount >= 100 && !built, line 197; rendered lines 199-224) are computed server-side per request. Nothing re-reads them after the button fires.

So the stale-display behaviour is genuine: the count and 'Ready to build' state remain at their request-time values until a manual reload. Confirmed exactly as described.

Severity stays low (matching the claim's own honest framing): the done=true guard prevents re-firing within the render, so there is no duplicate-job harm. The actual embedding is async via Inngest, so even a router.refresh() immediately after queueing would usually still show the same N (work hasn't completed) — the count only meaningfully changes seconds-to-minutes later, when only a reload helps. The success toast ('Backfill queued — embeddings will appear shortly') plus the button flipping to 'Queued' DO give positive feedback that the action registered, which materially undercuts the 'founder thinks nothing happened / reloads repeatedly' impact. Net: real, cosmetic missing-feedback issue on the numeric count / eligibility line; no functional, data, or tenancy harm.

title: Pending-invite 'time ago' renders with server-now then client-now, risking a hydration mismatch and inconsistent inviter label
description: TeamInvites is a Client Component that renders real pending rows (initialInvites) with formatTimeAgo(row.created_at) and no fixed `now` reference, so the server-rendered string uses the server clock and the client hydration uses the browser clock. Across a bucket boundary (e.g. server 'just now' vs client '1 minute ago') React emits a hydration mismatch warning and swaps text. Separately, the optimistic add sets inviterLabel: 'you' for the ghost while the server resolves the real inviterLabel from the members list, so the same invite can flash 'Invited by you' then 'Invited by <name>'. Cosmetic, but on a demo screen it flickers.
userImpact: Brief flicker of the 'invited X ago' text and 'invited by' label on the Team screen on load; in dev, console hydration warnings.
evidence: formatTimeAgo signature defaults now to `new Date()` per call: `formatTimeAgo(iso, now = new Date())`. In team-invites.tsx it is called as `formatTimeAgo(row.created_at)` with no stable now, inside a 'use client' component.
suggestedFix: Pass a single stable `now` captured once (e.g. via a useState(() => new Date()) or a prop from the server) into formatTimeAgo within the client component, and keep the optimistic inviterLabel consistent with the resolved label.
verifierReasoning: The core mechanism is real and verified in code. formatTimeAgo in src/lib/date.ts:39 has signature `formatTimeAgo(iso, now = new Date())`, so `now` is freshly captured per call. team-invites.tsx:156 calls `formatTimeAgo(row.created_at)` with no stable `now`, and team-invites.tsx is a `'use client'` component (line 1). It is rendered by the async Server Component at src/app/(app)/settings/team/page.tsx:132 via a static import — there is no `ssr: false` or dynamic() wrapper (verified by grep) — so on initial page load the Client Component is server-rendered to HTML using the server clock, then hydrated using the browser clock. When a pending invite's created_at sits near a bucket boundary (e.g. the 30s 'just now' threshold), the SSR string and the hydration string differ, producing a React hydration text mismatch (dev-console warning) and a single text-node swap. The function's own JSDoc (lines 33-38) explicitly documents this contract: 'Deterministic given a stable now reference — pass now to keep SSR + CSR output identical when used in client components after hydration.' The caller violates that documented contract, and there is no suppressHydrationWarning anywhere in the codebase (grep confirmed). The optimistic inviterLabel sub-claim is also factually accurate: the ghost row sets inviterLabel: 'you' (line 211) and the server resolves the real label from the members list (page.tsx:81); the dedup on lines 92-93 drops the ghost once the revalidated real row arrives, so the label changes from 'you' to the resolved name. However that part is intended optimistic-then-reconcile UI (a row replacement keyed optimistic-<email> -> UUID, not an in-place flicker) and 'you' is semantically correct for an invite the current user just sent, so it is barely a defect. Net: the headline hydration-mismatch finding is genuine and correctly graded low. It is narrow (only fires within the SSR->hydration timing delta when a row's age is near a bucket boundary), the visible effect is a single token swap plus a dev-only warning, and the same unstable-now pattern exists in ~10 other files (candidate-table, jobs-table, client-management-tabs, shortlist-list, etc.). The 'ui-broken' category slightly overstates it — nothing is functionally broken — but the underlying issue holds.

title: Shared MSAL singleton can persist another user's refresh token during concurrent Outlook token refresh
description: getMsal() returns a process-wide singleton ConfidentialClientApplication (cachedMsal at line 102-126) with a shared in-memory token cache. In getValidAccessToken, after refreshing, the code reads the rotated refresh token back out by serialising the WHOLE shared cache and picking the first RefreshToken entry whose secret differs from the current user's plaintext RT: `const match = entries.find((rt) => rt.secret && rt.secret !== refreshTokenPlaintext)`. If the same Node/Vercel-Lambda process has refreshed tokens for more than one user (the 6-hourly refresh-outlook-subscription cron iterates every expiring user sequentially in one run; server actions can also call getValidAccessToken concurrently), the cache contains multiple users' RefreshToken entries and `find` can match a DIFFERENT user's secret. That foreign RT is then encrypted and written onto THIS user's outlook_credentials row via updateOutlookAccessToken (line 354-360).
userImpact: Recruiter A's Outlook row can silently end up storing recruiter B's Microsoft refresh token. On the next sync, A's connection authenticates as B — pulling B's inbox emails (potentially a different tenant/mailbox) onto candidate/contact timelines, and breaking A's own connection. Cross-user credential corruption and a data-exposure path that is invisible until someone notices the wrong emails appearing.
suggestedFix: Do not read the rotated RT from a shared cache by 'any entry that isn't mine'. Either (a) construct a fresh ConfidentialClientApplication per refresh call (no shared cache state), or (b) scope the lookup to the current account's home_account_id (match.home_account_id === the user's MSAL account id) so you can only ever pick this user's rotated token, or (c) use a cache plugin keyed per user. Until fixed, the cron's sequential per-user refresh and any concurrent server-action refresh are unsafe.
verifierReasoning: Verified against actual code and the installed MSAL libraries. (1) getMsal() (src/lib/integrations/outlook.ts:104-127) returns one process-wide ConfidentialClientApplication with NO cachePlugin configured, so its in-memory NodeStorage token cache is shared across every getValidAccessToken call in the process. (2) @azure/msal-common@16.6.1 generateCredentialKey (CacheHelpers.mjs:10-29) keys refresh tokens by homeAccountId first; ResponseHandler.mjs derives homeAccountIdentifier from the refresh response's client_info/id_token (line 105), creates a new RefreshToken entity under that key (line 220), and saveCacheRecord (line 143) WITHOUT clearing the cache. So sequentially refreshing User A then User B leaves BOTH users' RefreshToken entries resident under distinct keys. (3) The refresh-path lookup at outlook.ts:347 — entries.find((rt) => rt.secret && rt.secret !== refreshTokenPlaintext) — picks the first RT entry whose secret differs from the CURRENT user's OLD plaintext. With A's and B's entries both present, find() can return A's secret (iteration order is not user-scoped). That foreign RT is encrypted (line 354) and written onto User B's outlook_credentials row via updateOutlookAccessToken (lines 355-360). (4) Trigger paths are real: the 6-hourly cron (refresh-outlook-subscription.ts:98-105) iterates EVERY expiring user sequentially in one run on the same singleton; concurrency:{limit:1} only stops overlapping cron RUNS, not sequential users within a run. Server-action sendMail -> getValidAccessToken adds concurrent pressure on warm lambdas. (5) Downstream: sync-outlook-history.ts uses the resulting token against /me inbox, so on B's next sync, B's row (now holding A's RT) mints A's access token and pulls A's inbox, writing email activities attributed to B. Notably the CORRECT pattern already exists in the SAME file: exchangeCodeForTokens (lines 224-226) scopes the lookup by home_account_id === response.account?.homeAccountId, and the serialized entry exposes home_account_id (declared in the type at lines 343-344) — but the refresh path ignores it. The bug is real and the suggested fix (scope by homeAccountId, or use response.account.homeAccountId which MSAL already returns) is exactly correct. Severity held at high rather than critical only because it requires >=2 connected Outlook users whose refreshes land in the same warm process; for the 2-3 person single-org anchor this is mostly same-org wrong-recruiter cross-contamination + broken connections, but the multi-tenant SaaS design makes true cross-tenant leakage possible.

title: Per-call Math.ceil rounding inflates embedding spend on the Usage dashboard by up to ~400x
description: ai_usage.cost_pence is an integer column (phase1 schema line 378). calcEmbedCostPence does `Math.ceil((5 * totalTokens) / 1_000_000)`. A typical single-candidate embed (~300-800 tokens) costs Math.ceil(0.0015..0.004) = 1 penny, when the true cost is a tiny fraction of a penny. The same ceil-to-integer pattern is in claude.ts calcCostPence (line 46). Because record_ai_usage is called once PER embed/parse call, every sub-penny call is rounded up to a whole penny independently, so the error compounds linearly with call volume.
userImpact: The /settings/usage 'This month' total, the 'By purpose' breakdown, and 'Top 10 expensive calls' all massively overstate real AI spend for the high-volume embedding/parsing purposes (candidate_embed, job_embed, linkedin_candidate_embed, cv_parse). An owner backfilling 1,000 candidates sees ~£10 of 'embedding' cost when the real Voyage bill is ~£0.03. This defeats the dashboard's entire purpose (per-tenant cost visibility for SaaS pricing) and will alarm the client in the demo.
suggestedFix: Stop rounding each call up to a whole penny. Store cost in a finer unit (e.g. millipence/microcurrency as numeric, or store raw token counts and compute cost in the dashboard) so aggregate spend reflects reality. If the integer column must stay, accumulate fractional pence and only round the displayed monthly aggregate, not each individual row.
verifierReasoning:
The rounding mechanism is real and confirmed. `calcEmbedCostPence` (src/lib/ai/voyage.ts:55-57) and `calcCostPence` (src/lib/ai/claude.ts:44-47) both compute `Math.ceil((pence_per_mtok * tokens) / 1_000_000)`, so any nonzero-usage call is forced to a minimum of 1 penny. `cost_pence` is `integer not null` (confirmed in supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:378 and in the record_ai_usage RPC param at line 136). The /settings/usage dashboard (src/app/(app)/settings/usage/page.tsx) sums `cost_pence` per row in all three surfaced sections: `totalPence` (line 99), `byPurpose` (lines 101-110), and `topExpensive` (lines 119-121). Since `record_ai_usage` fires once per embed/parse call, sub-penny costs are independently rounded up and then summed, so the per-row ceiling error does accumulate linearly with call count. So the inflation phenomenon genuinely exists for paths that log one row per low-token call (single CV parse `cv_parse`, single candidate embed in parse-cv, `linkedin_candidate_embed`, `search_query_embed`).

HOWEVER the claim's headline magnitude is materially wrong. The cited worst case ("backfilling 1,000 candidates sees ~£10 of embedding cost when the real bill is ~£0.03", "up to ~400x") assumes one rounded-up ai_usage row per candidate. The actual bulk backfill path (src/lib/inngest/functions/embed-batch.ts:132) chunks rows by `VOYAGE_BATCH_CAP = 128` (line 32) into a SINGLE embed() call per 128 candidates, and the batch embed text passes `cvText = null` (line 133), so each candidate contributes only a short structured string (~30-60 tokens). 1,000 candidates is therefore ~8 batches = ~8 rows = ~8p recorded, not £10 — the example is overstated by roughly 125x. Only the single-call paths (cv_parse Claude call, parse-cv candidate_embed which DOES include up to ~7,500 CV tokens, linkedin_candidate_embed) round one penny each; the absolute overstatement there is at most ~1p per candidate event (e.g. 1,000 CV uploads -> ~£10 recorded where true is maybe ~£0.40). Meaningful for cost-visibility accuracy, but the absolute amounts are pennies-to-low-pounds and the highest-volume operation is batched, so it will not produce the alarming demo number the claim describes.

Net: the bug class and the suggested fix (store finer-grained units / accumulate fractional pence, round only the displayed aggregate) are valid, but the impact is much smaller than claimed. Adjusted to low.

title: Usage page silently shows £0 spend if the ai_usage query errors
description: The ai_usage read destructures only `data` and ignores `error`: `const { data: rowsRaw } = await usageClient...` then `const rows = rowsRaw ?? []`. If the query fails (transient error, RLS edge case, malformed cast), rowsRaw is null, rows becomes [], and the whole dashboard renders £0.00 across the headline total, per-purpose table, ceiling bar, and top-10 list — identical to a genuinely-zero-spend org.
userImpact: The owner opens the Usage page (the single source of truth for AI cost) during the demo, a transient DB hiccup occurs, and they are shown a confident '£0.00 — No AI usage recorded yet this month.' They conclude AI costs nothing / nothing is being tracked. There is no error state to distinguish 'query failed' from 'truly zero'.
evidence:
const { data: rowsRaw } = await usageClient
  .from('ai_usage')
  .select('id, organization_id, ...')
  .gte('created_at', monthStart)
  .order('created_at', { ascending: false })
const rows: AiUsageRow[] = rowsRaw ?? []
suggestedFix: Capture and handle the error: destructure `{ data, error }`, and on error render an explicit 'Couldn't load usage — try again' state (and Sentry.captureException) rather than collapsing to a zero dashboard that reads as 'no spend'.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed at src/app/(app)/settings/usage/page.tsx:90. The read destructures only `{ data: rowsRaw }` and discards `error`. On a PostgREST query failure, `data` is null, so line 97 `const rows = rowsRaw ?? []` collapses to an empty array. Every downstream aggregate then computes zero: `totalPence` = 0 (line 99), `purposeBreakdown` = [] , `matchSpendPence` = 0, `topExpensive` = []. The render shows a 0 headline, a 0% ceiling bar, and — for the By-purpose and Top-10 sections — the literal copy "No AI usage recorded yet this month." (lines 184, 226). This is rendering-identical to a genuinely zero-spend org. The code never throws, so the existing src/app/(app)/error.tsx boundary (which would otherwise catch and Sentry-report it) does NOT fire, and there is no other Sentry capture on this path. So a transient DB/network error silently presents a confident, zeroed-out cost dashboard with no error state — exactly as described. Two minor inaccuracies in the claim's evidence do not change the substance: (1) formatPence(0) renders "0p", not "£0.00" (formatPence only uses the £x.xx form for >=100p); (2) an "RLS edge case" is an unlikely trigger because RLS filters rows rather than erroring — the realistic trigger is a genuine transient/connection error. The omission is also inconsistent with the project's own norm: the sibling error.tsx already imports and uses Sentry.captureException, and CLAUDE.md explicitly warns against swallowing failures. Severity held at medium (not higher): it is a read-only RSC over RLS on a small table with no exotic joins, so transient-failure probability during a short demo is low, and the consequence is a misleading display / credibility hit rather than data loss or a broken core flow. The suggested fix (destructure error, render an explicit "couldn't load" state, Sentry.captureException) is appropriate.

title: Match-scoring spend ceiling fails open when the spend lookup errors, allowing unbounded Sonnet spend
description: The on-demand Explain action enforces the same month-to-date match ceiling shown on /settings/usage, but the guard only fires when the lookup succeeded: `if (spendResult.ok && spendResult.data >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE) { ...block }`. If getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth returns `{ ok: false }` (it returns this on any query error, ai-summaries.ts:241-246), the `&&` short-circuits and the synchronous Sonnet call proceeds. The ceiling that the Usage page advertises ('Sonnet match-scoring stops automatically when the org crosses this cap') is therefore silently disabled whenever the spend query fails.
userImpact: If the spend-aggregation query starts failing, the advertised cost cap stops protecting the org. A recruiter clicking 'Explain' repeatedly can burn through and past the £100/month Sonnet budget with no brake, and the Usage page's 'ceiling' indicator becomes a false promise. The same fail-open shape exists in the precompute path.
suggestedFix: Fail closed on an unknown spend: treat `!spendResult.ok` as 'cannot confirm we're under the cap' and refuse the Sonnet call (return the same 'match scoring paused' message) plus a Sentry warning, rather than proceeding. Only run the model when you can positively confirm spend < ceiling.
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code; the claim's mechanics are exactly correct. In src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/matches/actions.ts:131-154 the guard is `if (spendResult.ok && spendResult.data >= env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE) { ...block }`. getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth (src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:225-249) returns `{ ok: false, code: 'internal' }` on any PostgREST error (lines 241-246). When ok===false the `&&` short-circuits, the block is skipped, and execution falls straight through to the synchronous Sonnet call at line 157 with no else/no-ok handling. So when the ai_usage aggregate read errors, the advertised ceiling does not fire. The Usage page (src/app/(app)/settings/usage/page.tsx:162-163) does advertise it as automatic: "Sonnet match-scoring stops automatically when the org crosses this month-to-date spend cap." The £100 figure is real: MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE defaults to 10_000 pence (src/lib/env.ts:83). The precompute path (src/lib/inngest/functions/precompute-matches-for-job.ts:124-140) shares the same fail-open outcome but does it DELIBERATELY: on !ok it logs to Sentry and returns spendPence:0 with a comment "better to over-spend by one batch than to permanently block scoring when the aggregate read flakes." So the precompute fail-open is intentional/observable; the Explain action's is silent (no Sentry on the !ok branch) and contradicts its own fix comment at line 127. The bug is genuine. I downgrade from medium to low because the realistic blast radius is bounded: it only manifests while an independent ai_usage query failure is already occurring, and the Explain action is a single per-click manual Sonnet call (a few pence each) with no internal loop — burning "past £100" would require thousands of manual clicks during a sustained query outage, so the "unbounded runaway spend" framing overstates it. The correct, low-cost fix is to fail closed (or at minimum mirror the precompute path's Sentry-logged explicit decision) instead of silently proceeding.

title: Reconnecting Outlook shows the original connection date, not the reconnect date
description: On reconnect (status was 'revoked'), the callback UPDATE path resets revoked_at to null and overwrites tokens/email but does not touch created_at. The integrations page passes `connectedAt={outlookRow?.created_at}` (page.tsx:151) and the card renders `Connected ${new Date(connectedAt).toLocaleDateString()}` (connect-outlook-card.tsx:134-136), so a reconnected account still displays the date of the very first connection.
userImpact: A recruiter who disconnects and reconnects sees a 'Connected <old date>' that predates their actual reconnection, which is mildly confusing when verifying the integration is freshly working — low impact but visible in the demo if reconnect is shown.
suggestedFix: On the reconnect UPDATE, also set a dedicated connected_at/reconnected_at timestamp (or set created_at = now() if that column is intended to mean 'current connection start'), and surface that in the card so the displayed date reflects the live connection.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the full lifecycle and confirmed the claim holds.

1. Disconnect (src/app/(app)/settings/integrations/outlook-actions.ts:121) calls revokeOutlookCredentials, which sets revoked_at and nulls tokens — it does NOT delete the row. So the original created_at survives a disconnect.

2. Reconnect (src/app/api/outlook/callback/route.ts:166-187): because outlook_credentials.user_id is UNIQUE, the INSERT in upsertOutlookCredentials collides (writeResult.ok === false), and the UPDATE fallback runs. Its .update({...}) payload (lines 168-177) sets microsoft_tenant_id, microsoft_user_id, microsoft_email, refresh/access tokens, access_token_expires_at, scopes, and revoked_at: null. created_at is never in the payload. Confirmed exactly as the evidence cited.

3. Schema (supabase/migrations/20260519092945_outlook_credentials.sql:70): created_at timestamptz not null default now(). There is a set_updated_at trigger on UPDATE (lines 116-118) but NO trigger that touches created_at, so created_at is frozen at the first INSERT's value.

4. Display: page.tsx:151 passes connectedAt={outlookRow?.created_at ?? null}; connect-outlook-card.tsx:134-136 renders `Connected ${new Date(connectedAt).toLocaleDateString()}` next to a green 'Active' badge.

Net: after disconnect+reconnect, the card shows 'Connected <original first-connection date>', not the reconnect date. The bug is real and materially as described.

Severity is correctly low: it requires the specific disconnect->reconnect path, the displayed value is still a valid real date (the original connection, not NaN/broken), there is no functional breakage, no data corruption, and no tenancy/security implication. The only impact is a mildly stale/confusing label visible if a reconnect is demonstrated. 'low' is the right grade — not higher (purely cosmetic, no functional/security impact), not non-issue (the misleading display genuinely occurs).

title: Send check-in succeeds but the dormant client stays on the dashboard (no client refresh after revalidatePath)
description: The Send check-in flow lives entirely on the dashboard. On a successful send, handleSend() calls onSent() which only flips the modal's local open state to false. The server action sendOutreachAction (outreach-actions.ts:343) calls revalidatePath('/') and revalidatePath(`/clients/${clientId}`), but the dashboard page the recruiter is currently looking at was already rendered by the RSC and there is no router.refresh() or navigation on the client. revalidatePath marks the server cache stale for the NEXT request — it does not re-render the page the user is staring at. So after sending a check-in, the just-contacted client remains in the 'Dormant clients' widget with its old 'last contact X days ago' text and a live 'Send check-in' button.
userImpact: During the demo, the founder clicks 'Send check-in', the email sends, the modal closes — and the dormant client is still sitting in the list looking un-contacted. They'll likely click 'Send check-in' again on the same client, sending a second email, because the UI gave no sign the first one landed. Only a manual page reload clears it.
evidence: Modal: `if (result.ok) { onSent(); return }` where onSent = `() => onOpenChange(false)`. Action: `revalidatePath('/')` + `revalidatePath(\`/clients/${clientId}\`)` then `return { ok: true }`. Nowhere does the client call router.refresh().
suggestedFix: After a successful send, call router.refresh() in the modal (import useRouter) before/after onSent() so the dashboard RSC re-fetches getDormantClients and drops the now-contacted client. Alternatively show an explicit success toast so the recruiter knows it sent even though the list hasn't updated.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the full chain. In src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx the success branch is `if (result.ok) { onSent(); return }` (line 190-192), and `onSent` is wired as `() => onOpenChange(false)` (line 82) — it only flips the dialog's local open state to false. There is no `useRouter`/`router.refresh()` anywhere in the modal or its parent row (dormant-client-row.tsx); grep across the entire _dashboard/ directory returned zero matches for router.refresh/useRouter. The action sendOutreachAction (outreach-actions.ts:343-345) is `'use server'` and calls `revalidatePath('/')` + `revalidatePath(`/clients/${clientId}`)` then `return { ok: true }`.

Crucially, the action is invoked PROGRAMMATICALLY: the form uses `onSubmit={(e)=>{e.preventDefault(); void handleSend()}}` (lines 286-289) and handleSend does `await sendOutreachAction({...})` (line 185) — it is NOT passed as a `<form action={serverAction}>` prop. In the App Router, `revalidatePath` invalidates the server Data/Route cache for subsequent requests; the automatic refresh of the currently-displayed route happens for form-action-prop invocations, not for a value-returning programmatic call. To re-render the page the user is staring at, the client must call `router.refresh()`.

The codebase's own sibling pattern proves the team knows this: logNoteAction (actions.ts:167) is structurally identical — it calls `revalidatePath(`/clients/${id}`)` then `return { ok: true }` — yet its caller log-note-form.tsx:46 ADDITIONALLY calls `router.refresh()` after success. The outreach modal omits exactly that step.

The dashboard page (src/app/(app)/page.tsx, the `/` route — the `(app)` group does not affect the URL) is an RSC that fetches getDormantClients fresh on each render and feeds DormantClientsWidget. So a router.refresh() WOULD drop the contacted client; without it, the widget keeps the old 'last contact X days ago' text and a live 'Send check-in' button until manual navigation/reload. The duplicate-send risk is real: re-opening the modal re-requests a fresh draft and allows another send, with no UI signal the first one landed.

Refutation attempts failed: (a) revalidatePath('/') does target the dashboard route, so it's not a wrong-path issue — but revalidate alone doesn't re-render the live page for a programmatic action; (b) the form is not a form-action submission, so no framework auto-refresh applies; (c) no parent re-key or subscription exists.

Adjusted to medium rather than the claimed high: the modal DOES close on success, giving partial (if ambiguous) feedback, there is no data corruption or tenant/security impact, and the staleness self-heals on any navigation. But it remains a genuine cache-invalidation/refresh gap with a plausible duplicate-email outcome and a real demo-credibility hit. The suggested fix (add useRouter + router.refresh() after onSent, and ideally a success toast) is correct and matches the existing log-note-form precedent.

title: Send check-in gives zero success feedback — modal just vanishes
description: On a successful Outlook send the only thing that happens is the dialog closes (onSent → onOpenChange(false)). There is no toast, no inline 'Sent' confirmation, and (per the previous finding) the dormant list doesn't change. A disappearing modal is ambiguous: it looks identical to the recruiter accidentally clicking outside the dialog or hitting Escape, which also closes it without sending. The surface inventory itself flags this: 'No toast on success — modal just closes.'
userImpact: The recruiter cannot tell whether the email actually went to their client or whether they just dismissed the dialog. For an action that sends a real email to a paying client under the recruiter's own name, ambiguous success is dangerous — they may re-send (duplicate email to the client) or assume it failed and chase it another way.
evidence: `async function handleSend() { setStatus({ kind: 'sending' }); const result = await sendOutreachAction(...); if (result.ok) { onSent(); return } ...}` — onSent only closes the dialog; no sonner toast or success state is rendered.
suggestedFix: On result.ok, show a clear success affordance (toast.success('Check-in sent to {clientName}') or a brief in-modal 'Sent ✓' state) before closing, and refresh the dashboard so the row disappears — together these make success unambiguous.
verifierReasoning: Verified directly in src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx. handleSend (lines 183-199): on result.ok it calls onSent() and returns. onSent is wired at line 82 to () => onOpenChange(false) — i.e. the ONLY observable effect of a successful Outlook send is the dialog closing. Confirmed there is no toast (the file imports no sonner/toast — grep at lines 1-22 and a targeted grep returned nothing), no success-state UI (the Status discriminated union at lines 45-52 has no 'sent' variant; showForm at lines 230-233 only renders for ready/sending/needs_consent), and no router.refresh. Both callers (dormant-client-row.tsx line 57-62 and client-checkin-button.tsx line 35-40) only manage an open boolean and pass setOpen as onOpenChange; neither imports useRouter or calls router.refresh (grep returned nothing). The Dialog onOpenChange (line 71) is the same handler that fires on Escape / outside-click, so a successful send is visually indistinguishable from an accidental dismissal — exactly the ambiguity described. This also violates the codebase's own established convention: comparable mutations (e.g. log-activity-form.tsx lines 60-63) do toast.success(...) AND router.refresh() with an explicit comment, and float-row-actions / candidate-applications / cv-review-panel all toast on success. The send-checkin success path uniquely skips both. The claim's parenthetical that 'the dormant list doesn't change' is materially correct from the user's view: although the server action sendOutreachAction does call revalidatePath('/') and revalidatePath('/clients/[id]') (outreach-actions.ts lines 343-344), App Router revalidatePath only marks the server cache stale — without a router.refresh() the open page does not re-render, so the dormant row stays visible until manual navigation/reload. So the user-facing impact (row appears unchanged after closing) holds. The action is real: the email is genuinely sent via Microsoft Graph to a paying client's contact under the recruiter's identity.

title: Each abandoned/retried Send check-in leaves an orphan 'email_draft' activity that pollutes the dashboard Recent Activity feed
description: Every time the modal opens (or Retry is pressed), requestOutreachDraftAction fires a fresh 'outreach-draft/requested' event, and the Inngest draft-outreach-email function unconditionally INSERTs a new activities row with kind='email_draft' (no upsert, no 'replace previous unsent draft for this company' guard). sendOutreachAction only flips the single most-recent email_draft row to kind='email' (outreach-actions.ts:285-313). So opening the modal 3 times for one client and sending once leaves 2 orphan email_draft rows. These rows are NOT filtered out of getRecentActivity (dashboard.ts:84 selects all kinds), and recent-activity-feed.tsx maps email_draft to the label 'Drafted an email' (KIND_META, line 34). They render on the company entity so entity_type='company' resolves an href to /clients/[id].
userImpact: The dashboard's Recent Activity feed — the first thing shown in the demo — fills up with 'Someone · Drafted an email · {Client}' entries every time the recruiter opens the Send check-in modal and backs out or retries. To a buyer this reads like the system logs noise/half-actions, and it buries the real notes/calls/stage-changes that the feed is meant to surface.
evidence: Inngest fn: `await supabase.from('activities').insert({ ... kind: 'email_draft' ... })` with no dedupe. Send action flips only `.limit(1).maybeSingle()` newest draft. Feed: `email_draft: { icon: Mail, label: 'Drafted an email' }` and getRecentActivity SELECTs '*' with no kind exclusion.
suggestedFix: Either (a) exclude kind='email_draft' from getRecentActivity's dashboard query (drafts are an internal polling artifact, not a feed-worthy activity), or (b) have the Inngest function delete/overwrite any prior unsent email_draft for the same company before inserting, so at most one draft exists per client at a time.
verifierReasoning:
Every link in the claim's chain is verified in code, so the mechanism is real.

1. Trigger: SendCheckinModalBody is only rendered while open (send-checkin-modal.tsx:79-85), so each open mounts it fresh and its useEffect immediately calls requestOutreachDraftAction({ clientId }) (lines 143-181, fire at 148). Retry calls it again (handleRetryDraft, line 204). Each call dispatches a fresh `outreach-draft/requested` Inngest event (outreach-actions.ts:84-92).

2. Insert: draftOutreachEmailFn unconditionally INSERTs a new activities row with kind='email_draft', entity_type='company', entity_id=company_id on every event (draft-outreach-email.ts:141-167). There is no upsert, no "replace prior unsent draft" guard, and no delete anywhere in the codebase (grep for activity .delete() returned nothing; no TTL/cron culling).

3. Send only flips ONE row: sendOutreachAction selects the single newest email_draft via .order(occurred_at desc).limit(1).maybeSingle() and updates only that row to kind='email' (outreach-actions.ts:284-314). Older email_draft rows from prior opens/retries are left untouched. So N opens + 1 send => N-1 orphan email_draft rows.

4. Feed includes them: getRecentActivity does .select('*').order(occurred_at desc).limit(20) with NO kind exclusion (dashboard.ts:84-88). The dashboard landing page (app/(app)/page.tsx:32,85) calls it and renders RecentActivityFeed. The component's KIND_META maps email_draft -> { icon: Mail, label: 'Drafted an email' } (recent-activity-feed.tsx:34-38) and resolveEntity maps entity_type='company' to href /clients/[id] (dashboard.ts:250-253). So orphan drafts render as "<Actor> · Drafted an email · <Client>" linking to the client page.

5. The email_draft enum value genuinely exists (migration 20260520031300, database.ts:1533/1718), so the inserts succeed; this is not a no-op.

Net: the bug is exactly as described — abandoned/retried Send check-in attempts leave orphan email_draft rows that occupy slots in the 20-row dashboard feed.

Severity adjustment: I downgrade medium -> low. The impact is purely cosmetic feed noise — no data loss, no functional break, no cross-tenant leakage (rows are RLS-scoped to the recruiter's own org). It also requires the recruiter to repeatedly open/abandon the modal, and any genuine note/call/stage_change activity will interleave and push old drafts off the 20-row window over time. It is a legitimate polish/idempotency issue worth fixing (suggested fixes are both valid), but it does not rise to medium given no harm beyond visual clutter that real activity naturally dilutes.

title: Retry draft loop keeps running after the modal closes — setState on an unmounted body
description: The initial draft loop in the useEffect guards against unmount with a `cancelled` flag and bails on every await boundary. But handleRetryDraft (triggered by the 'Retry' button on timeout) is a plain async handler that does NOT capture any cancellation ref. It runs its own 10-iteration poll loop (await setTimeout 1s each) and unconditionally calls setSubject/setBodyText/setStatus inside it. If the recruiter closes the modal (clicks outside / Escape / Cancel) mid-retry, SendCheckinModalBody unmounts but the in-flight retry loop continues for up to ~10 seconds, calling setStatus on the unmounted component. The surface inventory flags this exact gap: 'handleRetryDraft does not guard against cancellation (no cancelled ref).'
userImpact: React warns about state updates on an unmounted component, and in fast re-open scenarios a stale retry loop can overwrite the freshly-mounted modal's draft (the new mount's effect and the old loop both setStatus/setSubject), showing the recruiter the wrong client's draft or flipping a loading modal to 'timeout' unexpectedly. Intermittent and confusing during a live demo.
evidence: Initial loop: `let cancelled = false ... if (cancelled) return ... return () => { cancelled = true }`. Retry loop: `async function handleRetryDraft() { ... for (...) { await new Promise(...); const got = await getLatestOutreachDraftAction(...); ... setStatus(...) } }` — no cancelled check anywhere.
suggestedFix: Hoist the cancellation flag to a ref (cancelledRef) shared by both the mount effect and handleRetryDraft, check it after every await in the retry loop, and set it true in the effect cleanup. Or refactor Retry to re-trigger the same effect via a key/counter so the existing cleanup handles it.
verifierReasoning:
The CODE gap described is real: handleRetryDraft (src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx:201-228) runs the same 10-iteration / ~10s poll loop as the mount effect but has NO cancellation guard. The effect's `cancelled` flag (lines 144,149,157,159,179) is a local closure variable inaccessible to the handler, and there is no shared `cancelledRef`/AbortController (confirmed by grep). handleRetryDraft calls setStatus/setSubject/setBodyText unconditionally after each await (lines 203,206,209,213-227). Closing the modal mid-retry unmounts SendCheckinModalBody because the parent gates rendering on `open` (line 79), so the loop does continue against an unmounted instance. So far the surface-inventory note and the evidence are accurate.

However, every claimed USER-FACING IMPACT is wrong:
(1) "React warns about state updates on an unmounted component" — FALSE here. The project runs React 19.2.4; React removed that warning in v18. Post-unmount setState is now a silent no-op with no console output.
(2) "a stale retry loop can overwrite the freshly-mounted modal's draft / show the wrong client's draft / flip a loading modal to timeout" — FALSE. React state setters are per-instance. The leaked loop's closure captured the OLD mount's setStatus/setSubject; a re-open creates a brand-new SendCheckinModalBody instance with brand-new setters. The old loop's calls hit the dead instance and cannot mutate the new mount's state. Cross-mount corruption is structurally impossible with this pattern.
(3) "wrong client's draft" — additionally impossible: both callers (dormant-client-row.tsx:57, client-checkin-button.tsx:35) bind a fixed clientId per SendCheckinModal instance; clientId never changes across re-opens of the same modal.

Net: a benign fire-and-forget leak — up to ~10s of harmless getLatestOutreachDraftAction polls + no-op setStates after unmount. No warning, no visible symptom, no data/draft corruption, no demo-breaking behavior. The suggested cancelledRef fix is still good hygiene, so the gap is real, but the severity and the entire impact narrative are overstated.

title: Invalid custom date range silently snaps back to the default 90-day window with no error shown
description: onCustomSubmit validates `!from || !to || from > to` but the if-block body is an empty comment — it does NOT show any error and falls through to navigate() with the invalid params anyway. The server resolver (resolveSourceAttributionRange / resolveBuyerValueRange) then silently discards the invalid range and falls back to the default 90-day window (range.ts:113 'Malformed custom range — silently fall back'). The page header still shows the (default) window dates, but no message tells the user their custom input was rejected. The identical pattern exists in the buyer-value date-filter.tsx (line 72).
userImpact: A recruiter sets a custom From date AFTER the To date (or leaves one blank), clicks Apply, and the report quietly shows the last-90-days numbers instead of their requested window — with no indication their input was ignored. They may present or trust figures for the wrong period during the client call.
evidence: `if (!from || !to || from > to) { // Server helper falls back to the default window; we still navigate ... }` — empty block, then `next.set('from', from); next.set('to', to); navigate(next)`. Both source-attribution and buyer-value date filters share this.
suggestedFix: On invalid input, render an inline validation message (e.g. 'To date must be on or after From date') and return early instead of navigating, OR have the page detect that requested custom params were discarded and show a banner ('Showing default 90 days — the custom range you entered was invalid').
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed the claim against the actual code. In both src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/date-filter.tsx:69-72 and src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/date-filter.tsx:72-75, onCustomSubmit's validation `if (!from || !to || from > to)` has a body that is ONLY a comment ("Server helper falls back... we still navigate so the user sees... any error UI the page renders.") — no error is shown, no early return; execution falls through to set the params and navigate(next) with the invalid range.

The server resolvers confirm the silent fallback: resolveSourceAttributionRange (source-attribution-range.ts:106-114) and resolveBuyerValueRange (buyer-value-range.ts:110-118) require `from && to && isYmd(from) && isYmd(to) && from <= to`; otherwise they `return defaultWindow(now)` with the comment "Malformed custom range — silently fall back to the default window."

Critically, I verified the page components do NOT render the "error UI" / "small inline note" the comments promise. source-attribution/page.tsx only shows a destructive Card when `!result.ok` (RPC failure); buyer-value/page.tsx only shows one for `failedMetrics` (RPC failures). Grepping the entire reports tree for "invalid/discarded/fell back/on or after/range you entered" found nothing — that feedback UI was never built. So the comments are aspirational, not implemented.

End-to-end trace: a recruiter on ?preset=custom enters From after To and clicks Apply → navigates to custom params → resolver discards them and returns the 90d default → page header subtitle shows the default-window dates and the report shows last-90-days figures. There IS implicit feedback (resolver returns preset '90d', so the "Last 90 days" button becomes the active/aria-pressed variant and the custom date form collapses), but no explicit message explains why the custom input was ignored.

Two accuracy nuances: (1) the inputs are native `type="date"` + `required`, so the browser blocks truly-empty submissions — the `!from || !to` branch is mostly unreachable via the form (still reachable via hand-typed/deep-linked URLs, which the resolver also silently swallows). (2) The `from > to` case (the core of the claim) is fully reachable via the UI. So the missing-feedback gap genuinely exists for the most likely real input mistake.

Severity adjusted from medium to low: the bug is real but mitigated by visible (if implicit) state changes — the preset toggle visibly jumps to "Last 90 days", the custom form disappears, and the header subtitle updates to the actual (default) dates. An attentive user would notice the period changed. The risk is a hurried recruiter missing the implicit cues and trusting wrong-period figures, which is a real but modest UX/missing-feedback issue, not a data-correctness or security defect. The figures shown are always internally consistent with the (default) window displayed in the header.

title: Buyer-value report (the acquirer-due-diligence dashboard) is not linked from the Reports hub — unreachable via the UI
description: The REPORTS array on the reports hub contains only the source-attribution card. The buyer-value report page exists and works (/reports/buyer-value with its 5 metric cards) but has NO entry point anywhere in the navigation. The only way to reach it is to type the URL. The page header even bills it as 'Five acquirer-due-diligence metrics' — precisely the report you'd want to show a prospective buyer.
userImpact: The single most sales-relevant report — the one framed around what an acquirer wants to see — cannot be found by clicking. In a live demo the founder will navigate to Reports, see only 'Source attribution', and have no way to surface the buyer-value dashboard they may have intended to show off.
evidence: `const REPORTS: ReportCard[] = [ { href: '/reports/source-attribution', title: 'Source attribution', ... } ]` — only one entry; no '/reports/buyer-value' card. Page exists at src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/page.tsx.
suggestedFix: Add a second ReportCard for { href: '/reports/buyer-value', title: 'Buyer-value report', description: 'Placements per recruiter, time-to-fill, source ROI, pipeline value, and commission — the acquirer due-diligence view.' } to the REPORTS array.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed exactly as claimed. The REPORTS array at src/app/(app)/reports/page.tsx:27-34 contains a single entry (source-attribution); there is no /reports/buyer-value card. The buyer-value page is fully built at src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/page.tsx and bills itself "Buyer-value report" (line 141) with subtitle "Five acquirer-due-diligence metrics" (line 144), matching the cited text. A codebase-wide grep for "buyer-value" shows zero navigation links: every reference outside the page's own directory is either a code comment (charts, shortlist action) or a lib helper (lib/db/buyer-value.ts, lib/reports/buyer-value-range.ts, lib/format.ts). Neither the desktop top-nav (top-nav.tsx NAV_ITEMS) nor the mobile-nav-drawer (mobile-nav-drawer.tsx SECONDARY_NAV) links to it — both only link to /reports. The buyer-value page's own only link is a "Back to reports" link (line 131) pointing the opposite direction, and the reports hub does not link forward to it. Therefore the route is reachable only by typing the URL. It is genuinely orphaned. Re-graded to medium (sits at the medium/low boundary): no functional breakage, data loss, or tenancy/security concern — it is purely a discoverability/navigation omission with a one-line fix. But the impact framing is fair: the report explicitly positioned for acquirer due diligence is invisible to a founder clicking through Reports in a live demo, which is the single most sales-relevant context for this product. The suggested fix (add a second ReportCard for /reports/buyer-value) is correct and trivial.

title: Source-attribution RPC failure shows £0 / 0 placements totals alongside the error card, looking like real data
description: When getSourceAttribution returns ok:false, the page sets rows = [] (line 100) and then unconditionally renders the 'In this window' headline card computing totalPlacements / totalFeePence from that empty array — so it confidently displays 'Placements: 0' and 'Fee revenue: £0.00'. The inline error card IS rendered above it, but the big-number summary card below presents 0/£0 as if they were the real figures for the window. The two contradict each other.
userImpact: If the RPC errors during the demo (e.g. transient DB hiccup or a not-yet-applied migration), the recruiter sees a small error sentence at the top but a prominent, authoritative '0 placements / £0 fee revenue' headline beneath it. A client glancing at the screen reads it as 'this agency made no placements', which is both wrong and damaging — the failure should suppress the numbers, not render them as zeros.
evidence: `const rows: SourceAttributionRow[] = result.ok ? result.data : []` then the headline card always renders `{totalPlacements}` and `{formatPence(totalFeePence)}` computed from rows, regardless of result.ok. The !result.ok branch only adds a card, it does not suppress the totals card.
suggestedFix: When !result.ok, render only the error card and skip the totals/table/top-sources cards (or replace their numbers with an em-dash placeholder) so a failed load never masquerades as zero-value data.
verifierReasoning: Verified against src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/page.tsx. Line 100 is exactly `const rows: SourceAttributionRow[] = result.ok ? result.data : []`, so on RPC failure rows=[]. Lines 102-103 derive totalPlacements/totalFeePence by reducing over rows (both 0 when empty). The !result.ok branch (lines 135-142) only ADDS an inline error card via `{!result.ok && (<Card>...)}`; it does not gate or suppress anything below it. The "In this window" headline card (lines 144-169) renders UNCONDITIONALLY and displays {totalPlacements} (→0) and {formatPence(totalFeePence)} (→formatPence(0)). The "By source" table (lines 184-219) and "Top sources by revenue" card (lines 233-256) likewise render their empty states regardless of result.ok. So on an RPC error a user sees both an error sentence AND an authoritative "Placements: 0 / Fee revenue: <zero>" headline — the contradiction the claim describes. getSourceAttribution (src/lib/db/source-attribution.ts) confirms result.ok=false is returned on any RPC error (line 72), so the failure path is reachable (transient DB error, unapplied source_attribution_summary migration, RLS issue). One factual nit in the claim: formatPence(0) returns "0p", not "£0.00", because the function (src/lib/format.ts:16) returns `${p}p` for p<100. This does not change the substance — a zero fee headline still renders as real data. The mechanism (no suppression of totals/table on failure) is materially exactly as described.

title: Completed welcome-checklist steps remain keyboard/screen-reader activatable despite looking disabled
description: Completed steps render a Next <Link> with the real href still present, relying on `pointer-events-none` + `aria-disabled` + `tabIndex={-1}` to disable them. pointer-events-none blocks mouse clicks and tabIndex=-1 removes it from tab order, but the href is live in the DOM. aria-disabled is advisory only — it does not prevent activation. A screen-reader user navigating by links (not tab order) can still follow a 'done' step, and any programmatic focus + Enter still navigates. The surface inventory flags this: 'the href is still present in the DOM — screen readers can still activate them.'
userImpact: Minor: a screen-reader user could be taken to /candidates/new for a step marked complete. Not data-affecting, but an accessibility correctness gap on the first-run onboarding surface.
evidence: `<Link href={step.href} className={... step.done ? 'pointer-events-none' : ...} tabIndex={step.done ? -1 : undefined} aria-disabled={step.done}>` — href is unconditional; aria-disabled does not block activation.
suggestedFix: For done steps, render a non-interactive element (a <div>/<span> with the same styling) instead of a <Link>, so there is no navigable href at all.
verifierReasoning: The claim is accurate to the code. At src/app/(app)/_dashboard/welcome-checklist.tsx:89-101, both done and not-done steps render a next/link `<Link href={step.href}>` with the href set unconditionally (line 90). The only attempt to disable done steps is `pointer-events-none` (line 94), `tabIndex={step.done ? -1 : undefined}` (line 99), and `aria-disabled={step.done}` (line 100). All three cited accessibility assertions are technically correct: (1) pointer-events-none only blocks mouse/pointer events, not keyboard or programmatic activation; (2) tabIndex=-1 removes the link from sequential tab order but it stays in the accessibility tree as a link, reachable via a screen reader's links rotor/elements list; (3) aria-disabled is advisory only — the native `disabled` attribute does not apply to anchor elements, so a real <a> with a live href will still navigate on Enter or programmatic click. Therefore a screen-reader user navigating by links, or any programmatic focus + Enter, can still follow a step marked complete (e.g. to /candidates/new). The component is live, not dead code: it is imported and rendered in src/app/(app)/page.tsx:55, fed by real DB onboarding counts. I could not refute the claim.
