On severity: the issue is genuinely minor — it is not data-affecting, crosses no auth or tenancy boundary (all destinations are authenticated app routes the user already accesses), and the exposure window is narrow (only when the onboarding card is shown, i.e. partially-complete and not dismissed, and only for screen-reader/keyboard-rotor navigation). The claim's self-graded 'low' severity and 'minor accessibility correctness gap' impact framing are honest and not overstated. The suggested fix — render a non-interactive <div>/<span> with matching styling for done steps so there is no navigable href — is the correct remedy.

title: 'Placements this month' metric card is hardcoded to 0 and shows on the live dashboard
description: getDashboardMetrics returns placementsThisMonth: 0 unconditionally (Phase 4 stub), and page.tsx renders it as a top-line metric card labelled 'Placements this month' with a caption 'Lands in Phase 4'. So the dashboard always shows '0' placements this month even for an agency that has made placements (the data exists — the buyer-value and source-attribution reports compute real placement counts from the applications table).
userImpact: On the main dashboard the founder presents to the client, one of four headline metrics permanently reads 'Placements this month: 0'. To a buyer that either looks broken or like the agency makes no placements — and it directly contradicts the placement figures shown in the Reports section. The 'Lands in Phase 4' caption is internal jargon a client shouldn't see.
evidence: dashboard.ts: `placementsThisMonth: 0,` with comment 'until then the card renders 0'. page.tsx: `<MetricCard label="Placements this month" value={metrics.placementsThisMonth} caption="Lands in Phase 4" />`.
suggestedFix: Either compute placementsThisMonth from applications where stage='placed' and stage_changed_at in the current month (the same source the reports already use), or hide this card until it's wired up rather than shipping a permanent hardcoded 0 with a Phase-4 caption visible to clients.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed exactly as claimed. src/lib/db/dashboard.ts:66 returns `placementsThisMonth: 0` as a hardcoded literal inside getDashboardMetrics — there is no query for it (the only three real count queries are candidates, open jobs, open applications). The stale comment at lines 29-30 says "Phase 4 lands the placements table; until then the card renders 0". src/app/(app)/page.tsx lines 76-80 render `<MetricCard label="Placements this month" value={metrics.placementsThisMonth} caption="Lands in Phase 4" />` unconditionally as the 4th of 4 headline metric cards in the "Key metrics" section. This page is the authenticated app root (src/app/(app)/page.tsx), shown whenever the org has any candidates or open jobs (the !isEmpty path) — no feature flag gates the card. MetricCard (src/components/app/metric-card.tsx:21-23) renders the `caption` prop directly to the DOM, so "Lands in Phase 4" is visible to the client. Crucially, the data exists and is computable: the comment claiming placements arrive in "Phase 4" is factually wrong — placements shipped in Phase 3. The application_stage enum has 'placed' (migration 20260513152244), applications.placed_at + fee_pence columns exist and are NOT-NULL-enforced when stage='placed' (20260520023100, 20260523160000), and the LIVE Reports section computes real placement counts from this data: source_attribution_summary and the buyer-value RPCs (placements_by_recruiter_quarter, commission_summary_by_recruiter, etc.) all aggregate `where a.stage = 'placed'` over `coalesce(a.placed_at, a.stage_changed_at)` (migrations 20260520023200, 20260524000200, 20260601000000), surfaced at /reports/source-attribution and /reports/buyer-value. So an agency with placements sees real figures in Reports but a permanent hardcoded 0 on the dashboard — the self-contradiction the reviewer described is genuine. No conditional hides the card; grep found no other guard.

title: Dashboard shows the 'Welcome / add your first candidate' empty state if the count queries error out, hiding an established org's real data
description: getDashboardMetrics swallows query errors (logs to Sentry, then returns `candidates: candidates.count ?? 0`, `openJobs: openJobs.count ?? 0`). The page then computes `const isEmpty = metrics.candidates === 0 && metrics.openJobs === 0` and, when true, renders the first-run EmptyState ('Welcome to Altus — Add your first candidate'). If the two count queries transiently fail (network blip, RLS context not yet warm, Postgres hiccup), both counts fall back to 0 and an agency with hundreds of candidates is shown the brand-new-account onboarding screen instead of their dashboard.
userImpact: During the live demo, a momentary DB error makes the established anchor agency's dashboard look completely empty and brand new — as if all their candidates and jobs vanished. Deeply alarming for a CRM whose whole pitch is 'your data, instantly searchable'.
evidence:
const isEmpty = metrics.candidates === 0 && metrics.openJobs === 0
if (isEmpty) { return (<EmptyState heading="Welcome to Altus" ... cta={{ href: '/candidates/new', label: 'Add your first candidate' }} /> ) }  // getDashboardMetrics returns `candidates.count ?? 0` even when candidates.error is set
suggestedFix: Have getDashboardMetrics return a discriminated result (or an `errored` flag) when any count query errors, and only render the first-run EmptyState when the queries succeeded AND returned 0. On error, render the normal dashboard chrome with a 'couldn't load metrics' inline note rather than the onboarding screen.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed in code. src/lib/db/dashboard.ts getDashboardMetrics (lines 34-68) runs three count queries in a Promise.all, logs any error to Sentry, then returns `candidates: candidates.count ?? 0` and `openJobs: openJobs.count ?? 0`. On a PostgREST error the response shape is { count: null, error: <err> }, so an errored count collapses to 0 — indistinguishable from a genuinely empty org. The return type DashboardMetrics is a plain object with NO error/discriminant field (unlike DbResult used by the sibling list helpers). src/app/(app)/page.tsx:38 computes `const isEmpty = metrics.candidates === 0 && metrics.openJobs === 0` and at lines 40-51 renders the first-run EmptyState ("Welcome to Altus" / "Add your first candidate"), fully replacing the real dashboard. Because the helper catches the error and returns normally (does not throw), the route's error.tsx boundary never fires, so there is no compensating guard. The contrast is explicit: src/app/(app)/candidates/page.tsx:63-79 uses the DbResult discriminated union, renders a distinct "Couldn't load candidates. Please refresh." error state when !result.ok, and only shows its empty state when total===0 on a SUCCESSFUL query — the correct pattern the dashboard fails to follow. So the claim's mechanism is exactly right. Severity adjusted from high to medium: the trigger requires BOTH the candidates and open-jobs count queries to fail (open-applications failing alone does not flip isEmpty). They share one client/connection in the same Promise.all, so a connection-level blip (network, pooler, JWT/RLS warm-up, statement timeout) would plausibly fail both together — the failures are correlated, making it more than theoretical. But it is a transient condition that self-heals on refresh, not a deterministic data-loss bug, which is why medium rather than high. The suggested fix (return an errored flag and only show the first-run EmptyState when the queries succeeded and returned 0) is correct and matches the existing DbResult pattern already used everywhere else in this file.

title: Send check-in modal: Retry-draft loop keeps running and calls setState after the modal is closed/unmounted
description: The initial draft effect (lines 143-181) wires up a `cancelled` ref and checks it after every await, so closing the modal mid-poll is clean. handleRetryDraft (lines 201-228) duplicates the same request+poll loop but has NO cancellation guard. If the recruiter clicks Retry on a timeout, then closes the modal (or the row's open state flips) before the 10s poll completes, SendCheckinModalBody unmounts but the retry loop keeps awaiting getLatestOutreachDraftAction every 1s and calls setSubject/setBodyText/setStatus on the unmounted component. React logs a state-update-on-unmounted warning and, more importantly, the work keeps firing server actions for up to 10 seconds against a dead component.
userImpact: After a slow AI draft + Retry, closing the modal leaves a zombie poll firing server actions; in dev/preview this surfaces as React warnings and occasionally a flash of stale content if the modal is reopened quickly. Looks flaky during a demo.
suggestedFix: Factor the request+poll loop into a single function that takes a `cancelled` accessor, and drive both the initial draft and Retry through it so the existing useEffect cleanup (`cancelled = true`) cancels a retry too. Or move the cancelled flag to a ref that both paths consult after each await.
verifierReasoning: Verified in src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx. The initial draft effect (lines 143-181) declares `let cancelled = false`, checks `if (cancelled) return` after every await (lines 149, 157, 159), and returns a cleanup `() => { cancelled = true }` (lines 178-180) — clean. handleRetryDraft (lines 201-228) is a near-verbatim copy of that request+poll loop but contains NO reference to `cancelled` anywhere: it awaits requestOutreachDraftAction (204), then loops up to MAX_POLL_ATTEMPTS (10) awaiting setTimeout (211) and getLatestOutreachDraftAction (212), unconditionally calling setStatus/setSubject/setBodyText (203, 206, 209, 214, 220-222, 227). Both are genuine `'use server'` server actions (confirmed in src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts), so the retry loop fires real server-action POSTs every ~1s. SendCheckinModalBody is conditionally mounted only when open=true (line 79), and the Dialog closes on Escape/overlay-click via onOpenChange (line 71); during the timeout/polling states there is no in-form Cancel button (Cancel only renders inside showForm), but Escape/overlay-click still flip open to false and unmount the body. On unmount the useEffect cleanup sets `cancelled = true`, which cancels the INITIAL loop only — the retry loop captured nothing referencing cancelled, so it keeps awaiting and calls setState on the unmounted instance, and keeps hitting server actions for up to 10s. So the bug exists exactly as located. On severity: the trigger is narrow — the first draft must time out (10s, no row), the recruiter must click Retry, then close the modal again before the second poll resolves. The setState-on-unmounted warning is dev-only and was removed in React 18+, so in production it is silent. The redundant server-action calls are the real residual cost. The 'flash of stale content on quick reopen' is overstated: reopening mounts a fresh SendCheckinModalBody instance with status 'requesting' and empty subject/body, so the zombie loop's setStates land on the discarded old instance and cannot leak into the remounted one. Suggested fix (route both paths through a single loop consulting a cancelled accessor/ref) is correct.

title: Source-attribution report shows £0 / 0 placements totals alongside the error banner when the RPC fails
description: When getSourceAttribution returns `{ ok: false }`, the page sets `rows = []` and still renders the 'In this window' headline card with `totalPlacements = 0` and `formatPence(0)` = '0p', plus the 'By source' empty state ('No placements in this date range') and the 'Top sources by revenue' empty state. The small inline error card (line 135) is rendered, but it sits above three cards that confidently state there were zero placements and £0 of revenue. A recruiter who skims past the error banner reads it as a genuine business result ('we placed nobody and earned nothing this quarter') rather than a load failure.
userImpact: On a transient RPC failure during the demo the report asserts the agency made 0 placements and £0 revenue in the window — the opposite of the buyer-value story the report exists to tell. Misleading rather than merely degraded.
evidence:
const rows: SourceAttributionRow[] = result.ok ? result.data : []
const totalPlacements = rows.reduce((acc, r) => acc + r.placements_count, 0)  // = 0 on error
const totalFeePence = rows.reduce((acc, r) => acc + r.total_fee_pence, 0)      // = 0 on error
// then the headline + 'By source' + 'Top sources' cards all render the 0/empty case under the error banner
suggestedFix: When `!result.ok`, suppress the totals/table/top-sources cards entirely and render only the error card (or replace their bodies with a 'metric unavailable' placeholder), so the page never presents a fabricated £0/0-placements result as fact.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code at src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/page.tsx. The claim is precisely accurate:

- Line 100: `const rows: SourceAttributionRow[] = result.ok ? result.data : []` — on RPC failure (`{ ok: false }`), rows becomes `[]`.
- Lines 102-103: totalPlacements and totalFeePence reduce over rows, so both compute to 0 on error.
- Lines 135-142: The error card is rendered only inside `{!result.ok && (...)}`.
- Lines 144-169: The "In this window" headline card renders UNCONDITIONALLY (no `result.ok` guard), showing `{totalPlacements}` = 0 and `{formatPence(totalFeePence)}`. Confirmed via src/lib/format.ts: `formatPence(0)` returns '0p' (0 < 100 branch).
- Lines 171-221: The "By source" card renders unconditionally; on error `rows.length === 0` triggers EmptyState "No placements in this date range".
- Lines 223-258: The "Top sources by revenue" card renders unconditionally; on error `topByRevenue.length === 0` shows "No placements in this date range."

So on a transient RPC failure, the page shows the inline error banner plus three cards that present a fabricated zero-result indistinguishable from a genuine "no placements" business outcome. The failure path is identical to the legitimate empty state. DbResult (src/lib/db/types.ts) confirms `{ ok: false }` is the only error shape; getSourceAttribution returns it whenever the RPC errors (Sentry-captured).

Why I did NOT confirm at the claimed 'medium' severity:
- This is a transient-only failure path, not normal rendering. The genuine empty case renders identically and correctly.
- An error card IS rendered directly above the misleading cards — there is feedback present, so this is not a silent-wrong-data bug. The complaint reduces to the error signal being visually under-weighted relative to three confident zero cards.
- It is a read-only reports page: no data corruption, no cross-tenant leak, no mutation, no money movement. The harm is a momentary misleading impression that an existing visible red error banner already counteracts.

Real issue, genuinely worth the suggested fix (suppress totals/table/top-sources when `!result.ok`), but the user-facing impact is minor and gated behind a visible error message on a transient-only path — low, not medium.

title: Buyer-value report is unreachable from the Reports hub — only discoverable by typing the URL
description: The REPORTS array driving the Reports hub contains a single card (source-attribution). /reports/buyer-value is a fully-built page (5 acquirer-due-diligence metric cards) but has no entry point in the hub or anywhere else in nav, so a user can only reach it by knowing and typing the URL. The buyer-value report is explicitly the acquirer/buyer-value story — the most strategically important report for the 'anchor becomes a sale' thesis — and it is invisible in the UI.
userImpact: During a client/investor demo the founder cannot navigate to the buyer-value report through the product; it appears not to exist. The single most sale-relevant report is effectively hidden.
suggestedFix: Add a `{ href: '/reports/buyer-value', title: 'Buyer-value report', description: 'Acquirer due-diligence metrics — placements per recruiter, time-to-fill, source ROI, pipeline value, commission.' }` entry to the REPORTS array.
verifierReasoning: Verified the claim directly against the code. (1) reports/page.tsx lines 27-34 define REPORTS with exactly one card: { href: '/reports/source-attribution', ... }. There is no '/reports/buyer-value' entry. (2) reports/buyer-value/page.tsx is a fully-built RSC, not a stub — it auth-guards, resolves a date range, fetches 5 metrics in parallel (getPlacementsByRecruiterQuarter, getTimeToFillBySector, getSourceAttribution, getPipelineValueSparkline, getCommissionSummary) and renders 5 metric Cards (placements per recruiter per quarter, time-to-fill by sector, source ROI, pipeline value, commission summary) plus a Methodology block, titled 'Buyer-value report'. (3) A repo-wide grep for all '/reports' hrefs/router.push and for 'buyer-value' confirms there is NO inbound entry point: TopNav NAV_ITEMS (top-nav.tsx:15) only links '/reports'; mobile-nav-drawer.tsx:49 only links '/reports'; the Reports hub only links '/reports/source-attribution'; the dashboard widgets and (app)/page.tsx contain zero references to reports or buyer-value. The only '/reports/buyer-value' references in src are the page's own date-filter router.push (fires only once you're already on the page) and code comments. The page's own back-link points TO /reports, not from it. Therefore the buyer-value report is reachable solely by manually typing the URL. The claim, evidence, and suggested fix (add a REPORTS array entry) are all accurate.

title: Invalid custom date range (from > to or blank) silently snaps back to the 90-day default with no error shown
description: onCustomSubmit validates `if (!from || !to || from > to)` but the if-block is empty (only a comment) — it then navigates regardless. Server-side resolveSourceAttributionRange treats any from>to / malformed range as invalid and silently returns the 90d default window. The result: the user types e.g. From=1 Jun, To=1 Jan, clicks Apply, and the page jumps back to showing the last-90-days data with the preset still reading 'custom' and their typed dates echoed in the inputs — no message explains why the numbers don't match the dates they entered. Same empty-validation pattern exists in the buyer-value date-filter.
userImpact: Recruiter enters a deliberate custom window, the report shows different (default-window) numbers, and nothing tells them their range was rejected. They may quote the wrong period's placement/revenue figures to a client believing they're looking at their chosen dates.
suggestedFix: Show an inline validation error (e.g. set local error state 'From date must be on or before To date' and `return` without navigating) when the range is invalid, instead of navigating into a silent server fallback. Apply the same fix to the buyer-value date-filter.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed at the code level in both date-filters. In src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/date-filter.tsx the onCustomSubmit handler (lines 64-78) has an empty `if (!from || !to || from > to) { /* comment only */ }` block at line 69-72, then unconditionally builds the URLSearchParams and calls navigate(). The buyer-value clone (src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/date-filter.tsx lines 67-81) is byte-for-byte identical, so the "same pattern in buyer-value" part of the claim is correct.

The server resolvers confirm the silent fallback: resolveSourceAttributionRange (source-attribution-range.ts:103-114) and resolveBuyerValueRange (buyer-value-range.ts:107-118) only accept a custom range when `from && to && isYmd(from) && isYmd(to) && from <= to`; any reversed/malformed range hits `return defaultWindow(now)` which yields preset '90d' silently.

Critically, I checked whether the page renders the "error UI" the date-filter comment promises ("any error UI the page renders") and the range-helper comment claims ("a small inline note handled in the page UI"). It does not. The only error UI on source-attribution/page.tsx is the `!result.ok` card (lines 135-142), and on buyer-value/page.tsx the `failedMetrics` card (155-162) — both fire ONLY on RPC load failure, never when a custom range was silently rejected. There is no code path that detects requested-preset != resolved-preset and surfaces a note. So the justifying comments are false.

Net user-facing behaviour for the From=1 Jun / To=1 Jan reversed-range case: navigation succeeds, server falls back to 90d, page re-renders showing last-90-days numbers, the subtitle shows the 90d window (range.from/range.to), and because range.preset is now '90d' the active preset button switches back to '90d' and the custom date form (gated on currentPreset === 'custom', lines 102/105) collapses — all with zero feedback. This is a genuine missing-feedback defect.

Two refinements that do NOT refute the core issue: (1) The claim's wording "preset still reading 'custom' and their typed dates echoed in the inputs" is inaccurate — after fallback the preset reverts to 90d and the custom inputs disappear entirely; arguably more confusing, not less. (2) The blank-field sub-case is mostly intercepted by the inputs' `required` attribute + browser-native form validation, so the substantive scenario is the reversed range (from > to), which has no native cross-field constraint and fully reaches the silent fallback.

Severity stays medium: it's a real missing-feedback bug on a low-traffic reports surface. The displayed figures are correct for the window actually used, just not the window requested, so there is a plausible (if not certain) path to a recruiter misquoting a period's placement/revenue numbers to a client. Not high — no data corruption, no cross-tenant leak, and the dates shown in the subtitle do reflect the actual window (a careful reader could notice the mismatch).

title: Send check-in: after a send fails, editing the subject/body does not clear the error, and the modal stays open with a stale error message
description: On a failed send, handleSend sets `status = { kind: 'error', message }`. But `showForm` is only true for `ready | sending | needs_consent` — once status becomes 'error', the subject/body form is unmounted and only the red error text shows. The recruiter cannot edit and re-send without closing and reopening the modal (which re-runs the whole AI draft request + 10s poll from scratch). There is also no Retry/Send button in the error state for the final-send failure path, so the only recovery is a full modal reset.
userImpact: If the Outlook send fails (Graph 5xx, transient network), the recruiter is stuck on a dead error screen inside the modal with their edited draft gone and must redo the entire AI-draft wait. Friction on a flow that is supposed to be one-click.
evidence:
const showForm = status.kind === 'ready' || status.kind === 'sending' || status.kind === 'needs_consent' // 'error' not included // error branch renders only:
{status.message}
suggestedFix: Keep the form mounted in the 'error' state (add 'error' to showForm, or render the form whenever subject/body are populated) and show the error message above the form so the recruiter can retry the send without re-drafting. Optionally include a Retry button that re-calls handleSend.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed in code. In src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx, handleSend (line 198) sets status={kind:'error', message} on the non-reconnect send-failure path. This is reachable on a transient Outlook/Graph failure: sendOutreachAction (src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/outreach-actions.ts:274) returns {ok:false, error:'Could not send the email. Please try again.'} for any sendMail failure other than needs_consent/not_connected. showForm (lines 230-233) is true only for ready|sending|needs_consent, so on 'error' the subject/body form unmounts and only lines 256-260 render a bare red
{status.message}
with no Send/Retry button. The subject/bodyText useState values survive in memory but are not rendered and there is no UI path to re-submit them. The only recovery is Cancel/close, which unmounts SendCheckinModalBody and on reopen re-runs requestOutreachDraftAction + the up-to-10s poll from scratch AND overwrites the recruiter's edited subject/body with the fresh AI draft (lines 167-168/221-222) — so edits are lost and a Sonnet re-draft is incurred. Note: the title's 'editing the subject/body does not clear the error' framing is technically moot since the form isn't even rendered in the error state, but the substantive defect — dead-end error state, no in-place retry, lost edited content, error copy says 'Please try again' while offering no try-again control — is materially equivalent to and worse than the claim. The 'timeout' state by contrast DOES provide a Retry button (lines 247-254), showing the omission for the send-error path is an inconsistency, not intentional. Severity stays low: this is an uncommon error path (requires an actual Graph 5xx/network failure during send), and a clumsy but functional recovery exists (reopen the modal). The suggested fix (keep the form mounted in 'error' and/or add a Retry button) is correct and low-risk.

title: Buyer-value report page is unreachable from the UI
description: The /reports/buyer-value page is fully built (5 charts, methodology, date filter) but the Reports hub's REPORTS array only contains the source-attribution card. There is no link to /reports/buyer-value anywhere in the hub, and the top nav only links to /reports. The only way to reach the buyer-value report is to type the URL by hand.
userImpact: During the demo, the founder cannot show the buyer-value / acquirer-due-diligence dashboard (the headline 'sale-ready' report) by clicking. It looks like the feature does not exist. If a client asks 'where are the placement / commission / pipeline-value charts?' there is no clickable path to them.
suggestedFix: Add a second ReportCard for { href: '/reports/buyer-value', title: 'Buyer-value report', description: '...' } to the REPORTS array so the card renders and is clickable on the hub.
verifierReasoning: Verified directly against the code. src/app/(app)/reports/page.tsx:27-34 defines REPORTS with a single entry (/reports/source-attribution); the hub renders cards exclusively from this array (lines 58-77), so /reports/buyer-value never appears as a card. A full-codebase grep for any href/Link/router.push/redirect targeting /reports/buyer-value returns ZERO actual navigation references — every match is inside a code comment (charts-bundle, date-filter, buyer-value-range, chart components). The top nav (src/components/app/top-nav.tsx:15) and mobile drawer (mobile-nav-drawer.tsx:49) link only to /reports. The dashboard has no reports link. The source-attribution page only links back to /reports (line 112), never onward to buyer-value. Meanwhile src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/page.tsx is genuinely complete: 5 metric cards (stacked bar, horizontal bar, source-ROI table, pipeline-value sparkline, commission table), a DateFilter, and a Methodology details block. So a fully-built, working page is reachable only by hand-typing the URL — confirming the dead-button claim exactly as described. Severity stays high: in-code comments label this the 'acquirer-due-diligence' dashboard, which is precisely the founder's headline 'sale-ready' demo asset per the project's strategic framing; not being clickable means it looks nonexistent during a live client demo. It is not 'critical' only because the page itself is not broken and the fix is a trivial one-line array append.

title: Send check-in 'Retry' poll loop has no cancellation guard — setState on unmounted modal
description: handleRetryDraft runs the same async request+poll loop as the mount effect (up to ~10s of setTimeout polling) but, unlike the effect's go() which checks a `cancelled` flag, it has zero cancellation guard. If the recruiter clicks Retry then closes the modal (Cancel, Escape, backdrop, or the row re-renders after revalidate), the loop keeps running and calls setStatus/setSubject/setBodyText after the body component has unmounted.
userImpact: Triggers a React 'state update on unmounted component' warning and can resurrect/replace state on a stale closure. In practice the modal can briefly flash content or the next time it opens behave oddly; in dev it spams the console which is alarming during a live demo.
suggestedFix: Hoist a shared `cancelledRef`/AbortController used by both go() and handleRetryDraft, or refactor handleRetryDraft to flip a `retryNonce` state that the existing effect keys on, so unmount cancels it. At minimum add `if (cancelled) return` checks after each await using a ref that the effect cleanup sets.
verifierReasoning: The structural defect is exactly as described. In src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx the mount effect's go() (lines 147-175) guards every await with a `cancelled` flag (declared line 144, set true in cleanup line 179, checked lines 149/157/159). handleRetryDraft() (lines 201-228) runs the identical request + up-to-10x setTimeout(1s) poll loop but has NO cancellation guard at all — it calls setStatus/setSubject/setBodyText (lines 206, 209, 214, 221, 222, 227) after each await with no flag/ref the unmount cleanup can flip. The unmount path is genuine: dormant-client-row.tsx holds `open` in useState and the parent renders `{open ? <SendCheckinModalBody/> : null}` (line 79) inside a plain Radix Dialog (no forceMount), so Cancel (onCancel line 83), Escape, backdrop, or X all set open=false and unmount the body while the retry loop keeps running. So the code-smell / inconsistency the reviewer points to is real and worth fixing (hoist a shared cancelledRef used by both go() and handleRetryDraft).\n\nHowever, the claimed USER IMPACT is overstated for this stack. The repo is React 19.2.4. React removed the legacy 'Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component' warning back in React 18, so the central claimed symptom — console spam that is 'alarming during a live demo' — does not actually occur here. Post-unmount setState calls are silent no-ops bound to the dead fiber; when the modal reopens a fresh SendCheckinModalBody mounts with fresh useState, so the stale closure's setters cannot resurrect/replace the new instance's state — the 'flash content' / 'behave oddly on reopen' outcomes are not supported. The only genuine residual effect is a handful of redundant getLatestOutreachDraftAction server-action round-trips firing for up to ~10s after close: wasted work, no visible bug, no warning, no tenancy/PII concern. Hence real but low, not medium.

title: Send check-in success gives no confirmation — modal just vanishes
description: On a successful Outlook send, handleSend calls onSent() which immediately closes the modal. There is no toast, no inline 'Sent' state, and no other confirmation. The user just sees the dialog disappear. CLAUDE.md explicitly requires submit feedback (toast or inline) on every form handler.
userImpact: After clicking 'Send via Outlook' the recruiter cannot tell whether the email actually sent or whether the modal closed by accident. They may re-open and re-send, or assume it failed. For a feature that sends real client-facing email, the silent close erodes trust during the demo.
suggestedFix: Before/after closing, surface a success toast (sonner) e.g. toast.success('Check-in sent to ' + clientName). Alternatively show a brief 'Sent' state in the modal before auto-closing, mirroring the feedback FAB's success screen.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is factually accurate. In /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx, handleSend (lines 183-199): on result.ok it calls onSent() (line 191) and returns. onSent is wired to () => onOpenChange(false) (line 82), so the dialog simply closes. The Status union (lines 45-52) has no 'sent'/'success' member, and there is no toast.success call anywhere in the success path. The caller dormant-client-row.tsx (lines 57-62) also adds no confirmation — it only passes setOpen as onOpenChange. So a successful real client-facing email send (Microsoft Graph) yields no positive confirmation; the modal just vanishes.

This deviates from an established codebase convention: sonner's Toaster is mounted globally in src/app/layout.tsx, and toast.success(...) is used for success confirmation in ~10+ sibling handlers (e.g. log-note-form.tsx:45 'Note added.', log-activity-form.tsx:60, candidate-applications.tsx:102, float-form.tsx:36). The reviewer's cited comparison is also valid: the feedback FAB (floating-feedback-button.tsx:108-109) renders a 'Thanks — sent.' success screen via a { kind: 'success' } state, then auto-closes after 1500ms — exactly the pattern suggested. So this modal is the odd one out and does violate the CLAUDE.md 'surface submit feedback' convention.

I downgraded severity from medium to low because: (a) the error and reconnect/consent paths ARE handled inline (lines 256-281), so this is purely missing positive feedback, not a silent failure of the dangerous kind; (b) the modal closing is itself a (weak) completion signal, and the dormant-clients widget's 'Last contact' will subsequently reflect the logged activity; (c) it is a polish/UX gap rather than a functional or data-integrity defect. It is real and worth a one-line fix (toast.success or a brief 'Sent' state before auto-close), but its real-world demo impact is modest.

title: Custom date range form accepts invalid input silently (no error shown, server falls back)
description: onCustomSubmit checks `if (!from || !to || from > to)` but the block body is empty (only a comment). It then navigates regardless. The server's resolveSourceAttributionRange silently falls back to the default 90-day window for an invalid range. The user typed e.g. From 2026-05-01, To 2026-01-01, clicked Apply, and the page silently shows the default window with no explanation. The identical bug exists in the buyer-value date-filter (src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/date-filter.tsx line 72).
userImpact: A recruiter who picks a reversed or partial date range sees results for a completely different window than they asked for, with no error message. They will mistrust the numbers ('I asked for January but it's showing the last 90 days'). In a demo this looks like the date filter is broken.
suggestedFix: Set local error state when from>to or fields are empty, render an inline role=alert message under the form, and return early WITHOUT navigating. Or clamp/swap the dates before navigating. Apply the same fix to both date-filter.tsx files.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed in the actual code. In both src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/date-filter.tsx (lines 69-78) and src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/date-filter.tsx (lines 72-81), onCustomSubmit has an `if (!from || !to || from > to) { ...comment only... }` block with an EMPTY body — no error state set, no early return — then unconditionally builds the URLSearchParams and calls navigate(next). The server helpers resolveSourceAttributionRange/resolveBuyerValueRange (source-attribution-range.ts:103-115, buyer-value-range.ts:107-119) require `from <= to`; a reversed range fails that guard and returns defaultWindow(now), the 90-day default. So a recruiter who enters From 2026-05-01 / To 2026-01-01 and clicks Apply gets the default 90-day window with no error.

The page components confirm there is NO error UI for this case: both pages only render a text-destructive card for data-fetch failures (!result.ok / failedMetrics), and neither compares the requested vs resolved range. Worse, after fallback the resolved preset becomes '90d', which is echoed back as currentPreset to <DateFilter>, collapsing the custom form entirely (it renders only when currentPreset === 'custom') and showing the 90-day subtitle. The user sees the filter silently snap back to "Last 90 days" with zero explanation. The comment's claim that navigating exposes "any error UI the page renders" is false — no such UI exists. The bug is duplicated in both files as claimed.

One nuance affecting severity: both date inputs carry the HTML5 `required` attribute, so the `!from || !to` (empty/partial) sub-case is blocked by browser validation on UI submit (reachable only via hand-typed URL). The reversed-range case (`from > to`) is fully reachable through the UI with two valid dates and is the live failure mode.

Adjusting severity from the claimed medium to low: it is a genuine missing-feedback defect with a confusing demo outcome, but (a) the only UI-reachable trigger is a reversed range, which is an uncommon user action, (b) it produces no crash, no data corruption, and no cross-tenant leak — just a wrong/default window, and (c) the page clearly displays the actual window in the subtitle, so an attentive user sees the dates they got. Real, worth a quick fix (set inline role=alert error and return early, or swap the dates), but not medium-grade impact.

title: Dashboard has no loading.tsx — blank screen while 6 parallel queries run
description: DashboardPage awaits Promise.all of 6 Supabase round-trips (metrics, onboarding counts, recent activity with multiple batched sub-fetches, stale apps, follow-ups, dormant clients) before rendering anything. There is no loading.tsx in the (app) route group, so during the fetch the user sees the previous page / a blank content area. The recent-activity helper alone does up to ~8 sequential+parallel queries (initial activities, then 6 entity lookups, then 2 extra lookups), so this can be visibly slow.
userImpact: On first load or navigation back to the dashboard, the founder/client sees a blank or stale screen for a noticeable beat with no skeleton, making the flagship landing page feel sluggish/broken on the demo connection.
evidence:
const [metrics, onboardingCounts, activityResult, staleResult, followUpResult, dormantResult] =
  await Promise.all([ ...6 calls... ])  // no loading.tsx in (app)/
suggestedFix: Add src/app/(app)/loading.tsx (or a dashboard-scoped loading.tsx) rendering skeleton metric cards + widget placeholders. Optionally stream the slow widgets with <Suspense> so the metric cards paint immediately.
verifierReasoning:
Tried to refute it; every checkable fact holds.

1. No loading.tsx in the (app) route group root. `find` over src/app returns only one loading.tsx — src/app/(app)/candidates/loading.tsx. The dashboard root (src/app/(app)/) has page.tsx, layout.tsx, error.tsx but no loading.tsx (and no template.tsx anywhere). Confirmed.

2. The dashboard awaits all six helpers before rendering. src/app/(app)/page.tsx:28-36 is `const [...6...] = await Promise.all([getDashboardMetrics, getOnboardingCounts, getRecentActivity(20), getStaleApplications(20), getFollowUpCandidates(10), getDormantClients])`. Nothing renders until the slowest resolves. Confirmed.

3. Recent-activity is multi-wave. src/lib/db/dashboard.ts getRecentActivity does 3 sequential round-trip waves: wave 1 = fetch activities; wave 2 = Promise.all of 6 entity lookups (candidates/jobs/companies/contacts/applications/users); wave 3 = Promise.all of 2 extra lookups (extraCandidates/extraJobs). Up to ~9 queries across 3 sequential waves — matches the claim's "~8 sequential+parallel" description. On a high-latency demo connection this is the dominant cost.

4. The page is dynamic, so the boundary matters. Layout (src/app/(app)/layout.tsx) calls supabase.auth.getUser() (cookie read) and the page runs uncached Supabase queries with no `revalidate`/`cache`/`force-static`, so the RSC re-executes on every visit — it is never statically prerendered.

Next.js behavior with no loading.tsx and no Suspense: on client-side (soft) navigation the router holds the previous segment mounted and shows the stale page for the full fetch duration with zero skeleton/progress; on cold load the browser shows blank until first byte streams. The claim's mechanism is therefore materially correct.

Notably candidates/ DID get a skeleton loading.tsx (5 rows x 6 cells) but the flagship dashboard did not — an inconsistency that marks this as an oversight rather than a deliberate choice.

Why not higher than low: nothing is functionally broken. Data loads correctly, every helper guards errors (Sentry capture + `.ok ? data : []` fallbacks in the page), and there is no crash, no wrong data, no security/tenancy issue. The only impact is perceived latency / missing skeleton on the landing page. "Medium" overstates it for a pure no-functional-breakage polish gap; "low" is the honest grade, sitting at the upper end of low given it's the demo-critical flagship page. The suggested fix (add src/app/(app)/loading.tsx with skeleton metric cards + widget placeholders, optionally stream slow widgets via Suspense so metric cards paint immediately) is the correct remedy.

title: Completed checklist steps remain keyboard/screen-reader activatable
description: Completed checklist steps get `pointer-events-none`, `tabIndex={-1}` and `aria-disabled`, but they are still rendered as a real <Link href=...> with the href present in the DOM. aria-disabled is advisory only — it does not block activation. A screen reader user navigating by links, or anyone using browser link-list navigation, can still follow a 'done' step (e.g. jump to /candidates/new for an already-added candidate).
userImpact: Minor confusion for assistive-tech users: a step shown as complete/struck-through can still be activated and navigates them to an add form. Not blocking, but an a11y inconsistency.
evidence: <Link href={step.href} className={... step.done ? 'pointer-events-none' : ...} tabIndex={step.done ? -1 : undefined} aria-disabled={step.done}>
suggestedFix: When step.done, render a non-interactive <span>/<div> (no href) instead of a <Link>, so the completed row is truly inert for all input modalities.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed in src/app/(app)/_dashboard/welcome-checklist.tsx lines 87-114. Every step — done or not — renders a real `<Link href={step.href}>`. The href is unconditionally present in the DOM. For a completed step the only differences are: className adds `pointer-events-none` (line 94), `tabIndex={-1}` (line 99), and `aria-disabled={true}` (line 100). There is no conditional that swaps the completed row to a non-interactive `<span>`/`<div>`, and there is no onClick/preventDefault guard on the Link (the only onClick in the file is the Dismiss button on line 79).

The a11y facts in the claim check out: (1) `pointer-events-none` blocks pointer/touch input but does NOT block keyboard activation or AT-driven navigation; (2) `tabIndex={-1}` removes the anchor from sequential Tab order but leaves it in the accessibility tree, and screen-reader users routinely navigate by links-list / rotor rather than Tab, so they can still land focus on it and press Enter; (3) `aria-disabled="true"` is purely advisory — it changes the announced state ("dimmed") but the browser does not suppress default navigation for a focused anchor with a valid href. So a 'done' step (e.g. 'Add your first candidate' -> /candidates/new) remains reachable and activatable by keyboard/screen-reader users, exactly as described.

Caveat that bounds severity: the whole card returns null when every step is done (line 54/58 `allDone`), so a completed row is only ever visible while at least one other step is incomplete. The link target is a normal, working add-form page, so following it leaks nothing and breaks nothing — it just sends the AT user to an 'add another' form when the row presents as complete/struck-through. That is a real but cosmetic a11y inconsistency. The suggested fix (render a non-interactive element with no href when step.done) is correct and trivial.

title: Declined activity rows can show 'Declined — reason' twice (headline + body)
description:
entryHeadline() renders 'Declined — ' for stage_change rows whose metadata carries decline_reason. The raw activity body for those same rows (written by move_application as 'Declined — ') is then ALSO rendered below in the body

. So a declined row shows the decline twice — once as a nice human label, once as the raw enum body.
userImpact: The recent-activity feed looks repetitive/unpolished for declined candidates: e.g. '· Declined — Skills mismatch' on one line and 'Declined — skills_mismatch' immediately below. Cosmetic but visible in the demo feed.
evidence:
return `Declined — ${formatDeclineReason(reason)}` // headline ... {entry.body ? (

{entry.body}

) : null} // body also prints 'Declined — '
suggestedFix: When the headline already encodes the decline reason, suppress the raw body for that row (e.g. don't render entry.body when kind==='stage_change' and a decline_reason headline was produced).
verifierReasoning: Confirmed end-to-end. move_application (supabase/migrations/20260523160100_move_application_with_placement_fields.sql:134-163) writes decline rows with body = 'Declined — ' || decline_reason::text (raw enum, e.g. 'Declined — client_rejected_skills') and metadata carrying decline_reason but NO 'label' key. getRecentActivity (src/lib/db/dashboard.ts:84) does select('*'), so body and metadata arrive raw. In recent-activity-feed.tsx, entryHeadline's first branch requires 'label' in meta (absent for declines), so it falls through to the decline_reason branch (lines 59-68) which returns `Declined — ${formatDeclineReason(reason)}` (human label, e.g. 'Declined — Client rejected — skills'). That headline renders at line 112; the raw entry.body then renders unconditionally at lines 120-124. There is no suppression of body when the headline already encodes the decline. formatDeclineReason (src/lib/legal/decline-reasons.ts:43) maps the enum to a human label, so the two lines differ in text and the duplication is visible. The component is live (wired at src/app/(app)/page.tsx:85) and the decline path is production-used (DeclineModal → move_application). The earlier migration (20260518201900) wrote the same raw-enum body format, and body is never null for declines, so there's no scenario that avoids the duplicate. Severity stays low: purely cosmetic, both lines are factually correct, no data/function impact — but it is visible in the demo feed for any declined candidate, so a real polish item.

title: Reports hub and report pages do a getProfile fetch whose result is unused for rendering
description: ReportsPage (and both report pages) fetch getProfile(supabase, user.id) and redirect to /sign-in if !profile.ok, but the profile data is never used to render anything. This duplicates the auth guard already enforced by middleware + the (app) layout, and adds an extra round-trip. More importantly, a transient profile-fetch failure (network blip) would bounce an authenticated user to /sign-in mid-demo.
userImpact: Adds latency to every report page load and, on a flaky connection, can eject a logged-in user back to the sign-in screen even though they are authenticated — surprising and embarrassing in a live demo.
suggestedFix: Drop the getProfile call from these read-only report pages (the getUser check + middleware + (app) layout already guard auth), or if org context is genuinely needed later, only fetch it where used and do not redirect to sign-in on a transient fetch failure.
verifierReasoning: Verified against the actual code. All three report pages — src/app/(app)/reports/page.tsx:44, src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/page.tsx:89, and src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/page.tsx:81 — call `const profile = await getProfile(supabase, user.id)` then `if (!profile.ok) redirect('/sign-in')`, and `profile.data` is never referenced again (grep confirms profile appears only in the import, the assignment, and the .ok guard). The fetched data is genuinely unused: the data helpers (getSourceAttribution, getBuyerValue metrics) deliberately do NOT take an organization_id — source-attribution.ts documents that org isolation is enforced by `security invoker` RLS on the underlying tables, so the page never needs profile.data.organization_id for scoping. The guard is also redundant: the parent (app) layout (src/app/(app)/layout.tsx:18-25) already runs the identical getUser + getProfile + redirect-on-!profile.ok sequence on the same request, and actually USES profile.data (org id, email, name) — the report pages duplicate the call without using the result. So the claim is accurate: dead getProfile fetch, duplicated auth guard, extra DB round-trip, and on a transient `code:'internal'` failure it redirects an authenticated user to /sign-in. The one fact that caps severity: the transient-eject risk is not introduced by these pages — the (app) layout already redirects to /sign-in on the same !profile.ok condition for every authenticated route, so a network blip would bounce the user via the layout regardless. The report-page calls add a redundant round-trip (a single users-row PK lookup, sub-millisecond) and a redundant failure point, not a novel one. Real issue, genuinely low impact; the suggested fix (drop getProfile from these read-only pages) is correct and safe.

title: Source-attribution load failure shows £0 / 0 placements alongside the error card
description: When getSourceAttribution returns !ok, rows falls back to [] and the page renders the inline error card AND the headline cards computed from [] — i.e. 'Placements 0' and 'Fee revenue £0' and the 'No placements in this date range' empty state — simultaneously. The zeroed metrics read as real data sitting next to the error, conflating 'failed to load' with 'genuinely zero'.
userImpact: On a real load failure the recruiter sees both an error message and a confident '0 placements / £0 revenue / No placements in this date range', which is misleading — they may conclude the agency genuinely placed no one rather than that the report failed to load.
evidence:
const rows: SourceAttributionRow[] = result.ok ? result.data : []
const totalPlacements = rows.reduce(...)  // 0 on error
const totalFeePence = rows.reduce(...)    // 0 on error
{!result.ok && (<Card>...We couldn't load...</Card>)}  // shown alongside the 0/£0 cards
suggestedFix: When !result.ok, render ONLY the error card and skip the metric/table/top-3 cards (early-return the error UI), so a failed load never masquerades as zeroed data.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual file. Line 100: `const rows: SourceAttributionRow[] = result.ok ? result.data : []` — on an RPC error, getSourceAttribution returns `{ ok: false, code: 'internal' }`, so rows falls back to []. Lines 102-103 reduce [] to totalPlacements=0 and totalFeePence=0. There is NO early return on error. The render tree unconditionally outputs: (a) the error card at lines 135-142 ({!result.ok && <Card>...We couldn't load...</Card>}), AND (b) the "In this window" headline card at lines 144-169 showing Placements "0" and Fee revenue (formatPence(0) → "0p"), AND (c) the "By source" table EmptyState "No placements in this date range" (lines 184-189), AND (d) the "Top sources by revenue" "No placements in this date range." (lines 233-236). All four render together on a failed load. So the structural problem described is accurate: a failed load presents both an error and a confident zeroed report side by side, conflating "failed to load" with "genuinely zero". One minor factual nit in the claim's evidence: formatPence(0) returns the string "0p", not "£0" (formatPence returns `${p}p` for p<100) — but this is cosmetically equivalent (still reads as zero revenue), so it doesn't change the substance. Severity stays low: this is a degraded-error UX/clarity issue, not data corruption or a security/tenancy bug; it only triggers when the RPC actually errors (a Sentry-captured internal failure, off the happy path), and crucially the user is NOT left with zero signal — the explicit error card "We couldn't load the source-attribution report" is shown. The defect is that the zeroed metric/empty-state cards aren't suppressed alongside it. The suggested fix (early-return only the error card) is correct and trivial.

title: Source-attribution report crashes on render the moment any placement exists (numeric arrives as string, .toFixed undefined)
description: The page renders `{row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)} days` for every source row. `avg_time_to_place_days` is declared `numeric(10,1)` in the RPC (20260520023200_phase3_source_attribution_rpc.sql). PostgREST serializes Postgres `numeric` (and `bigint`) as JSON STRINGS to preserve precision — so at runtime `avg_time_to_place_days` is a string like "12.3", not a number. `String.prototype.toFixed` does not exist, so this throws `TypeError: row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed is not a function` during the Server Component render, which is caught by src/app/(app)/error.tsx and replaces the entire report with the generic error screen. The proof this is the runtime behaviour is in the SAME codebase: buyer-value/page.tsx line 106-107 wraps the sibling numeric columns `median_days`/`p90_days` in `Number(...)` precisely because the author already hit this — but the fix was never applied to `avg_time_to_place_days`. Empty result set (zero rows) does NOT trigger it (the table isn't rendered), so it will pass an empty-tenant smoke test and only blow up once the demo has real placement data.
userImpact: On the live demo, clicking into the Source Attribution report (the ONLY report linked from the /reports hub) shows a generic error page instead of the report, as soon as the agency has any placed candidate. The headline feature of the reports section appears broken.
evidence:
Page: `{row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)} days`  // numeric → string at runtime
RPC: `avg_time_to_place_days numeric` and `...::numeric(10,1) as avg_time_to_place_days`
Contrast (same migration's numeric handled correctly): buyer-value/page.tsx `median: Number(r.median_days ?? 0), p90: Number(r.p90_days ?? 0)`
suggestedFix: Coerce at the boundary in getSourceAttribution (src/lib/db/source-attribution.ts), mapping each row to `{ ...row, total_fee_pence: Number(row.total_fee_pence), avg_time_to_place_days: Number(row.avg_time_to_place_days) }` (placements_count is int and already a number). Then every consumer (.toFixed, formatPence, reduce) gets real numbers. Apply the same coercion in getCommissionSummary, getPipelineValueSparkline, and the TTF mapping for completeness.
verifierReasoning:
The bug is real and verified end-to-end.

1) Crash site confirmed: src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/page.tsx:213 renders `{row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)} days` directly on the RPC value, with no coercion.

2) Wire type confirmed: supabase/migrations/20260520023200_phase3_source_attribution_rpc.sql declares the return column `avg_time_to_place_days numeric` and casts the body to `::numeric(10, 1)` (line 71). `total_fee_pence` is `bigint` (line 61). `placements_count` is `int` (line 60). PostgREST serializes `numeric` and `bigint` as JSON STRINGS (to avoid IEEE-754 precision loss); only `int4` comes back as a JSON number.

3) No client-side coercion: postgrest-js@2.105.4 parses the response body with a plain `JSON.parse(body)` (dist/index.mjs line 352) — no reviver, no BigInt handling. The default server client (src/lib/supabase/server.ts) adds no custom fetch/transform. So `avg_time_to_place_days` is the runtime string "12.3". `String.prototype.toFixed` does not exist → `TypeError: row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed is not a function` thrown during the Server Component render → caught by src/app/(app)/error.tsx → entire report replaced by the generic "Something went wrong" screen.

4) getSourceAttribution (src/lib/db/source-attribution.ts) types the row as `{ ...: number }` but that is a compile-time cast only (the rpc surface is cast via `unknown`), so TS never catches the runtime string. No boundary coercion is applied.

5) Codebase self-corroborates the mechanism: src/lib/db/candidates.ts:235 wraps a bigint `total_count` in `Number(...)`, and src/lib/db/clients.ts:92 wraps `total` in `Number(...)` — the team already learned that bigint counts arrive as strings. The buyer-value page also `Number()`-coerces the time-to-fill RPC's `median_days`/`p90_days` (lines 106-107). The source-attribution fields were simply never coerced.

6) Empty-set guard confirmed: page.tsx:184 renders an EmptyState when `rows.length === 0`, so a zero-placement tenant never reaches line 213 — the crash only surfaces once any placement exists, exactly as claimed. This will pass an empty-tenant smoke test and blow up on a demo with real data.

7) Severity: /reports links only this one report (src/app/(app)/reports/page.tsx REPORTS array has a single entry → source-attribution), so the headline reports feature is fully broken on any populated demo.

One imprecision in the claim (does not refute it): the claim frames buyer-value as having "fixed" this. Buyer-value only coerced the time-to-fill RPC fields. Its own Source ROI card components (source-roi-table.tsx and source-roi-cards.tsx) use the IDENTICAL un-coerced `row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)` and would crash the same way. So the defect is broader than described (three render sites: the standalone page plus both buyer-value sub-components), not narrower. The suggested fix — coerce `total_fee_pence` and `avg_time_to_place_days` to Number in getSourceAttribution so all consumers receive real numbers — is correct and is the right single-point remediation.

Note: formatPence(row.total_fee_pence) does NOT crash even though total_fee_pence is a bigint-string, because JS arithmetic (`p / 100`) coerces the string to a number; the comparison `p < 100` also coerces. Only `.toFixed` called as a method on a string throws. So the precise and only crash point is the `avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed` call, as the claim states.

title: Total fee revenue headline is wrong (string concatenation) — shows nonsense like £150000250000
description: `const totalFeePence = rows.reduce((acc, r) => acc + r.total_fee_pence, 0)`. `total_fee_pence` is a Postgres `bigint`, which PostgREST returns as a JSON string. With the accumulator seeded at the number `0`, the first `0 + "150000"` produces the STRING "0150000", and every subsequent `+` concatenates further: two rows of 150000 and 250000 pence yield `"0150000250000"`. That string is passed to `formatPence`, where `("0150000250000")/100` coerces to a huge number, producing a grossly inflated 'Fee revenue' headline. (Per-row `formatPence(row.total_fee_pence)` happens to display correctly because the single string coerces cleanly in the division, masking the bug at row level — only the summed headline is visibly wrong.) Note: if finding source-attr-tofixed-crash is fixed but this one isn't, the page renders but the headline is garbage.
userImpact: The 'Fee revenue' marquee number on the Source Attribution report is wildly inflated and meaningless once more than one source channel has placements — exactly the acquirer-facing number a recruiter would point at in a demo.
evidence:
`const totalFeePence = rows.reduce((acc, r) => acc + r.total_fee_pence, 0)` `
{formatPence(totalFeePence)}
` RPC return type: `total_fee_pence bigint` (string at runtime via PostgREST)
suggestedFix: Same boundary fix as source-attr-tofixed-crash: coerce total_fee_pence to Number in getSourceAttribution so the reduce does real addition. Do NOT just wrap in Number() at the call site piecemeal — fix it once in the data helper so all consumers (headline, topByRevenue sort, per-row) are consistent.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed at every load-bearing point. (1) The code is exactly as claimed: src/app/(app)/reports/source-attribution/page.tsx:103 has `const totalFeePence = rows.reduce((acc, r) => acc + r.total_fee_pence, 0)` and line 165 renders `formatPence(totalFeePence)`. (2) total_fee_pence is a Postgres bigint — confirmed in supabase/migrations/20260520023200_phase3_source_attribution_rpc.sql line 50 (`total_fee_pence bigint`) and line 61 (`coalesce(sum(a.fee_pence), 0)::bigint`); the RPC is NOT redefined elsewhere (the 20260524000200 migration explicitly reuses it). supabase-js/PostgREST serialize bigint as a JSON string to avoid >2^53 precision loss, so at runtime r.total_fee_pence is a string despite the TS annotation `number` in source-attribution.ts:29. (3) I reproduced the runtime behaviour in Node: reduce seeded with the number 0 concatenates the strings — two rows of "150000" and "250000" produce "0150000250000", and formatPence runs `("0150000250000"/100).toFixed(2)` = "£1500002500.00" instead of the correct "£4000.00". (4) Per-row masking is real: formatPence("150000") = "£1500.00" (single string coerces cleanly in the division), and topByRevenue's `b.total_fee_pence - a.total_fee_pence` sort works because subtraction forces numeric coercion — both exactly as the claim states. (5) totalPlacements on line 102 is fine because placements_count is an int4 (RPC line 49), which PostgREST returns as a real JSON number, so only the fee headline breaks. (6) The codebase's sibling buyer-value page (page.tsx:106-107) already wraps numeric RPC columns in Number(...), proving the maintainers know PostgREST returns non-int numerics as strings; the source-attribution page is the only place that reduces a bigint column with a numeric seed, which is the unique trigger. Caveat: I could not run the live app/DB, but the chain is deterministic and faithful to documented supabase-js bigint-as-string behaviour. Impact: the acquirer/demo-facing 'Fee revenue' marquee number is wildly inflated and nonsensical once 2+ source channels have placements — high severity for a live client demo. Suggested fix (coerce to Number once inside getSourceAttribution so headline, sort, and per-row stay consistent) is correct.

title: Buyer-value report: pipeline value, total fee, and commission all use bigint columns rendered raw — wrong/garbled money figures
description: Same numeric-as-string class across the buyer-value page. (1) `currentPipelineValuePence = lastSparkRow.pipeline_value_pence` (bigint→string) is fed to `formatGbpRound(p)` → `gbpRound.format(p / 100)`; division coerces so a single value renders, but the sparkline `y: r.pipeline_value_pence` feeds string y-values to Recharts (line chart tolerates string-numbers inconsistently and the tooltip shows the raw string). (2) source-roi-table.tsx / source-roi-cards.tsx line 59/47 call `row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)` — identical .toFixed-on-string crash as the source-attribution page. (3) commission-table.tsx and commission-cards.tsx render `formatPence(row.total_fee_pence)` and `formatPence(row.estimated_commission_pence)` (both bigint→string); single values coerce in division but are never validated. The author fixed only `median_days`/`p90_days` (numeric) via `Number()` at page.tsx:106-107 and missed every bigint pence column. This page is not linked from the /reports hub (separate inventory finding) so it may not surface in the demo, but it is reachable by URL and is the 'acquirer due-diligence' report.
userImpact: If the buyer-value report is opened (it exists at /reports/buyer-value), the Source ROI sub-table throws and is replaced by the error boundary; pipeline-value and commission money figures are unreliable.
evidence:
page.tsx: `const currentPipelineValuePence = lastSparkRow ? lastSparkRow.pipeline_value_pence : 0` then `formatGbpRound(currentPipelineValuePence)`
source-roi-table.tsx:59 `{row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)} days`
commission-table.tsx:43 `{formatPence(row.estimated_commission_pence)}`
Fix already applied to siblings: page.tsx:106 `Number(r.median_days ?? 0)`
suggestedFix: Coerce all bigint/numeric columns to Number inside the buyer-value data helpers (getPipelineValueSparkline → pipeline_value_pence; getCommissionSummary → total_fee_pence, estimated_commission_pence; getSourceAttribution → total_fee_pence, avg_time_to_place_days). Map rows once in src/lib/db/buyer-value.ts and src/lib/db/source-attribution.ts.
verifierReasoning:
Confirmed against the actual code. The buyer-value RPCs (supabase/migrations/20260524000200_buyer_value_rpcs.sql) declare pipeline_value_pence, total_fee_pence, estimated_commission_pence as `bigint` and median_days/p90_days as `numeric`; the source_attribution RPC (20260520023200) declares total_fee_pence `bigint` and avg_time_to_place_days `numeric(10,1)`. PostgREST serializes Postgres bigint (int8) and numeric as JSON STRINGS (to preserve precision JS Number can't), so at runtime these arrive as strings even though the TS types declare `number`. The types are unsafe because src/lib/db/buyer-value.ts callRpc casts the result through `unknown`, so tsc never catches the mismatch. The author's own code corroborates this: at page.tsx:106-107 median_days/p90_days are wrapped in Number(...) — pointless unless those values arrive as strings.

CRASH (the material finding, confirmed): source-roi-table.tsx:59 and source-roi-cards.tsx:47 call `row.avg_time_to_place_days.toFixed(1)` directly. A string has no .toFixed, so this throws TypeError during render of a Client Component. There is NO error.tsx in reports/ or buyer-value/, only src/app/(app)/error.tsx, so the throw bubbles to the (app)-level boundary and replaces the ENTIRE authenticated page (worse than the claim's 'sub-table replaced'). It fires whenever ≥1 source-attributed placement exists in the window (empty state guards rows.length===0). The identical un-coerced pattern at source-attribution/page.tsx:213 confirms the bug is real, not benign.

MONEY FIGURES (claim partly overstated): formatGbpRound does `p/100` and formatPence does `p<100 ? ... : (p/100).toFixed(2)`. With string inputs, `/100` and `<` coerce, so the headline pipeline value and the commission table figures actually render CORRECTLY by accidental coercion — they are unvalidated, not 'wrong/garbled'. The sparkline `y: r.pipeline_value_pence` passes strings to Recharts: line position coerces but the tooltip shows the raw string (minor). So the title's 'wrong/garbled money figures' is inaccurate for the headline/commission values; the truly broken thing is the Source ROI crash, which the claim arguably understates.

title: Reports pages redirect signed-in users to /sign-in if getProfile returns not-ok (e.g. transient error)
description: All three reports pages do `const profile = await getProfile(...); if (!profile.ok) redirect('/sign-in')`. `getProfile` returns `{ ok: false }` not only for 'no profile row' but also for any internal/transient DB error (the DbResult pattern collapses all failure modes to ok:false). The user IS authenticated (getUser succeeded immediately above), yet a transient profile-query hiccup bounces them to the sign-in screen mid-session — confusing, and on the reports hub the profile is never even used for rendering. The (app) layout already enforces auth, so this extra gate adds a failure mode without adding security.
userImpact: During the demo, a transient Supabase blip on the profile query throws the recruiter back to the sign-in page from the Reports section, looking like a session/login bug rather than a momentary glitch.
suggestedFix: On the reports hub, drop the getProfile call entirely (auth is already guaranteed by getUser + the (app) layout). On the two report pages, if profile is genuinely needed, only redirect on a true 'not found' (profile.ok===false AND code==='not_found'), and otherwise degrade gracefully rather than redirecting to sign-in on internal errors.
verifierReasoning:
Verified all cited code. All three reports pages (reports/page.tsx:44-47, source-attribution/page.tsx:89-92, buyer-value/page.tsx:81-84) do `const profile = await getProfile(supabase, user.id); if (!profile.ok) redirect('/sign-in')`. Grep confirms `profile` is assigned and `.ok`-checked but never referenced again (no `profile.data.*`) in any of the three render bodies — it is genuinely unused beyond the gate. getProfile (src/lib/db/profiles.ts:20-24) does collapse failure modes: it returns `{ok:false, code:'internal'}` on ANY Postgrest error and `{ok:false, code:'not_found'}` only when the row is missing; DbResult (src/lib/db/types.ts) carries that `code` discriminant, and the pages discard it — so a transient DB error on the profile query would indeed redirect an already-authenticated user to /sign-in. That part of the claim is factually correct, and dropping the dead getProfile call from the hub is a reasonable cleanup.

However the claim's framing that this 'adds a failure mode without adding security' overstates impact. The (app) layout (layout.tsx:22-25) runs the IDENTICAL getProfile + `if (!profile.ok) redirect('/sign-in')` for EVERY authenticated page, and it actually USES profile.data (org_id, email, name) — so it cannot be removed. That means: (a) the transient-blip→sign-in symptom already exists app-wide via the layout and would still occur after removing the page-level checks; (b) the page-level check is pure redundancy, not a NEW failure mode — it can only fire in the razor-thin window where the layout's getProfile succeeded but a second sequential getProfile in the same request failed. So the page code is removable dead weight, but removing it does not eliminate the described demo risk (the layout still does it). Real issue, but minor: it's redundant/unused-variable code, the demo-breaking scenario is doubly-conditional, and the dominant cause lives in the layout, not these pages.

title: Buyer-value report exists but has no link from the Reports hub — undiscoverable in the demo
description: The REPORTS array driving the Reports hub contains only the source-attribution card. /reports/buyer-value is a fully built page (5 metrics, charts, methodology) but is unreachable through normal navigation — there is no card or link to it anywhere in the hub. A recruiter (or the founder demoing) cannot reach the acquirer-value report without typing the URL by hand.
userImpact: The buyer-value dashboard — explicitly framed as the 'acquirer due-diligence' report and clearly significant for the agency's eventual sale — is invisible to the user. Effort built, zero discoverability.
evidence:
```
const REPORTS: ReportCard[] = [
  { href: '/reports/source-attribution', title: 'Source attribution', ... },
]
```
No entry for '/reports/buyer-value'. The route file src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/page.tsx exists and renders a full report.
suggestedFix: Add a second ReportCard `{ href: '/reports/buyer-value', title: 'Buyer-value report', description: '...' }` to the REPORTS array. (But gate the demo on fixing the buyer-value numeric-string bugs above first, since the Source ROI sub-card currently crashes.)
verifierReasoning: Verified directly in source. src/app/(app)/reports/page.tsx lines 27-34: the REPORTS array driving the Reports hub contains exactly one entry — /reports/source-attribution. There is no entry for /reports/buyer-value, and the hub only renders cards by mapping over REPORTS (lines 58-77), so no link is generated for it. Meanwhile src/app/(app)/reports/buyer-value/page.tsx is a fully built 343-line Server Component rendering 5 acquirer-due-diligence metric cards (placements per recruiter per quarter, time-to-fill by sector, source ROI, pipeline value sparkline, commission summary), a date filter, and a Methodology block — explicitly labeled 'Five acquirer-due-diligence metrics' (line 144). A full grep of src/ for 'buyer-value' found NO inbound Link/router.push/redirect to /reports/buyer-value anywhere: the top nav (top-nav.tsx:15) links only to /reports, the hub links only to source-attribution, and the only other matches are code comments in chart components and the route's own files. Strong corroboration: the buyer-value page itself has a 'Back to reports' link to /reports (lines 130-136), proving it was designed to be reached from the hub — but the hub was never updated to link forward. The route is reachable only by typing the URL by hand. Claim is accurate as described.

title: Welcome checklist 'Invite a teammate' step uses teamMembers > 1, but solo agency may never satisfy it — and counts pending invites incorrectly
description: Step 'Invite a teammate' is marked done when `teamMembers > 1`, where teamMembers is a head-count of the `users` table (getOnboardingCounts in dashboard.ts:477 counts public.users rows via RLS). For the anchor 2-3 person agency this is roughly fine, but: (a) a genuinely solo operator can never complete the checklist, so it never auto-hides via `allDone` and they must dismiss manually; (b) if invited-but-not-yet-accepted teammates do not yet have a public.users row, inviting someone does NOT flip the step to done, which is the opposite of what the 'Invite a teammate' label implies (the user invited, but the step stays open). This is a derived-completion edge case rather than a data-loss bug.
userImpact: A solo recruiter sees a permanently-incomplete 'Get started' card until they dismiss it; an owner who has invited a teammate may see the step still showing as not-done, which reads as 'my invite didn't work'.
evidence:
`{ label: 'Invite a teammate', href: '/settings/team', done: teamMembers > 1 }`
teamMembers source: `supabase.from('users').select('id', { count: 'exact', head: true })` (counts accepted member rows, not pending invitations).
suggestedFix: Either base the 'invite' step's done-state on whether an invitation has been sent (count pending invites too), or relabel/treat it as optional so allDone can be reached by a solo user. Lowest-risk: keep the count but document that a solo org dismisses the card.
verifierReasoning: Both behaviors described in the claim are verified in the actual code. (1) welcome-checklist.tsx:50 marks 'Invite a teammate' done only when teamMembers > 1. (2) dashboard.ts:470-507 (getOnboardingCounts) derives teamMembers from `supabase.from('users').select('id', { count: 'exact', head: true })` — a head-count of public.users via RLS, not of pending invitations. (3) There is a dedicated org_invitations table (migration 20260524000100) holding pending invites, and a public.users row is created only when the invitee actually signs in/accepts (the handle_new_user trigger in migration 20260517204503 inserts into public.users on auth-user creation). So sending an invite does NOT increment teamMembers until acceptance — confirming claim (b): the step stays open after inviting. (4) The auto-hide is gated by allDone = steps.every(s => s.done) (line 54) which returns null only if every step including teamMembers>1 is satisfied (line 58); a genuinely solo org permanently has teamMembers===1, so allDone is never reached and the card never auto-hides — confirming claim (a). However, mitigations cap the impact: a working Dismiss button (lines 60-63, 75-82) permanently hides the card via localStorage, so the solo operator is not stuck; and the card only renders once the org is non-empty (page.tsx:38-51 shows EmptyState otherwise), so it isn't shown on a brand-new account. No data loss, no tenancy breach, no broken functionality — the invite genuinely succeeds (row in org_invitations); only the checklist's derived completion label lags until acceptance. This is a cosmetic derived-completion UX nuance.

title: Send check-in 'Retry' loop has no cancellation guard — setState on unmounted modal if closed mid-retry
description: The initial draft loop in `useEffect`/`go()` correctly checks a `cancelled` ref after every await (lines 149-172) and the cleanup sets `cancelled = true` on unmount. But `handleRetryDraft` (lines 201-228) duplicates the polling loop WITHOUT any cancellation guard — it has no access to the effect's `cancelled` flag and no ref of its own. If the recruiter clicks Retry and then closes the modal (or the parent unmounts SendCheckinModalBody, which happens because the body only mounts while open===true), the retry loop keeps running for up to 10s and calls `setSubject`/`setBodyText`/`setStatus` on the unmounted component. This is a React 'state update on unmounted component' warning / wasted work, not a user-facing data error, hence low severity — but it is the kind of latent leak that compounds if the modal is reopened.
userImpact: No visible breakage in the happy path; in dev a console warning, and a small chance of a stale status flash if the modal is reopened quickly after a retry-then-close. Minor.
evidence: go() loop guards: `if (cancelled) return` after each await, with `return () => { cancelled = true }`. handleRetryDraft loop has NO such guard: `for (...) { await new Promise(...); const got = await getLatestOutreachDraftAction(...); if (got.data) { ... setStatus({kind:'ready'}) } }` with nothing checking mount state.
suggestedFix: Hoist the polling into a single helper that takes a `() => boolean` isCancelled accessor, and have both the effect and Retry use the same ref. Or move the retry to re-trigger the effect (e.g. bump a `retryNonce` state used as an effect dependency) so the existing cancelled-guard cleanup applies.
verifierReasoning:
The structural code claim is accurate. In src/app/(app)/_dashboard/send-checkin-modal.tsx, the effect's go() loop guards every await with `if (cancelled) return` (lines 149, 157, 159) and the cleanup sets `cancelled = true` (lines 178-180). handleRetryDraft (lines 201-228) duplicates the same poll-up-to-10s loop (await new Promise + await getLatestOutreachDraftAction) but has NO cancellation guard and no ref of its own. Since the parent only renders SendCheckinModalBody while open===true (lines 79-85), closing the modal mid-retry unmounts the body, the effect cleanup fires, but the in-flight handleRetryDraft closure keeps polling for up to ~10s and calls setStatus/setSubject/setBodyText on the dead instance. So the un-cancelled-async-loop is real.

However, the claim's stated USER IMPACT is materially wrong for this stack: (1) This is React 19.2.4 (package.json) — the 'state update on unmounted component' warning was removed in React 18, so there is NO dev console warning; setState on an unmounted component is a silent no-op. (2) 'Stale status flash on quick reopen' is impossible: the body has no persisted state and no reused key, so each reopen mounts a fresh instance at {kind:'requesting'}; the orphaned loop's setters are bound to the old dead instance and cannot touch the new one. (3) No 'compounding leak' — the orphaned loop self-terminates within <=10s and is GC'd; getLatestOutreachDraftAction (outreach-actions.ts:126) is a pure read (select on activities) with no mutation/email side effect, so the only cost is a handful of wasted read-only server-action round-trips after close.

Net: a genuine but cosmetic code-hygiene asymmetry with effectively zero user-facing impact for a live client demo. Worth fixing for cleanliness (the suggested retryNonce-dependency approach is good), but the severity rationale in the claim rests on three symptoms that don't actually occur on React 19.

title: Apply form silently discards salary expectation and availability on every submission
description: The public apply form collects, validates (Zod) and renders inputs for `availability` and `salary_expectation` (apply-form.tsx lines 294-338; schema.ts lines 58-67). But submitApplyAction's createCandidate call only writes full_name, email, phone, location, current_role_title, current_company, market_status, source, source_detail and consent_* (actions.ts lines 266-282). It never references `availability` or `salary_expectation` at all — `grep salary_expectation|availability actions.ts` returns nothing. The `candidates.salary_expectation` column EXISTS as integer (phase1_domain_schema.sql), so this is genuinely lost data, not a missing column. The re-application path (lines 234-261) writes neither field either. The applicant sees a success page and toast; the recruiter never sees the stated salary or availability.
