userImpact: An applicant types their salary expectation and availability, submits, and is told 'Application received'. The recruiter opening that candidate sees no salary and no availability — the two fields that most drive whether they bother calling the candidate. On a live demo this looks like the form is broken/lying. The data is gone, not recoverable.
evidence:
const insertRes = await createCandidate(supabase, {
  full_name: parsed.data.full_name,
  email: parsed.data.email,
  phone: parsed.data.phone ?? null,
  location: parsed.data.location ?? null,
  current_role_title: parsed.data.current_role_title ?? null,
  current_company: null,
  market_status: 'actively_looking',
  source: 'apply_form',
  source_detail: parsed.data.source_detail || slug,
  organization_id: org.id,
  consent_basis: 'consent',
  consent_at: new Date().toISOString(),
  consent_text_version: CURRENT_CONSENT_VERSION,
})  // <- no salary_expectation, no availability
suggestedFix: Map salary_expectation (parse the digit string to integer: parsed.data.salary_expectation ? Number(parsed.data.salary_expectation) : null) into the createCandidate payload, and add it to CreateCandidateInput. For availability, either add a column (ask before migrating) or store it on the activity/metadata you already write so the recruiter can see it. At minimum stop collecting fields you throw away.
verifierReasoning:
Verified directly against the code. The apply form collects and validates both fields: schema.ts:58 `availability: z.enum(AVAILABILITY_VALUES)` (REQUIRED, not optional) and schema.ts:63-67 `salary_expectation` (optional digit string). apply-form.tsx renders both as inputs (availability Select at 297-318, salary text input at 321-337) with defaults at lines 91-92.

The new-candidate insert in submitApplyAction (actions.ts:266-282) writes only full_name, email, phone, location, current_role_title, current_company, market_status, source, source_detail, organization_id, consent_basis, consent_at, consent_text_version. Neither `salary_expectation` nor `availability` is referenced. CreateCandidateInput (candidates.ts:314-333) does not even include these fields, and createCandidate's payload builder (candidates.ts:358+) has no slot for them — so even if passed they would be dropped. The re-application path (actions.ts:234-261) also writes neither (it only bumps market_status cold→actively_looking and logs an activity whose metadata holds only apply_form/slug/marketing_consent — confirmed via grep, activities carry no salary/availability). So the claim's core mechanics are correct: collected + validated + rendered, then silently discarded. The applicant gets a success page regardless. This is a genuine silent-fail data-loss bug, not a missing-column false positive.

Schema check: candidates.salary_expectation EXISTS as `integer` (phase1_domain_schema.sql:214) and no later migration adds it elsewhere — so salary IS a real column that could hold the value. There is NO `availability` column anywhere in migrations (grep returned nothing), so availability has no destination at all — storing it would require a migration or stashing it in activity metadata.

Severity adjustment (critical -> medium) for two reasons the original write-up omits: (1) The CV parse pipeline IS fired for apply-form submissions (confirmApplyAction:535 sends cv/uploaded -> parse-cv.ts:261 writes salary_expectation via markCandidateFieldsFromCV). That helper is empty-field-merge ONLY (D-08 comment, parse-cv.ts:247-248), and since the apply action never sets salary_expectation, the column is null at parse time, so a CV that states a salary WILL populate it. The recruiter is therefore not guaranteed to see a blank salary — only the applicant's explicitly-typed number is lost, and only when it differs from / is absent from the CV. (2) availability is genuinely and unconditionally lost with no fallback. Real user-facing harm exists (form collects data it throws away, the explicit typed salary can silently diverge from CV-derived value, availability is always gone), which is bad for a live client demo, but it is not data-corruption or cross-tenant leakage and the candidate/CV records are still created and parsed — hence medium, not critical.

title: LinkedIn upsert returns generic 500 on concurrent capture instead of converting the unique-violation into an update
description: The route comment (route.ts lines 147-155) and the unique-index migration (20260520065652) both claim the partial unique index on (organization_id, source_detail) where source='linkedin' 'converts the concurrent-write race into a deterministic 23505 that the upsert helper's existing dedup branch already handles.' It does NOT. upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn does a read (getCandidateByLinkedInUrl) THEN an INSERT. On a double-capture / retry of the same profile, both calls miss the read, then the second INSERT hits the unique index and gets a 23505 — but the INSERT error branch (lines 236-247) just Sentry-captures and returns { ok:false, code:'internal' }, which the route maps to a 500 `upsert_failed`. There is no catch-23505-then-retry-as-update. So the claimed idempotency guard is not wired up.
userImpact: A recruiter double-clicks Capture in the extension (or the extension auto-retries on a flaky network) and the second hit fails with a hard error toast, even though the candidate was actually saved. Looks like the capture failed when it succeeded, and the 'Updated existing candidate' UX the comment promises never fires for the racing case.
evidence:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('candidates')
  .insert(insertPayload)
  .select('id')
  .single()
if (error || !data) {
  Sentry.captureException(error ?? new Error('linkedin-upsert: empty insert'), {...})
  return { ok: false, code: 'internal' }   // 23505 not distinguished, no retry-as-update
}
suggestedFix: In the INSERT error branch, detect (error as {code?:string}).code === '23505' and on conflict re-run the linkedin_url dedup read and fall into the UPDATE path (return created:false). Then the comment's claim becomes true and a double-capture resolves to an 'updated' result rather than a 500.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed by code inspection — see below.

title: Apply form shows hardcoded careers@altus.co.uk in failure messages and consent text — wrong brand domain
description: The contact email is hardcoded to 'careers@altus.co.uk' in three places: the consent text rendered into the legal block (page.tsx:46 → renderConsentTextV2 contact_email) and both apply-form failure toasts (apply-form.tsx:176 and :192). Project memory states the brand domains are altusmove.com (email sender) and altusrecruit.com (web host); 'altus.co.uk' is neither and is almost certainly an unmonitored / non-existent mailbox. The consent text is the GDPR data-subject contact for copy/correction/deletion requests, so an unmonitored address there is a compliance liability, not just cosmetic.
userImpact: On the demo, if a CV upload fails the applicant is told to email careers@altus.co.uk — a mailbox the agency does not own/monitor, so the lead is lost. In the consent block, the applicant is told to use that same address to exercise their GDPR rights. A client evaluating the product will spot the wrong brand domain immediately.
suggestedFix: Replace with a real monitored address on the correct brand domain (e.g. careers@altusmove.com) or, better, derive it from the org record / an env var so per-org branding (already flagged as Phase 5) doesn't ship a hardcoded wrong-brand address tonight.
verifierReasoning: All three cited locations verified in the actual code. page.tsx:46 hardcodes contactEmail: 'careers@altus.co.uk' and passes it to renderConsentTextV2 (src/lib/legal/consent.ts), which substitutes it into the {contact_email} placeholder of CONSENT_TEXT_V2 — the GDPR copy/correction/deletion contact (consent.ts:40). That rendered consent text is displayed in the public DOM at apply-form.tsx:384 (the "Consent & permissions" block), so it is unconditionally visible to every applicant. apply-form.tsx:176 and :192 are the CV-upload-failure and confirm-failure toasts, both containing 'careers@altus.co.uk'. A repo-wide grep shows altus.co.uk appears ONLY in these three spots. The brand-domain claim also holds: project memory establishes altusrecruit.com as the web host and altusmove.com as the email sender; altus.co.uk is neither (and not even altus-consultancy.com, the Resend account address) — it is a fourth, unrelated domain the agency almost certainly does not own/monitor. There is no contact_email/support_email column in src/types/database.ts, so the address is genuinely hardcoded with no per-org field being ignored. The developer left an explicit comment (page.tsx:41-43) acknowledging this is a placeholder pending Phase 5 per-org branding, so it is intentional — but intentional does not mean correct: the placeholder they chose is a wrong-brand, likely-unmonitored mailbox that ships live to a public page, used both as the GDPR data-subject contact (compliance liability) and as the fallback contact on upload-failure (lost lead). On a live client demo both paths are reachable.

title: Apply re-application dedup is read-then-write with no unique constraint — concurrent/retried submits create duplicate candidates
description: submitApplyAction dedupes by reading getCandidateByEmailForOrg then branching to update-or-insert. There is NO unique constraint on (organization_id, email) — only a plain index (candidates_email_idx) — and createCandidate's own comment confirms 'the candidates table has no UNIQUE constraint on email in Phase 1'. The read-then-insert is a TOCTOU: two near-simultaneous applications from the same email (double-submit despite the client guard, a retried network request, or two browser tabs) both miss the read and both insert, creating duplicate candidate rows with separate CVs. The server action has no idempotency key and no DB-level guard to collapse them.
userImpact: A candidate who submits twice (impatient double-tap, or the page retries) becomes two candidate records in the recruiter's pipeline. The recruiter then sees and may contact a duplicate, or the 'has this person applied before' signal is wrong. Recoverable but sloppy, and visible if it happens during the demo.
suggestedFix: Add a partial unique index on (organization_id, lower(email)) where email is not null (ask before migrating), and have createCandidate map 23505 → re-read + append-CV-to-existing instead of returning a hard error. This makes the re-application path idempotent at the DB level rather than relying on a racy read.
verifierReasoning:
The factual core of the claim checks out against the code. submitApplyAction (src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts:225-315) is a classic read-then-write: it calls getCandidateByEmailForOrg, then branches to updateCandidate+activity if found, else createCandidate. There is no idempotency key and no DB-level collapse. The schema confirms no protective unique constraint: phase1_domain_schema.sql:235 creates candidates_email_idx as a PLAIN index on (organization_id, email) — non-unique. The only partial-unique index on candidates is candidates_linkedin_source_detail_uniq_idx (migration 20260520065652), scoped `where source = 'linkedin'`, which does NOT apply to apply-form rows (source = 'apply_form', set at actions.ts:274). And createCandidate's own comment (src/lib/db/candidates.ts:386-388) literally states 'The candidates table has no UNIQUE constraint on email in Phase 1 so this should never fire.' So the TOCTOU window genuinely exists: two near-simultaneous applications from the same email both miss the read at getCandidateByEmailForOrg and both reach createCandidate, producing two candidate rows + two CVs, since the 23505 mapping at candidates.ts:390 can never trigger for this case (no constraint to violate). The fix suggestion (partial unique index on (organization_id, lower(email)) + 23505→re-read in createCandidate) is technically sound.

However, the claimed MEDIUM severity overstates real-world impact. (1) Probability is low: triggering requires two full submit cycles racing within the read-to-insert window — and the apply flow is multi-step (submitApplyAction mints a signed URL, client PUTs the file, then confirmApplyAction fires), so a casual double-tap usually just re-enters submit before any candidate is duplicated only if both submits land in the same sub-second window. (2) A per-IP+org rate limiter runs at step 7 (checkApplyFormRateLimit), which dampens rapid repeat submits from the same network (though it is fail-open). (3) Blast radius is benign and recoverable: a duplicate candidate row in one tenant's pipeline — no cross-tenant leak, no data corruption, no security boundary crossed. The recruiter can merge/delete. This is an idempotency-hygiene defect, not a functional or security defect. For a live client demo specifically, the demo operator is not racing concurrent identical applications, so demo-visibility risk is marginal. Real but low.

title: confirmApplyAction reports success and navigates to the success page even when the parse pipeline never queues
description: confirmApplyAction wraps inngest.send('cv/uploaded') in try/catch and, on failure, swallows the error (Sentry only) and still returns { ok:true, redirectTo }. The CV row and storage object persist, but parsing_status stays 'pending' and no parse/embed ever runs. This is a deliberate M-8 decision (documented inline) so the applicant still reaches success — defensible for the applicant. The gap is on the recruiter side: there is no surfaced signal that this candidate's CV never got parsed/embedded other than a Sentry event; the recruiter has to notice the candidate has no extracted data and manually hit Retry.
userImpact: For the applicant this is fine — their CV is safe. For the recruiter, a candidate can silently sit un-parsed and un-embedded (so invisible to semantic search) with no in-app indication until someone happens to open that candidate and notice the empty profile. On a demo where 'AI parses every CV automatically' is the pitch, a silently-unparsed candidate undercuts the story.
suggestedFix: Keep returning ok for the applicant, but make the un-queued state visible to the recruiter: set/keep parsing_status='pending' AND ensure the candidate list/detail shows a 'parse not started — retry' affordance for pending CVs (the Retry button referenced in the comment), or have the batch sweep re-fire cv/uploaded for pending rows so it self-heals.
verifierReasoning:
The cited code is verbatim-accurate. confirmApplyAction (src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts:534-565) wraps inngest.send('cv/uploaded') in try/catch; on send failure it captures to Sentry only and returns { ok: true, redirectTo } so the applicant still reaches the success page. The candidate_cvs row was already written at parsing_status='pending' (default in migration 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:251; set in candidate-cvs.ts:97), so the parse/embed pipeline never runs and the row stays 'pending'.

Verified the recruiter-side gap is genuine and, in one respect, WORSE than the claim states. The M-8 inline comment says "Phase 1's Retry button on the candidate detail page re-fires cv/uploaded" — but I traced the UI: CvReviewPanel (cv-review-panel.tsx:260-268) routes parsing_status==='pending' to PendingState, which renders an indefinite "Parsing… usually under 30 seconds" spinner with NO retry button and a poll that simply stops after 5 minutes. The Retry/"Try again" button only exists in FailedState (parsing_status==='failed'). So a row stuck at 'pending' shows a permanent spinner with no in-app retry affordance — the documented recovery path does not actually exist for this state. The codebase itself already recognized this exact bug class on the recruiter upload/retry paths: retryParseAction (candidates/[id]/actions.ts) carries a comment "Review fix H1: ...empty catch left the row stuck at 'pending' with no Retry button... flip the row back to 'failed' so the user sees the Retry button instead of an indefinite spinner" and applies that fix — but confirmApplyAction was NOT given the same fix.

No self-heal exists for parsing: parse-cv.ts only triggers on the cv/uploaded event (no stuck-pending sweep). The candidates list page surfaces no parsing-status indicator.

One claim sub-assertion is OVERSTATED in the reviewer's favor: "invisible to semantic search" is not fully correct. embed-batch (embed-batch.ts, registered in api/inngest/route.ts) is a 10-min cron that sweeps candidates WHERE candidate_embedding IS NULL and embeds them from structured fields (full_name, current_role_title, location — all written by the apply form at actions.ts:266-282). So the candidate IS reachable by semantic search, just with a thin/degraded embedding (no CV-extracted skills, seniority, sector_tags, or CV text via candidateEmbeddingText).

Net: real silent-fail gap. Trigger requires a transient Inngest outage (uncommon). Applicant impact is benign by design. Recruiter impact: a candidate sits with no extracted profile data, a permanently-spinning "Parsing…" panel, no in-app retry, and only a Sentry event — and the documented recovery affordance is absent for this state. Searchability is partially preserved via the embedding cron. Low severity is the correct grade: low probability, partially mitigated, but a genuine un-fixed instance of a bug class the team fixed elsewhere.

title: LinkedIn ingest validates the bearer token but runs all DB queries as the anon role — the capture feature is broken (and its org-scoping depends on a session that never exists)
description: The route builds a cookie-based client with `const supabase = await createClient()` (src/lib/supabase/server.ts), then calls `supabase.auth.getUser(token)` to validate the extension's Bearer token. `getUser(token)` only authenticates that one /auth/v1/user call — it does NOT attach the token to the client, so every subsequent PostgREST call (`getProfile`, the dedup SELECTs in candidates-linkedin.ts, and the candidates INSERT) executes with the client's persisted session. The request originates from `chrome-extension://` and carries NO Supabase auth cookies, so there is no session: those queries run as the `anon` role. RLS on `public.users` (`members read org users` USING organization_id = current_organization_id()) and on `public.candidates` (`tenant select`) both gate on current_organization_id(), which returns NULL for anon. Result: getProfile returns no row → the route returns 500 `profile_not_found`; even if it got past that, the candidates INSERT's `candidates_set_org` trigger calls current_organization_id() = NULL and the set_organization_id() function raises 'organization_id is required and could not be resolved from auth context'. The whole capture path 500s. The route's entire tenancy model (comment: 'RLS enforces tenancy — no explicit organization_id filter needed because RLS reads auth.uid()') is built on an auth context that does not exist for this caller. The route was never exercised end-to-end — route.test.ts is entirely `it.todo`.
userImpact: If LinkedIn capture is shown in tonight's demo, clicking 'Capture' in the extension returns a 500/'profile_not_found' error and nothing is saved. The feature appears completely dead.
suggestedFix: Do not rely on a phantom session. Either (a) create a per-request authenticated client that actually carries the bearer token to PostgREST — e.g. createClient(url, anonKey, { global: { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } } }) after validating it with getUser(token) — so RLS resolves current_organization_id() correctly; or (b) use the service-role client and pass organization_id (resolved from getProfile via service-role) explicitly into upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn and the dedup queries, adding explicit .eq('organization_id', orgId) on every candidates query. Then write the real route tests that route.test.ts only stubs.
verifierReasoning:
I attempted to refute the claim and could not — every link in the chain is confirmed by the actual code and library internals.

1. Route (src/app/api/linkedin/ingest/route.ts:110) builds a cookie-based SSR client via createClient() (src/lib/supabase/server.ts), which passes NO global.headers Authorization and NO accessToken option. The request comes from chrome-extension:// with no Supabase cookies (confirmed by the route's own comment and src/app/api/linkedin/_cors.ts).

2. Verified in @supabase/auth-js 2.105.4 (GoTrueClient.js): getUser(jwt) -> _getUser(jwt) issues a single one-off GET /user with the jwt as a header. It does NOT call _saveSession/setSession, so no client session is created. The token is NOT attached to subsequent calls.

3. Verified in @supabase/supabase-js 2.105.4 (SupabaseClient.ts:536 _getAccessToken + lib/fetch.ts fetchWithAuth) and @supabase/ssr 0.10.3 (createServerClient.js, which does NOT set the accessToken option): every from() PostgREST call resolves its token via _getAccessToken -> await this.auth.getSession(); with no cookies there is no session, so it returns this.supabaseKey (the publishable/anon key). fetchWithAuth then sets Authorization: Bearer <anon key>. So every getProfile/dedup SELECT/INSERT runs as the anon role.

4. getProfile (src/lib/db/profiles.ts) selects from public.users. The RLS SELECT policy `members read org users` (migration 20260513151021) is granted `to authenticated` only and gates on organization_id = current_organization_id(). Anon has NO select policy on public.users at all -> zero rows -> getProfile returns not_found -> route returns 500 profile_not_found (route.ts:139-143). Confirmed.

5. Even if it got past that, the candidates policies (migration 20260513152244) are all `to authenticated`, and the candidates_set_org BEFORE INSERT trigger runs set_organization_id() (migration 20260517204504), which raises 'organization_id is required and could not be resolved from auth context' when current_organization_id() is NULL (anon has no auth.uid()).

6. route.test.ts is entirely it.todo (7 todos, zero real assertions) — the path was never exercised end-to-end.

No anon policies exist on users/candidates (grep confirmed none). There is no alternate auth wiring that would salvage this path. The feature 500s for the only caller it has (the extension). For a live demo, clicking Capture returns 500/profile_not_found and saves nothing — exactly the claimed user impact. The suggested fix is correct: either per-request client with global.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`, or service-role with explicit organization_id scoping.

Severity remains high: it is a complete, demo-breaking functional failure of a headline AI-capture feature, not a partial/edge-case bug. (Not critical only because it is a feature-dead failure, not a data-leak/security breach.)

Relevant files: /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/api/linkedin/ingest/route.ts, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/supabase/server.ts, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/candidates-linkedin.ts, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/profiles.ts, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/supabase/migrations/20260513151021_init_organizations_and_users.sql, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/supabase/migrations/20260517204504_harden_set_organization_id.sql, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/api/linkedin/ingest/route.test.ts.

title: LinkedIn dedup SELECTs (by URL and by email) have no organization_id filter and rely entirely on RLS that the calling context does not satisfy
description: getCandidateByLinkedInUrl and getCandidateByEmailLowercase query `candidates` with only `.eq('source_detail', linkedinUrl)` / `.eq('email', normalised)` and NO `.eq('organization_id', ...)`. The module comment explicitly states this is safe because 'the route handler is the authenticated context, so RLS enforces tenancy.' That premise is false for the only caller (the LinkedIn ingest route, which runs as anon — see linkedin-ingest-runs-as-anon). Today the practical effect is a broken feature, but the design is fragile: if anyone later switches the route to the service-role client (a natural fix for the 500), these queries would silently bypass tenancy and could return / dedup-merge against a candidate row from a DIFFERENT organization that happens to share a LinkedIn URL or email — a cross-tenant write. The in-function `existing.organization_id !== organizationId` assertion in the upsert only fires AFTER a foreign row is fetched, and only inside upsert — the two getter helpers are exported and could be reused elsewhere without that guard.
userImpact: As written, contributes to the capture feature failing. If 'fixed' by moving to service-role without adding org filters, one agency's LinkedIn capture could read or overwrite another agency's candidate — a cross-tenant data leak.
evidence:
const { data, error } = await supabase
    .from('candidates')
    .select('id, organization_id, email')
    .eq('source_detail', linkedinUrl)
    .limit(1)
    .maybeSingle()   // no .eq('organization_id', ...) — relies on RLS that anon caller doesn't satisfy
suggestedFix: Add an explicit organizationId parameter to both getter helpers and append `.eq('organization_id', organizationId)` to each query, so tenancy holds regardless of whether the caller is RLS-scoped or service-role. Keep RLS as the primary control but make the helpers safe-by-construction.
verifierReasoning:
The factual core of the claim is verified. In src/lib/db/candidates-linkedin.ts, getCandidateByLinkedInUrl (lines 43-48) and getCandidateByEmailLowercase (lines 68-73) query `candidates` with only `.eq('source_detail', ...)` / `.eq('email', ...)` plus `.limit(1).maybeSingle()` and NO `.eq('organization_id', ...)`. The module comment (lines 17-19, 24-27) explicitly justifies this by asserting "the route handler is the authenticated context, so RLS enforces tenancy."

That premise is genuinely false for the sole caller. The ingest route (src/app/api/linkedin/ingest/route.ts:110) builds the client via `createClient()` from src/lib/supabase/server.ts — a cookie-only SSR client using the publishable (anon) key. The bearer token is validated with `supabase.auth.getUser(token)` (line 111) but is NEVER injected into the client session: I grepped the route and found no `setSession`, no `accessToken` option, no `global.headers` Authorization. chrome-extension:// origins carry no Supabase cookies, so the client's `.from()` queries run as anon. This matches the cross-referenced "linkedin-ingest-runs-as-anon" finding — getProfile at line 138 would itself fail under anon RLS and 500 before the dedup helpers run. So today the feature is broken, not leaking.

The latent cross-tenant risk is also technically real: RLS on candidates is `to authenticated` only (migration 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:467-475 use `current_organization_id()`), so a service_role client bypasses these policies. If someone "fixed" the 500 by swapping to service-role, the un-filtered getters would scan all orgs and `.limit(1)` could return a foreign org's row.

HOWEVER, the claim's strongest assertion — that this could "overwrite another agency's candidate, a cross-tenant write/merge" — is mitigated by an existing compensating control the author documented: upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn (lines 134-148) fails closed with `if (existing.organization_id !== organizationId) return { ok: false, code: 'internal' }` before any UPDATE. So even under a naive service-role switch, the write path aborts rather than clobbering a foreign row. The unique index is also org-scoped: candidates (organization_id, source_detail). The residual exposure is a cross-tenant READ via the two exported getters if reused elsewhere without the guard — but I grepped and confirmed they have NO independent callers today (only upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn uses them, and only the route uses that).

Net: the factual claim (missing org filter, false comment premise, service-role would bypass) is accurate and the suggested fix (add explicit organizationId param + `.eq('organization_id', organizationId)` to both getters) is sound defense-in-depth aligned with the project's "safe-by-construction" ethos. But it is a latent/hypothetical fragility, not a present-tense exploitable leak: no live cross-tenant data flows today (feature runs anon and 500s), and the only write path that could touch a foreign row is already guarded fail-closed. High severity is not warranted for a live client demo; low is the honest grade.

title: Apply-form rate limiter fails open on every error and its select-then-update has a race that lets the cap be exceeded
description: checkApplyFormRateLimit returns { allowed: true } on a select error, on an update error, and on any thrown exception (three fail-open paths). Combined with the non-atomic select→update increment (acknowledged in the header comment), a burst of concurrent submissions all read the same count and all pass before any update lands. For an unauthenticated, service-role-backed writer that creates candidate rows + mints Storage signed URLs, this means the documented '3 per 5 min' cap is best-effort only: a determined bot (or a transient DB hiccup the bot can induce) bypasses it entirely. Turnstile is the real gate, but Turnstile itself is OPTIONAL in env (TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY is optional) — if it is unset in production the verify helper fails closed, but the whole anti-abuse posture then rests on the honeypot + this fail-open limiter.
userImpact: Under load or a transient Supabase error, the public apply form can be flooded — creating many candidate rows and signed upload URLs per IP — degrading the recruiter's inbox and Storage usage. Not a data leak, but a real abuse surface on a public endpoint.
suggestedFix: Move the increment into a single SECURITY DEFINER plpgsql function doing an atomic UPSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET count = count + 1 RETURNING count, and deny when the returned count exceeds the cap. Reconsider fail-open: on a select/insert error for a public endpoint, fail-closed-with-friendly-retry is safer than unlimited writes. Ensure TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY is mandatory in production.
verifierReasoning: The code-level facts in the claim are accurate. checkApplyFormRateLimit (src/lib/integrations/apply-form-rate-limit.ts) returns { allowed: true } on three paths: select error (line 111), update error (falls through, lines 155-158), and any thrown exception (line 168). The header comment (lines 31-33) explicitly documents the fail-open posture as intentional. The select-then-update increment (lines 95-158) is non-atomic and the header comment (lines 27-29) itself acknowledges the race window and recommends a SECURITY DEFINER plpgsql function for Phase 5. The backing table (migration 20260519092946) has PK (ip_hash, organization_id, window_start), so concurrent requests contend on one row and a burst can all read the same count and pass before any update lands — the TOCTOU race is real. Downstream, a passed check leads to createCandidate row inserts and createSignedUploadUrl mints (actions.ts lines 266-345). So the mechanical claim is correct.\n\nHowever, the claim's central thesis about user-facing impact is overstated. In the caller (src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts) the gate order is honeypot -> Turnstile -> zod -> blocklist -> file-meta -> rate limit -> writes. Turnstile runs BEFORE the rate limiter and before any DB write or signed-URL mint. verifyTurnstileToken (src/lib/integrations/turnstile.ts:38-40) fails CLOSED when TURNSTILE_SECRET_KEY is unset, returning success:false, which the action rejects (line 147) — so a missing key disables the whole form rather than exposing the rate limiter as the sole gate. The dev-bypass token is hard-rejected in production (actions.ts:138). There is therefore no production configuration where the fail-open limiter is the only line of defense: either the key is set (Cloudflare gates every request with a single-use, 300s token) or it is unset (form fully closed). To flood the form a bot must defeat Turnstile on every request first; the rate limiter is explicit defense-in-depth, not the primary gate. The transient-DB fail-open is real but bounded — an attacker can't reliably induce Supabase errors, and each such request still passed Turnstile.

title: LinkedIn ingest CORS allows any chrome-extension origin when LINKEDIN_EXTENSION_ID is unset, with Allow-Credentials: true
description: When LINKEDIN_EXTENSION_ID is not set (it is optional in env.ts), corsHeadersFor falls back to allowing ANY origin matching /^chrome-extension:\/\/[a-p]{32}$/ and echoes it back with `access-control-allow-credentials: true`. So any installed Chrome extension on a recruiter's machine could make credentialed cross-origin requests to /api/linkedin/ingest. The real auth gate is the Bearer token (which a foreign extension wouldn't have), so this is defence-in-depth rather than a direct leak, and the header comment flags it as a dev-only soft fallback. But 'optional in production' means a misconfigured deploy silently widens the allowlist to all extensions. Allow-Credentials:true with a reflected, broadly-matched origin is exactly the pattern CORS guidance warns against.
userImpact: Low in practice (token still required), but a production deploy that forgets LINKEDIN_EXTENSION_ID quietly exposes the ingest endpoint's CORS to every Chrome extension, undermining the intended single-extension pinning.
evidence:
if (pinnedExtensionId) { if (origin === `chrome-extension://${pinnedExtensionId.toLowerCase()}`) return true }
  return EXTENSION_ID_RE.test(origin)   // any [a-p]{32} extension id
...
headers.set('access-control-allow-credentials', 'true')
suggestedFix: Make LINKEDIN_EXTENSION_ID required in production (env: required when NODE_ENV === 'production') and drop the regex fallback outside development, OR keep the regex fallback but only when NODE_ENV !== 'production'. In production, echo Allow-Origin only for the exact pinned id.
verifierReasoning:
Code confirms the claim exactly. In src/app/api/linkedin/_cors.ts, when pinnedExtensionId is undefined, isAllowedExtensionOrigin falls through to EXTENSION_ID_RE.test(origin) (line 37), matching ANY chrome-extension://[a-p]{32} origin. corsHeadersFor then reflects that origin and sets access-control-allow-credentials:true (lines 46-47). In src/lib/env.ts:93, LINKEDIN_EXTENSION_ID is z.string().min(32).max(64).optional() with no NODE_ENV/superRefine production guard (grep found none), so a production deploy that forgets the var silently widens the allowlist to every Chrome extension. The cited evidence and suggested fix are technically correct, and the reflected-broad-origin + allow-credentials:true pattern is indeed what CORS guidance warns against — so this is a genuine config-hygiene gap.

However, real-world impact is minimal, matching the claim's own 'low' grade. The route's auth gate is an explicit Authorization: Bearer token validated via supabase.auth.getUser(token) (ingest/route.ts:100-114), NOT cookies. A foreign extension cannot obtain the recruiter's Supabase access token (it lives in the Altus app origin's storage, isolated from other extensions), so it gets a 401 regardless of CORS. Critically, access-control-allow-credentials:true is effectively moot here because the endpoint never authenticates via cookies — it only trusts the caller-supplied bearer header. There is no path to cross-tenant data access; the 'cross-tenant' category is overstated. This is defence-in-depth hardening, not an exploitable leak, and poses no risk to a live client demo.

title: Apply success page 'our website' link sends the unauthenticated candidate to /, which middleware redirects to the recruiter sign-in page
description: After a successful application the success page tells the candidate 'You can close this window or visit our website' with a Link to `/`. `/` is not in PUBLIC_PATHS (src/lib/supabase/middleware.ts), and the candidate is unauthenticated, so updateSession redirects them to /sign-in?next=%2F — the recruiter's internal sign-in screen. A just-applied candidate clicking the friendly link lands on a login form for a CRM they have no account for.
userImpact: A candidate who just applied clicks 'our website' and is dumped on the agency's internal sign-in page — confusing and off-brand at the exact moment you want a clean finish. Visible to anyone who completes the public form, including in the demo.
evidence: <Link href="/" className="underline">our website</Link>  // `/` → middleware → /sign-in (candidate is unauthenticated)
suggestedFix: Point the link at the org's actual public marketing site (or remove the link) rather than `/`. If a home destination is needed, add a genuine public landing route to PUBLIC_PATHS, or use the org's website URL once per-org branding lands.
verifierReasoning: Verified the full chain in the actual code. (1) src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/success/page.tsx line 26 does render `<Link href="/" className="underline">our website</Link>`. (2) In src/lib/supabase/middleware.ts, PUBLIC_PATHS = ['/sign-in','/sign-up','/auth/callback','/auth/auth-code-error','/api/inngest','/apply','/api/outlook/callback','/api/outlook/webhook','/accept-invite']. `/` is not present. isPublic uses `pathname === p || pathname.startsWith(`${p}/`)`; for pathname '/', no equality matches and no startsWith matches (e.g. '/'.startsWith('/apply/') is false), so isPublic = false. (3) At line 68, `if (!user && !isPublic)` redirects an unauthenticated user to `/sign-in?next=%2F` (next set via safeNext(pathname)). (4) The matcher in src/middleware.ts ('/((?!_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|manifest.webmanifest|robots.txt|sitemap.xml|icon$|apple-icon$|...image extensions).*)') intercepts '/'. (5) `/` is served by src/app/(app)/page.tsx — an authenticated dashboard route; there is no src/app/page.tsx public landing page. (6) The success page is reached by every applicant: actions.ts line 565 returns `redirectTo: /apply/${slug}/success`. So an unauthenticated, just-applied candidate who clicks 'our website' is redirected to the recruiter's internal sign-in form. The claim is materially accurate in every particular.

title: LinkedIn ingest queries run as anon role — capture always returns 500 'profile_not_found'
description: The route authenticates the extension's bearer token with `supabase.auth.getUser(token)`, but the Supabase client it uses for the subsequent DB queries is `createClient()` from src/lib/supabase/server.ts, which is a COOKIE-based SSR client. The chrome-extension background worker POSTs from a `chrome-extension://` origin and sends only `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (chrome-extension/src/background/ingest.ts:709-716) — it sends NO Supabase auth cookies. `getUser(token)` validates the token against the auth server, but it does NOT wire that token into the PostgREST client's Authorization header. So `getProfile(supabase, user.id)` (line 138) and `upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn(supabase, ...)` (line 158) execute as the Postgres `anon` role. Every RLS policy on `users` and `candidates` is `to authenticated` with `using (organization_id = public.current_organization_id())` (migration 20260513152244 lines 467-474; current_organization_id() reads auth.uid() which is null for anon). The `users` SELECT returns nothing → getProfile returns `not_found` → the route returns `{ ok:false, error:'profile_not_found' }` with HTTP 500. The LinkedIn capture feature is broken end-to-end. The route's own test file is entirely `it.todo(...)` (route.test.ts:9-17), so this path was never executed/verified.
userImpact: The recruiter clicks 'Save to Altus' on a LinkedIn profile and always gets a generic 'Ingest failed (500)' error. No candidate is ever created from LinkedIn. A headline AI-first feature is dead — and would fail live on the demo if shown.
suggestedFix: Construct a token-scoped client for the queries, e.g. createServerClient(url, key, { global: { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } }, cookies: noop }) (or supabase-js createClient with the same global header), so PostgREST runs as the authenticated user and RLS resolves current_organization_id(). Then replace the it.todo tests with a real run before the demo.
verifierReasoning:
I verified every link in the claim's chain against the actual code and dependency source.

1. CLIENT: src/app/api/linkedin/ingest/route.ts:110 uses `createClient()` from src/lib/supabase/server.ts, which is the COOKIE-based SSR `createServerClient` (reads cookies via next/headers, no `accessToken` option).

2. getUser(token) does NOT authorize PostgREST. In @supabase/auth-js@2.105.4 GoTrueClient.js, `getUser(jwt)` -> `_getUser(jwt)` (lines 2467-2503) makes a single GET to the GoTrue `/user` endpoint with that jwt purely to validate it. It never calls setSession and never mutates the client's headers/session. Separately, in @supabase/supabase-js@2.105.4 SupabaseClient.ts:536-544, every `.from()` query resolves its bearer via `_getAccessToken()`, which (with no `accessToken` option) calls `this.auth.getSession()` and returns `data.session?.access_token ?? this.supabaseKey`. With no Supabase cookies on the chrome-extension request, getSession() yields no session, so PostgREST falls back to the publishable/anon key. Queries therefore run as the Postgres anon role. The claim's central mechanism is correct.

3. EXTENSION: chrome-extension/src/background/ingest.ts:709-717 sends only `authorization: Bearer <token>` (+ content-type, x-altus-extension-version); no `credentials: 'include'`, and it is a cross-origin chrome-extension:// request, so no Supabase cookies are sent. Confirmed.

4. RLS: candidates policies (migration 20260513152244 lines 467-475) are `to authenticated` with `using/with check (organization_id = public.current_organization_id())`. The users SELECT policy (migration 20260513151021 lines 102-106) is `to authenticated`. current_organization_id() (lines 61-69) reads `auth.uid()`, which is null for anon. There is NO anon policy on either table, and RLS is default-deny, so anon SELECT returns zero rows.

5. RESULT: getProfile (src/lib/db/profiles.ts:14-24) uses `.maybeSingle()`, so an empty (RLS-filtered) result returns data:null, error:null -> `{ ok:false, code:'not_found' }`. route.ts:139-143 then returns `{ ok:false, error:'profile_not_found' }` with HTTP 500. The extension surfaces `Ingest failed (500).` (ingest.ts:732-733). Matches the claimed user impact exactly.

6. The test file route.test.ts is entirely `it.todo(...)` (7 placeholders) — the path was never executed, consistent with the bug shipping undetected.

ADDITIONAL FINDING (makes it worse, not better): `/api/linkedin/ingest` is NOT in PUBLIC_PATHS (src/lib/supabase/middleware.ts:8-34), and the middleware matcher (src/middleware.ts:18) matches all non-static paths including /api. For a cookie-less request, middleware getUser() finds no user and (line 68-75) returns a 307 redirect to /sign-in — including for the OPTIONS preflight, which gets no CORS headers and fails the preflight. So the handler may be pre-empted entirely; the request fails even earlier than the claim states. This means the reviewer's suggested fix (token-scoped client) is necessary but NOT sufficient on its own — the route must also be allowlisted in PUBLIC_PATHS (or middleware taught to honor the bearer) or the handler never runs.

Net: the substantive defect (cookie SSR client + getUser(token) does not authorize PostgREST -> anon-role queries -> profile_not_found 500) is real and the feature is broken end-to-end. Critical for a live client demo of a headline AI-first feature.

title: Server 'Invalid CV file' error maps to a non-existent RHF field → no message shown to applicant
description: When submitApplyAction rejects the file metadata it returns `fieldErrors: { cv: ['Invalid CV file.'] }` (actions.ts:191). The client loops the fieldErrors and calls `form.setError(field as keyof ApplyFormInput, ...)`. But `cv` is NOT a field in applyFormSchema (schema.ts has no `cv` key) and the file is held in React state, not in react-hook-form. Setting an error on a key RHF doesn't manage renders nowhere — there is no `<FormMessage>` bound to `cv`. The applicant sees the spinner stop and the button re-enable, but no error text anywhere explaining the CV was rejected. The same silent gap applies to any future server fieldError whose key isn't a real form field.
userImpact: An applicant whose CV the server rejects (e.g. tampered/edge-case mime) gets zero feedback — the form just appears to do nothing on submit. They retry the same file repeatedly with no idea why it fails, then abandon. On a public apply form this is lost candidates.
suggestedFix:
Special-case the `cv` key: route it to the existing `setFileError(...)` state used for the file input (which renders a visible role=alert

), and only call form.setError for keys that exist in applyFormSchema. Or add a fallback toast.error when a fieldError key isn't a managed RHF field.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code. (1) submitApplyAction returns `{ ok: false, fieldErrors: { cv: ['Invalid CV file.'] } }` at src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts:191. (2) `cv` is genuinely NOT a key in applyFormSchema — schema.ts:36-83 defines only full_name, email, phone, location, current_role_title, availability, salary_expectation, source_detail, consent_confirmed, marketing_consent, hp, turnstile_token. (3) The client handler loops fieldErrors and calls `form.setError(field as keyof ApplyFormInput, { message: messages[0] })` at apply-form.tsx:149-153. (4) The file lives in React state (`useState` line 72), and its UX error renders via a separate `fileError` state in a role="alert"

at lines 372-376 — NOT via RHF. (5) shadcn's FormMessage (src/components/ui/form.tsx:138-156) only renders an error read through useFormField()/getFieldState scoped to a FormField name; there is no `FormField name=\"cv\"` anywhere, no errors.root reader, and no fallback toast in the fieldErrors branch (the toast.error calls at lines 157/175/191 are in the formError / upload / confirm branches only). So form.setError('cv',...) stores an error that nothing in the tree displays. The `as keyof ApplyFormInput` cast also suppresses the TS guard that would otherwise flag the unknown key. Reachability: through the legitimate UI (no tampering needed) at least two triggers hit this branch — a 0-byte file (client onFileChange only rejects size > MAX_BYTES, never size <= 0, so a valid-mime 0-byte PDF/DOCX passes client checks and enables submit, but the server rejects on `fileMeta.size <= 0`) and a filename longer than 255 chars (client never checks name length; server rejects on `name.length > 255`). Both yield the silent failure: spinner stops, button re-enables (resetTurnstile + return), no text shown. The common cases (oversized/wrong-type files) are caught client-side with visible messages, and the email blocklist fieldError maps to the real `email` field so it works — `cv` is the only orphaned key. So the bug is real and materially as described; I downgrade high→medium because the practically-reachable triggers through the normal UI are narrow edge cases rather than the everyday path, and the fix is trivial.

title: Apply success page 'our website' link and start_url point to /, which redirects unauthenticated candidates to recruiter sign-in
description: After a successful application the candidate lands on /apply/[slug]/success, which renders a link: `<Link href="/">our website</Link>`. The `/` route is the authenticated dashboard (src/app/(app)/page.tsx). `/` is NOT in PUBLIC_PATHS (src/lib/supabase/middleware.ts:8-34), so middleware redirects the unauthenticated candidate to `/sign-in?next=/`. The candidate, who is not a recruiter and has no account, is dumped onto an internal recruiter magic-link sign-in form — the opposite of 'our website'. (The PWA manifest start_url:'/' has the same problem but that's secondary.)
userImpact: A candidate who just applied clicks the friendly 'our website' link and is confronted with an internal 'Sign in to Altus' screen they can't use. Looks broken and unprofessional — exactly the surface a client will poke at during a demo of the public funnel.
evidence:
<Link href="/" className="underline">our website</Link>
// PUBLIC_PATHS does not include '/', so middleware: if (!user && !isPublic) redirect('/sign-in')
suggestedFix: Point the link at the org's public marketing site (or omit it) rather than the app root. Until per-org branding exists, link to a real external URL (e.g. https://altusrecruit.com) or remove the link and keep only the 'you can close this window' copy.
verifierReasoning: Every link in the claim's chain is verified in source. (1) success/page.tsx:26 renders `<Link href="/" className="underline">our website</Link>` verbatim. (2) The only routable root page is src/app/(app)/page.tsx — a full recruiter dashboard (DashboardPage, fetches org-scoped metrics/activity) requiring an authenticated session; the (app) route group adds no URL segment, so `/` resolves there. (3) PUBLIC_PATHS (middleware.ts:8-34) = ['/sign-in','/sign-up','/auth/callback','/auth/auth-code-error','/api/inngest','/apply','/api/outlook/callback','/api/outlook/webhook','/accept-invite']. The isPublic test on line 66 matches exact path or startsWith(`${p}/`); a bare `/` matches none. So line 68 `if (!user && !isPublic)` fires and line 73-74 redirect to `/sign-in?next=/`. (4) The applicant is genuinely unauthenticated: apply-form.tsx:198-201 hard-navigates (window.location.href) an 'otherwise unauthenticated' candidate to /apply/<slug>/success (actions.ts:565 returns that redirectTo). The candidate never authenticates, so clicking 'our website' lands them on the recruiter magic-link sign-in form. (5) Secondary claim also holds: manifest.ts:12 sets start_url:'/', so an installed PWA opened by an unauthenticated person hits the same redirect. The claim is accurate and not refutable. Adjusted to low rather than medium because: the failure occurs only AFTER the application has already succeeded (no funnel/data loss), the same page already offers a graceful exit ('You can close this window'), and the defect is a cosmetic/trust mislink rather than a functional break of the public flow. The 'client demo' concern is legitimate but the impact is perception-only.

title: Re-application path does not record the freshly-ticked consent (consent_at / consent_text_version not updated)
description: On the duplicate-email re-application branch, the action only bumps market_status cold→actively_looking and writes an activity log. It never updates the candidate's `consent_at` or `consent_text_version`, even though the applicant has just read and ticked the current v2 consent text again on this new submission. The new candidate branch correctly stamps consent_at=now and consent_text_version=CURRENT_CONSENT_VERSION (lines 277-281), but the returning-applicant branch silently keeps whatever consent timestamp/version was stored on first contact.
userImpact: For a candidate who applies again months later, the agency's audit trail shows a stale consent date/version, not the consent the candidate actually just gave. Under UK GDPR Art. 7 'demonstrable consent' this weakens the audit-ready posture the project treats as foundational — and a compliance-savvy buyer may probe it.
suggestedFix: In the existing-candidate branch, also pass consent_basis:'consent', consent_at:new Date().toISOString(), consent_text_version:CURRENT_CONSENT_VERSION to updateCandidate so the latest consent the applicant gave is recorded.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed by reading src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts. The returning-applicant branch (lines 234-261) only bumps market_status cold→actively_looking (lines 237-241) and writes a 'Re-applied via public form' activity log (lines 242-256). It never updates consent_at or consent_text_version. The new-candidate branch (lines 262-282) DOES correctly stamp consent_basis:'consent', consent_at:new Date().toISOString(), and consent_text_version:CURRENT_CONSENT_VERSION ('v2'). The three columns exist in supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:221-223. The applicant genuinely re-reads/re-ticks the v2 consent on each submission (the form renders the v2 text inline and marketing_consent is captured in the activity metadata, line 251), so the freshly-given consent is real and goes unrecorded as a stamped value. The bug is therefore real and materially as described. Two caveats lower the practical severity: (1) a contemporaneous 'Re-applied via public form' activity-log row IS written on every re-application, so there is dated evidence of the re-submission — the candidate's consent record is stale, not absent; (2) it only affects the duplicate-email re-application path, has zero user-visible/demo impact, and only surfaces under a detailed GDPR audit. Note on the suggested fix: updateCandidate's UpdateCandidateInput type (candidates.ts:433-443) does not currently permit consent_* fields, so the fix as written would not type-check without also extending that type — a minor implementation caveat, not a refutation. Given the project's stated 'audit-ready by default / every consent has timestamp + basis' principle this is a legitimate compliance-accuracy gap, but the real-world impact (compensating activity log, narrow path, no demo impact) puts it at low rather than medium.

title: Confirm-stage failure leaves a committed candidate/CV but no Inngest parse, and re-submitting re-creates rows
description: The three-stage submit commits the candidate + candidate_cvs rows and uploads the file during stages 1-2. If stage 3 (confirmApplyAction) returns !ok, the client shows a toast and calls resetTurnstile(), leaving the user on the form with all fields intact and the submit button re-enabled. If they press Submit again, submitApplyAction runs from scratch: it finds the candidate by email (dedup) so it won't duplicate the candidate, but nextCVVersion + createCandidateCV mint a brand-new CV row and a new signed upload URL each retry, and the file is re-PUT. There is no idempotency key tying a retry to the first attempt — repeated confirm failures accumulate orphaned candidate_cvs rows (with parsing_status='pending') and duplicate storage objects. Note confirmApplyAction's own Inngest-send failure is non-fatal and still returns ok (good), so this only bites on the cv-row-not-found / storage-list paths.
userImpact: A candidate hitting a transient confirm error and retrying ends up with multiple pending CV versions; the recruiter later sees duplicate/half-parsed CVs on the candidate and has to reconcile. Degrades data quality of the inbound funnel.
suggestedFix: On confirm failure, either disable further resubmission (the CV is uploaded; tell them it's received and to email support), or make submitApplyAction reuse the most recent pending candidate_cvs row for the same (org,candidate,storage already-uploaded) instead of always creating a new version on retry.
verifierReasoning:
The claim is accurate in every traced detail. apply-form.tsx onSubmit (lines 142-202) is a three-stage flow: stage 1 submitApplyAction commits candidate + candidate_cvs rows and mints a signed upload URL; stage 2 PUTs the file to Storage; stage 3 confirmApplyAction. On !confirmResult.ok (line 190) the handler only fires toast.error + resetTurnstile() + return — it does NOT reset the form, navigate, or permanently disable submit. Form fields and the File (held in React state) persist; the button re-disables only until a fresh Turnstile token, after which the user can resubmit.

A resubmit re-runs submitApplyAction from scratch. getCandidateByEmailForOrg (candidates.ts:406) dedupes the candidate by normalised email within org, so the candidate is NOT duplicated. But the code then unconditionally calls nextCVVersion (candidate-cvs.ts:120 — returns max(version)+1) and createCandidateCV (candidate-cvs.ts:83 — always INSERTs with parsing_status hardcoded to 'pending'), and builds a brand-new storage path `${org.id}/applicants/${candidateId}-${crypto.randomUUID()}.${ext}` (actions.ts:321) with a new signed upload URL. The client re-PUTs the file. There is no idempotency key tying a retry to the first attempt and no reuse of an existing pending row. The schema (20260513152244 lines 243-257) confirms parsing_status default 'pending' and unique(candidate_id, version); the unique constraint does NOT prevent this because the version increments each retry — it only guards truly-concurrent same-version inserts. Result: each transient-confirm-failure retry accumulates an orphaned candidate_cvs row (parsing_status='pending') plus a duplicate Storage object.

The claim's caveat is also correct: confirmApplyAction's own inngest.send failure is caught and still returns ok (actions.ts:534-565), so this only bites on the cv-row-not-found/tenant-mismatch path (lines 487-493), the storage.list error path (lines 505-517), or objectExists===false (lines 519-525).

Downgrading severity from medium to low: the bug is real but the trigger window is narrow and transient. The CV row was just created in stage 1 with the exact ids confirm re-reads, so cv-row-not-found requires a DB read error/replication lag; the most plausible path is objectExists===false from Storage list eventual-consistency right after a successful PUT. No data loss, no crash, no cross-tenant leakage — it only produces duplicate pending CV versions for a recruiter to reconcile, and only when a candidate retries after a rare transient confirm error. For a live client demo this is a low-impact data-quality edge case, not a medium-severity defect.

title: PKCE/RPC invite failures surface only as a ?error= query param the user can't see if they re-navigate
description: All invite-acceptance failure paths in /auth/callback redirect to /sign-in?error=invalid-invite (or expired-invite). The sign-in form renders that as a banner via errorBannerMessage (sign-in-form.tsx:32-40). That works, but the user has, by this point, been signed into a freshly-bootstrapped org by the handle_new_user trigger (the invite RPC failed AFTER exchangeCodeForSession). They are authenticated, so if they navigate to '/' the middleware redirect back to /sign-in (for authed users hitting /sign-in) sends them to '/' and they never see the error banner — they silently end up in the wrong (auto-created) org with no indication the invite didn't attach.
userImpact: An invited teammate whose invite RPC fails (expired token race, email mismatch) is silently dropped into a brand-new empty org instead of the inviter's org, with the error banner easily missed. They think they joined the team but see none of the team's data.
evidence:
if (user && (pathname === '/sign-in' || pathname === '/sign-up')) { ... redirect('/') }
// in callback: on rpc failure → redirect('/sign-in?error=invalid-invite') but user IS already authenticated
suggestedFix: On invite failure, sign the user out (or surface a blocking interstitial) before redirecting to /sign-in?error=..., so an authenticated user isn't immediately bounced past the banner into the wrong org. At minimum, render the invite error on the dashboard for authed users too.
verifierReasoning:
The claim holds, and the verified code shows the impact is actually stronger than described. Chain confirmed end-to-end:

1) Authenticated-before-RPC: src/app/auth/callback/route.ts:31 calls exchangeCodeForSession(code) before any invite logic. createClient() (src/lib/supabase/server.ts:18-22) sets session cookies via cookieStore.set() from next/headers, which in a route handler are merged onto the returned NextResponse.redirect. So on every invite-failure path (lines 54, 75, 91, 104) the browser already holds a valid session — the user IS authenticated when redirected to /sign-in?error=invalid-invite.

2) Wrong-org premise confirmed: sign-in-form.tsx:102-108 calls signInWithOtp with shouldCreateUser:inviteMode but passes NO invited_to_org metadata. The handle_new_user trigger (migration 20260517204503 lines 30-61) only attaches to the inviter org when raw_user_meta_data.invited_to_org is present; since the magic-link sign-up has none, it takes the normal path and creates a brand-new org with the invitee as 'owner'. The accept_invitation RPC in /auth/callback is the only thing that reassigns them. If the RPC fails (rpcError, !result.ok, email_mismatch, or throw), they stay in the fresh empty org.

3) Banner never shown: middleware (src/lib/supabase/middleware.ts:77-82) does `if (user && pathname === '/sign-in') { url.search=''; redirect('/') }`. Because the invitee is authenticated, the very first landing on /sign-in?error=invalid-invite is itself intercepted, the error param is stripped (url.search=''), and they are bounced to '/'. The errorBanner in sign-in-form.tsx:135-139 is never rendered even once — worse than the claim's 'easily missed'.

4) No mitigation: no signOut on any failure path; (app)/page.tsx has no error-param surfacing; (app)/layout.tsx loads the wrong-org data without complaint.

Net effect for a client demo: an invited teammate whose invite RPC fails is silently dropped into a brand-new empty org with zero feedback — they believe they joined the team but see none of its data. This is the silent-wrong-tenant failure class the project explicitly treats as severe. Trigger conditions are narrow (expired-token race or email mismatch on an invite that already passed the /accept-invite lookup), and failures are logged to Sentry, so it is not an everyday occurrence — hence medium rather than high. The claimed severity of 'low' understates the silent-wrong-org outcome.

title: Sign-up accepts whitespace-only / unvalidated organisation + full name, creating a blank-named org via trigger
description: SignUpForm sends organization_name and full_name straight into signInWithOtp metadata with only the browser's `required` attribute as validation. `required` permits a single space, and there is no trim/min-length client or server check before the on_auth_user_created trigger reads the metadata to create the organizations + users rows. A user who types a space (or leaves it as a stray value) bootstraps an org whose name is blank/whitespace, which then renders as an empty org name in TopNav and everywhere org.name is shown.
userImpact: A new signup can create an organisation with an empty or whitespace name, which looks broken across the app header and any client-facing surface, with no way to fix it from the current UI.
evidence:
<Input id="organizationName" required value={organizationName} ... />
// onSubmit: data: { full_name: fullName, organization_name: organizationName } — no trim/min-length, no server action validation
suggestedFix: Trim and enforce a min length (e.g. 2 chars) on organizationName and fullName before calling signInWithOtp, and/or add a NOT-blank check in the trigger. Mirror the apply form's z.string().trim().min(2) pattern.
verifierReasoning:
The core mechanism is genuine. In src/app/(auth)/sign-up/sign-up-form.tsx the form passes organization_name/full_name straight into signInWithOtp metadata (lines 33-36) with no trim/min-length check, and the form is even rendered with `noValidate` (line 58), so the HTML `required` attribute never fires at all. The trigger handle_new_user (operative version in supabase/migrations/20260517204503_handle_new_user_invite.sql, lines 41-44 — later migrations only reference it in comments, they don't change the org-name logic) does `v_org_name := coalesce(nullif(new.raw_user_meta_data->>'organization_name', ''), new.email)`. `nullif(x, '')` only converts an EMPTY string to NULL; a whitespace-only value like a single space survives, so the organizations.name column is stored as ' '. The slug correctly degrades to org-<id8> (regexp_replace + trim + 'org' fallback, lines 47-51), but the human-readable name persists as whitespace. In TopNav (src/components/app/top-nav.tsx line 92) the guard `{organizationName && ...}` treats ' ' as truthy, so it renders an effectively blank org-name line. So a whitespace-only org name CAN be created and DOES render blank. Truly empty/null inputs are already safe (fall back to email).

Two parts of the claim are materially overstated, which is why this stays low rather than escalating: (1) The claim asserts there is 'no way to fix it from the current UI.' This is FALSE — src/app/(app)/settings/organization-form.tsx lets the org owner edit the name, validated server-side by updateOrganizationSchema = z.string().trim().min(1) in settings/schema.ts, so the blank state is fully self-recoverable in-product. (2) The trigger is NOT entirely unguarded — it specifically defends empty/null; only the narrow whitespace-only case slips through, and that requires a user to deliberately type only space characters into a field placeholdered 'Acme Recruitment'. It is not an accidental 'stray value' as framed.

title: Apply form collects salary expectation + availability, validates them, then silently discards both — never written to the candidate
description: The public apply form asks the applicant for `availability` (immediate / 2 weeks / 1 month / other) and `salary_expectation` (a validated GBP number). Both are part of `applyFormSchema` and arrive in `parsed.data`. But `submitApplyAction` only writes full_name, email, phone, location, current_role_title, source, source_detail, consent fields to the candidate row (createCandidate call at line 266-282). `salary_expectation` and `availability` are never persisted — not on the candidate row, not in the activity metadata (lines 248 and 306 only carry apply_form/slug/marketing_consent), nowhere. The candidates table HAS a `salary_expectation integer` column (migration 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:214), so this is a true silent drop, not a missing-column constraint.
userImpact: A candidate types '£55,000' as their salary expectation and picks 'available immediately' — both feel captured (the form validates and accepts them) — but the recruiter opening the candidate record sees neither. For a recruitment CRM this is high-value data that quietly evaporates on every single application. In a live demo, submitting an application and then showing the empty candidate record exposes the gap directly.
evidence:
const insertRes = await createCandidate(supabase, {
  full_name: parsed.data.full_name,
  email: parsed.data.email,
  phone: parsed.data.phone ?? null,
  location: parsed.data.location ?? null,
  current_role_title: parsed.data.current_role_title ?? null,
  current_company: null,
  market_status: 'actively_looking',
  source: 'apply_form',
  source_detail: parsed.data.source_detail || slug,
  organization_id: org.id,
  consent_basis: 'consent',
  consent_at: new Date().toISOString(),
  consent_text_version: CURRENT_CONSENT_VERSION,
})  // <-- parsed.data.salary_expectation and parsed.data.availability never used
suggestedFix: Add `salary_expectation: parsed.data.salary_expectation ? Number(parsed.data.salary_expectation) : null` to the createCandidate payload (and to CreateCandidateInput). For availability, either add a column or fold it into the activity metadata / a candidate note so the recruiter sees it. At minimum, if these fields are deliberately out of scope for Phase 3, remove them from the form so candidates don't enter data that is thrown away.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code. The apply form schema (src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/schema.ts lines 58, 63-67) defines `availability` (required enum) and `salary_expectation` (optional digit-string), and the UI renders both (apply-form.tsx lines 297, 321, labelled "Availability" and "Salary expectation (optional, GBP)"). submitApplyAction re-validates via applyFormSchema.safeParse at actions.ts:156, so both land in parsed.data. But the createCandidate payload (actions.ts lines 266-282) never references parsed.data.salary_expectation or parsed.data.availability; the CreateCandidateInput type (candidates.ts lines 314-333) does not even declare them, and the constructed payload (lines 358-376) omits them. Activity metadata at lines 248-252 and 306-310 carries only apply_form, slug, marketing_consent. A grep over the apply route confirms parsed.data.salary_expectation / parsed.data.availability are read nowhere. So both validated fields are silently discarded — confirmed silent-fail.

The column-existence sub-claim is correct: candidates.salary_expectation integer exists (migration 20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:214) and is in src/types/database.ts, so this is a true silent drop, not a missing-column constraint. The candidate detail page displays "Expected salary" from candidate.salary_expectation (page.tsx:264), so a recruiter sees an empty field — the demo-visibility claim holds. Note: `availability` has no column anywhere in the schema, so it has no destination at all and is lost 100% of the time.

Severity nuance: the subsequent CV upload fires parse-cv (cv/uploaded), which CAN populate salary_expectation — but ONLY from what Claude extracts from the CV body (parse-cv.ts:261), NOT from the form input, and only into an empty column (D-08 fill-only, candidate-cvs.ts). Most CVs don't state a salary expectation, so the form value is genuinely lost in the common case, and even when populated it's the CV's value not the applicant's typed value. The bug is real and demo-visible, but it doesn't break any flow (application still completes; candidate + CV created correctly) and salary may be partially backfilled from the CV in some cases. That puts the honest user-facing severity at medium rather than high.

title: Re-application path ignores all fresh contact-detail updates from the new submission
description: When an existing candidate (matched by email) re-applies, the action only bumps market_status from 'cold' to 'actively_looking' and writes a 'Re-applied via public form' activity. The freshly-submitted full_name, phone, location, current_role_title (and the new CV is appended) are NOT merged into the candidate row. If a candidate re-applies six months later with a new phone number, new location, and a new job title, the recruiter still sees the stale values from the first application.
userImpact: A returning candidate updates their phone/location/role on the apply form; the new CV lands but the contact details the recruiter will actually call are still the old ones. The candidate believes they updated their profile; they didn't. Silent staleness on the exact fields a recruiter relies on to make contact.
suggestedFix: On the re-application branch, fill-empty-only update of phone/location/current_role_title/full_name (mirror the LinkedIn upsert's fill-empty pattern in candidates-linkedin.ts) so a re-application can populate fields that were blank, without clobbering recruiter edits. Record what changed in the activity metadata.
verifierReasoning:
The code claim is factually correct. In src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts:234-261, the existing-candidate (re-application) branch only bumps market_status cold->actively_looking (line 237-241) and writes a 'Re-applied via public form' activity (line 242-256). It never merges the freshly-submitted full_name/phone/location/current_role_title into the candidate row. The apply form genuinely collects these fields (schema.ts:37-57), updateCandidate already supports them (UpdateCandidateInput, candidates.ts:433-443), and the columns exist in the schema. The LinkedIn fill-empty precedent the reviewer cites is real (upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn, candidates-linkedin.ts:112).

However, the severity is materially overstated due to a mitigation the claim ignores: the re-applied CV is parsed by the cv/uploaded Inngest pipeline, which calls markCandidateFieldsFromCV (candidate-cvs.ts:312, invoked at parse-cv.ts:249-274). That helper does a fill-empty-only merge of phone, location, current_role_title, etc. from the parsed CV. So any field that was BLANK on the original candidate already gets populated by the new CV parse — the proposed form-field merge is largely redundant with the existing path.

Crucially, the reviewer's flagship scenario ('candidate re-applies six months later with a NEW phone number') would NOT be fixed by the reviewer's own suggested fix, which is explicitly fill-empty-only ('populate fields that were blank, without clobbering recruiter edits'). A populated-but-stale phone is left untouched by both the current code AND the proposed fix, by design (D-08: never clobber recruiter/existing data). The real residual gap is narrow: a returning candidate fills a form field that was previously blank AND the new CV parse fails to extract that same field. That blank then stays blank. Genuine, but small — not the 'recruiter calls the old number' disaster the description paints.

title: LinkedIn upsert does NOT handle the unique-violation it was redesigned around — concurrent capture returns 500, not a clean 'updated'
description: The route handler (api/linkedin/ingest/route.ts lines 147-155, CR-01 comment) explicitly states the advisory-lock was removed because the unique partial index on (organization_id, source_detail) where source='linkedin' 'converts the concurrent-write race into a deterministic 23505 that the upsert helper's existing dedup branch already handles.' But upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn's INSERT path has NO 23505 handling — on any insert error it captures to Sentry and returns { ok: false, code: 'internal' }. The route then returns 'upsert_failed' with HTTP 500. There is no catch-23505-then-re-dedup-and-update branch anywhere.
userImpact: When the same LinkedIn profile is captured twice in quick succession (double-click on the extension, or two recruiters in the same org capturing the same person at the same moment), the loser of the race gets a hard error ('upsert_failed' / generic failure toast) instead of the intended 'Updated existing candidate'. The first write still lands, so no data loss, but the recruiter sees a confusing failure for an action that actually succeeded.
suggestedFix: In the insert error branch, detect Postgres code 23505 and recover by re-running the linkedin_url dedup lookup and switching to the UPDATE path (returning created:false). This is what the route's CR-01 comment already promises exists.
verifierReasoning: Verified against actual code. (1) Migration 20260520065652_phase3_candidates_linkedin_unique_source_detail.sql creates a real partial unique index on (organization_id, source_detail) WHERE source='linkedin' AND source_detail IS NOT NULL, so a concurrent duplicate LinkedIn-URL insert into the same org genuinely raises Postgres 23505. (2) The INSERT error branch in src/lib/db/candidates-linkedin.ts:236-246 has NO 23505 detection — every insert error (including 23505) is captured to Sentry and returns { ok: false, code: 'internal' }. The DbResult type (src/lib/db/types.ts) only allows 'not_found' | 'internal', with no recover-as-update path. There is no catch-23505-then-re-dedup-and-update branch anywhere. (3) route.ts:174-176 maps any !upsertResult.ok to { ok:false, error:'upsert_failed' } at HTTP 500. (4) Repo-wide grep confirms '23505' appears ONLY in the comment at route.ts:154 and the migration text — never in executable code; no .upsert()/onConflict/retry exists in the LinkedIn path. (5) The race is structurally real: dedup SELECT (line 119) and INSERT (line 230) are separate PostgREST round-trips in separate transactions, the advisory lock was removed per CR-01, and nothing wraps them in a transaction. So two concurrent captures both miss dedup, both insert, and the loser gets 23505 -> 500 'upsert_failed' instead of the promised 'Updated existing candidate'. The comments at route.ts:147-155 and migration lines 1-7 explicitly claim the upsert helper 'already handles' this 23505 — that claim is false; the code-vs-intent gap is exactly as described. I downgraded severity from medium to low: the bug is genuine but the trigger window (the ~100-300ms gap between dedup SELECT and INSERT, requiring a double-click or two recruiters in the same org capturing the identical profile simultaneously) is narrow, the first write still lands (no data loss, no cross-tenant leak), and the only impact is a confusing generic failure toast plus a spurious Sentry event for an action that partially succeeded.

title: Email dedup is case-sensitive against manually-added candidates because /candidates/new doesn't lowercase email
description: candidates.email is plain `text` (no citext, no lower() index — migration 20260513152244:203). The apply form schema and the LinkedIn ingest both lowercase email at write time, and their dedup lookups (getCandidateByEmailForOrg, getCandidateByEmailLowercase) lowercase the search term and use .eq. But the recruiter-facing /candidates/new schema does NOT lowercase email (no .toLowerCase()). So a recruiter who manually adds 'Alice@Example.com' creates a row stored mixed-case. A later apply-form or LinkedIn dedup searching for 'alice@example.com' .eq misses it, creating a duplicate candidate for the same person.
userImpact: Same human ends up as two candidate records — one from the recruiter's manual entry (mixed case) and one from their apply-form submission or LinkedIn capture (lowercased). Defeats the explicit dedup logic the apply/LinkedIn flows were built around; the recruiter sees split history/CVs across two records.
suggestedFix: Add .toLowerCase() to the /candidates/new email schema (and the candidates update path) so every write normalises email, OR add a lower(email) unique/functional index and normalise in createCandidate. Normalising at one consistent boundary closes the dedup hole.
verifierReasoning: Verified every load-bearing fact against the code. /candidates/new/schema.ts lines 39-47 define email with no .toLowerCase() (only .trim().max().optional().refine()). The write path createCandidate (src/lib/db/candidates.ts:358-376) stores email verbatim (line 360: `email: input.email || null`) with no normalization, and the manual-add action (candidates/new/actions.ts:29-44) passes parsed.data.email straight through. So a recruiter adding 'Alice@Example.com' persists a mixed-case row. By contrast, the apply form schema lowercases at the boundary (apply/[orgSlug]/schema.ts:49) and the LinkedIn ingest lowercases on insert (candidates-linkedin.ts:216). The dedup lookups both normalise the SEARCH TERM and do exact-match .eq against the stored column: getCandidateByEmailForOrg (candidates.ts:414-419 -> `normalisedEmail = args.email.toLowerCase().trim()` then `.eq('email', normalisedEmail)`) and getCandidateByEmailLowercase (candidates-linkedin.ts:66-71, same pattern). The column is plain `text` (migration 20260513152244 line 203); there is NO citext, NO unique constraint, and NO lower(email) UNIQUE index. The only lower(email) index is a GIN trigram index for the search RPC (search_indexes.sql:10), which the .eq dedup path does not use. createCandidate even comments that there is no UNIQUE constraint on email (lines 386-390). Therefore the dedup chain: apply/LinkedIn searches for 'alice@example.com' via .eq, which does NOT match a stored 'Alice@Example.com' row, so a duplicate candidate is created for the same person — exactly defeating the dedup logic whose own comments (apply/schema.ts:42-45) were written to fix this very case-sensitivity problem. The bug is genuine and the mechanism is sound. Caveat on severity: it requires (a) a recruiter manually typing a non-lowercase email AND (b) that same person later arriving via apply form / LinkedIn with the same address; it is data-integrity (split history/CVs across duplicate records), not a crash or cross-tenant leak.
