The claimed category 'schema-mismatch' is imprecise (it is an inconsistent-normalisation / dedup-bypass bug, not a payload-vs-column mismatch), but that mislabel does not change reality. Medium is fair: it silently produces duplicate PII candidate records and undermines an explicitly-built dedup feature.

title: LinkedIn URL dedup lookup omits the source='linkedin' filter that the unique index uses
description: getCandidateByLinkedInUrl dedups with .eq('source_detail', linkedinUrl).limit(1).maybeSingle() and no `source = 'linkedin'` predicate. The unique partial index it relies on (migration 20260520065652) is scoped `where source = 'linkedin' and source_detail is not null`. source_detail is also used by other flows (the apply form writes source_detail = slug). The lookup could in principle match a non-LinkedIn candidate whose source_detail coincidentally equals the URL, and treat it as the LinkedIn dedup target.
userImpact: Edge-only: a candidate whose source_detail happens to equal a captured LinkedIn URL (extremely unlikely in practice, since slugs/URLs differ) could be mis-updated as if it were the LinkedIn record. Low real-world likelihood but the query and the uniqueness invariant disagree on scope.
evidence:
const { data, error } = await supabase
  .from('candidates')
  .select('id, organization_id, email')
  .eq('source_detail', linkedinUrl)   // <-- no .eq('source', 'linkedin')
  .limit(1)
  .maybeSingle()
suggestedFix: Add .eq('source', 'linkedin') to getCandidateByLinkedInUrl so the lookup matches exactly the rows the unique index governs.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code. getCandidateByLinkedInUrl at src/lib/db/candidates-linkedin.ts:43-48 filters ONLY on .eq('source_detail', linkedinUrl).limit(1).maybeSingle() with no source='linkedin' predicate. The unique partial index in supabase/migrations/20260520065652_phase3_candidates_linkedin_unique_source_detail.sql:14-16 is scoped `where source = 'linkedin' and source_detail is not null`. So the lookup scope and the uniqueness invariant genuinely disagree — confirmed.

The premise that source_detail is shared is also confirmed: src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts:275 writes source_detail = parsed.data.source_detail || slug with source='apply_form'. The schema (schema.ts:68) makes source_detail a free-text 'How did you hear about us?' textarea.

Impact analysis (honest): For a false match, a non-LinkedIn candidate's source_detail would have to EXACTLY equal a canonical https://www.linkedin.com/in/<slug> URL — .eq() is exact equality, not ilike/substring, and the incoming linkedin_url is regex-validated server-side (linkedin-ingest-schema.ts:30) to be exactly that canonical form with no surrounding text. The apply fallback value (the org slug) is never a LinkedIn URL, and a free-text answer being byte-for-byte a clean profile URL is extraordinarily unlikely. Even if it triggered, the cross-org assertion (lines 134-148) blocks cross-tenant harm; the only residual harm is one same-org candidate being mis-updated with a LinkedIn snapshot — no leak, crash, or loss of the LinkedIn record. Probability during a live demo is effectively nil.

Conclusion: real code/invariant mismatch, fix is correct (add .eq('source', 'linkedin')), but user-facing impact is genuinely low/edge-case. The reviewer's own grading (low, edge-case, extremely unlikely) is accurate and not overstated. Relevant files: /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/candidates-linkedin.ts (lines 43-48), /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/supabase/migrations/20260520065652_phase3_candidates_linkedin_unique_source_detail.sql, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/app/(public)/apply/[orgSlug]/actions.ts:275, /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/validation/linkedin-ingest-schema.ts:30.

title: Outlook subscription recreate reports success even when the state write or recreate sub-write fails
description: In tryRecreate(), updateOutlookSubscriptionState() and setOutlookDeltaLink() are called with their DbResult return values ignored (no `if (!result.ok)` check), and then recordRenewalAttempt(success:true) is unconditionally written at the end. So if the new subscription_id / client_state fails to persist to outlook_credentials, the function still increments `recreated++` and records a SUCCESS renewal attempt. Contrast with the inline renew path (line 207-221) which DOES check writeResult.ok and records failure. The recreate path is the one that runs after a real 404 expiry — exactly when correctness matters most.
userImpact: After a Microsoft subscription expires and is recreated, if the DB write of the new subscription_id silently fails, the credentials row keeps the stale/expired subscription_id while the dashboard and renewal log show 'recreated: success'. Inbound email→activity sync silently stops for that recruiter and nothing surfaces it. During a live demo of the Outlook email-logging feature, captured emails would simply never appear and the system would claim everything is healthy.
evidence:
await updateOutlookSubscriptionState(serviceClient, { userId, subscriptionId, subscriptionClientState: clientState, subscriptionExpiresAt: expirationDateTime })  // return value ignored
  await setOutlookDeltaLink(serviceClient, { userId, deltaLink: null, lastSyncedAt: ... })  // ignored
  ...
  await recordRenewalAttempt(serviceClient, { userId, success: true, error: reason })  // always success
suggestedFix: Capture the DbResult of both updateOutlookSubscriptionState and setOutlookDeltaLink inside tryRecreate; if either is `ok: false`, throw so the caller's catch records `recordRenewalAttempt(success:false)` and increments `failed++` instead of `recreated++`. Do not report a successful recreate unless the new subscription_id actually landed in the row.
verifierReasoning: Claim is accurate against the code. In tryRecreate() (src/lib/inngest/functions/refresh-outlook-subscription.ts:313-325), the return values of updateOutlookSubscriptionState (line 313) and setOutlookDeltaLink (line 321) are awaited but neither assigned nor checked, then recordRenewalAttempt(success: true) is called unconditionally at lines 352-356. Crucially, both DB helpers (src/lib/db/outlook-credentials.ts:218-234 and 272-288) return a discriminated DbResult ({ ok:false, code:'internal' }) on error and DO NOT throw — they only capture the error to Sentry. DbResult is confirmed in src/lib/db/types.ts as a non-throwing result union. Therefore the three callers of tryRecreate (lines 142, 173-181, 231-239), which all rely on a thrown exception to route into their catch blocks and increment failed++, will never see a DB-write failure: tryRecreate returns normally, the caller increments recreated++, and recordRenewalAttempt has already written SUCCESS with last_renewal_error=null. This directly contrasts with the inline renew path (lines 207-221), which correctly does `if (!writeResult.ok)` -> record failure + failed++. The post-404 recreate path is exactly when a NEW subscription_id/client_state exists in Microsoft Graph but, if the DB write fails, the row retains the stale/expired subscription_id and client_state. The webhook lookup (getOutlookCredentialsBySubscriptionId) then can't match the new id and/or clientState validation rejects notifications, so inbound email->activity sync silently stops while the renewal log/dashboard report 'recreated: success'. Partial mitigation: the DB error itself is captured to Sentry inside the helper, so it is not 100% invisible to an operator watching Sentry, and the double-failure alert in recordRenewalAttempt does not apply to this path because success:true is recorded. But the in-app state is affirmatively wrong (clears last_renewal_error, counts as recreated), which is the misleading-success condition described. Failure is plausible (transient DB/connection/timeout), not purely theoretical. The suggested fix (capture both DbResults; throw if !ok so the caller's catch records failure and increments failed++) is correct and matches the existing inline-renew pattern.

title: CV parse marks status 'complete' even when the candidate-field merge write fails
description: In the 'write-extracted' step, the first write (updateCandidateCVParse → status='complete') correctly checks `if (!updateResult.ok) throw`. But the second write, markCandidateFieldsFromCV() — which is the whole point of D-08 'auto-fill empty candidate fields from the parsed CV' — is awaited with its DbResult discarded. markCandidateFieldsFromCV returns `{ ok: false, code: 'internal' }` (and Sentry-captures) on a DB error rather than throwing, so a failed candidate update is swallowed and the CV row is still committed as 'complete'.
userImpact: A recruiter uploads a CV, sees 'Parsed ✓' and the extracted-data panel, but the candidate's empty fields (email, phone, skills, work history, etc.) were never populated because the candidates UPDATE failed. The recruiter has no signal that the auto-fill silently no-op'd — they assume the candidate record is enriched when it isn't, and there is no retry affordance for that step.
suggestedFix: Capture the result of markCandidateFieldsFromCV; on `!result.ok`, throw inside the step so Inngest retries (the merge is idempotent — it only fills empties) rather than silently committing 'complete' with an un-enriched candidate. At minimum, surface a partial-success indicator so the user knows the merge didn't run.
verifierReasoning: The core code-level claim is accurate and verified. At /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/inngest/functions/parse-cv.ts:249, the 'write-extracted' step awaits markCandidateFieldsFromCV but discards its DbResult with no ok-check — unlike the preceding updateCandidateCVParse write (lines 237-245) which correctly throws on !ok. In /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/db/candidate-cvs.ts, markCandidateFieldsFromCV returns { ok: false, code: 'internal' } and Sentry-captures on a DB update error (lines 416-421), and { ok: false, code: 'not_found' } if the candidate row is gone (line 333) — it does NOT throw. So a failed candidate-field merge does not abort the step, the candidate_cvs row is still committed as 'complete', Inngest never retries the merge, and the UI 'Parsed' badge keys solely off parsing_status === 'complete' (page.tsx:414, cv-review-panel.tsx:267). That the codebase author knew this DbResult must be checked is proven by the parallel manual path acceptCVFieldsAction (src/app/(app)/candidates/[id]/actions.ts:366-393), which captures mergeResult and surfaces a user-facing error on !ok. The Inngest path simply omits that guard. The suggested fix (throw on !result.ok so Inngest retries the idempotent fill-empties merge) is correct and safe.

title: Cost-log failures in claude.ts and voyage.ts pass the raw SDK error to Sentry (PII-leak risk)
description: The record_ai_usage cost-log catch blocks in claude.ts (runWithLogging) and voyage.ts (embed) call `Sentry.captureException(logErr, ...)` with the raw error object. The rest of the codebase (whisper.ts line 177, every Inngest function, observability/inngest.ts) deliberately wraps to `name + status` only because, per the in-repo VERIFICATION R4 policy and CLAUDE.md ('never log CV text, candidate names, or any PII to Sentry'), Anthropic/Voyage SDK errors can echo prompt fragments — i.e. raw CV text or candidate data — inside error.message. claude.ts and voyage.ts are the two wrappers that send the actual CV/candidate prompt to the model, so they are the highest-risk place to pass a raw error.
userImpact: Not user-visible, but a compliance/GDPR exposure: a failed ai_usage write during CV parsing or candidate embedding could ship a Claude/Voyage error whose message contains a slice of the candidate's CV (names, emails, salary) to Sentry, defeating the global beforeSend scrub the team relies on. For an 'audit-ready by default' product about to be shown to a client, PII landing in error tooling is a credibility risk.
suggestedFix: Mirror whisper.ts: wrap to name only — `const name = logErr instanceof Error ? logErr.name : 'UnknownError'; Sentry.captureException(new Error('record_ai_usage:' + name), {...})` — in both claude.ts and voyage.ts so no SDK error.message reaches Sentry from the CV-parse / embed paths.
verifierReasoning:
Code confirmed. claude.ts:93 (inside runWithLogging) and voyage.ts:138 (inside embed) both call Sentry.captureException(logErr, {...}) with the RAW error object. whisper.ts:177-178 deliberately wraps to name-only and cites the policy verbatim: "VERIFICATION R4 (Phase 1): never pass the raw error. Anthropic/Voyage SDK errors can echo prompt fragments in error.message... Wrap to name only." The same policy is codified in src/lib/observability/inngest.ts (formatErrorForSentry / readStatus) and applied consistently across every Inngest function and server action (grep shows ~25 call sites all using `new Error(`${name}: ...`)`). So claude.ts and voyage.ts are genuine, isolated violations of an explicit, documented in-repo policy and are inconsistent with every sibling file.

The severity-relevant nuance the claim gets slightly wrong: the `logErr` caught in these two catch blocks is NOT the Anthropic/Voyage SDK error. By the time the catch runs, the model call has already SUCCEEDED (claude.ts:71-97, voyage.ts:112-141) — the catch only wraps the subsequent supabase.rpc('record_ai_usage', ...) Postgres call. The RPC receives only non-PII scalars (org_id, model, purpose, token counts, cost, latency, user_id), so a realistic logErr here is a Postgres/Supabase RPC error (constraint/type/permission), not raw CV text. The probability of an actual CV fragment landing in THIS specific message is therefore much lower than the claim implies ("could ship a Claude/Voyage error whose message contains a slice of the candidate's CV").

That said, I confirmed the team's reliance on source-level wrapping is justified: the global beforeSend in sentry.server.config.ts is key-based (PII_KEYS over event.extra and event.contexts) and does NOT scrub event.exception message strings at all — so any PII that ever did reach an error.message would bypass the scrub. The defense-in-depth concern is real and the suggested fix (mirror whisper.ts / use formatErrorForSentry) is correct and matches the established pattern.

Net: real, materially-equivalent defect (policy/consistency violation + theoretical PII-leak path), but the immediate caught error is a DB RPC error rather than the model-SDK error, so the practical likelihood of CV PII leaking here is low. Not user-visible. Worth a quick fix for consistency and audit posture before a client demo, but not a medium-severity exposure.

title: POST /api/linkedin/ingest has no rate limiting — any authenticated session can drive unbounded billable AI cost
description: The LinkedIn ingest route accepts a payload, upserts a candidate, and fires `linkedin/captured`, which runs a Voyage embed (billable, logged to ai_usage) per request. Unlike the public apply form — which is protected by a Postgres-backed sliding-window rate limiter (supabase/migrations/20260519092946_apply_form_rate_limits.sql: 3 submissions / 5 min / IP / org) and Turnstile — this route has NO rate limiting of any kind. The only gate is a valid Supabase Bearer token (any recruiter session) plus a CORS allowlist (which the helper itself documents as non-security: 'CORS is just to round-trip a legitimate extension'). A compromised, leaked, or simply over-eager recruiter token can POST in a tight loop; each call costs a Voyage embed and writes a candidate row. There is no per-org or per-user throttle and no body-count cap per window.
userImpact: A single leaked/abused recruiter token (or a buggy extension loop) can run up the tenant's AI bill and flood their candidate list with junk rows, with no server-side brake. For a product whose pricing model depends on per-tenant AI cost visibility, this is an uncapped cost-amplification surface reachable from a normal user session.
suggestedFix: Add a per-user (or per-org) sliding-window throttle before the upsert/embed dispatch, reusing the same Postgres rate-limit pattern as the apply form (e.g. N captures / minute / user). Return 429 with a Retry-After when exceeded. This bounds both the AI spend and the junk-row creation from a compromised token.
verifierReasoning: Every factual element of the claim is confirmed against the code. (1) POST /api/linkedin/ingest (src/app/api/linkedin/ingest/route.ts:81-207) gates only on extension version, Bearer getUser(token), Zod validation, and profile lookup — then upserts and fires inngest.send('linkedin/captured'). No rate-limit table or counter is touched; grep confirms apply_form_rate_limits and the only rate-limit module (src/lib/integrations/apply-form-rate-limit.ts) are consumed solely by the public apply action, never by this route. (2) Each captured event runs a billable Voyage embed: src/lib/inngest/functions/embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts:123-131 calls embed(...), whose own comment states it 'Logs cost to ai_usage automatically (D3-24 non-negotiable)'. (3) The embed fires unconditionally — route.ts:178-187 sends the event regardless of upsertResult.data.created. (4) Dedup does NOT neutralize the abuse: upsertCandidateFromLinkedIn dedups on source_detail (the LinkedIn URL, attacker-controlled) then email, so varying linkedin_url per request forces the INSERT branch every time → new candidate row + new embed; even repeating the same URL still re-fires an embed via the update path. (5) CORS is explicitly self-documented as non-security in _cors.ts ('CORS is just to round-trip a legitimate extension'). (6) No platform-level brake exists — no vercel.json, no WAF/firewall config, no Upstash/middleware limiter anywhere in the repo. (7) The asymmetry the claim draws is real: the apply form enforces a 3/5min/IP/org sliding window (checkApplyFormRateLimit), this route enforces nothing. The gap is genuine: an authenticated recruiter token in a tight loop (compromised/leaked, or a buggy extension retry loop) can drive unbounded billable embeds and flood the candidate list with junk rows, with no server-side throttle and no per-window cap, in a product whose pricing model depends on per-tenant AI cost visibility.

title: Sentry beforeSend scrubs extra/contexts but NOT exception messages — PII safety rests entirely on every call site wrapping errors by hand
description: The global beforeSend hook only deletes cookies, strips event.user.email, and runs the key-based `scrub()` over `event.extra` and `event.contexts`. It does NOT touch `event.message` or `event.exception.values[].value` (the actual thrown error text). The entire defence against PII reaching Sentry therefore depends on every single capture call site manually wrapping the error as `new Error(`${name}: ${status}`)` — a convention repeated ~20 times across the Inngest functions and AI wrappers (the 'VERIFICATION R4' comments). This is correct everywhere I audited, but it is a fragile invariant: the moment one future captureException passes a raw Anthropic/Voyage/OpenAI SDK error (whose `.message` can echo prompt fragments / CV text) or a raw Postgres error (whose `.message` can echo row values), that PII flows straight to Sentry because the global net never inspects the message. The scrub is also key-name based, so a CV-text value stored under an unlisted key (e.g. `summary`, `body`, `transcript`, `description`) in `extra` passes through unredacted.
userImpact: A latent compliance landmine: candidate CV text or emails could reach Sentry from a single non-conforming capture call, violating the project's hard 'never log PII' rule, with no automated backstop to catch it. The current code is clean, but the safety is one careless edit away from breaking silently.
suggestedFix: Add a defence-in-depth pass in beforeSend that also runs over event.message and each event.exception.values[].value — at minimum redact strings longer than a sane threshold (CV/transcript text is long) and apply a regex scrub for email addresses. Keep the call-site wrapping convention, but stop relying on it as the only barrier.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the mechanism in sentry.server.config.ts:27-33 (and the identical sentry.edge.config.ts): beforeSend deletes cookies, strips event.user.email, and runs a key-ALLOWLIST scrub() (PII_KEYS = email, phone, cv_text, extracted_data, candidate_email, full_name) over event.extra and event.contexts ONLY. It never inspects event.message or event.exception.values[].value. So the actual thrown error text is never scrubbed, and any PII stored under a non-allowlisted key in extra (summary/body/transcript/description) passes through. Both factual sub-claims about the code are TRUE.

The claim's central narrative is partly WRONG but in a direction that worsens the risk rather than refuting it. The claim says PII safety 'rests entirely on every call site wrapping errors by hand' and that the wrapping convention 'is correct everywhere I audited'. In reality the wrapping convention (new Error(`${name}: ${status}`) / formatErrorForSentry in src/lib/observability/inngest.ts) is applied only at the ~20 AI/Inngest SDK-error sites (the VERIFICATION R4 sites in parse-cv.ts, embed-*.ts, whisper.ts, voyage.ts retries, transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts, etc.). The DOMINANT pattern across the codebase — essentially all of src/lib/db/** (candidates.ts:385 createCandidate, candidate-cvs.ts:109 createCandidateCV, candidates.ts:130/178 semantic search paths, applications.ts, dashboard.ts, etc.) — passes the RAW Postgres/PostgREST error object straight to Sentry.captureException. A Postgres constraint-violation message echoes the conflicting row value (e.g. 'Key (email)=(jane@example.com) already exists'), and PostgREST surfaces it in error.message/details. That raw error lands in event.exception.values[].value, which beforeSend never touches. So the latent PII-to-Sentry path the claim describes is not merely 'one careless edit away' — raw-error capture is already the prevailing pattern. The specific AI-SDK-prompt-fragment scenario the claim leads with is actually the WELL-defended one (R4 wrapping covers it); the genuinely exposed surface is the unwrapped DB layer.

Net: the finding is real and material. It's a defence-in-depth gap against the project's hard 'never log PII' rule. Severity 'medium' holds — impact requires a specific error (a constraint violation / PostgREST detail echoing a row value) to fire, reaches only the agency's own Sentry project (not cross-tenant, not user-facing in a demo), the candidates table currently has no UNIQUE email constraint so the email-collision path is latent, and the highest-volume PII path (CV/prompt text through AI SDK errors) is already wrapped. The suggested fix (add a beforeSend pass over event.message and event.exception.values[].value with a length-threshold + email-regex redaction) is the correct defence-in-depth backstop.

title: Inngest client module (holding INNGEST_EVENT_KEY) lacks `import 'server-only'`, unlike every sibling infra module
description: src/lib/supabase/service.ts, src/lib/encryption.ts, and every src/lib/ai/*.ts wrapper begin with `import 'server-only'` to guarantee they can never be pulled into a client bundle. src/lib/inngest/client.ts does NOT, yet it constructs `new Inngest({ eventKey: env.INNGEST_EVENT_KEY })`, where INNGEST_EVENT_KEY is declared in the SERVER block of env.ts. The `inngest` singleton is imported by server actions and route handlers throughout the app. If any 'use client' component ever imports it (directly or transitively, e.g. to call `inngest.send`), Next would attempt to bundle a server secret. t3-env's server/client split would in practice throw when the client schema is dereferenced, so this is mitigated, but the explicit `server-only` guard that all sibling modules use is missing here — leaving the boundary enforced only by a secondary mechanism rather than the intended primary one.
userImpact: No current user-facing impact (no client component imports it today), but it is an inconsistent guard on a module that holds a server secret, weakening the 'server-only modules cannot reach the client bundle' invariant the rest of the infra layer relies on.
suggestedFix: Add `import 'server-only'` as the first line of src/lib/inngest/client.ts to match service.ts / the AI wrappers, so an accidental client-side import becomes a compile-time error rather than relying on t3-env's runtime throw.
verifierReasoning:
Verified directly against the code. (1) src/lib/inngest/client.ts line 1 is `import { Inngest } from 'inngest'` — there is NO `import 'server-only'`. (2) It constructs `new Inngest({ eventKey: env.INNGEST_EVENT_KEY })`, and INNGEST_EVENT_KEY is declared in the server block of src/lib/env.ts (line 15). (3) The sibling-consistency claim holds: service.ts, encryption.ts, and every secret-holding src/lib/ai/*.ts wrapper (claude, voyage, whisper, cv-extract, embed-text, match, jd-extract, outreach-draft, ad-generate, ffmpeg) all begin with `import 'server-only'`. Only the pure-data file inclusivity-lexicon.ts lacks it, correctly. So client.ts is genuinely the odd one out among secret-bearing infra modules. The factual inconsistency is real and the suggested fix is valid hardening.

However, the claim is — by its own admission — a defense-in-depth/consistency nit, not a live defect. I confirmed all 28 importers of the `inngest` singleton are server actions (actions.ts), route handlers (route.ts), or Inngest function definitions; a `'use client'` scan found ZERO client components importing inngest directly or transitively. The server secret therefore cannot currently reach the client bundle. Even hypothetically, t3-env's server/client split throws if a server var is dereferenced from a client context, so a secondary guard is in place. There is no user-facing impact and no path to one in current code. For a live client demo this is severity ~nil; I keep it at 'low' rather than 'non-issue' only because the missing guard is factually present and the consistency point is legitimate code-hygiene.

title: Outlook OAuth state cookie cleared with bare path/maxAge while set with __Host- prefix — clear may not match, leaving stale CSRF state
description: The OAuth CSRF state cookie is named `__Host-outlook-oauth-state`. The `__Host-` prefix imposes browser-enforced constraints (Secure, Path=/, no Domain). When the callback clears it on the failure/success paths it sets `{ path: '/', maxAge: 0 }` but does NOT set `secure: true`. Browsers require a `__Host-`-prefixed cookie write to include Secure or they reject the write entirely; on the clear path this can mean the deletion silently no-ops, so a stale `state` value can persist in the browser. Because the state value is single-use per OAuth attempt and re-generated by startOutlookOAuthAction, the practical exposure is limited (a fresh attempt overwrites it), but the intended 'clear the cookie before bouncing' hygiene does not reliably happen. The CSRF check itself (cookie === stateParam) is sound.
userImpact: Low: the state-mismatch CSRF guard still works for the active flow, but the defensive cookie-clear on error/success may not take effect, so a stale state cookie can linger. Not exploitable on its own given per-attempt regeneration.
evidence:
const STATE_COOKIE = '__Host-outlook-oauth-state'
...
res.cookies.set(STATE_COOKIE, '', { path: '/', maxAge: 0 })  // no `secure: true`; __Host- writes require Secure
suggestedFix: Include `secure: true` (and keep path: '/') on every res.cookies.set(STATE_COOKIE, ...) clear so the __Host- prefixed deletion is accepted by the browser. Verify the set side in startOutlookOAuthAction uses the same attributes.
verifierReasoning:
Verified the claim against the actual code. The set side (src/app/(app)/settings/integrations/outlook-actions.ts:50-56) writes the cookie with httpOnly:true, secure:true, sameSite:'lax', path:'/', maxAge:600 — a valid __Host- prefix write. All four clear sites in src/app/api/outlook/callback/route.ts (lines 78, 184, 210, 221) call res.cookies.set(STATE_COOKIE, '', { path: '/', maxAge: 0 }) WITHOUT secure:true. I confirmed Next.js's cookie serializer (node_modules/next/dist/compiled/@edge-runtime/cookies/index.js:39) only emits the Secure attribute when `secure` is truthy and does NOT special-case the __Host- prefix. Per RFC 6265bis cookie-prefix rules, a __Host- Set-Cookie (including a Max-Age=0 deletion) must include Secure or the browser rejects the entire header — so the deletion silently no-ops and the original cookie persists. The mechanical premise of the claim is therefore correct: the defensive cookie-clear does not reliably take effect.

However, the user-facing/security impact is genuinely minimal, exactly as the claim states: (1) the CSRF guard itself is a strict equality check (stateCookie !== stateParam, line 71) and is unaffected; (2) the lingering cookie is HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite=Lax, so not JS-readable and not an XSS surface; (3) the set-side maxAge of 600s means the browser expires the cookie within 10 minutes even if the deletion is rejected; (4) every new OAuth attempt overwrites the cookie via a VALID __Host- write, so stale state never accumulates and cannot be replayed against a different issued state. Net effect: a hygiene/defense-in-depth gap, not an exploitable vulnerability. The suggested fix (add secure:true to each clear) is correct and trivial. Confirming as real at low severity for a live client demo.

title: Jobs created from an approved spec call are never reactively embedded or match-scored (wrong event name)
description: After inserting a job from an approved spec draft, the function fires `inngest.send({ name: 'jobs/jd-changed', ... })` to trigger embedding. But NO registered Inngest function listens for `jobs/jd-changed`. The embedder `embedJobOnJDChange` is triggered by `event: 'job/embed'` (embed-job-on-jd-change.ts line 40), and the two manual job-create server actions correctly send `job/embed`. Confirmed via grep: `jobs/jd-changed` is only ever sent, never consumed. As a result the spec->job path skips the entire reactive embed+score chain that embed-job-on-jd-change performs (embed, then step.sendEvent 'job/score-top-candidates'). The job only gets embedded when the 10-minute `embed-batch` cron sweep next runs (NULL-embedding selector), and match precompute (`precomputeMatchesForJob`) never fires for it at all, because that chain is only emitted from embed-job-on-jd-change, not from the batch sweep.
userImpact: In a live demo: recruiter records a spec call, approves the AI-extracted JD, a job is created — but for up to ~10 minutes it is invisible to semantic search, and it never gets the precomputed Sonnet match explanations that manually-created jobs get. The headline 'spec call -> job -> instantly matched candidates' AI flow silently degrades to a delayed, match-less job. The comment on line 211 even claims 'so embed-job-on-jd-change picks it up' — it does not.
evidence: create-job-from-spec.ts: `await inngest.send({ name: 'jobs/jd-changed', data: { organization_id, job_id: newJob.id, user_id } })`  vs  embed-job-on-jd-change.ts: `triggers: [{ event: 'job/embed' }]`. Grep confirms no `triggers: [{ event: 'jobs/jd-changed' }]` anywhere.
suggestedFix: Change the event name in create-job-from-spec.ts to `name: 'job/embed'` to match embedJobOnJDChange's trigger (and match the two job-create server actions that already use 'job/embed'). That single change restores the embed + match-score chain for spec-created jobs.
verifierReasoning:
Attempted to refute but the claim holds in full against the actual code.

(1) src/lib/inngest/functions/create-job-from-spec.ts:216 sends `name: 'jobs/jd-changed'`. A grep across all of src/ shows `jobs/jd-changed` appears in only three locations, ALL inside create-job-from-spec.ts (comment line 211, the send on 216, the Sentry tag string on 225). It is never used as a trigger.

(2) Full enumeration of every inngest.createFunction trigger (from src/app/api/inngest/route.ts registration list + grep of all functions) confirms no function listens for `jobs/jd-changed`. Registered triggers are: outlook/subscription-create-requested, admin/build-vector-index, job/embed, outlook/history-changed, outreach-draft/requested, job/score-top-candidates, spec/uploaded, embed/backfill-org, cv/uploaded, ops/probe-ffmpeg, linkedin/captured, spec-draft/approved. The embedder embedJobOnJDChange triggers on `job/embed` (line 40), not `jobs/jd-changed`.

(3) The two manual job-create server actions (src/app/(app)/jobs/new/actions.ts:61 and src/app/(app)/clients/[id]/jobs/new/actions.ts:66) both correctly send `name: 'job/embed'`, confirming the spec path is the lone inconsistent outlier.

(4) Downstream chain confirmed: precomputeMatchesForJob triggers ONLY on `job/score-top-candidates` (line 71). That event is emitted only from the two manual actions and from embed-job-on-jd-change.ts:116 (step.sendEvent after persist). The embed-batch cron sweep-jobs step (lines 179-244) embeds NULL-embedding jobs but never emits job/score-top-candidates. So a spec-created job, even after the 10-min cron eventually embeds it, never gets match precompute.

(5) The dispatch fails silently: Inngest accepts arbitrary event names, so inngest.send resolves successfully — the try/catch on 214-236 never fires and the function returns {created:true}. The comment on line 211 ('so embed-job-on-jd-change picks it up') is factually wrong.

Impact: the headline 'spec call -> approve JD -> instantly matched candidates' AI flow degrades silently. Spec-created jobs are invisible to semantic search for up to ~10 minutes and NEVER receive the precomputed Sonnet match explanations that manually-created jobs get. The suggested one-line fix (rename to 'job/embed') is correct and matches the two working call sites.

Adjusted severity stays high: this is a demoable headline feature that silently half-works, and the degradation (no match explanations ever) is the exact differentiator the product is built around.

title: Whisper always returns durationSeconds >= 1, so the 'unreadable audio' guard in the spec pipeline is dead code
description: transcribe() computes `const durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0))`. The Math.max(1, ...) clamp means the function can NEVER return a duration of 0 or less, even when Whisper reports no duration (verbose.duration undefined) — it returns 1. The function's own docstring (lines 91-95) claims 'durationSeconds <= 0 -> throws (probe returned malformed/no duration)', but it neither throws nor returns <= 0. Downstream, transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts lines 196-198 guard `if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0) { return { ..., durationSeconds: -1, ... } }` to detect an unreadable file — that branch is unreachable. A corrupt-but-technically-decodable file (or one Whisper returns with no duration) is billed for 1 second and proceeds into JD extraction instead of being failed cleanly with the 'Could not read the audio file. Please re-upload.' message.
userImpact: Recruiter uploads a corrupt/zero-length-after-decode spec recording. Instead of getting the intended clear 'Could not read the audio file, please re-upload' error, the pipeline proceeds, runs (and bills) a Sonnet extraction on whatever scraps Whisper returned, and may surface a garbage or empty JD draft as 'ready_for_review'. The intended safety net silently does nothing.
evidence: whisper.ts: `const durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0))` ... `return { text: verbose.text ?? '', durationSeconds, costPence }`. transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts: `if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0) { return { transcriptText: '', durationSeconds: -1, whisperCostPence: 0 } }`.
suggestedFix: In whisper.ts, separate the cost-clamp from the validity signal: if `verbose.duration` is missing or <= 0, throw (matching the docstring) or return durationSeconds: 0 so the caller's guard fires. Use Math.max(1, ...) only when computing cost, not as the returned duration. The empty-transcript guard (line 230) partially compensates but won't catch a non-empty-text/zero-duration case.
verifierReasoning:
The mechanical finding is correct and verifiable. whisper.ts:154 reads `const durationSeconds = Math.max(1, Math.round(verbose.duration ?? 0))`, which clamps the returned value to a minimum of 1 — it can never return 0 or negative, even when `verbose.duration` is undefined. Consequently the caller's guard (real path is src/lib/inngest/functions/transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts:196-198, NOT the src/lib/inngest/transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts path cited in the claim — the function lives under functions/) `if (transcript.durationSeconds <= 0) { return { transcriptText: '', durationSeconds: -1, whisperCostPence: 0 } }` is unreachable, and the downstream `if (durationSeconds === -1)` branch that emits "Could not read the audio file. Please re-upload." is dead. The docstring at whisper.ts:91-95 claiming "durationSeconds <= 0 -> throws" is also factually wrong: the function neither throws on that condition nor can it return <=0. So the dead-code + stale-docstring claim holds.

However, the claimed USER IMPACT is materially overstated. I traced the full pipeline:
1. Empty buffer -> whisper.ts:105 throws explicitly. Caught.
2. A genuinely corrupt / undecodable file never reaches Whisper at all: recompressToOpus (ffmpeg.ts) runs first and either fires ffmpeg's 'error' event -> throws (line 106), or produces empty output -> throws (line 114). So the "corrupt-but-technically-decodable file proceeds into JD extraction" scenario is blocked upstream of Whisper.
3. A file that decodes to silence (Whisper returns no/zero duration) gets durationSeconds=1, but its `text` is empty/whitespace, so the empty-transcript guard at line 230 fires markSpecFailed("Transcription returned no text. The recording may be silent.") + NonRetriableError. It does NOT proceed to JD extraction or get marked ready_for_review.

So there is no path to a "garbage/empty JD draft surfaced as ready_for_review" — empty transcripts are blocked, and extractJdFromTranscript only runs on non-empty text (line 240). The claim's own caveat concedes line 230 "partially compensates." The residual gap is the near-impossible case where Whisper returns NON-empty text but zero/undefined duration (verbose_json reliably reports duration whenever there is transcribable text); even then the only consequences are (a) ~1 second of Whisper over-billing = Math.ceil(0.48*1/60) = 1 pence, sub-penny, and (b) JD extraction running on real transcribable text rather than garbage. No data corruption, no silent success masquerading as a real result, no cross-tenant exposure, no broken demo flow. The worst observable effect in the realistic no-duration+silent case is a slightly less-apt-but-still-clean failure message ("...may be silent" vs "...re-upload"), both retry-able.

Net: real dead code and a real doc/contract mismatch worth fixing for correctness hygiene (separate the cost clamp from the validity signal as the suggested fix proposes), but the user-facing impact described (billed garbage extraction, garbage JD draft marked ready_for_review, safety net silently does nothing dangerous) does not materialize because of the upstream ffmpeg guard and the line-230 empty-transcript guard. Medium overstates it; this is a low-severity code-quality / dead-code finding.

title: scored / cache_hits counters are mutated inside step.run callbacks and report 0 after Inngest replays
description: `let cacheHits = 0; let scored = 0` are declared in the outer generator body, but `cacheHits++` and `scored++` happen INSIDE `await step.run(\`score-${candidate.id}\`, ...)` callbacks. Inngest re-executes the outer generator on every step boundary and restores memoized step results WITHOUT re-running the step callback. So on the final pass (when all per-candidate steps are memoized), the increment side-effects never re-run and the closure variables remain at their initial 0. The function's return `{ scored, cache_hits: cacheHits, top_n: topCandidates.length }` therefore reports scored=0, cache_hits=0 in the normal multi-step case. The actual DB writes (upsertMatchSummary) are correct — only the returned summary is wrong.
userImpact: No user-facing data corruption — match summaries are still written. But the function's return payload (visible in the Inngest dashboard / any future telemetry) misreports work as 'scored: 0' even when 10 candidates were scored, which will mislead the founder when debugging 'did matching actually run?' during/after the demo.
evidence: precompute-matches-for-job.ts: outer `let cacheHits = 0; let scored = 0`, inside `await step.run(\`score-${candidate.id}\`, async () => { ... cacheHits++ ... scored++ })`, then `return { scored, cache_hits: cacheHits, top_n: topCandidates.length }`.
suggestedFix: Have each `step.run('score-...')` RETURN a small result (e.g. `{ outcome: 'scored' | 'cache_hit' | 'skipped' }`) and accumulate the counts from the awaited return values in the outer scope, so the totals are derived from memoized step outputs rather than from in-callback closure mutation.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against the actual code and the installed Inngest engine (v4.4.0).

CODE (src/lib/inngest/functions/precompute-matches-for-job.ts): The evidence is exact. Lines 205-206 declare `let cacheHits = 0` / `let scored = 0` in the outer function body. The increments only execute inside the per-candidate callback `await step.run(`score-${candidate.id}`, async () => { ... })`: `cacheHits++` at line 258 (cache-hit branch) and `scored++` at line 295 (after a successful upsert). The function returns the outer closure values at line 299: `return { scored, cache_hits: cacheHits, top_n: topCandidates.length }`.

INNGEST SEMANTICS (node_modules/inngest/components/execution/engine.js): Confirmed the memoized-replay model the claim relies on. `startExecution` re-invokes the user function from the top each request (`return this.userFnToRun(this.fnArg)` at line 944), and its return value becomes the `function-resolved` checkpoint. In the fulfilled-step `handle()` path (lines 1377-1416), a memoized step resolves directly from `this.state.stepState[hashedId].data` and the user callback `step.fn` is NOT executed. So on any replay where a per-candidate step is already memoized, its `cacheHits++`/`scored++` side-effect does not re-run. In the standard async model used here (plain `serve()` in src/app/api/inngest/route.ts with no custom checkpointingConfig), the function reaches its `return` on a pass where all N per-candidate steps are memoized, so both counters are 0 at the return.

REACHABILITY: getMatchSummary (src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts) returns `{ ok: true, data }`, so the cache-hit branch is genuinely reachable; the scored++ path follows a successful upsertMatchSummary. Both increment paths are real, not dead code.

CONTRAST CONFIRMS BUG (not intent): embed-batch.ts does its counting inside a single step.run and returns the count — the correct pattern. precompute-matches is the outlier that mutates outer-scope counters from inside per-iteration step callbacks.

IMPACT: As the claim itself concedes, there is NO data corruption — upsertMatchSummary runs inside the (correctly memoized) step callbacks, so match summaries and ai_usage are written correctly. Only the function's return payload is wrong. That payload is not surfaced in any user-facing UI; it appears only in the Inngest run-output/dashboard and any future telemetry. The harm is purely observability: a founder debugging "did matching run?" sees scored=0/cache_hits=0 despite real work. Real and correctly diagnosed, but cosmetic. 'low' stands. The suggested fix (return a small outcome object from each step and accumulate from the awaited memoized return values) is the correct remedy.

title: Voyage embed maps to empty vectors with no count/order validation; candidates can be silently skipped without being re-queued
description: `embed()` builds `const vectors = (response.data ?? []).map((d) => d.embedding ?? [])` with no check that `response.data.length === args.inputs.length` and no use of any per-item index. Callers zip by positional index: embed-batch.ts pairs `vectors[i]` with `usable[i]` and `if (!pair || !vector || vector.length === 0) continue`. Two latent issues: (1) if Voyage ever returns fewer/reordered items than inputs, candidates silently get the wrong vector or get skipped, and because the skip is silent the candidate's `candidate_embedding` stays NULL — which the next sweep will retry, but a misalignment (wrong vector assigned) would NOT be retried and would corrupt search results. (2) An item that comes back with `embedding` null/empty is dropped via `continue` with no Sentry breadcrumb, so a partial-failure embed batch leaves rows un-embedded with zero observability.
userImpact: Best case: a few candidates stay un-embedded and invisible to semantic search until a later sweep (delay only). Worst case (reorder): a candidate is searchable under the wrong person's vector, so 'senior Python dev' returns the wrong human — the exact failure the AI-search core promise is supposed to prevent — with no log to explain it.
evidence: voyage.ts: `const vectors = (response.data ?? []).map((d) => d.embedding ?? [])` (no length/index assertion). embed-batch.ts: `const vector = vectors[i]; if (!pair || !vector || vector.length === 0) continue` (silent skip).
suggestedFix: In embed(): assert `response.data.length === args.inputs.length` (throw or Sentry-warn on mismatch), and if Voyage returns an `index` field, re-sort by it before mapping. In the batch callers, when a vector is empty/missing, emit a Sentry breadcrumb instead of a bare `continue` so silent NULL-embedding stalls are observable.
verifierReasoning:
The code is exactly as cited. src/lib/ai/voyage.ts:120 builds `const vectors = (response.data ?? []).map((d) => d.embedding ?? [])` with no length check against args.inputs.length and no use of the per-item index. I confirmed against the bundled SDK type at node_modules/voyageai/dist/esm/api/types/EmbedResponseDataItem.d.mts that EmbedResponseDataItem carries an `index` field documented as "An integer representing the index of the embedding within the list of embeddings" — the SDK explicitly provides the ordering safeguard the wrapper discards, mapping purely by array position. src/lib/inngest/functions/embed-batch.ts:149-152 (candidates) and :220-223 (jobs) zip vectors[i] with usable[i] and do a bare `if (!pair || !vector || vector.length === 0) continue` — a silent skip with no Sentry breadcrumb. So both cited mechanical facts are accurate.

However, two material corrections to the claimed impact reduce severity:

(1) The "silently skipped without being re-queued" framing is wrong for the skip-on-empty case. embed-batch's sweep query filters `.is('candidate_embedding', null)` / `.is('job_embedding', null)` and runs every 10 minutes, so a row left NULL by a `continue` IS re-queued automatically — it is self-healing. The claim itself concedes this. The only real cost of the silent skip is a ~10-minute delay plus zero observability (no breadcrumb).

(2) The genuinely dangerous case — reorder/misalignment assigning a candidate the wrong person's vector (non-null, so never retried, corrupting search) — is purely latent. Voyage's batch API returns `data` in input order today; reordering or partial-subset returns are not documented behavior the provider exhibits. The bug only fires under a provider behavior that does not currently occur. It's a real robustness gap (relying on undocumented ordering and throwing away the SDK's `index` safety field), not an active failure.

Scope is also narrow: of the five embed() call sites, only embed-batch.ts uses multi-input batches. parse-cv.ts:308, embed-job-on-jd-change.ts:85, embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts:124, and the search path in candidates.ts:120 all pass single-element `inputs: [...]` and read vectors[0], so positional mapping is irrelevant and a missing vector throws (not a silent skip) for them.

Net: real code smell with a sound fix (assert response.data.length === inputs.length, re-sort by d.index, emit a breadcrumb instead of bare continue), but no user-facing failure under current Voyage behavior and the silent path is recovered by the NULL sweep. Defensive hardening, not a live-demo bug — low, not medium.

title: Sonnet cost formula is duplicated in three files; drift will silently desync denormalised cost columns from ai_usage
description: The canonical Sonnet pricing (240 input / 1200 output pence per MTok) lives in claude.ts's PRICING_PENCE_PER_MTOK and calcCostPence (which uses a single Math.ceil over the summed total). jd-extract.ts re-derives it as `Math.ceil(inputCost + outputCost)` (line 186-188) for spec_drafts.sonnet_cost_pence, and ad-generate.ts re-derives the same constants again (lines 34-41) for job_ads.cost_pence. All three currently agree numerically, but the constants are copy-pasted in three places with only a comment ('if it drifts there, sync here too') guarding them. When Anthropic pricing is reverified and claude.ts is updated, the two denormalised columns will silently keep billing at the old rate while ai_usage (the source of truth) uses the new rate, producing inconsistent per-call cost figures on /settings/usage.
userImpact: Not demo-breaking today, but a future pricing update will make the per-spec-call and per-ad cost hints disagree with the authoritative ai_usage ledger — exactly the per-tenant cost visibility the product sells as a SaaS-pricing tool. The discrepancy would be subtle and hard to trace.
evidence: jd-extract.ts: `const inputCost = (240 * response.usage.input_tokens) / 1_000_000; const outputCost = (1200 * response.usage.output_tokens) / 1_000_000; const costPence = Math.ceil(inputCost + outputCost)`. ad-generate.ts: `const SONNET_INPUT_P_PER_MTOK = 240; const SONNET_OUTPUT_P_PER_MTOK = 1200`.
suggestedFix: Export the pricing table and a `calcCostPence(model, inputTokens, outputTokens)` from claude.ts and import it in jd-extract.ts and ad-generate.ts instead of re-declaring the constants, so there is a single source of truth for the formula and the rounding semantics (note: claude.ts rounds the summed total once; the duplicated versions round the sum once too, but ensure they never diverge to per-term rounding).
verifierReasoning:
Verified all three files. The Sonnet pricing constants are genuinely duplicated in three places: (1) /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/ai/claude.ts:38-47 — canonical PRICING_PENCE_PER_MTOK ({input:240,output:1200} for claude-sonnet-4-6) and calcCostPence, which is the value written to the authoritative ai_usage ledger via runWithLogging/record_ai_usage; (2) /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/ai/jd-extract.ts:186-188 — hardcoded 240/1200 re-derivation for the spec_drafts.sonnet_cost_pence denormalised column; (3) /Users/aj_mac/altus-recruitment/src/lib/ai/ad-generate.ts:34-41 — SONNET_INPUT_P_PER_MTOK=240 / SONNET_OUTPUT_P_PER_MTOK=1200 constants feeding calcSonnetCostPence, written to job_ads.cost_pence (used at lines 304 and 355).

Confirmed the denormalised columns exist: spec_drafts.sonnet_cost_pence (supabase/migrations/20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql:79) and job_ads.cost_pence (supabase/migrations/20260520020702_phase3_job_ads.sql:57). Confirmed the only guard is the comment at ad-generate.ts:31-33 ('if it drifts there, sync here too').

The three formulas are numerically identical TODAY: canonical = Math.ceil((240*in + 1200*out)/1e6); both duplicates = Math.ceil((240*in)/1e6 + (1200*out)/1e6) — algebraically the same single-ceil result (the reviewer's note about rounding semantics is correct: all three round the summed total once, none use per-term rounding). The only theoretical divergence today is a one-ULP float boundary flip in Math.ceil, which is negligible and not the substance of the claim.

The genuine risk is exactly as described: claude.ts's comment (lines 26-31) explicitly says 'Re-verify before next major launch.' A future reverification that updates only claude.ts would leave the two denormalised cost columns billing at the old rate while ai_usage (source of truth) uses the new rate — desyncing the per-call cost hints from the authoritative ledger that the product sells as a SaaS per-tenant cost-visibility tool. (outreach-draft.ts also mentions 240/1200 but only in a comment, line 22 — no code duplication there, so the count of three derivation sites is accurate.)

Severity 'low' is correct: zero user-facing impact for a live demo, no current numerical bug, purely a latent maintainability/single-source-of-truth risk conditional on a future one-sided pricing edit. The suggested fix (export calcCostPence/pricing from claude.ts and import it) is sound. The 'schema-mismatch' category label is a slight misnomer — this is code duplication / DRY, not an actual schema/column mismatch — but the technical substance of the finding is accurate.

title: Ad generation + inclusivity scoring Sonnet calls have NO per-org monthly spend ceiling (only match_score is capped)
description: The match-scoring path enforces env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE in BOTH the Inngest precompute function and the on-demand explainCandidateMatchAction (matches/actions.ts:131). But generateAdAction and scoreInclusivityAction call Sonnet (~1.8p and ~0.7p per call) with no spend guard whatsoever. getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth only sums `purpose='match_score'` rows, so ad_generate / ad_inclusivity_score / spec_jd_extract / dormant_outreach_draft / cv_parse spend is entirely uncapped. A recruiter (or a runaway client) repeatedly clicking 'Generate ad' or pasting ads to score can burn unbounded Sonnet cost with no product-side brake.
userImpact: On a live SaaS, one org's enthusiastic (or buggy/scripted) use of the ad generator silently inflates the AI bill with no cap and no operator alert — the opposite of the 'track cost per tenant, protect SaaS pricing' principle. The match feature is protected; the ad/outreach/spec features are not.
suggestedFix: Generalise the spend ceiling: make getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth (or a sibling) sum all Sonnet purposes for the org/month, and apply a ceiling check in generateAdAction, scoreInclusivityAction, and the spec/outreach Inngest functions. Even a generous shared cap with a Sentry warning gives operators a brake before the bill surprises them.
verifierReasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual code. (1) getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth in src/lib/db/ai-summaries.ts:238 filters .eq('purpose', 'match_score'), so it only sums match-scoring spend — its own doc comment says so. (2) The spend ceiling env.MAX_MONTHLY_MATCH_SPEND_PENCE is enforced in exactly two AI paths: matches/actions.ts:131 (on-demand explain) and src/lib/inngest/functions/precompute-matches-for-job.ts (precompute batch). No other source file imports getOrgMatchSpendThisMonth as a guard. (3) generateAdAction and scoreInclusivityAction in src/app/(app)/jobs/[id]/ad-panel/actions.ts call generateAdWithInclusivity / scoreInclusivityOnly, which (ad-generate.ts:259, :312) call runWithLogging with model 'claude-sonnet-4-6' and purpose 'ad_generate' (~1.8p) / 'ad_inclusivity_score' (~0.7p). Neither the actions nor the helpers reference env, the ceiling constant, or any spend check. (4) runWithLogging (claude.ts:65) logs cost AFTER the call; it has no pre-call spend gate. (5) Other Sonnet purposes (dormant_outreach_draft, spec_jd_extract, cv_parse) are in separate ai_usage buckets the ceiling function never reads, so they are uncapped too. Callers are recruiter-clicked UI buttons (ad-panel.tsx:145, :263), so repeated clicks are a realistic unbounded-cost path. The claim is materially accurate in every specific. I down-grade from medium to low because a real visibility backstop exists: every call writes ai_usage and settings/usage/page.tsx surfaces per-tenant spend, per-call cost is small, and exploitation needs unusually heavy or scripted use rather than a passive flow — this is a hardening/consistency gap, not a demo-breaking or data-integrity bug.

title: Browser Supabase client uses default navigator.locks (no noopLock) — risks the lock-stolen auth wedge documented as a hard-won lesson
description: Global CLAUDE.md explicitly documents the @supabase/ssr browser-lock contention lesson: createBrowserClient defaults to navigator.locks for auth-token serialisation, and single-account SPAs get bitten by the lock-stolen wedge (verifyOtp + AuthProvider init in parallel, signOut hangs). The documented default is to pass `auth: { lock: noopLock }`. This client passes no auth options at all, so it uses the default lock. The project is exactly the single-agency single-account shape the lesson warns about, and the package is @supabase/ssr 0.10.3 (within the affected range).
userImpact: Intermittent 'clicked sign-in/sign-out and nothing happened, had to refresh' wedges during the live demo — the classic symptom. Hard to reproduce on demand, embarrassing if it fires in front of the client.
suggestedFix: Add the documented noopLock: `const noopLock = async (_n, _t, fn) => fn()` and pass `{ auth: { lock: noopLock } }` to createBrowserClient, per the global CLAUDE.md guidance, unless cross-tab session serialisation is genuinely needed (it isn't for a 2-3 person agency).
verifierReasoning:
The factual evidence is accurate. src/lib/supabase/client.ts:6-11 calls createBrowserClient with no `auth` options, so it uses the default navigator.locks lock — exactly as cited. The installed @supabase/ssr is 0.10.3, and the browser client is genuinely used for live auth methods: signInWithOtp (sign-in-form.tsx:102, sign-up-form.tsx:27), signInWithPassword (E2E-gated, line 84), and signOut (sign-out-button.tsx:16). The global CLAUDE.md does document a preference to pass noopLock by default, so omitting it is a real deviation from project convention, and adding it is a cheap, reasonable hardening. That makes the claim technically real.

However, the claimed user-facing IMPACT is materially overstated on two grounds. (1) Version: the documented lesson explicitly scopes the lock-stolen wedge to "@supabase/ssr ≤ v0.10.2". The installed version is 0.10.3 — newer than the documented affected ceiling, not "within the affected range" as the claim asserts. (2) Trigger pattern absent: the lesson's deadliest documented trigger is "verifyOtp + AuthProvider init in parallel". This codebase has NO verifyOtp call (grep confirms only exchangeCodeForSession, which runs server-side in callback/route.ts), and NO client-side AuthProvider/onAuthStateChange subscriber at all. The browser client is instantiated fresh per click inside isolated handlers (not a long-lived singleton), each guarded by a pending flag, with no concurrent auth operation racing for the lock. Sign-in doesn't even establish a session in that tab (it sends a magic link). So the canonical parallel-init race that produces the wedge cannot form here. The "classic intermittent sign-in/sign-out wedge during the demo" symptom is not substantiated.

title: ai_usage cost-log failure is swallowed on every wrapper — a Claude/Voyage/Whisper call can succeed while its cost is silently dropped from the per-tenant ledger
description: runWithLogging (claude.ts:80-96), embed (voyage.ts:125-141) and transcribe (whisper.ts:159-181) all wrap the record_ai_usage RPC in a try/catch that captures to Sentry but returns the AI result anyway. This is documented as 'non-fatal by design', and that is defensible for not breaking the feature. But the consequence is that ai_usage — described in CLAUDE.md as the non-negotiable source of truth for per-tenant cost — can systematically undercount if the RPC flakes (e.g. a transient service-role auth blip or a Postgres connection limit). There is no reconciliation, no retry of just the log write, and no counter the operator can watch. Because the RPC is granted only to service_role and createServiceClient() is constructed per-call, a misconfigured SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY would make EVERY cost log fail while every AI call still succeeds — and the only signal is a stream of Sentry events the non-technical owner won't read.
userImpact: The founder believes ai_usage is authoritative for SaaS pricing decisions; under RPC failure it silently under-reports real spend, so pricing/margin decisions are made on incomplete cost data. The feature looks healthy while the ledger lies.
suggestedFix: Keep non-fatal, but (a) add a single retry of the log write before giving up, and (b) capture as a Sentry message with level:'error' and a distinct tag (e.g. ai_usage_drop) so an alert rule can page on a sustained failure rather than burying it among normal exceptions. Optionally emit an Inngest event to a dead-letter table so dropped cost rows can be reconciled.
verifierReasoning: All mechanical facts in the claim are verified in the actual code. (1) The swallow exists exactly as described: src/lib/ai/claude.ts:80-96 awaits supabase.rpc('record_ai_usage', ...) inside try/catch, captures to Sentry, then `return response` at line 97; src/lib/ai/voyage.ts:125-141 does the same before returning vectors; src/lib/ai/whisper.ts:159-181 does the same before returning the transcript. (2) record_ai_usage (supabase/migrations/20260513152244_phase1_domain_schema.sql:130-154) is a single INSERT with no internal retry/idempotency, granted only to service_role (line 540). It is the SOLE write path to ai_usage — all sibling wrappers (match.ts, ad-generate.ts, jd-extract.ts, outreach-draft.ts) route through runWithLogging, so the swallow is centralized. (3) /settings/usage (page.tsx:29-31) reads ai_usage directly with no independent cost source, so a dropped row is unreconcilable and invisible to the operator. (4) Grep confirms NONE of the claim's mitigations exist: no log-write retry, no 'ai_usage_drop' tag, no level:'error' escalation on these captures, no dead-letter table, no reconciliation. (5) The misconfigured-key amplification is real: SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY is validated only as z.string().min(1) (env.ts:13), so a wrong-but-nonempty key passes boot validation and would then fail every record_ai_usage RPC at runtime while every AI call still succeeds. So the claim is factually accurate as a silent-undercount risk on the per-tenant cost ledger that CLAUDE.md calls non-negotiable. Where I diverge from the reviewer is on severity for the stated context (a live client demo). This is purely an internal observability/data-integrity gap: the recruiter- and candidate-facing flows are unaffected, the behaviour is explicitly documented as 'non-fatal by design' (a defensible fail-open choice — do not break AI features to log cost), and the failure is NOT fully silent — it does emit a Sentry exception on every drop. The real gap is prioritization (default-level Sentry exception buried among normal noise, no alertable counter) plus absence of reconciliation, which together mean a sustained failure could go unnoticed and skew the founder's pricing math. That is a legitimate hardening item but low user-facing impact, so 'medium' slightly over-states it; low is the honest grade.

title: LinkedIn embed purpose 'linkedin_candidate_embed' is forced through the EmbedPurpose union with `as never`, defeating the type guard that prevents typo'd purposes
description: voyage.ts defines EmbedPurpose as a closed union: 'candidate_embed' | 'job_embed' | 'search_query_embed'. The LinkedIn function passes `purpose: 'linkedin_candidate_embed' as never` to slip a new value past the compiler. It works at runtime (ai_usage.purpose is free text), and the cost IS logged, so this is not a missing-log bug. But the `as never` cast disables the very type safety that would catch a future typo'd purpose string (e.g. 'linkedin_candiate_embed') that would silently fragment the per-tenant cost breakdown in /settings/usage. The other purposes are type-checked; this one is not.
userImpact: Cost is still recorded, so no immediate user impact. Risk is a future silent mis-categorisation of LinkedIn embed spend if the literal is ever mistyped, since the compiler is told to ignore it.
suggestedFix: Add 'linkedin_candidate_embed' to the EmbedPurpose union in voyage.ts and drop the `as never` cast, so the purpose string is compiler-checked like every other one.
verifierReasoning: Every cited fact is verified in the actual code. voyage.ts:75 defines `type EmbedPurpose = 'candidate_embed' | 'job_embed' | 'search_query_embed'` — a closed union that does NOT include 'linkedin_candidate_embed'. embed-candidate-from-linkedin.ts:127 passes `purpose: 'linkedin_candidate_embed' as never`, which is precisely a cast to slip an off-union literal past the compiler (the `EmbedArgs.purpose` field is typed `EmbedPurpose` at voyage.ts:80). The runtime works: ai_usage.purpose is `text not null` with no enum/CHECK constraint (migration 20260513152244, line 375), and embed() unconditionally logs cost to record_ai_usage with args.purpose (voyage.ts:127-136) — so this is NOT a missing-log or cost-gap bug, exactly as the reviewer states. /settings/usage groups by `r.purpose` as a free string (page.tsx:101-110), so the value renders as its own correct breakdown row today, but a future typo in the literal would silently fragment that category. The reviewer's self-grade of 'low' is accurate and honest: there is zero current user-facing impact (feature works, cost logged, breakdown correct), and the risk is purely a hypothetical future mistype. I confirm the 'low' grade rather than escalating — for a live client demo this is invisible. Minor caveats: (1) the 'schema-mismatch' category is slightly mislabeled — there is no schema mismatch, the text column accepts the value cleanly; it's a type-safety/cast hygiene issue. (2) Claude-based purposes (claude.ts:53 types purpose as plain `string`) are already unchecked, so the only type-checked purpose surface is the three Voyage embed purposes, and `as never` removes that one remaining guard for the LinkedIn path. The suggested fix (add 'linkedin_candidate_embed' to the union, drop the cast) is correct and is the right way to restore the guard. Real issue, correctly low severity.

title: Sonnet pricing constants are duplicated in three files (claude.ts, ad-generate.ts, jd-extract.ts) — drift will desync the denormalised cost_pence columns from the ai_usage ledger
description: The canonical Sonnet pricing (240 input / 1200 output pence per MTok) lives in claude.ts PRICING_PENCE_PER_MTOK. ad-generate.ts re-declares SONNET_INPUT_P_PER_MTOK=240 / SONNET_OUTPUT_P_PER_MTOK=1200 to compute job_ads.cost_pence, and jd-extract.ts re-derives the same 240/1200 inline for spec_drafts.sonnet_cost_pence. All three currently agree, and the code comments acknowledge the duplication and say 'if it drifts there, sync here too' — but that is a manual coupling with no test asserting equality. When Anthropic pricing is re-verified (the comments say re-verify before next launch), updating claude.ts alone will silently leave the two denormalised display columns wrong while ai_usage stays correct.
userImpact: After a future pricing update, the per-ad / per-spec cost shown on the job_ads / spec_drafts UI would disagree with the authoritative ai_usage total, eroding trust in the cost numbers the owner is using to reason about margin.
suggestedFix: Export a calcCostPence(model, inputTokens, outputTokens) helper from claude.ts and have ad-generate.ts and jd-extract.ts import it for their costPence return values, so there is one pricing source. Or add a unit test asserting the three constants match.
verifierReasoning: All factual assertions in the claim are verified against the code. (1) claude.ts:38-42 holds the canonical PRICING_PENCE_PER_MTOK with sonnet-4-6 = {input:240, output:1200}, and its calcCostPence (line 44) is NOT exported. (2) ad-generate.ts:34-35 re-declares SONNET_INPUT_P_PER_MTOK=240 / SONNET_OUTPUT_P_PER_MTOK=1200 to compute job_ads.cost_pence (column confirmed in migration 20260520020702_phase3_job_ads.sql:57, wired via the action that inserts the ad). (3) jd-extract.ts:186-187 re-derives 240/1200 inline for spec_drafts.sonnet_cost_pence (column confirmed in migration 20260520003437_phase3_spec_drafts.sql:79, wired through transcribe-and-structure-spec.ts:271). (4) The only relevant test (tests/unit/lib/ai/jd-extract.test.ts:142) asserts merely costPence > 0 and itself hardcodes 240/1200 in a comment, so it would NOT catch drift; no test asserts equality of the three constants. (5) The code comments (ad-generate.ts:33 'if it drifts there, sync here too'; claude.ts:31 'Re-verify before next major launch') explicitly acknowledge the manual coupling. So the duplication, the absence of a guard, and the future-drift mechanism are all real. However, the impact is correctly bounded: all three values currently agree, so there is NO present bug — today's cost_pence/sonnet_cost_pence columns are correct. The harm is purely latent and conditional on a future pricing edit that updates claude.ts but forgets the two siblings, and even then it only desyncs a denormalised DISPLAY hint on /settings/usage (ai_usage remains the source of truth) — no data corruption, no tenant leak, no broken flow, and nothing visible in a client demo. The 'schema-mismatch' category label is a slight misnomer (the columns exist and types match; this is a duplicated-constant/DRY maintainability issue), but the substance of the finding is accurate.
